domains.description: Backend development routing skill that coordinates two specialized sub-skills for systems-level (Go, Rust, databases, caching) and enterprise web backends (Java, C#, PHP, Ruby). Routes tasks based on technology stack and application needs. Covers microservices, database design, ORM patterns, and enterprise application development.

Covers architecture patterns, code decomposition, refactoring, code review practices, and tech debt management across multiple paradigms. Includes clean architecture, DDD, CQRS, event sourcing, code review processes, development practices like XP and pair programming, and technical debt lifecycle management.

methodologies.name: code-coverage
methodologies.description: Code coverage measures what portion of your source code is executed during testing, helping identify untested paths. Apply it to set minimum coverage standards in CI/CD and prioritize testing efforts on critical business logic. This methodology delivers visibility into gaps and guides testing focus, though high coverage alone doesn't guarantee quality—aim for 80%+ on critical paths rather than chasing 100% on trivial code.

methodologies.name: code-decomposition-patterns
methodologies.description: Decomposition patterns provide concrete techniques for breaking large code units into manageable pieces: extract service (move logic from views), extract component (split UI into focused parts), extract module (group related functionality), extract configuration (separate environment-specific settings), and extract types (consolidate definitions). Use these patterns when files exceed size limits or when multiple people edit the same file, reducing conflicts and improving readability.

methodologies.name: code-decomposition-principles
methodologies.description: Code decomposition principles guide when and how to split files based on single responsibility, optimal file size (50-200 lines for most types), and LLM-friendly context budgeting. Apply the checklist: split when lines exceed 300, multiple classes exist, or responsibilities mix; don't split tightly coupled code or small units under 100 lines. This delivers 40-60% complexity reduction in core codebase and enables parallel development.

methodologies.name: code-quality-trends
methodologies.description: Code quality trends 2026 consolidates current best practices including TypeScript 5 strict mode, React 19 server components, Python 3.12+ type hints, and 81% team adoption of AI coding tools. Reference this to align your stack with production-ready standards: TypeScript strict, React 19, Next.js 15, Python 3.12+, 80%+ test coverage, and Lighthouse >90.

methodologies.name: code-review
methodologies.description: Code review is systematic examination of source code to find bugs, improve quality, ensure consistency, and share knowledge. This methodology is split into fundamentals (basics.md covering principles and checklists) and process (process.md covering templates and scenarios). Apply reviews to all code changes before merging, especially security-sensitive and architectural changes, delivering 20-30% reduction in production defects.

methodologies.name: code-review-basics
methodologies.description: Code review basics covers what to examine (correctness, design, maintainability, testing, performance, security), how to write constructive comments (distinguish blocking bugs from suggestions and nitpicks), and how to respond promptly. Use this to establish review standards that balance thoroughness with velocity—review within 4 hours, distinguish comment types clearly, and approve when good enough rather than perfect.

methodologies.name: code-review-process
methodologies.description: Code review process provides PR templates, automated checks, and concrete review scenarios (bugs, design issues, security problems, N+1 queries). Implement this to standardize review workflow: keep PRs under 400 lines, provide clear descriptions, use CI checks for formatting/linting, track metrics like time-to-review and rework rate. This delivery speeds feedback cycles and catches issues before production.

methodologies.name: documentation
methodologies.description: Documentation creation (CLAUDE.md style) establishes AI-readable context files of 100-150 lines per directory, covering purpose, structure, key concepts, entry points, and common operations. Create CLAUDE.md for every module with source code, using progressive detail and self-contained sections. This methodology delivers clarity for LLM-assisted development, enables faster onboarding, and serves as a single source of truth for architectural context.

methodologies.name: mob-programming
methodologies.description: Mob programming is all-hands-on-deck development where the whole team works on one thing together with driver (keyboard), navigators (direction), and optional facilitator (timer/focus). Apply this to complex features, critical bugs, and team learning—rotate driver every 5-15 minutes with clear communication. This delivers superior code quality, distributed knowledge, eliminates silos, and reduces onboarding time despite perceived efficiency loss.

methodologies.name: pair-programming
methodologies.description: Pair programming pairs two developers at one workstation: driver types while navigator reviews and thinks strategically, switching roles frequently. Use it for complex/unfamiliar code, knowledge transfer, critical logic, and difficult debugging—skip it for routine tasks or when deep focus is required. This methodology reduces bugs, spreads knowledge, and prevents single points of failure, with studies showing net positive productivity.

methodologies.name: refactoring-patterns
methodologies.description: Refactoring patterns (extract method/function, extract class, replace conditional with polymorphism, introduce parameter object, replace magic numbers, decompose conditional) improve code readability and maintainability without changing behavior. Apply incrementally in small steps with tests green throughout; use IDE refactoring tools for safety. This delivers cleaner code that's easier to maintain and extend.

methodologies.name: tech-debt-basics
methodologies.description: Technical debt represents the cost of choosing quick solutions over better approaches, tracked in debt registers with type (code/design/test/infrastructure), severity, location, and estimated fix effort. Manage debt explicitly: classify by Martin Fowler's quadrant (reckless/prudent, deliberate/inadvertent), measure complexity and coverage, and budget time for paydown. This methodology prevents debt accumulation and maintains feature velocity.

domains.description: Orchestrator for developer tooling coordinating two sub-skills: code quality/architecture (DDD, CQRS, code review, refactoring, tech debt) and automation/tooling (browser automation, CI/CD, monorepo management, performance testing). Addresses both improving code quality and automating development workflows.

Specializes in JavaScript and TypeScript development for React, Node.js, Next.js, and modern JavaScript ecosystem. Covers component architecture, hooks, state management, Next.js app router and server components, Express and Fastify backends, TypeScript patterns, and modern JavaScript features including Bun runtime support.

methodologies.name: bun-runtime-simple
methodologies.description: Bun is a high-performance JavaScript runtime with built-in tooling (bundler, transpiler, test runner, package manager) and native APIs (password hashing, WebSocket, file operations). Use Bun for new TypeScript-first projects needing maximum performance; stick with Node.js for legacy code and maximum compatibility. This runtime delivers 1.5-3x speed improvements over Node.js.

methodologies.name: decomposition-react
methodologies.description: React decomposition patterns establish LLM-friendly structure with components under 150 lines, separate hooks (40-60 lines), and feature-based organization rather than layer-based. Break god components into presentational components, custom hooks for state, and services for API calls. This approach improves readability, testability, and reusability while keeping files manageable for AI-assisted development.

methodologies.name: javascript-modern
methodologies.description: Modern JavaScript/TypeScript 2025-2026 establishes core patterns: TypeScript strict mode, prefer const and arrow functions, named exports over default, explicit return types for public APIs. Use Node.js 22 LTS or Bun for runtimes; React 19 or Next.js 15 for frontend; Express 5 or Fastify 5 for backend. This stack delivers type safety, better tooling, and performance improvements.

methodologies.name: javascript-testing
methodologies.description: JavaScript testing with Vitest and Jest covers unit tests (isolated fast tests), component testing (React Testing Library), mocking (vi.mock), and API testing (MSW). Structure tests with describe/it blocks, focus on user behavior not implementation, use type-safe mocks. Aim for 80%+ coverage on critical paths, test edge cases and error conditions, and keep test files maintainable.

methodologies.name: nodejs-express
methodologies.description: Express is a mature, ecosystem-rich Node.js framework for REST APIs with composable middleware, centralized error handling, and graceful shutdown. Use Express when ecosystem compatibility matters or for teams familiar with middleware patterns; structure with routers, validation middleware, auth, and error handlers. This framework offers maximum flexibility and community support with moderate learning curve.

methodologies.name: nodejs-fastify
methodologies.description: Fastify is a high-performance Node.js framework with plugin architecture, built-in schema validation (TypeBox/JSON Schema), type safety, and native async support. Use Fastify for performance-critical APIs or TypeScript-first projects; it runs 2-3x faster than Express in benchmarks. This framework delivers automatic OpenAPI docs, cleaner code, and better DevX.

methodologies.name: nodejs-patterns
methodologies.description: Node.js patterns establish backend structure: controllers (HTTP handlers), services (business logic), middleware (auth, logging, validation), models (entities), and error handlers. Apply dependency injection, async/await, and proper error handling. Use Pino for structured logging, Zod for validation, custom error classes for context, and graceful shutdown hooks.

methodologies.name: react-hooks
methodologies.description: React hooks enable functional components with state, side effects, and custom logic extraction. Master useState (with lazy initialization), useEffect (with cleanup and dependencies), useCallback/useMemo (for memoization), useRef (for persistent values), useReducer (for complex state), and Context (for global state). Follow the rules of hooks strictly, manage dependencies carefully, and avoid common pitfalls like stale closures.

methodologies.name: react-patterns
methodologies.description: React patterns guide component architecture (feature-based directory structure, reusable UI primitives), functional components with explicit props typing, custom hooks for stateful logic, Context for cross-component state, and performance optimization (memo, useMemo, useCallback). Decide state management based on: server state (TanStack Query), form state (React Hook Form), global UI state (Zustand), or local state (useState).

methodologies.name: typescript-patterns
methodologies.description: TypeScript patterns enable advanced type safety: utility types (Partial, Pick, Omit), generics with constraints, type guards, discriminated unions, assertion functions, Zod schema validation, and branded types. Use template literal types for type-safe strings, conditional types for transformations, and const assertions for literal inference. This delivers compile-time safety and prevents runtime errors.

methodologies.name: typescript-react-2026
methodologies.description: TypeScript & React 2026 patterns establish modern stack: TypeScript 5.x strict mode, React 19 with Server Components, Next.js 15 App Router. Key practices: server components for data fetching (zero JS), minimal client boundaries, Server Actions for mutations, type-safe props, and streaming for performance. This architecture delivers 38% faster initial loads.

methodologies.name: typescript-strict-mode
methodologies.description: TypeScript strict mode enables all type safety checks: noImplicitAny, strictNullChecks, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, exactOptionalPropertyTypes. Master null handling with optional chaining, type narrowing with guards, branded types for primitive safety, and const assertions for literal types. Use unknown over any, avoid non-null assertions, and validate at runtime. This delivers production-ready type safety.

domains.description: Specializes in Python backend development with Django full-stack applications, FastAPI async APIs, pytest testing frameworks, and Python best practices. Covers Django models, services, API design with DRF, async patterns, type hints, Celery task queues, and Python code quality with tools like ruff and mypy.

methodologies.name: django-api
methodologies.description: Django REST API development compares Django REST Framework (DRF 3.15+, mature, 1000+ packages, best for enterprise) with Django Ninja (1.x, modern, fast, best for greenfield projects). Choose DRF for complex permissions and large teams; choose Ninja for performance and modern Python. Implement ViewSets/routers for CRUD, APIViews for custom endpoints, JWT authentication with 15-30 min access tokens, cursor pagination for large datasets, and drf-spectacular for OpenAPI docs.

methodologies.name: django-base-model
methodologies.description: The BaseModel pattern provides a consistent foundation for all Django models with UUID and timestamp fields, preventing ID enumeration attacks and enabling audit trails. Use this when starting any Django project or refactoring existing models to eliminate duplicate code across all model classes. It delivers security, maintainability, and a single source of truth for common behaviors across your data layer.

methodologies.name: django-constants
methodologies.description: Eliminates magic strings and integer literals from Django apps by mandating TextChoices and IntegerChoices for every CharField or IntegerField with a fixed set of values, and a per-app constants.py module for numeric limits and thresholds. Apply it whenever a model has status, type, role or category fields, when business limits like MAX_ORDERS_PER_USER are repeated across services and tests, and when filter() calls compare against string literals such as filter(status='pending') that should reference OrderStatus.PENDING. The methodology lays out the constants.py layout (enumerations, pagination, business limits, validation length caps), enumerates TextChoices attributes (.choices, .values, .labels, .names, .label, .value) most agents miss, and warns against dict-of-tuples lookups and post-launch TextChoices→IntegerChoices switches that force data migrations. The payoff is refactor-safe code, IDE autocomplete on enum members, single-point edits for limits, and migrations that fire automatically when a value changes.

methodologies.name: django-decision-tree
methodologies.description: A comprehensive decision framework that guides you through Django architecture choices: framework selection, API framework choice, database selection, and deployment strategy. Use this when starting a new project or evaluating architectural alternatives to avoid costly refactoring later. It delivers clarity on trade-offs and confidence in your technical decisions.

methodologies.name: django-imports
methodologies.description: This guide solves circular import problems and import organization chaos through cross-app aliases, TYPE_CHECKING guards, and lazy imports. Apply it to prevent runtime errors, improve import clarity, and enable IDE autocomplete across your codebase. It delivers import statements that clearly show dependency direction and zero circular dependencies.

methodologies.name: django-models
methodologies.description: A comprehensive guide to modern Django model design covering BaseModel patterns, soft delete, custom managers, QuerySets, and field choices using TextChoices. Use this when designing models to ensure they remain thin, database-correct, and performant through the lifecycle of your project. It delivers models that are safe to refactor, efficient to query, and maintainable.

methodologies.name: django-project-structure
methodologies.description: Defines a domain-driven Django layout that scales predictably from small APIs to 100K-LOC monoliths: apps/ for business domains, core/ for shared infrastructure (BaseModel, exceptions, pagination, permissions), config/ for environment-split settings and root URLs, and a top-level tests/ with conftest.py and factories/. Apply it on greenfield projects, when refactoring layer-based legacy structures, and on team projects where consistent file placement reduces cognitive overhead. Each app is self-contained with models.py, services.py, selectors.py, apis.py, serializers.py, urls.py, constants.py, admin.py, and a tests/ subdir mirroring the four layers; core/ is imported by apps but apps never import each other directly to avoid circular dependencies. The methodology also pins naming conventions (snake_case plural app dirs, entity_action services, entity_query selectors, UPPER_SNAKE_CASE constants) and provides a startapp checklist plus a pyproject.toml template covering isort, mypy with django-stubs, pytest and coverage.

methodologies.name: django-pytest-factories
methodologies.description: Replaces hand-crafted Django test data with Factory Boy DjangoModelFactory classes wired to pytest via pytest-factoryboy, so every model gets a singular fixture and a _factory callable for batch creation with no per-test boilerplate. Use it whenever pytest tests need realistic Django model instances, related-object graphs (User → Profile → Order → OrderItem), or batch creation for list and filter coverage. The methodology specifies SubFactory for FKs, Faker for realistic values, LazyAttribute for derived fields, post_generation for M2M and password hashing, skip_postgeneration_save=True on Django 4.2+ to avoid double saves, string model references to break circular imports, register(Factory, _name='admin_user') to avoid fixture collisions, and factory.django.mute_signals(post_save) to silence email and Celery side effects during unrelated tests. It also enumerates frequent failure modes — asserting on Faker-literal values, bulk_create skipping signals, manually overriding pytest-factoryboy fixtures — that silently break test suites if not caught at review.

methodologies.name: django-pytest-fixtures
methodologies.description: Codifies the rules for pytest-django fixtures so suites stay reliable and fast: pick the right database fixture (db with auto-rollback by default, transactional_db only when the test asserts on commit, rollback, post-commit hooks or select_for_update, django_db_reset_sequences for ID ordering), pick the right scope (function for mutable data, module for read-only reference data, session only for immutable expensive setup like loaddata), and centralise reusable plumbing in a root conftest.py. Apply it when writing unit or integration tests for models, services and DRF endpoints, when migrating off Django TestCase setUp/tearDown, or when debugging flakiness rooted in transaction or scope mismatches. The methodology prescribes APIClient, authenticated_client and admin_client fixtures using force_authenticate with yield-based teardown for 10–100x speed over real login flows, and warns that mutating session-scoped fixtures or skipping @pytest.mark.django_db creates the most common silent test-suite failures.

methodologies.name: django-pytest-integration
methodologies.description: Provides the full integration-test playbook for Django plus DRF: every endpoint gets coverage for 401 anonymous, 403 wrong-permissions, 200/201 happy path, 400 validation errors, and 404 not-found, with parametrize used for validation matrices and class-based grouping (TestUserListAPI, TestUserCreateAPI) keeping related cases together. Apply it when verifying viewsets and APIView end-to-end, asserting that authentication and permission classes are wired correctly, exercising filter and pagination params, and testing transaction rollback or atomic operations in services. The methodology mandates format='json' on every request, asserting on response.data fields rather than browsable HTML, testing services directly (without HTTP) for business-logic precision, mocking Celery tasks and external APIs at the boundary with unittest.mock.patch, and using pytest.raises(..., match=...) so type-only matches do not produce false passes. The result is an integration suite that becomes the primary safety net for refactoring.

methodologies.name: django-pytest-mocking
methodologies.description: Defines how to isolate Django tests from slow, flaky or paid external services without breaking the integration boundary: patch at the import site of the module under test (apps.payments.services.stripe), not at the definition site, and always verify call arguments with assert_called_once_with so the integration contract itself is part of the test. Apply it for tests touching Stripe, Twilio, SendGrid, AWS, partner webhooks, current-time logic, and Celery task enqueueing. The methodology covers unittest.mock.patch correctly, the responses library for intercepting HTTP made via requests so tests assert on URL/method/body, freezegun (or the faster time-machine drop-in) for datetime.now/timezone.now/today determinism including before/after-expiry context-manager scenarios, and explicit anti-patterns: mocking Django ORM internals, patching at the wrong import path, or mocking time.sleep instead of patching the rate limiter directly.

methodologies.name: django-pytest-parametrize
methodologies.description: Collapses repetitive Django tests into a single test body driven by a data table, using @pytest.mark.parametrize over (input, expected) pairs or three-tuple (field, value, expected_error) matrices for DRF endpoints. Apply it for validator suites, API validation grids that mutate one field of an otherwise-valid payload, permission role grids that vary user-role and expected status code, and any group of three or more tests that differ only in data. The methodology spells out when to refactor — same body structure, ≥3 similar tests, copy-paste required to add a case — and when not to (different setup, more than ~20 rows, or control-flow-varying logic). It mandates descriptive ids= so failure messages read FAILED test_validation[email-invalid] instead of [0], stresses that each combination must be independent with no shared mutable state, and shows refactor patterns that turn five near-identical email-validation tests into one parametrized case.

methodologies.name: django-quality-linting
methodologies.description: Establishes the consolidated 2025–2026 Django quality stack — Ruff for linting and formatting, mypy plus django-stubs for static type checking, pre-commit hooks committed to the repo, and a single pyproject.toml as the only config source. Wire it on day one of a new Django project, when setting up CI gates that block PRs on ruff, mypy, security scan and coverage threshold, when adding type hints incrementally to a legacy codebase, or when onboarding new developers who need mechanical convention enforcement. The methodology pins concrete settings: target-version, line-length 88, rule groups E/W/F/I/B/C4/UP/SIM/DJ/T20/S/RUF/ARG/TCH with Django-aware ignores; per-file overrides for tests/ and migrations/; mypy with check_untyped_defs, warn_unused_ignores, disallow_untyped_defs, the django-stubs plugin and django_settings_module pointer; and incremental --strict adoption per module so legacy code does not avalanche. Every # type: ignore must carry a justification comment so suppressions stay auditable.

methodologies.name: django-quality-logging
methodologies.description: Replaces freeform Python logging with the production observability triad — structlog for structured event objects, django-structlog for automatic request-scoped binding of request_id, user_id and ip, and Sentry for unhandled-exception capture with full request context. Apply it on any Django service shipping to production, when migrating to JSON-ingesting platforms like Datadog, Loki, GCP or CloudWatch, when correlating trace IDs across multi-service systems, or during Sentry rollout. The methodology trades regex-parsable strings for queryable JSON events that support alert rules and dashboards, eliminates per-call logger.bind() boilerplate via RequestMiddleware, and connects Sentry alerts back to specific functions and database queries through the bound context. Throwaway prototypes, logger-already-on-python-json-logger codebases, and self-hosted projects without log aggregation are explicitly out of scope so the cost of the stack matches its payoff.

methodologies.name: django-quality-queries
methodologies.description: Targets Django's most common performance leak — the N+1 query — by mandating select_related for FK and one-to-one traversal, prefetch_related (and custom Prefetch with its own queryset) for reverse and M2M, only/defer for large columns, and assertNumQueries (or django_assert_num_queries) as a regression lock that fails the build when query counts drift. Apply it on any view iterating a queryset, list APIs returning more than ten rows, admin list_display with FK fields, Celery batch processors, and any endpoint with SLA targets; skip it for single-object detail views that do not traverse relationships. The methodology pairs the optimisation rules with index hygiene (an unindexed WHERE clause defeats a perfect select_related) and points to Django Debug Toolbar for development visibility and nplusone for automatic detection in tests, so accidental N+1 reintroductions are caught in code review rather than after a 20-second response in production.

methodologies.name: django-quality-security
methodologies.description: Enforces the non-negotiable Django production-security baseline — DEBUG=False, SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT and HSTS, CSRF and session cookies marked Secure and HttpOnly, X-Frame-Options DENY, Content Security Policy, rate limiting on auth endpoints, input validation through forms or DRF serializers, and python manage.py check --deploy --fail-level WARNING wired into CI. Apply it before any public launch, on any Django app handling authentication or sensitive data, when exposing previously internal endpoints, during post-incident remediation, and during quarterly security audits. Each setting maps to a specific attack class: stack-trace leakage, credential sniffing, SSL stripping, cookie theft, clickjacking, XSS injection, brute-force credential stuffing, SQL injection and data corruption. The methodology treats Django's deploy check as a CI-blocking gate so configuration drift cannot ship, and explicitly relaxes the stack only for VPN-only admin tools or HTTP-less data pipelines where it does not apply.

methodologies.name: django-selectors
methodologies.description: Centralises every Django read operation in keyword-only selector functions inside selectors.py that return QuerySet objects already wired with select_related and prefetch_related, so query logic never leaks into views, serializers, tests or management commands. Apply it for list queries with filtering, ordering or pagination, single-object lookups that need related data, any query reused across views, tasks, admin and management commands, and any endpoint where N+1 must be prevented. The methodology makes selector tests trivial — a single call to django_assert_num_queries locks the query count — and means a single optimisation (one extra prefetch) propagates to all callers automatically rather than being copy-pasted across five places. Trivial Model.objects.get(pk=pk) calls, write operations (which belong in services), and display-only annotations via SerializerMethodField are explicitly out of scope.

methodologies.name: django-serializers
methodologies.description: Defines DRF serializer hygiene: separate Input serializers for request validation and Output serializers for response formatting, no business logic inside serializers, explicit field declarations rather than ModelSerializer's automatic discovery, and nested serializers that consume pre-fetched data from selectors so they never trigger their own queries. Apply it on every DRF endpoint that accepts input or returns model data, whenever a payload must be validated before passing to a service, and for endpoints with nested related data that must be efficient. A single shared serializer for input and output silently exposes sensitive fields like password, is_staff or internal IDs in responses or ignores them on input; explicit declarations also prevent accidental exposure when new model fields are added later. The methodology keeps business logic in services where it can be tested in isolation, makes the API surface auditable, and is bypassed only by Django Ninja projects (Pydantic) and Django admin built-ins.

methodologies.name: django-service-layer
methodologies.description: Concentrates every Django write operation — create, update, delete, side effects — in service functions inside services.py, never in views, serializers or model save() overrides. Service functions take keyword-only arguments to prevent argument-order bugs, wrap multi-model writes in transaction.atomic(), call full_clean() before save() to fire model-level validation that Model.objects.create() skips, and raise domain exceptions (NotFoundError, ValidationError) instead of HTTP exceptions so they remain reusable in Celery tasks, admin actions and management commands. Apply it for any write spanning multiple models, operations with side effects (email, queue, audit log), and logic invoked from more than one entry point. Trivial create wrappers, read-only operations (which belong in selectors) and view-only HTTP glue stay out of services, keeping views thin and business logic independently testable without HTTP machinery.

methodologies.name: django-testing
methodologies.description: A reference covering pytest-django with Factory Boy, model_bakery, Django REST Framework testing, and coverage optimization. Use when building test suites to distinguish between pytest and unittest, understand factory patterns, and integrate testing into your workflow. It delivers comprehensive test coverage that catches real bugs.

methodologies.name: python-async
methodologies.description: Modern asyncio patterns including TaskGroup, Semaphore, timeout context managers, and async best practices for Python 3.11+. Apply this when building I/O-bound applications, APIs, or concurrent operations to unlock performance without threading complexity. It delivers applications that handle thousands of concurrent connections efficiently.

methodologies.name: python-basics
methodologies.description: Comprehensive foundation covering data structures, control flow, functions, OOP, error handling, and modern tooling with uv and Ruff. Use when learning Python or onboarding developers to establish shared understanding of modern practices and idioms. It delivers productive Python developers who follow current best practices.

methodologies.name: python-code-quality
methodologies.description: Modern Python code quality using Ruff (10-100x faster than Black+flake8), mypy/pyright for type checking, and pre-commit hooks for CI/CD. Apply it to your project setup to catch style, type, and quality issues before code review, dramatically reducing feedback cycles. It delivers consistent, high-quality code with minimal friction.

methodologies.name: python-fastapi
methodologies.description: Modern FastAPI patterns for production async APIs with Pydantic v2 validation, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async integration, and dependency injection. Use when building high-performance REST APIs to leverage automatic OpenAPI docs, type safety, and the fastest Python web framework. It delivers APIs that are fast, well-documented, and typesafe.

methodologies.name: python-modern-2026
methodologies.description: Python 3.12+ features including PEP 695 generics, improved f-strings, free-threading (3.13+), and modern tooling with uv and Ruff. Apply this when starting new projects to use the latest language features, unlock performance improvements, and avoid technical debt. It delivers future-proof code that runs 15% faster with better type safety.

methodologies.name: python-overview
methodologies.description: The Python ecosystem landscape covering frameworks, domains, tooling trends, and modern practices for 2025-2026. Use this as a reference to understand where Python fits in your technology decisions and what tools are current in the ecosystem. It delivers informed decisions on framework and tool selection.

methodologies.name: python-poetry-setup
methodologies.description: Poetry 2.x setup with PEP 621 compliance, dependency management, and lock file best practices. Apply this when initializing new projects to ensure reproducible builds, deterministic dependency resolution, and easy team collaboration. It delivers consistent development and production environments.

methodologies.name: python-pytest-async
methodologies.description: Wires pytest-asyncio so async def test functions actually execute inside an event loop instead of silently passing as truthy coroutine objects, and prescribes the correct fixture and mocking primitives for async code: pytest_asyncio.fixture for awaitable setup/teardown, AsyncMock for async callables, asyncio_mode=auto in pyproject.toml for the modern default, and httpx.AsyncClient with ASGITransport for FastAPI in-process testing. Apply it whenever tests contain await — FastAPI/ASGI endpoints, asyncio.gather/TaskGroup/ExceptionGroup behaviour, asyncio.wait_for or asyncio.timeout cancellation, and async-library calls (httpx, asyncpg, aioredis, motor). The methodology calls out the dangerous failure mode where a regular Mock returned in place of AsyncMock makes the test pass without ever asserting async behaviour, and warns against mixing sync Django ORM into async tests where the blocked event loop appears as a hang.

methodologies.name: python-pytest-fixtures
methodologies.description: Treats pytest fixtures as the dependency-injection backbone of a test suite: @pytest.fixture functions composed via parameter declarations, scoped function/class/module/session to control lifecycle, yield for inline teardown that runs even on failure, and the factory-fixture pattern that returns a callable for per-test-customised data without copy-paste. Apply it for shared setup like database connections, API clients and sample data, for tests where teardown matters (closing connections, cleaning temp files, flushing caches), and for composing complex scenarios from small reusable pieces. The methodology warns against autouse=True for fixtures with DB or HTTP side effects (it surprises every test in scope), against mutable state shared through wide-scoped fixtures (which pytest-randomly will surface as flakes), and against fixtures so trivial they belong inline — keeping the dependency graph deliberate rather than incidental.

methodologies.name: python-pytest-mocking
methodologies.description: Standardises on pytest-mock's mocker fixture instead of raw unittest.mock so every patch is reverted automatically after each test (even on failure), the API stays terse, and four critical helpers are first-class: autospec=True to mirror the real callable's signature and catch call-site drift, AsyncMock for async callables (a regular Mock silently returns a non-awaitable and the test passes asserting nothing), spy() to record calls while still executing the real implementation, and mocker.patch.object plus side_effect for triggering hard-to-reproduce error paths like timeouts and auth failures. Apply it for isolating units from HTTP, email, file system or database collaborators, controlling non-determinism (time, random, UUID), verifying that a dependency was called with the right arguments, and replacing async callables in async tests. The methodology pushes back hard on over-mocking, mocking private methods, mocking the database in integration tests (use a real test DB with rollback) and mocking when testcontainers or a real service is available — mocks drift from real contracts over time.

methodologies.name: python-pytest-parametrize
methodologies.description: Eliminates near-duplicate tests by feeding one test body a matrix of (input, expected) pairs through @pytest.mark.parametrize, with pytest.param() for per-case marks and ids, stacked decorators for cartesian products (statuses × roles, multiple backends), and indirect=True to route params through fixtures for setup-time customisation. Apply it for validators with many valid/invalid pairs, business-logic branches that need individual coverage, boundary conditions (empty, None, zero, max, off-by-one), cross-product coverage, and any time three or more tests differ only in data. The methodology mandates descriptive ids so failure messages read test_email[empty_email] rather than test_email[arg0], caps a single decorator at roughly 20 rows before splitting into focused tests, and refuses to parametrise when cases need different setup or when the test body becomes a conditional — that signal indicates separate tests, not data variation.

methodologies.name: python-pytest-setup
methodologies.description: Bootstraps pytest into a Python project so the day-one configuration prevents whole classes of maintenance pain: install the essential plugin set (pytest-cov, pytest-xdist, pytest-randomly, pytest-mock, pytest-asyncio), configure pyproject.toml with strict-markers (typos fail at collection), filterwarnings=['error'] (deprecations fail before upgrades break you), branch coverage thresholds, and a tests/ directory mirroring src/. Apply it on new Python projects, when migrating off unittest.TestCase (the styles can coexist), when wiring CI for coverage reporting, parallel xdist execution and artifact upload, and on team projects that need enforced marker discipline and reproducible ordering. The methodology also describes the agentic TDD loop for feature development and explicitly excludes use cases where pytest is the wrong tool: doctest-only libs, single-script utilities, Gherkin-first BDD teams (behave), serious benchmark regression tracking (asv), and hard-real-time hardware-in-loop suites.

methodologies.name: python-type-hints
methodologies.description: Complete guide to Python type hints from basics through advanced patterns like Protocols, TypeVar, and TypeIs for modern type safety. Apply this to add type hints to your codebase, enabling better IDE support, documentation, and early error detection through type checkers. It delivers self-documenting code that catches bugs before runtime.

methodologies.name: python-typing
methodologies.description: Deep dive into Python's typing system covering TypedDict, Protocol, generic syntax, and framework-specific patterns for Django and FastAPI. Use this reference when implementing complex type structures to ensure type safety across your application layers. It delivers type-safe code that scales with your codebase.

methodologies.name: python-web-frameworks
methodologies.description: Comparison of Django, FastAPI, and Flask covering architecture, performance, features, and decision criteria for each framework. Use this to select the right framework for your project based on requirements like async support, admin panels, or API performance. It delivers technology decisions aligned with your actual needs.

domains.description: Full-stack development orchestrator coordinating seven specialized sub-skills: Python, JavaScript, backend systems (Go/Rust), frontend, API design, testing, and devtools. Routes tasks based on technology stack, code area (backend/frontend/full-stack), and existing project patterns. Handles 184 methodologies across development domains.

methodologies.name: api-testing
methodologies.description: Testing strategies for APIs including contract testing with Pact, integration testing, OpenAPI validation, and Postman collections. Apply this when building APIs to ensure contracts between consumer and provider, validate responses against specs, and catch integration bugs early. It delivers reliable APIs that meet consumer expectations.

methodologies.name: best-practices-2026
methodologies.description: Latest software development patterns for AI-assisted coding, TypeScript 5.x strict modes, React 19 Server Components, Python 3.13, and AI testing tools. Use this as a current reference for 2026 best practices in AI tools, type safety, and testing to stay current with the industry. It delivers modern, production-grade code using latest tooling.

methodologies.name: bun-runtime
methodologies.description: Bun runtime fundamentals covering project setup, native HTTP serving, Hono framework integration, file operations, and testing. Use this when building TypeScript projects that prioritize startup speed and development experience with all-in-one tooling. It delivers fast development cycles with 10-100x faster package installation.

methodologies.name: code-coverage
methodologies.description: Comprehensive coverage guide covering coverage types, tools, configuration, CI/CD integration, and the philosophy that coverage is a guide not a goal. Apply this to identify untested code paths and set quality gates without chasing 100% that tests trivial code. It delivers focused testing that covers what matters.

methodologies.name: code-review
methodologies.description: Code review process templates, scenarios, and metrics for effective feedback on bugs, design, security, and performance issues. Use this to establish code review practices that catch problems before deployment while maintaining positive team dynamics. It delivers higher code quality through collaborative improvement.

methodologies.name: css-in-js
methodologies.description: CSS-in-JS fundamentals with styled-components and Emotion for component-scoped styling, theming, and dynamic props-based styles. Apply this when building component libraries to achieve scoped styles without class name conflicts and seamless theming support. It delivers maintainable, scalable styling without global namespace pollution.

methodologies.name: django-api
methodologies.description: Django REST Framework API patterns provide thin, maintainable views that delegate business logic to services while using OpenAPI documentation for clarity. Apply when building REST endpoints to separate HTTP concerns from business logic and ensure consistent API documentation across your project. This delivers cleaner code organization, easier testing of business logic, and auto-generated API documentation that stays in sync.

methodologies.name: django-base-model
methodologies.description: The Base Model pattern establishes a consistent foundation for all Django models with UUID, timestamps, and soft-delete support, ensuring data integrity and audit trails across your application. Use this for any new Django project or when refactoring legacy models to improve consistency and avoid duplicating common fields. The value includes automatic tracking of all changes, secure external identifiers via UUIDs, and reduced migration friction.

methodologies.name: django-coding-standards
methodologies.description: Django coding standards enforce consistent project structure, import conventions, and service-layer patterns that make code predictable and maintainable across large teams. Apply these when starting a new Django project or onboarding new developers to establish team-wide expectations for code organization. The outcome is reduced cognitive load during code reviews, faster onboarding, and fewer architectural debates.

methodologies.name: django-decision-tree
methodologies.description: A decision tree for code placement eliminates ambiguity about whether functions belong in services, utils, models, or views based on their responsibilities and side effects. Use this during feature development and code reviews to ensure consistent architecture without lengthy architectural discussions. This delivers predictability—any team member can find code in seconds and implement features in the right layer the first time.

methodologies.name: django-imports
methodologies.description: Django import patterns with aliases prevent circular dependencies and naming conflicts while maintaining clean, readable code across apps. Apply this convention when starting a new project and enforce it in code reviews to avoid the subtle bugs that come from poor import organization. The benefit is eliminating common import-related errors and making the dependency graph of your codebase explicit and navigable.

methodologies.name: django-models
methodologies.description: Django model patterns cover field design, ForeignKey strategies, indexing, and Django 5.0+ features for building efficient, maintainable database schemas. Use this reference when designing models for new features to avoid N+1 queries, index inefficiencies, and accidental data loss from CASCADE deletes. The value includes better query performance, fewer migration headaches, and data safety through proper relationship handling.

methodologies.name: django-pytest
methodologies.description: pytest fixtures and parametrization provide a powerful, readable approach to testing Django applications that's faster than the built-in TestCase class. Apply this when writing unit tests for services, integration tests for APIs, and factory-based test data generation to avoid brittle test setups. This delivers faster test suites, clearer test intent, and easier test maintenance as your codebase evolves.

methodologies.name: django-quality
methodologies.description: Django quality tools (Black, isort, flake8, mypy) enforce consistent formatting and catch bugs early through pre-commit hooks. Use this in every Django project with ruff as the primary tool to avoid manual formatting debates and catch type errors before runtime. The outcome is cleaner diffs, fewer linting discussions in code review, and fewer silent bugs from type mismatches.

methodologies.name: django-testing
methodologies.description: Testing with pytest offers factories (model_bakery, factory_boy) and parametrization for creating realistic test data efficiently. Use this when writing integration tests for API endpoints and database operations where you need real data fixtures. The benefit is eliminating test maintenance burden from hardcoded mock data and making tests readable to team members unfamiliar with the codebase.

methodologies.name: documentation
methodologies.description: CLAUDE.md documentation follows a structured, AI-readable format that provides context for any directory without overwhelming readers with detail. Create one for every module folder when starting a new project or onboarding new developers to make the codebase self-documenting. This delivers reduced onboarding time, better codebase navigation, and clearer understanding of architectural intent.

methodologies.name: e2e-testing
methodologies.description: E2E tests with Playwright validate complete user workflows through the entire application stack using Page Object Models for maintainability. Apply selectively to critical user journeys (signup, checkout, payment) rather than testing everything to avoid slow, brittle suites. The value is catching integration issues that unit and integration tests miss while staying fast enough to run regularly.

methodologies.name: error-handling
methodologies.description: Standardized error responses using RFC 7807 Problem Details provide consistent, actionable error information to clients with proper HTTP status codes and trace IDs. Use this pattern in all APIs to make errors debuggable and ensure clients can handle them programmatically. This improves developer experience, enables better monitoring, and reduces support burden from unclear error messages.

methodologies.name: files-reference
methodologies.description: A comprehensive index of all files in a knowledge domain helps navigators find content by purpose, language, or type without memorizing the structure. Maintain this when documenting large knowledge bases to enable quick access to specific patterns or techniques. The benefit is reducing cognitive load when searching for methodology details and making knowledge portable across teams.

methodologies.name: go-backend
methodologies.description: Go backend patterns using Gin or Echo provide clean routing, middleware composition, and error handling for building performant REST APIs and microservices. Use when building Go APIs to standardize project layout and HTTP patterns across your team. This delivers clean, testable code organization and makes Go projects approachable for developers from other languages.

methodologies.name: go-concurrency
methodologies.description: Goroutine patterns, worker pools, and context-based cancellation provide safe concurrent programming without data races or goroutine leaks. Apply when building high-performance services that need to process multiple requests or tasks simultaneously without overwhelming resources. The outcome is predictable concurrency behavior, easy resource management, and systems that scale without crashing.

methodologies.name: go-error-handling
methodologies.description: Custom error types with HTTP status codes and error codes enable consistent error handling across Go services. Use this pattern in production Go services to provide meaningful errors to clients and make application behavior testable. This improves debugging, enables proper HTTP status code handling, and makes error recovery strategies explicit.

methodologies.name: go-http-handlers
methodologies.description: HTTP handler patterns for Gin and Echo provide consistent routing, middleware chains, and request validation. Apply when building Go APIs to standardize how HTTP concerns are handled and make handler code focused and testable. This delivers readable routing configurations, composable middleware, and handlers that are easy to test in isolation.

methodologies.name: integration-testing
methodologies.description: Integration tests using real databases (Testcontainers), APIs (Supertest), and service mocks (WireMock) verify component interactions work correctly end-to-end. Use these tests for database interactions, API endpoints with authentication, and external service integrations where mocks would hide real problems. The benefit is catching integration issues early while maintaining reasonable test speed.

methodologies.name: javascript
methodologies.description: JavaScript/TypeScript standards with strict mode, explicit types, and modern syntax patterns provide safety and clarity in JavaScript development. Apply from the start of any new JavaScript project to prevent common errors and make refactoring safer. This delivers fewer runtime errors, better IDE support, and code that's easier to understand months later.

methodologies.name: language-framework-guide
methodologies.description: A selection matrix for languages and frameworks helps teams choose the right tool for each component based on requirements and strengths. Use when starting new services or migrating legacy components to make intentional technology decisions rather than defaulting to what you know. The value is picking tools that fit the actual problem and avoiding over-engineering with powerful but inappropriate frameworks.

methodologies.name: methodologies
methodologies.description: An index of all development methodologies organized by problem type enables quick discovery of relevant patterns and practices. Maintain and update this regularly as you discover new patterns to make methodology knowledge discoverable across your organization. This ensures best practices are applied consistently and new team members learn from documented solutions rather than reinventing approaches.

methodologies.name: mob-programming
methodologies.description: Mob programming with rotation, clear roles, and agreement-based decisions leverages group intelligence for complex problems and knowledge sharing. Use for critical features, complex bugs no one can solve alone, and team learning to accelerate decision-making and distribute knowledge. The outcome is higher-quality solutions, faster onboarding for new team members, and stronger team alignment.

methodologies.name: mobile-responsive
methodologies.description: Mobile-first CSS with fluid layouts, responsive images, and touch-friendly interactions ensures optimal user experience across all devices. Apply to every web project from the start—designing mobile first prevents later retrofitting and keeps stylesheets simpler. This delivers better mobile performance, improved accessibility, and reduced maintenance burden from device-specific workarounds.

methodologies.name: mocking-strategies
methodologies.description: Mocking strategies distinguish between stubs (state), spies (behavior verification), fakes (working implementations), and mocks (expectations) to isolate tests properly. Use the right double for each test—stubs for state verification, fakes for complex scenarios, mocks sparingly to verify critical interactions. This delivers fast, focused tests that don't break on refactoring and clearly communicate intent.

methodologies.name: nodejs-express-fastify
methodologies.description: Express and Fastify patterns provide application structure, middleware composition, validation, and error handling for building production Node.js APIs. Use Express for maximum ecosystem compatibility or Fastify for better performance and TypeScript support when building REST APIs or microservices. The value is cleaner routing organization, consistent error handling, and applications that are testable and maintainable.

methodologies.name: pair-programming
methodologies.description: Pair programming brings two developers to one workstation, where driver and navigator roles rotate to maintain focus and catch errors in real-time. It excels for complex code, knowledge transfer, and critical logic, reducing defects by up to 15% with minimal slowdown. Use it selectively on challenging tasks rather than routine work, alternating roles every 25-30 minutes and maintaining high-quality communication throughout the session.

methodologies.name: pnpm-package-management
methodologies.description: pnpm is a fast, disk-efficient package manager using content-addressable storage and symlinks, enforcing strict dependency isolation by default. It prevents phantom dependencies that npm allows and provides superior workspace support for monorepos. Commit pnpm-lock.yaml for reproducible builds, use strict-peer-dependencies=true to catch version conflicts early, and leverage workspaces for managing multiple packages in a single repository.

methodologies.name: python
methodologies.description: Python is a versatile language enabling rapid development for backend services, data pipelines, and automation. Use type hints with modern syntax (Python 3.9+), follow PEP 8 conventions, and leverage the rich ecosystem of frameworks like Django and FastAPI. Consider Python for I/O-bound operations and scripting, but be aware of the GIL limitation for CPU-bound parallelism; async/await patterns optimize performance for network-bound tasks.

methodologies.name: python-fastapi
methodologies.description: FastAPI combines asynchronous support, automatic OpenAPI documentation, and Pydantic validation to build production-grade APIs with minimal boilerplate. Use dependency injection via Depends() for testability, define routers by domain, and separate business logic into service layers. The framework shines for microservices and high-throughput APIs where async/await prevents blocking I/O.

methodologies.name: python-poetry-setup
methodologies.description: Poetry simplifies Python project setup with deterministic lock files, workspace monorepo support, and dependency groups for separating dev/test/docs requirements. Always commit poetry.lock for reproducible environments across machines and CI/CD pipelines. Use dependency groups to keep production images slim, configure virtual environments in-project for easier debugging, and leverage Poetry's publishing tools for PyPI distribution.

methodologies.name: python-type-hints
methodologies.description: Type hints document intent and enable static analysis with mypy to catch bugs before runtime without changing runtime behavior. Use modern syntax (str | None instead of Optional), prefer specific types over Any, and leverage Protocol for duck typing when inheritance is overkill. Enable strict mode in mypy to enforce complete coverage, use TypedDict for structured dicts, and apply generic types for reusable patterns.

methodologies.name: react-hooks
methodologies.description: React hooks enable state and side-effect management in functional components, replacing class-based patterns with simpler composition. Use useState for state, useEffect for subscriptions with proper cleanup, and useCallback/useMemo judiciously to optimize only performance bottlenecks. Custom hooks extract logic for reuse; follow the Rules of Hooks strictly and depend on all used values to prevent stale closures.

methodologies.name: refactoring-patterns
methodologies.description: Refactoring improves code structure without changing behavior by extracting methods, removing duplication, and renaming for clarity. Always keep tests green to catch regressions, work in small incremental steps, and use IDE refactoring tools rather than manual edits. Refactor before adding features to reduce friction; common patterns include Extract Method, Extract Class, and Replace Conditional with Polymorphism.

methodologies.name: rust-error-handling
methodologies.description: Rust uses Result and Option types for explicit error handling instead of exceptions, preventing unexpected panics in production. Use the ? operator for ergonomic error propagation, define custom error types with thiserror crate, and leverage pattern matching for exhaustive handling. Never unwrap in production code; prefer Result with clear error context for APIs and validate inputs early to fail fast.

methodologies.name: rust-ownership
methodologies.description: Rust's ownership system enables memory safety without garbage collection by tracking who owns each value at compile time. Understand the three rules: each value has one owner, ownership transfers on assignment, and references enable borrowing. Smart pointers like Arc and Rc provide shared ownership for specific scenarios; use Cow for copy-on-write optimization and RAII ensures resources cleanup.

methodologies.name: rust-testing
methodologies.description: Rust's test framework lives in the same file as production code under #[cfg(test)] modules with mockall for mocking complex dependencies. Use table-driven tests with parametrization, leverage tokio::test for async testing, and separate unit tests (fast, isolated) from integration tests (real dependencies). Mock only external boundaries; test behavior, not implementation details.

methodologies.name: shadcn-ui
methodologies.description: shadcn/ui provides copy-paste component primitives built on headless Radix UI with Tailwind styling, not npm packages. Extend components via composition rather than modifying ui/ files, use class-variance-authority (CVA) for variant management, and ensure semantic HTML and ARIA attributes for accessibility. Theme with CSS variables, customize Tailwind config for your design system.

methodologies.name: storybook-setup
methodologies.description: Storybook isolates component development from application context, enabling visual testing and design system documentation. Write stories demonstrating use cases with args for interactivity, use Chromatic for visual regression detection, and leverage interaction testing with userEvent to catch behavioral issues. Organize by feature hierarchy (Components/Buttons/Button) and keep stories up-to-date with component changes.

methodologies.name: tailwind
methodologies.description: Tailwind's utility-first approach enables rapid UI development with JIT compilation producing minimal CSS. Use design tokens in tailwind.config.js rather than hardcoded values, apply concentric CSS ordering for readability, and extract repeated patterns into components instead of @apply. Mobile-first responsive design with md: and lg: prefixes; leverage the cn() helper for conditional class merging.

methodologies.name: tdd-workflow
methodologies.description: Test-Driven Development writes failing tests first, then minimal code to pass, then refactors while tests stay green. This Red-Green-Refactor cycle ensures code meets requirements and is testable by design. Use TDD for complex logic, API design, and bug fixes; it documents expected behavior through tests and enables safe refactoring with confidence.

methodologies.name: test-fixtures
methodologies.description: Fixtures provide consistent test data and setup, reducing duplication and improving readability compared to inline setup. Use pytest's fixture system with appropriate scopes (function for isolation, session for expensive setup), leverage factory patterns for flexible data generation, and ensure cleanup via yield for resource management. Fixtures compose—higher-level fixtures depend on lower-level ones.

methodologies.name: testing
methodologies.description: Testing validates behavior across unit, integration, and E2E levels; unit tests catch logic errors fast, integration tests verify component interaction, and E2E tests validate user journeys. Follow the Test Pyramid (70% unit, 20% integration, 10% E2E) to balance speed and confidence. Mock external dependencies in unit tests, use real or containerized services for integration, and test critical workflows end-to-end.

methodologies.name: typescript-strict-mode
methodologies.description: TypeScript strict mode enables comprehensive type checking preventing common errors like null/undefined access and implicit any. Enable strict in tsconfig.json, use unknown instead of any for type-safe assertions, and leverage type narrowing with guards and discriminated unions. Branded types prevent mixing similar primitives, const assertions enable literal types, and template literal types create domain-specific type patterns.

methodologies.name: unit-testing
methodologies.description: Unit tests verify individual components in isolation with fast execution and high coverage to catch regressions early. Follow Arrange-Act-Assert structure, use parametrization to test multiple scenarios efficiently, and keep tests independent so they run in any order. Mock external dependencies, test behavior not implementation, and avoid brittle tests that break on refactoring without behavior change.

domains.description: Testing strategies covering unit, integration, end-to-end, and security testing alongside test-driven development. Includes test design patterns (AAA, Given-When-Then), mocking strategies, fixtures, language-specific testing frameworks (pytest, Jest, Vitest, Go testing), and test automation best practices.

methodologies.name: e2e-testing
methodologies.description: End-to-end testing simulates real user workflows through the entire application stack from UI to database, catching integration issues unit tests miss. Use Playwright for modern E2E testing with built-in mobile emulation and multi-browser support, Page Object Model for maintainable selectors, and mock external APIs while keeping your application and database real. Run only critical user journeys (10% of test pyramid) to keep suite fast.

methodologies.name: integration-testing
methodologies.description: Integration testing verifies multiple components work together with real or containerized dependencies, catching configuration and database interaction errors. Use Testcontainers to spin up ephemeral PostgreSQL/Redis instances, transaction rollback for test isolation and speed, and factory fixtures for consistent test data. Balance between transaction rollback (fast, local) and database reset (accurate, catches constraints) based on your needs.

methodologies.name: mocking-strategies
methodologies.description: Mocking isolates code under test from slow, unreliable, or expensive external dependencies like APIs and databases. Mock at system boundaries, never internal logic; use test doubles taxonomy (stubs for responses, mocks for behavior verification, fakes for working implementations). Keep mocks simple, prefer interfaces for dependency injection, and avoid over-mocking which decouples tests from reality.

methodologies.name: tdd-workflow
methodologies.description: Test-Driven Development cycles through Red (failing test), Green (minimal code to pass), and Refactor (improve without breaking tests) to ensure testability and requirement alignment. Write tests before code to design clean APIs, use TDD for bug fixes by first reproducing the issue in a test, and benefit from comprehensive test coverage enabling fearless refactoring throughout the codebase.

methodologies.name: test-fixtures
methodologies.description: Fixtures provide reusable test setup and data, reducing duplication and improving clarity across test suites using factory patterns, builders, and parametrization. Use fixture composition where higher-level fixtures depend on lower-level ones, apply appropriate scopes (function for fresh data, session for expensive resources), and ensure cleanup via yield statements. Fake implementations work better than mocks for complex scenarios.

methodologies.name: testing-go
methodologies.description: Go's built-in testing package enables unit tests, benchmarks, and fuzzing without external frameworks, with table-driven tests and subtests as idiomatic patterns. Define interfaces at the consumer for dependency injection and mocking, use httptest for HTTP handler testing, and leverage testcontainers-go for integration tests with Docker. Run tests with -race flag to detect concurrency issues and -cover for coverage reporting.

methodologies.name: testing-javascript
methodologies.description: Choose between Vitest (10-20x faster, native ESM, TypeScript) for modern Vite projects and Jest (mandatory for React Native, legacy CommonJS codebases). Use the test pyramid with 70% unit tests, 20% integration, 10% E2E. Set up MSW for API mocking and Playwright for cross-browser testing. This framework comparison and setup guide helps you pick the right tool for your JavaScript/TypeScript project and avoid lock-in regrets.

methodologies.name: testing-patterns
methodologies.description: Master core testing patterns—AAA (Arrange-Act-Assert), Given-When-Then (BDD), Test Data Builder, Page Object Model—that make tests readable and maintainable across teams. Learn when to use mocks vs stubs, how to structure tests to prevent flaky failures, and why the test pyramid (70/20/10 ratio) beats the ice-cream-cone anti-pattern. These patterns apply to Python, JavaScript, Go, and other languages, saving time and preventing test rewrites.

methodologies.name: testing-pytest
methodologies.description: pytest 8.x brings improved assertion diffs, better async support (pytest-asyncio), and 1000+ plugins for parallel testing, coverage, mocking, and more. Master fixtures (dependency injection with function/class/module/session scopes), parametrization (run same test with multiple inputs), and markers (tag and filter tests). LangSmith integration and configuration in pyproject.toml make pytest the default for modern Python testing.

methodologies.name: unit-testing
methodologies.description: Apply FIRST principles (Fast, Isolated, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely) to write unit tests that verify individual components in isolation. Use AAA (Arrange-Act-Assert) structure, follow one assertion per concept, and avoid testing implementation details. Know when to mock (external APIs, databases) vs test behavior (domain logic). Aim for 80%+ coverage on public APIs and business logic; skip trivial getters and framework-generated code.

domains.description: Pure marketing orchestrator coordinating seven specialized sub-skills: GTM strategy, content marketing, growth analytics, conversion optimization, PPC management, SEO, and social media management. No methodologies in this skill itself—all execution delegated to sub-skills based on marketing goal and business model.

Communicator provides universal communication methodologies for stakeholder dialogue, customer validation, requirements gathering, conflict resolution, feedback delivery, pitching ideas, storytelling, negotiation, and brainstorming. Use for any communication challenge—validating business ideas with customers, gathering requirements, resolving team conflicts, pitching to stakeholders, or facilitating ideation. Includes 10 frameworks like Mom Test, RASA listening, and Pyramid Principle.

methodologies.name: active-listening
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of people not feeling heard by using the RASA framework (Receive, Appreciate, Summarize, Ask) with techniques like paraphrasing, mirroring, and reflective listening. Apply in requirements gathering, interviews, and conflict situations to understand both content and emotion, shift from selective to empathic listening, and create psychological safety for people to share genuine concerns.

methodologies.name: brainstorming-ideation
methodologies.description: Addresses the challenge that brainstorming ideation differs from group facilitation by providing structured methods like SCAMPER, Mind Mapping, and Starbursting for generating and developing ideas systematically. Use SCAMPER to improve existing products through 7 lenses, Mind Mapping for complex topics with visual connections, and Starbursting to generate critical questions before decisions.

methodologies.name: brainstorming-techniques
methodologies.description: Solves the problem that teams struggle with creative solutions when dominant voices overshadow others and criticism kills ideas by applying Osborn's 4 rules (defer judgment, go for quantity, encourage wild ideas, build on others). Use techniques like classic brainstorming, 6-3-5 brainwriting, and round robin to generate diverse ideas, ensure equal participation, and move from divergence to convergence systematically.

methodologies.name: business-storytelling
methodologies.description: Tackles the challenge that data alone doesn't persuade by structuring narratives around the Pyramid Principle (lead with answer), SCQA framework (Situation-Complication-Question-Answer), and Pixar's story structure. Apply when pitching projects, writing executive summaries, or presenting change initiatives to make data memorable, create emotional connection, and move audiences to action.

methodologies.name: conflict-resolution
methodologies.description: Addresses unresolved conflicts that damage relationships and kill productivity by choosing appropriate conflict modes (Competing, Collaborating, Compromising, Avoiding, Accommodating) based on importance and urgency. Use Nonviolent Communication (Observation-Feeling-Need-Request) to separate people from problems, focus on underlying interests, and find creative solutions that meet both parties' needs.

methodologies.name: difficult-conversations
methodologies.description: Solves the problem that avoiding difficult conversations creates festering resentment and passive-aggressive behavior by using the Crucial Conversations framework with seven skills including starting with heart, making it safe, and mastering stories. Apply when addressing high-stakes issues with strong emotions and differing opinions to maintain relationships while being honest, restore safety when needed, and move toward clear agreements.

methodologies.name: feedback
methodologies.description: Addresses the challenge that most feedback is either too vague or too harsh by using the SBI model (Situation-Behavior-Impact) for specific, observable, objective feedback combined with Radical Candor (Care Personally + Challenge Directly). Apply when giving feedback to make it actionable and kind, or receiving feedback to listen without defending, explore specifics, and reflect privately before responding.

methodologies.name: ideation-methods
methodologies.description: Provides structured frameworks for systematic creativity beyond group brainstorming by using SCAMPER (apply 7 lenses to existing ideas), Mind Mapping (show connections visually), and Starbursting (generate critical questions). Use SCAMPER when improving products, Mind Mapping for complex topics with many connections, and Starbursting when planning projects and identifying unknowns before research.

methodologies.name: mom-test
methodologies.description: Solves the problem that people lie when you ask about your idea by using three rules: talk about their life not your idea, ask about specifics in the past not generics about the future, and listen more than you pitch (80% rule). Apply when validating business ideas to get past politeness, focus on real behavior over hypotheticals, and collect actual commitments (time, money, reputation) instead of compliments.

methodologies.name: negotiation
methodologies.description: Tackles most people negotiating positions instead of interests by using Principled Negotiation (separate people from problem, focus on interests, generate options, use objective criteria) with BATNA analysis. Apply when negotiating contracts, salaries, or agreements to find win-win solutions, understand your walk-away point, and use tactics like anchoring and silence to move toward mutually beneficial outcomes.

methodologies.name: selling-ideas
methodologies.description: Addresses the challenge that great ideas die from bad pitches focusing on features instead of benefits by using SPIN Selling (Situation-Problem-Implication-Need-Payoff) to uncover pain and make it urgent. Apply when pitching to stakeholders to understand their context first, amplify the pain through implications, lead them to recognize the need, then present your solution as the natural answer.

methodologies.name: stakeholder-communication
methodologies.description: Solves misunderstandings and resistance when different stakeholders have different communication needs by using five dialogue modes: Interview (gather requirements), Brainstorm (generate options), Clarification (resolve ambiguity), Validation (confirm understanding), and Socratic (deep exploration). Use stakeholder mapping to prioritize communication frequency, apply the right mode for each situation, and document all agreements in writing.

methodologies.name: storytelling
methodologies.description: Addresses the fact that people remember stories 22x better than facts by structuring narratives with the Pyramid Principle (lead with answer), SCQA framework (build tension), and Pixar formula (Once upon a time... Until finally...). Apply when writing executive summaries, case studies, or presentations to make data memorable, create emotional connection, and move audiences toward decisions.

domains.description: API Developer handles API design and implementation—REST design principles, GraphQL APIs, OpenAPI specifications, authentication patterns (OAuth, JWT, API keys), versioning strategies, rate limiting, error handling, testing, and real-time APIs (WebSockets). Use when designing APIs, documenting specifications, implementing authentication, versioning endpoints, or building GraphQL schemas. Includes 19 methodologies with context-aware decision trees.

methodologies.name: api-authentication
methodologies.description: Compares authentication methods (API Keys for server-to-server, JWT for stateless sessions, OAuth 2.0 for third-party access, mTLS for high-security) with tradeoffs between simplicity and security. Choose API Keys for simple integrations, JWT for scalable stateless auth, OAuth 2.0 when granting third-party access, and mTLS when maximum security is required, implementing proper key rotation, hashing, and token revocation.

methodologies.name: api-contract-first
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of client-server mismatches by designing the OpenAPI specification first, then generating server stubs, client SDKs, and tests from the contract. Apply this workflow to review specs before implementation, generate code consistently, validate implementations against contracts, and catch breaking changes early through CI/CD integration and diff tools.

methodologies.name: api-documentation
methodologies.description: Addresses poor API adoption when documentation is incomplete or unclear by using tools like Swagger UI for interactive testing, Redoc for clean reference docs, and providing working code examples in multiple languages. Apply when building APIs to include quick start guides, authentication flows, comprehensive error codes, rate limit information, and copy-paste ready curl commands that users can run immediately.

methodologies.name: api-error-handling
methodologies.description: Tackles confusing error responses that don't help clients fix problems by implementing RFC 7807 Problem Details format with type URIs, status codes, detailed messages, field-level errors, and trace IDs. Use this when designing errors to provide actionable messages instead of generic responses, always include trace IDs for debugging, log detailed errors server-side, and document all error codes in API specs.

methodologies.name: api-gateway-patterns
methodologies.description: API gateways centralize routing, authentication, rate limiting, and request transformation between clients and microservices. Use this pattern when you need to manage multiple backend services through a single entry point, provide consistent API interfaces across different service implementations, or implement cross-cutting concerns like rate limiting and CORS at the infrastructure level.

methodologies.name: api-graphql
methodologies.description: GraphQL provides a flexible query language for APIs that allows clients to request exactly the data they need, reducing over-fetching and under-fetching problems. Implement this when you have complex, interconnected data models, diverse client types with different data requirements, or want to provide a self-documenting API with powerful developer tooling.

methodologies.name: api-monitoring
methodologies.description: Continuous monitoring of API health through metrics, logs, and alerts ensures production reliability and enables rapid incident response. Set up liveness and readiness probes, collect structured metrics on latency and error rates, configure SLO-based alerting, and establish baseline performance thresholds to catch degradation early.

methodologies.name: api-openapi-spec
methodologies.description: OpenAPI specifications provide machine-readable documentation that enables code generation, testing, and client library creation. Use this to define API contracts formally, generate SDK clients in multiple languages, validate request/response conformance, and serve as a source of truth for API design across teams.

methodologies.name: api-rate-limiting
methodologies.description: Rate limiting protects APIs from abuse and ensures fair resource allocation across users and clients.
