Implement appropriate algorithms (sliding window, token bucket) based on your scale, provide clear rate limit headers in responses, support tiered limits for different customer plans, and use Redis for distributed rate limiting across multiple servers.

methodologies.name: api-rest-design
methodologies.description: REST principles guide consistent API design through resource-oriented URLs, standard HTTP methods, and appropriate status codes. Follow conventions like using nouns for resources, plural names for collections, hyphens for readability, and semantic status codes to make APIs intuitive for developers and easy to maintain.

methodologies.name: api-testing
methodologies.description: Comprehensive API testing across unit, integration, and contract layers ensures API reliability and prevents breaking changes. Use contract testing to verify consumer-provider agreements, validate responses against OpenAPI specs, test error scenarios and edge cases, and implement E2E tests on staging environments.

methodologies.name: api-versioning
methodologies.description: API versioning strategies let you evolve APIs while maintaining backward compatibility with existing clients. Choose URL path versioning for clarity, announce deprecation 6+ months ahead with sunset headers, support at least two versions simultaneously, and provide migration guides to ease client transitions.

methodologies.name: graphql-api-design
methodologies.description: GraphQL schema design requires careful planning for pagination, authorization, error handling, and performance. Use input types for mutations, implement cursor-based pagination following the Relay spec, prevent N+1 queries with DataLoaders, add depth and complexity limits to prevent abuse, and use union types for effective error handling.

methodologies.name: websocket-design
methodologies.description: WebSockets enable real-time, bidirectional communication for chat, notifications, and live data feeds. Implement connection managers with subscription tracking, add heartbeat/ping mechanisms to detect stale connections, handle reconnection with exponential backoff, scale horizontally using Redis Pub/Sub, and monitor connection counts closely.

domains.description: Automation & Tooling specialist covers browser automation (Puppeteer, Playwright), CI/CD pipelines, monorepo management (Turborepo, pnpm), performance testing, feature flags, trunk-based development, and logging patterns. Use when automating tests, building CI/CD pipelines, managing monorepos, implementing feature flags, or setting up performance testing. Includes 23 methodologies across automation and developer tooling.

methodologies.name: ab-testing-basics
methodologies.description: A/B testing validates product decisions through controlled experimentation with measurable success metrics. Design experiments with clear hypotheses, use deterministic bucketing to ensure consistent variant assignment, calculate statistical significance before drawing conclusions, and track primary and secondary metrics to measure both intended and unintended effects.

methodologies.name: ab-testing-implementation
methodologies.description: Implementing A/B tests requires event tracking infrastructure, statistical analysis, and result reporting systems. Track exposures to avoid selection bias, log conversion events with rich context, calculate confidence intervals and statistical power, create dashboards showing trend analysis, and segment results by user cohorts to identify segment-specific effects.

methodologies.name: best-practices-2026
methodologies.description: Modern development demands staying current with language features, framework patterns, and ecosystem tools. This hub guides you through TypeScript 5 strict mode, React 19 server components, Python 3.13 patterns, and AI-assisted development workflows to maximize productivity and code quality.

methodologies.name: browser-automation-overview
methodologies.description: Browser automation tools like Puppeteer and Playwright enable web scraping, E2E testing, and PDF generation. Choose Puppeteer for simple, fast Chrome automation, or Playwright for cross-browser testing and complex scenarios with built-in waiting and retries. Use stealth plugins to evade detection and implement respectful rate limiting.

methodologies.name: cd-basics
methodologies.description: Continuous Delivery ensures code is always production-ready through automated testing, feature flags, and safe database migrations. Implement expand-contract migrations for backward compatibility, use feature flags to deploy code separately from releases, maintain comprehensive automated testing, and establish deployment readiness criteria before merging.

methodologies.name: cd-pipelines
methodologies.description: CD pipelines orchestrate multi-stage deployments from code commit through production with automated tests and deployment strategies. Structure pipelines with build, test, staging, and production stages, choose appropriate deployment strategies (blue-green, canary, rolling), implement automated rollback on health check failures, and track DORA metrics.

methodologies.name: continuous-delivery
methodologies.description: Continuous Delivery is the practice of keeping code in a perpetually releasable state through automation and testing. Enable multiple deployments per day by automating builds and tests, deploying to staging automatically, using feature flags to control feature visibility, and providing one-button production deployments.

methodologies.name: dev-methodologies-architecture
methodologies.description: Backend architecture patterns provide proven solutions for database migrations, API responses, authentication, caching, and observability. Apply these patterns to establish consistency across your codebase: use migration frameworks for schema changes, standardize error response formats, choose appropriate auth patterns (JWT, sessions, OAuth2), and implement structured logging.

methodologies.name: feature-flags-core-implementation
methodologies.description: Solves the anti-pattern of scattered ad-hoc environment variable checks by centralizing flag evaluation in a single FeatureFlagManager that registers typed FeatureFlag dataclasses and loads overrides from FF_* env vars and a JSON config file. Apply when building an in-process flag system without a SaaS dependency, wrapping new code paths for progressive rollout, adding kill switches to risky integrations, or integrating flag checks into FastAPI/Django middleware. The methodology delivers two clean integration points (a @feature_flag decorator that swaps between new and fallback implementations, and a context-manager middleware that pre-evaluates all flags into a ContextVar at request start), so call sites stay clean and the flag state is observable in incident logs. It also enforces the safety rule that unknown flag names always return False to prevent missing registrations from leaking new behavior to production.

methodologies.name: feature-flags-rollout-targeting
methodologies.description: Solves the broken-experience problem of non-deterministic rollout where the same user sees the new feature on one page load and the old feature on the next, by combining MD5 hash bucketing on the flag_name + user_id key with explicit allowlists and group membership evaluated in priority order. Apply for gradual 1 percent to 10 percent to 50 percent to 100 percent progressions, beta programs that pre-enroll employees and opted-in users, A/B experiments requiring stable per-user variant assignment, and tier-based access via groups like beta_testers or premium_tier. The practical value is consistent per-user assignment with uniform distribution, a documented rollout progression coupled to error-rate gates that automatically revert percentage to zero on a 0.5 percent regression, and a parallel LaunchDarkly recipe using Context.builder so the same priority logic works against a managed provider. It also surfaces the failure modes of session-scoped assignment, missing user context, and SDK outages causing app outages without a stale-while-revalidate cache.

methodologies.name: feature-flags-services-testing
methodologies.description: Closes the four operational gaps that complete a production-grade flag system: choosing a service, testing both branches, auditing stale flags, and wiring an agent to do the lifecycle work. Apply when picking between LaunchDarkly, Flagsmith, Unleash, GrowthBook, ConfigCat, PostHog, Statsig, or the OpenFeature CNCF spec, when writing pytest fixtures that must cover both ON and OFF code paths, when setting up a weekly GitHub Actions audit that opens an issue for flags past their expiry, and when configuring an agent to generate flag definitions, dual-branch tests, and SDD cleanup tasks. The value comes from forcing parametrized or fixture-based tests of the disabled path (the most common feature-flag bug), a working stale-flag finder script paired with a cron-driven audit workflow, and explicit prompts and gates that block agent failure modes such as writing only the happy-path test, leaving orphaned OFF branches, or flipping the wrong flag during incidents.

methodologies.name: feature-flags-types-lifecycle
methodologies.description: Solves flag debt by mandating that every flag declares one of five categories (release, experiment, ops, permission, kill-switch), each with distinct lifespan, ownership, and cleanup obligation. Apply at flag creation time, when running quarterly cleanup rituals, and when classifying existing flags during a debt audit; reach for it whenever a flag is registered, because category determines default lifespan, default value (release and experiment off, kill-switch on), and the rules around expiry. The practical value is a five-stage lifecycle (planning, development, rollout, full rollout, cleanup) with the rule that quarterly review either removes flags past expiry or formally extends them with written justification, plus a flag registry pattern with audit_stale_flags() that surfaces overdue flags and their owners. The methodology also documents the antipatterns that drive flag debt: nested flags, flag dependencies, using flags for config values, and version-drift naming like new_checkout_v2/v3.

methodologies.name: internationalization
methodologies.description: Internationalization prepares software for multiple languages and locales without code changes. Externalize all strings, support plural and gender rules via ICU format, format dates and currencies by locale, design layouts for text expansion (30% longer in other languages), implement RTL support with logical CSS properties.

methodologies.name: logging-patterns
methodologies.description: Structured logging provides production visibility for debugging, monitoring, and auditing while maintaining performance. Use JSON format for machine parsing, add correlation IDs for request tracing, redact sensitive data before logging, configure appropriate log levels (debug/info/warning/error/critical), and monitor log volume to prevent storage issues.

methodologies.name: monorepo-turborepo
methodologies.description: Turborepo enables scalable monorepos with intelligent caching, parallel execution, and incremental builds. Structure workspaces with clear app/package separation, configure task pipelines with explicit dependencies, use remote caching to avoid rebuilding unchanged code, and leverage Turborepo filters for targeted builds.

methodologies.name: perf-test-basics
methodologies.description: Performance testing validates applications meet speed and scalability requirements under realistic load conditions. Profile applications to find bottlenecks, conduct load testing under expected usage, stress testing to find breaking points, and endurance testing to detect resource leaks. Establish baselines and measure regressions systematically.

methodologies.name: perf-test-tools
methodologies.description: Performance testing tools address the problem of detecting system bottlenecks under load before they affect users in production. Use k6 and Locust to simulate realistic traffic patterns, identify response time degradation, and validate capacity thresholds. This methodology delivers confidence that your application scales reliably and catches performance regressions early in the development cycle.

methodologies.name: playwright-automation
methodologies.description: Playwright solves cross-browser automation for testing and web scraping by providing a unified API across Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit. Use it for end-to-end testing, screenshot validation, and headless browser tasks where reliability and modern browser support matter. The value is consistent, fast test execution and the ability to test real browser behavior without flakiness.

methodologies.name: pnpm-package-management
methodologies.description: pnpm addresses the inefficiency and complexity of npm and yarn by using a content-addressable store and strict dependency isolation. Use pnpm for all new JavaScript projects to reduce disk space, enable monorepo workflows, and prevent phantom dependencies that hide issues. The benefit is faster installs, smaller lock files, and a cleaner dependency graph that catches bugs earlier.

methodologies.name: practices-backend-languages
methodologies.description: Provides a breadth-first cheat sheet of canonical patterns across six backend stacks (Go, Ruby on Rails, PHP Laravel, Java Spring Boot, C# .NET, Rust) so agents producing cross-language code stop extrapolating from training distribution and instead match the project's known-good shape. Apply for greenfield service scaffolding, cross-language onboarding where the same service shape is needed in two stacks, refactor passes aligning an existing module, and as a code-review gate. The value is one canonical snippet per pattern that catches the most common drifts: Go's cmd/internal/pkg layout with fmt.Errorf %w wrapping and consumer-side interfaces, Rails service/form/query/presenter objects, Laravel constructor-injected services with Form Requests, Spring constructor injection via @RequiredArgsConstructor instead of field @Autowired, .NET interface-based async/await all the way down, and Rust thiserror Result types with the rule that reqwest::blocking must never appear inside #[tokio::main]. Each section pairs the rule with a testable checklist item so review can mechanically verify compliance.

methodologies.name: practices-django-coding
methodologies.description: Eliminates the four most common Django drift patterns (fat views, inline DB writes, unaliased imports, missing standard model fields) by enforcing module-level import aliases for cross-app references, services as plain functions in services/ modules, thin views under 20 lines that delegate to one service call, and a BaseModel that supplies uid (UUID), created_at, and updated_at on every model. Apply when scaffolding a new Django service, refactoring an existing app to a known-good shape, onboarding agents to produce Django code that mirrors a Spring or Rails service, or running PR review against Django apps/views/services. The value comes from a code-placement decision tree (DB mutations to services/, external calls to services/ or integrations/, pure functions to utils/) plus a checklist that integrates with ruff and pytest gates, and a ready-to-use prompt template that constrains agents to apply the standards and run ruff check --fix && pytest before returning. It also documents the multi-line keyword-only parameter style required for any function with more than two parameters.

methodologies.name: practices-frontend-components
methodologies.description: Locks down the four conventions that prevent component-library drift: Storybook CSF3 stories co-located in the component folder, design tokens as TypeScript constants in tokens/ rather than magic hex strings, a strict per-component file layout (Name.tsx, Name.stories.tsx, Name.test.tsx, Name.module.css, index.ts), and a 100-150 line CLAUDE.md skeleton with a hard 200-line cap. Apply when adding a component to a shared library, setting up Storybook for the first time, creating or extending design tokens, or generating CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md documentation for any source folder. The value is type-safe color palettes with numeric scales (50-900) plus a semantic layer, CSF3 stories that get TypeScript inference for controls so deprecated CSF2 Template.bind patterns are rejected, and a documentation budget that stops agents from over-summarizing folders into 500-line walls of prose that consume the working context window.

methodologies.name: practices-js-ts-stack
methodologies.description: Pins down six JS/TS conventions that keep React/Next.js projects type-safe and prevent state-management churn: explicit interface props on every component, custom hooks in hooks/useName.ts that return {data, loading, error} for shared async logic, tsconfig strict mode with import type for type-only imports, a standard src/ Node.js layout with .env.example committed, the Next.js App Router file structure with route groups in (name)/ syntax, and a state-management selection rule (useState, useContext, Zustand, Redux Toolkit, TanStack Query) keyed to scope. Apply for greenfield React/Next scaffolding, adding TypeScript to a JS project, choosing a state library, or as a code review gate on any PR touching components or hooks. The value is concrete rules that catch the recurring mistakes (any-typed props, raw useEffect+useState fetching, Pages Router patterns leaking into App Router projects, Redux-for-server-state overengineering) and a CSS architecture decision matrix that forbids mixing Tailwind with CSS Modules in one project.

methodologies.name: practices-python-ecosystem
methodologies.description: Establishes four foundational Python project conventions to stop fragmentation across services: FastAPI with Pydantic models and Depends() for DB and auth injection, the src/ project layout with pyproject.toml and a committed lockfile, complete type annotations on every public function, and uv (preferred 2026) or poetry for dependency management with separated dev and runtime dependencies. Apply for greenfield FastAPI scaffolding, refactor passes adding type hints, onboarding new developers or agents, and dependency conflict troubleshooting. The value is reproducible installs across machines via committed uv.lock or poetry.lock, FastAPI routes that delegate logic to services so they remain testable without the HTTP layer, and a forbid-list (no inline DB queries in route handlers, no bare def f(x) without annotations, no raw pip install -r requirements.txt without a lockfile) backed by a checklist suitable for ruff gates. It also flags the Pydantic v1 vs v2 trap (@validator vs @field_validator) where agents silently mix syntaxes.

methodologies.name: puppeteer-agent-workflow
methodologies.description: Solves the unbounded-wait, memory-leak, and runaway-process failures that occur when LLMs drive Puppeteer inline in the same turn, by establishing a stateless worker-process contract: the agent writes a script (or fills a parametric template), launches it via Bash with a hard timeout, captures console/screenshots/HAR to disk, and reads structured JSON back. Apply when configuring an agent to generate one-shot Puppeteer scripts, building a daemonized runner that the agent talks to over a queue or HTTP, selecting between self-hosted Puppeteer and managed services like Browserless, Apify, or Bright Data, and auditing LLM-generated code before execution. The value is a minimal agent-droppable harness using puppeteer-extra+stealth with a finally-block close, an explicit antipattern catalog (waitForTimeout removed in v22, networkidle0 hangs on SPAs, screenshot-derived selectors, credentials in logs, fully automated CAPTCHA loops), and a 3-5x speedup recipe combining launch flags with request interception for DOM-only scraping.

methodologies.name: puppeteer-launch-setup
methodologies.description: Covers the foundation of any Puppeteer pipeline: installing puppeteer or puppeteer-core, configuring launch flags for Docker/CI environments, navigating with the right waitUntil setting, and choosing among the five reliable wait strategies (waitForSelector, waitForXPath, waitForFunction, waitForNetworkIdle, waitForResponse). Apply for headless Chrome jobs that need DevTools Protocol features, PDF or screenshot generation in a Node-only pipeline, light scraping where stealth is not the primary concern, and serverless deployments via @sparticuz/chromium. The practical value is preventing the two most common failures: scripts that hang on networkidle0 against WebSocket-heavy SPAs, and CI runs that crash because --no-sandbox and --disable-dev-shm-usage are missing. It also pins PUPPETEER_CACHE_DIR for read-only containers, requires browser.close() in a finally block to avoid orphan Chromium processes, and rejects the deprecated page.waitForTimeout (removed in Puppeteer 22) in favor of targeted waits.

methodologies.name: puppeteer-output-capture
methodologies.description: Covers the page.screenshot() and page.pdf() APIs end to end: full-page and element-scoped screenshots, type/quality/clip/base64 options, and PDF generation with format, margins, headers, footers, and custom dimensions. Apply when generating PDFs or screenshots from rendered HTML in a Node pipeline, capturing visual regression artifacts, producing invoice or report PDFs from a print-stylesheet layout, or attaching screenshots as evidence in monitoring workflows. The value is pixel-accurate output through Chrome's actual rendering pipeline (superior to wkhtmltopdf or HTML-to-image libraries for layouts that depend on web fonts, CSS variables, or JS-rendered content), the storage-saving rule to use jpeg or webp at quality 80 for high-volume pipelines, the requirement to pass printBackground: true so CSS backgrounds survive (Chromium strips them by default), and the antipattern of calling page.pdf() immediately after page.goto() before lazy-loaded images and charts have rendered.

methodologies.name: puppeteer-page-interaction
methodologies.description: Covers the entire user-facing interaction surface of Puppeteer: CSS and XPath element selection, click/type/keyboard/mouse simulation, every form input type (text, select, multi-select, checkbox, radio, file upload, date), and the advanced patterns for multi-tab flows, iFrames, JS dialogs, downloads via CDP Page.setDownloadBehavior, and mobile device emulation. Apply when automating form fills, multi-step wizards, login flows, extracting data from pages that require interaction before content loads, or testing UIs that span new tabs and embedded frames. The value is a discipline of attribute-based selectors (data-testid, id, name) over screenshot-derived positional selectors, plus reusable safeNavigate, safeClick, and safeExtract wrappers that retry on transient failures and classify errors into network, timeout, and HTTP categories. It also flags the per-session nature of Page.setDownloadBehavior, which silently breaks when pages are recycled without re-issuing the CDP command.

methodologies.name: puppeteer-session-management
methodologies.description: Covers session persistence and traffic shaping in Puppeteer: cookie get/set/delete and round-trip serialization across runs, localStorage and sessionStorage manipulation, HTTP request interception for blocking resources or injecting auth headers, mocking API responses for deterministic tests, and capturing live response data. Apply when automating authenticated workflows where re-login on every run is too slow or triggers CAPTCHA, blocking images/fonts/stylesheets to gain a 3-5x speedup on DOM-only scraping, mocking external APIs for isolated tests, or adding Bearer tokens to API requests without touching page JS. The value is the rule that userDataDir per logical identity (passed to puppeteer.launch) is more reliable than manual cookie serialization for complex auth flows because it persists the full Chromium profile including HSTS and cert cache, plus the safety checklist requiring setRequestInterception(true) before navigation and ensuring every request handler calls continue/abort/respond so requests never hang indefinitely.

methodologies.name: puppeteer-stealth-proxy
methodologies.description: Addresses bot detection and IP-based rate limiting via puppeteer-extra-plugin-stealth (which patches navigator.webdriver, plugin lists, language array, and other headless tells automatically), manual evaluateOnNewDocument navigator overrides for environments where puppeteer-extra is unavailable, and proxy support spanning single-proxy authentication and rotating proxy pools where each browser instance is bound to one proxy at launch. Apply when scraping targets with basic bot detection, distributing requests across IPs to dodge rate limits, or making automation appear as a regular browser. The value comes with explicit limits: stealth lags behind Cloudflare Enterprise and Datadome (switch to Playwright with playwright-extra/stealth or a managed unlock service like Bright Data), credentialed or 2FA flows must not be looped without human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and the rule to pick exactly one evasion stance (plugin OR manual overrides) per project so subsequent assertions do not contradict the patched navigator props. It also flags the antipattern of unbounded retries on 429 responses from managed services that burn budget fast.

methodologies.name: testing-backend-languages
methodologies.description: Provides one canonical test idiom per backend language so agents stop picking the wrong framework or over-using full-context boots that blow test time. Apply when bootstrapping a test suite in Ruby on Rails (RSpec with let blocks and factory_bot, not Minitest), PHP Laravel (Pest function syntax with RefreshDatabase, not raw PHPUnit class style), Java Spring Boot (JUnit 5 + AssertJ + Mockito with test slices like @DataJpaTest and @WebMvcTest, never @SpringBootTest for a service unit test), C# .NET (xUnit + Moq + FluentAssertions, not WebApplicationFactory unless integration is the explicit goal), and Rust (#[cfg(test)] mod tests with #[tokio::test] for async to avoid the silent never-polled-future failure). The practical value is that agents produce idiomatic tests on the first pass, reviews shift from fixing framework choice to adding edge cases, and the cross-language best practices section enforces speed budgets (unit under 50ms, integration under 1s, full unit suite under 60s), forbids network calls in unit tests, and requires mutation score on critical modules instead of treating line coverage as a target.

methodologies.name: testing-django-pytest
methodologies.description: Anchors Django test style to one canonical pattern: pytest function-style tests with @pytest.mark.django_db, factory_boy factories instead of raw ORM creates, parametrize for edge cases, and conftest.py fixtures (api_client, authed_client) that wire APIClient with force_authenticate. Apply when bootstrapping a new Django service, filling coverage holes, producing first-cut tests during SDD in-progress/, or standardizing test style across a Django monorepo where engineers reinvent layouts per app. The value is reduced review noise (agents stop drifting into unittest.TestCase by default), tests that survive model changes because factories evolve with the schema while raw ORM creates rot, and a documented agent workflow that runs pytest -x after every generation step and feeds failures back before moving on. It also flags the LLM failure mode where a failing test gets fixed by silently editing production code without surfacing the diff, requiring a human-in-loop checkpoint, and rejects assert True coverage padding by requiring mutation score on critical modules via mutmut.

methodologies.name: testing-js-ts-frontend
methodologies.description: Anchors frontend testing to @testing-library/react (or vue/svelte equivalents) with vitest as the default for Vite-based projects and jest only for legacy non-Vite projects. Apply when adding tests to a React/Vue/Svelte component with no coverage, standardizing test style across a frontend codebase, producing first-cut tests during SDD in-progress/, or working in any Vite-based project. The value is the Testing Library rule that queries go by accessible role, label text, or visible text in that priority order, with CSS classes and data-testid as last resorts requiring a comment explaining why no semantic query was possible, so tests track real user-visible regressions instead of breaking on style refactors. It also enforces explicit imports from 'vitest' (or globals: true in vitest.config.ts) to prevent the silent jest-globals pollution agents fall into, requires the mock API to match the runner (vi.fn() for vitest, jest.fn() for jest), and rejects snapshot-test-everything as a default strategy because 500-line snapshots get approved blindly.

methodologies.name: trunk-based-branch-by-abstraction
methodologies.description: Eliminates the long-lived branch excuse for large refactors by replacing components incrementally on trunk through a strict five-step sequence: (1) introduce a Protocol/interface, (2) wrap the old implementation to satisfy it, (3) add the new implementation behind the same interface, (4) gate the swap via feature flag and validate, (5) remove the old implementation and collapse the abstraction back to a direct call. Apply when replacing a backend service, data layer, or third-party integration that affects many callers, migrating between API clients, or any refactor estimated to take more than one day. The practical value is mergeable-per-day commits with audit-friendly granularity, mapping cleanly onto the SDD task lifecycle (one TASK_*.md per step), and protection against the canonical agent failure mode of dropping step 5 and leaving permanent indirection plus a dead branch in the factory; the rule is to file the cleanup ticket at step-1 merge time so the obligation outlives the implementation work.

methodologies.name: trunk-based-challenges
methodologies.description: Diagnoses and fixes the three predictable points where TBD adoption fails: trunk is always broken (missing CI gates, slow CI, no flags, oversized commits), teams keep creating week-long branches (missing decomposition or flag infrastructure), and code review becomes the bottleneck (large PRs with no SLA). Apply when trunk goes red more than once a week, when the team keeps making exceptions for week-long branches despite wanting TBD, when PRs sit unreviewed for more than four hours, or when adopting SDD task lifecycle alongside TBD and needing the mapping. The value is a concrete checklist for each failure mode (gate merges on green CI, drive CI under 10 minutes, hide partial work behind flags, hold PRs under 200 lines diff with a 4-hour review SLA, rotate reviewers, use stacked PRs for sequential review) plus an explicit SDD+TBD workflow where each TASK_*.md = one short-lived branch = one squash-merge, with .aidocs/feature-flags.md as the tracking artifact that lists every active flag with its task ID and cleanup ticket. It also catalogs the agent regressions (type: ignore as a fix, hard-coded True flag defaults, 2000-line PRs, dropped abstraction collapse, branch protection drift) that erode TBD over time.

methodologies.name: trunk-based-ci-gates
methodologies.description: Establishes the two-gate quality protection that keeps trunk continuously releasable for trunk-based development: a sub-second pre-commit hook running lint and type-check on staged files only, and a CI pipeline kept under 10 minutes wall time that gates merge to main. Apply when any repo pushes to main at least once a day, when CI runs are exceeding 10 minutes, or when developers have started reaching for --no-verify because hooks are too slow. The methodology prescribes branch protection that includes every required status check, an auto-revert bot using git revert -m 1 on red trunk, and a strict ban on running pytest in pre-commit so the gate stays fast enough that no one bypasses it.

methodologies.name: trunk-based-dev-patterns
methodologies.description: Trunk-based development patterns solve the problem of long-lived branches that diverge and cause merge hell by combining feature flags, branch-by-abstraction, and dark launching. Apply when you want continuous integration without waiting for large features to complete, using flags to hide incomplete work. The value is faster feedback, reduced merge conflicts, and the ability to deploy safely while features are still under development.

methodologies.name: trunk-based-dev-principles
methodologies.description: Trunk-based development is a branching strategy where developers commit frequently to a single main branch, solving the complexity and merge pain of traditional feature branches. Use it in teams practicing CI/CD with automated testing and code review discipline, especially for web applications requiring rapid iteration. The benefit is elite DORA metrics including multiple deploys per day, reduced lead time, and fewer integration conflicts.

methodologies.name: trunk-based-feature-flags
methodologies.description: Solves how to merge incomplete features to trunk daily without exposing them to users by gating new code at the call site with feature flags. Apply when a feature takes more than a day to build, when a new implementation must be validated against live production traffic before cutover (Dark Launch), or when a multi-step feature can be assembled in increments with only the final wire-up being user-visible (Keystone Interface). The discipline includes one-flag-per-task naming (area.verb_thing), a cleanup ticket created at flag birth, banned boolean negatives, try/except isolation around dark-launch comparisons, and tolerant diffs to handle non-deterministic outputs so flagged code stays safe and removable.

methodologies.name: web-scraping-agentic-workflow
methodologies.description: Provides the end-to-end workflow an agent should follow when scraping a site without an API: probe SSR vs JS-rendered with curl -I, choose httpx+selectolax for static HTML or Playwright for dynamic pages, validate every row with Pydantic/Zod, write to a versioned raw/YYYY-MM-DD/source.jsonl dataset, and detect schema drift by failing when more than 5% of fields disappear between runs. Apply when targets lack official APIs or RSS, for research corpus building, competitor monitoring, or one-off backfills of public data. Includes practical guidance on robots.txt compliance, descriptive UA with contact email, 1-3 RPS throttling with jitter, raw HTML snapshots for re-parse, and managed alternatives like Firecrawl or Jina Reader when the agent only needs clean markdown text (saving 70% or more tokens versus raw HTML).

methodologies.name: web-scraping-element-extraction
methodologies.description: Covers DOM extraction primitives for Puppeteer ($eval/$$eval) and Playwright (locator API) plus the data-cleaning step that turns noisy raw strings into typed values fit for storage. Apply when extracting product names, prices, image URLs, table rows, or attribute values (href, src, data-*) from scraped pages, and especially before persisting to a JSONL dataset or database. The methodology emphasizes preferring Playwright's auto-waiting locator API over raw $$eval, normalizing whitespace, currency, and locale-dependent dates at extraction time so downstream datasets stay clean, and waiting for network idle or a sentinel element on JS-rendered pages before attempting to read the DOM.

methodologies.name: web-scraping-pagination
methodologies.description: Codifies the three pagination patterns that cover virtually every paginated site (next-button loop, infinite-scroll height watch, load-more click) along with their distinct termination conditions, plus browser-pool and semaphore strategies for bounding memory in concurrent scrapes. Apply when a listing spans multiple pages, when a feed uses infinite scroll, when a Load More button appends without navigation, or when many URLs must be scraped in parallel without exhausting browser memory. Each pattern's correct end-state check (next absent or disabled, scrollHeight stops growing, button gone or out of viewport) prevents the off-by-one and infinite-loop failures that produce incomplete or duplicated datasets, and item deduplication by index or hash is mandatory.

methodologies.name: web-scraping-resilience
methodologies.description: Adds the three resilience layers that turn a fragile scraper into something safe to run on a schedule: 1-3 second jittered delays per request to avoid IP bans, exponential-backoff retry for transient failures, and safe extraction helpers with timeouts that return defaults instead of hanging when a selector is absent. Apply to any scraper running on a schedule against a live site, anything processing more than a handful of URLs in a batch, or sites enforcing rate limits and bot detection. Includes anti-detection tactics (header sanitization, webdriver property overrides, p-limit at 3 or fewer concurrent requests for unknown targets) while explicitly noting that hard interactive CAPTCHAs (Cloudflare Turnstile) need a paid stealth service, not header tricks.

domains.description: Code Quality focuses on code standards, linting, testing practices, architecture patterns, refactoring, and technical debt management. Use when establishing code standards, improving test coverage, reducing technical debt, or ensuring code maintainability. Provides frameworks for measuring and improving code quality metrics.

methodologies.name: framework-decomposition-patterns
methodologies.description: Framework decomposition patterns address code organization in large modules by breaking them into smaller, LLM-friendly pieces that are easier to understand and maintain. Use these patterns to refactor fat controllers or oversized components into service layers and repositories that follow your framework's conventions. The value is improved code readability, faster LLM context usage, and architecture that scales as your project grows.

methodologies.name: tech-debt-management
methodologies.description: Technical debt management solves the problem of accumulated shortcuts that slow down development by providing prioritization matrices and payoff strategies. Apply a continuous 20% debt allocation strategy paired with boy scout rule improvements to prevent buildup without blocking features. The benefit is sustainable velocity, easier onboarding, and code that becomes easier to work with over time instead of harder.

methodologies.name: xp-extreme-programming
methodologies.description: Extreme Programming delivers high code quality and team productivity by taking good practices to their limits: pair programming for review, TDD for confidence, and sustainable pace to prevent burnout. Use XP in small teams with rapidly changing requirements where code quality directly impacts iteration speed. The value is fewer bugs, happier developers, and requirements that stay in sync with implementation.

domains.description: Frontend Developer handles frontend styling and design systems—Tailwind CSS, CSS-in-JS, design tokens, UI component libraries (shadcn/ui), Storybook, PWA development, responsive design, and accessibility. Use when building UI components, implementing design systems, styling applications, or creating design tokens. Includes 18 methodologies grounded in modern frontend practices.

methodologies.name: accessibility
methodologies.description: Accessibility ensures web applications are usable by people with disabilities while improving usability for everyone, solving both ethical and legal compliance requirements. Apply WCAG principles to semantic HTML, ARIA labels, keyboard navigation, and color contrast throughout development, not as an afterthought. The benefit is reaching a broader audience, better SEO, and code that's clearer and more maintainable for all users.

methodologies.name: css-in-js-advanced
methodologies.description: CSS-in-JS advanced patterns solve performance and maintainability challenges by providing zero-runtime solutions like vanilla-extract and optimized patterns for styled-components. Use when you need server-side style extraction, dynamic theming, or component-scoped styles without runtime overhead. The value is smaller bundles, faster rendering, and type-safe styling that scales across large design systems.

methodologies.name: css-in-js-basics
methodologies.description: CSS-in-JS basics provide component-scoped styling that eliminates naming conflicts and enables dynamic styles based on props, solving the organizational challenges of global CSS. Apply styled-components or Emotion for theming, variant management, and co-locating styles with components. The benefit is cleaner architecture, easier refactoring, and styling that scales with your component library.

methodologies.name: design-tokens-basics
methodologies.description: Design tokens centralize design decisions (colors, spacing, typography) as data, solving the consistency problem across platforms and the synchronization challenge between design and code. Use a hierarchical structure with primitive, semantic, and component tokens to maintain a single source of truth. The value is brand consistency, easier theme switching, and a design system that scales across web, mobile, and future platforms.

methodologies.name: design-tokens-implementation
methodologies.description: Design tokens implementation provides tooling and integration patterns to generate tokens for web, iOS, Android, and other platforms from a single definition. Apply Style Dictionary or Figma token sync to automate generation and distribution of tokens as CSS, JavaScript, and platform-specific formats. The benefit is automated consistency, reduced manual updates, and tokens that stay synchronized across your entire product suite.

methodologies.name: frontend-design
methodologies.description: Frontend design bridges design and development by establishing workflows where designers and developers collaborate on component specifications and design systems. Use when building component libraries, establishing design handoff processes, or creating reusable UI patterns. The value is reduced rework, faster component development, and design specifications that developers can implement confidently.

methodologies.name: mobile-responsive
methodologies.description: Mobile responsive design ensures optimal experiences across all screen sizes, solving the challenge of building once for phones, tablets, and desktops without maintaining separate code bases. Apply mobile-first CSS with fluid typography, flexible images, and responsive layouts that adapt to content, not fixed breakpoints. The benefit is one codebase serving all devices, better SEO, and users who can access your app anywhere.

methodologies.name: pwa-advanced
methodologies.description: PWA advanced features add push notifications, offline data sync, and background sync to create app-like experiences on the web, solving the gap between web and native apps. Use when you need user engagement through notifications or reliable offline experiences with data synchronization. The value is improved retention, offline capability that competitors lack, and a user experience comparable to native apps.

methodologies.name: pwa-core
methodologies.description: Progressive Web Apps solve the limitation of web apps being unusable offline and uninstalable by providing service workers, web app manifests, and offline-first strategies. Apply PWA principles to enable offline functionality, installability to home screen, and app-like behavior without distribution overhead. The benefit is an app experience that works on any platform, no app store dependency, and users who engage more with installable apps.

methodologies.name: seo-for-spas
methodologies.description: SEO for single-page applications solves the indexing problem that arises when JavaScript generates all content, requiring server-side rendering, meta tag management, and structured data. Use Next.js, Nuxt, or similar frameworks to pre-render critical pages and manage dynamic meta tags per route. The value is organic search visibility, better social sharing previews, and discoverability that rivals traditional server-rendered sites.

methodologies.name: shadcn-ui
methodologies.description: shadcn/ui provides copy-paste component primitives built on Radix UI and Tailwind, solving the problem of starting from zero with accessible, unstyled components. Use for rapid UI development with full control over component implementation and the ability to customize without dealing with npm package complexity. The benefit is customizable, accessible components that fit your design system without being locked into a library.

methodologies.name: shadcn-ui-architecture
methodologies.description: shadcn/ui architecture teaches composition patterns where primitives combine into complex UIs, solving the problem of component bloat and prop complexity. Apply the copy-don't-install philosophy to own your component code, use CVA for variant management, and compose instead of inheriting. The value is a component system that stays simple as it grows, making future customization easy and maintenance straightforward.

methodologies.name: storybook-setup
methodologies.description: Storybook solves component isolation and documentation by providing a development environment where components render without app context, enabling visual testing and design collaboration. Use for building component libraries, documenting design systems, and keeping components in sync with specifications. The benefit is components that are tested in isolation, documentation that's always up to date, and designers who can review components without running the app.

methodologies.name: tailwind
methodologies.description: Tailwind CSS provides utility-first styling that enables rapid UI development while maintaining consistency through a centralized design token system. Use for projects where composition over custom CSS is faster, where design systems benefit from systematic constraints, and where you want to avoid context-switching between CSS and JavaScript. The value is faster styling, smaller CSS bundles through purging, and consistency that grows with your project.

methodologies.name: tailwind-architecture
methodologies.description: Tailwind architecture teaches designing around utility composition, avoiding @apply overuse, and organizing design tokens to create scalable design systems. Apply design token extraction, component composition, and responsive patterns to build interfaces that stay maintainable as they scale. The benefit is a design system that enforces consistency, CSS that's as refactorable as code, and performance that improves with automation.

methodologies.name: tailwind-patterns
methodologies.description: Tailwind CSS is a utility-first framework enabling rapid UI development through composed utility classes. Apply when building custom designs, design systems, or component libraries where you need responsive, accessible interfaces without writing traditional CSS. Delivers faster iteration, consistent design tokens, and maintainable component patterns through variant-based composition.

methodologies.name: ui-lib-basics
methodologies.description: UI component libraries provide reusable, consistent interface elements that accelerate development and ensure design consistency across applications. Use when building scalable design systems, enabling team collaboration on frontend development, and reducing code duplication while maintaining accessibility compliance. Creates atomic components, compound patterns, and consistent APIs that form the foundation for product UIs.

methodologies.name: ui-lib-patterns
methodologies.description: Advanced component patterns enable scalable UI libraries through compound components, render props, polymorphic components, and portals. Apply when building complex component libraries requiring composition, context management, and flexible APIs that work across multiple UI contexts. Delivers maintainable architectures for large component systems with proper separation of concerns and extensibility.

domains.description: JavaScript Developer specializes in JavaScript/TypeScript development for web applications including React, Vue, Next.js, Node.js, and related ecosystems. Use when developing frontend or full-stack applications with JavaScript. Covers framework patterns, async programming, testing, and build tooling.

methodologies.name: nextjs-app-router
methodologies.description: Next.js App Router provides modern routing with React Server Components, progressive rendering, and simplified data fetching patterns. Use for new projects requiring SEO-critical rendering, complex nested layouts, and separation of server vs client concerns. Enables faster page loads, reduced JavaScript bundle size, and simpler full-stack development with colocation of related code.

methodologies.name: nodejs-service-layer-architecture
methodologies.description: Service layer architecture separates business logic from controllers and data access, improving testability and maintainability in Node.js applications. Apply when building REST APIs with complex logic, orchestrating across repositories, and managing transactions or side effects. Creates clear separation of concerns through dependency injection, enabling comprehensive unit testing and easier evolution.

methodologies.name: nodejs-service-layer-implementation
methodologies.description: Practical patterns for implementing service layers in Node.js using repositories, services, and controllers. Apply when building production APIs that need clear structure, error handling, and dependency injection. Provides concrete implementations showing repository interfaces, service orchestration, error domain objects, and type-safe abstractions.

methodologies.name: react-component-architecture
methodologies.description: Scalable component structure and patterns organize React applications from primitives through features using composition and colocation. Use when scaling component codebase, establishing team standards, or implementing design systems. Enables component reusability, separation of UI from logic, proper abstraction boundaries, and easier testing through container/presenter patterns.

domains.description: Python Developer specializes in Python development including Django, FastAPI, data science, and backend applications. Use when building Python applications, APIs, or data pipelines. Covers framework patterns, async programming, database integration, and testing.

methodologies.name: decomposition-django
methodologies.description: LLM-friendly code organization for Django projects breaks monolithic files into focused modules when they exceed size guidelines. Apply when models exceed 300 lines, views exceed 200 lines, or when using services/selectors pattern for business logic. Optimizes code for AI agent understanding while maintaining Django conventions and improving human code navigation.

methodologies.name: django-celery
methodologies.description: Celery enables asynchronous task processing in Django applications for long-running operations, scheduled tasks, and distributed workloads. Use for background processing like email, report generation, image processing, or API calls that shouldn't block HTTP responses. Implements reliable execution with retries, scheduling, and separate worker resources for handling variable workloads.

methodologies.name: django-services
methodologies.description: Service layer pattern in Django separates business logic from models, views, and serializers for improved testability and maintainability. Apply when implementing CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE operations, complex business rules, or multiple model interactions. Uses HackSoft pattern conventions (entity_action naming, keyword-only args, type hints) to create explicit, testable business logic.

domains.description: Software Architect makes informed architecture decisions balancing quality attributes (scalability, performance, security, maintainability) with business constraints. It covers system design, technology selection, architecture styles (monolith, microservices, serverless), design patterns, data modeling, distributed systems, and ADRs. Use when designing new systems, selecting technology, documenting decisions, or evaluating architectural trade-offs. Includes 30+ methodologies with comprehensive decision frameworks and case studies.

methodologies.name: api-gateway-graphql
methodologies.description: Designs GraphQL federation at the gateway with Apollo Router as the supergraph entry point, independently deployed domain subgraphs, and the Federation v2 directives (@key, @shareable, @external, @requires) for cross-subgraph entity resolution; layers in JWT/JWKS auth, query depth and complexity limits, and persisted queries for security. Apply when multiple teams own independent GraphQL services that must appear as one unified API, when migrating from REST to GraphQL incrementally, or when entities like User and Product are owned by different services but cross-referenced in queries. The methodology insists on running rover supergraph compose --config to verify composition before deployment because agents routinely mix v1 and v2 directives and produce supergraphs that fail composition checks.

methodologies.name: api-gateway-observability
methodologies.description: Instruments the API gateway with the three observability pillars — Prometheus metrics (rate, latency percentiles, error rate, circuit breaker state, rate-limit hits), structured access logs with correlation IDs and consumer identity, and OpenTelemetry distributed tracing with W3C trace-context propagation — plus SLO-based alerts that suppress false positives versus raw thresholds. Apply when standing up initial gateway monitoring, integrating Jaeger/Zipkin/Tempo, defining alerts for error rate or latency, or correlating traces to logs during incident investigation. Practical guidance includes 10% adaptive trace sampling (100% for errors and slow requests), logging request metadata only (not bodies, for GDPR/HIPAA and storage cost), and anchoring alerts on percentages and percentiles rather than absolute counts so they stay meaningful regardless of traffic volume.

methodologies.name: api-gateway-patterns
methodologies.description: Helps pick the right gateway pattern (simple routing, BFF, aggregation, edge+mesh) and the right technology (Kong, AWS API Gateway, Traefik, Envoy, APISIX, Apigee, Apollo Router) for the system in front of you. Apply when designing the front door for a new microservices or serverless system, when multiple client types (web, mobile, IoT, partner) need optimized surfaces, when migrating from monolith to microservices and needing a stable external surface, or when consolidating cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limit, observability) at one layer. Explicitly warns against using a gateway for a single monolith (a reverse proxy like nginx is sufficient), for east-west service-mesh traffic, or for simple Lambda Function URL deployments where the gateway adds latency and ops cost without benefit.

methodologies.name: api-gateway-resilience
methodologies.description: Layers four resilience mechanisms at the gateway to protect backends and prevent cascading failures: multi-level rate limiting (global / per-API / per-consumer / per-route), circuit breakers per upstream, retries with exponential backoff and jitter on idempotent operations only, and tiered timeouts (connection, read, write, overall) matched to backend SLAs. Apply when configuring rate limit tiers, hardening the gateway against a single misbehaving client saturating capacity for everyone, planning fallbacks and graceful degradation, or implementing adaptive limits driven by backend health. Includes critical anti-patterns: never wire breakers on /health polls (causes self-inflicted outages), never retry POST/PATCH/DELETE without idempotency, and force redis or cluster mode for rate-limit storage in multi-node deployments because local counters do not share.

methodologies.name: api-gateway-security
methodologies.description: Composes the layered defense at the gateway edge: TLS termination, authentication (API keys, JWT, OAuth 2.0, mTLS), authorization (RBAC and scopes for coarse-grained checks), WAF, request size and schema validation, locked-down CORS, security headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options), header sanitization to strip internal X-Internal-* and JWTs before forwarding, and audit logging for GDPR/HIPAA/PCI-DSS. Apply when configuring authentication for public or partner APIs, implementing mTLS for high-security integrations, setting WAF/DDoS rules, locking CORS in production, or adding compliance audit logging. Stresses caching authorizer results (300s TTL, JWKS) at high RPS, leaving fine-grained business authorization ("does this user own this document?") in the service tier rather than the gateway, and verifying generated JWKS URLs and cert chains with curl/openssl since agents hallucinate them.

methodologies.name: arch-pattern-clean
methodologies.description: Implements Robert C. Martin's Clean Architecture as concentric rings — Domain Entities at the center, Application Use Cases, Interface Adapters, then Frameworks and Drivers outermost — with the strict rule that source-code dependencies always point inward. Apply when domains have non-trivial business rules, when multiple UI surfaces (REST + CLI + workers) must share the same logic, when long-lived applications expect framework or database changes during their lifetime, or when comprehensive unit-test coverage of business logic without infrastructure is needed. Skip for CRUD-only admin tools, throwaway prototypes, or tiny microservices where the domain fits in 200 lines. The pattern is a particularly natural fit for agentic development because explicit layer boundaries let agents generate domain entities and use cases first and architecture tests verify the dependency direction mechanically.

methodologies.name: arch-pattern-ddd
methodologies.description: Applies Eric Evans's Domain-Driven Design to tame domain complexity through ubiquitous language with domain experts, strategic patterns (bounded contexts, subdomains, context maps) and tactical building blocks (Entities, Value Objects, Aggregates, Domain Events, Repositories, Domain Services, Factories). Apply to complex domains with intricate business rules requiring close expert collaboration, systems where the same word (e.g. Customer) means different things in different contexts, large-scale apps where aggregate consistency boundaries are critical, or microservices systems where bounded contexts map to service boundaries. DDD is complementary to (not a replacement for) Clean or Hexagonal — each bounded context internally uses one of those, while DDD's strategic patterns govern the boundaries between contexts. Skip for simple CRUD, teams without domain-expert access, or time-pressured MVPs since the investment in language and context maps pays back over months, not days.

methodologies.name: arch-pattern-hexagonal
methodologies.description: Implements Alistair Cockburn's Ports and Adapters pattern: an isolated application core with explicit inbound (driving) ports for REST/CLI/gRPC primary adapters and outbound (driven) ports for database, email, payment, or third-party secondary adapters. Apply to multi-interface systems where REST API, CLI, gRPC, scheduled jobs, and queue consumers all share the same core, applications with several outbound dependencies that each need a testable seam, or any project where new input or output adapters will be added frequently. The symmetric treatment of inputs and outputs through one port vocabulary is the key distinction from Clean Architecture; both share dependency inversion but Hexagonal makes inbound and outbound boundaries explicit and uniform. Skip for CRUD-only tools, single-channel REST apps where Clean is less verbose, or microservices small enough that the entire domain fits in 200 lines.

methodologies.name: arch-pattern-onion
methodologies.description: Implements Jeffrey Palermo's Onion Architecture as four rings — Domain Model (entities, value objects, repository interfaces) at the center with zero outward dependencies, then Domain Services for cross-entity logic, then Application Services for use cases and orchestration, then Infrastructure outermost — all coupling inward. Apply primarily in enterprise .NET (C#/F#) where the Onion vocabulary is established and tooling maps naturally to projects/layers, in apps where a separate Domain Services ring is conceptually meaningful (e.g., a pricing engine spanning Product, Customer, and Promotion aggregates), or for teams already fluent in the Onion naming convention. The key distinction from Clean Architecture is the explicit Domain Services layer for cross-entity business logic that does not belong on a single entity. Skip for simple domains where the Domain.Services ring would be empty, non-.NET stacks where teams know Clean or Hexagonal vocabulary instead, or microservices where the four-layer split is overhead.

methodologies.name: architecture-decision-records
methodologies.description: ADRs document architecture decisions with context, rationale, alternatives, and consequences for future reference and team alignment. Write when making technology choices, architecture style decisions, or significant design trade-offs. Preserves decision knowledge, enables onboarding, facilitates trade-off discussions, and creates an architecture knowledge base.

methodologies.name: behavioral-patterns
methodologies.description: Behavioral patterns (Strategy, Observer, Command, State, etc.) define how objects interact, communicate, and distribute responsibilities through algorithms and state management. Apply when needing runtime algorithm switching, event notification systems, undo/redo, state machines, or middleware pipelines. Improves flexibility and decoupling through explicit communication patterns.

methodologies.name: c4-model
methodologies.description: C4 provides hierarchical visualization of software architecture at four levels: Context (system overview), Container (technology decisions), Component (internal structure), and Code (implementation). Create when documenting system architecture for multiple audiences (non-technical to developers) and storing architecture diagrams alongside code. Enables architecture-as-code using Structurizr DSL, PlantUML, or Mermaid.

methodologies.name: caching-architecture
methodologies.description: Caching reduces latency, database load, and infrastructure costs by strategically storing frequently accessed data at multiple layers (CDN, application, database). Design when achieving performance goals, handling traffic spikes, or reducing backend load. Implements patterns (cache-aside, write-through, write-behind) with appropriate invalidation strategies (TTL, event-based) for consistency.

methodologies.name: creational-patterns
methodologies.description: Creational patterns (Factory, Builder, Singleton, Prototype, DI) abstract object creation, improving flexibility and maintainability. Apply when object creation is complex, type varies at runtime, or objects need different initialization paths. Modern systems often replace Singleton and Factory with dependency injection frameworks, simplifying instantiation.

methodologies.name: data-modeling
methodologies.description: Data modeling bridges business requirements and database implementation across conceptual (entities), logical (attributes/relationships), and physical (tables/indexes) levels. Design when establishing database schema for new systems, migrating between paradigms (relational to document), or optimizing for specific access patterns. Ensures data consistency, performance, and correctness.

methodologies.name: database-selection
methodologies.description: Database selection framework categorizes options by primary access pattern (relational, document, key-value, time-series, graph, vector) and evaluates using CAP theorem. Choose based on consistency/availability tradeoffs, scale requirements, and team expertise. Covers 2026 landscape including NewSQL, vector databases for AI/RAG, and specialized time-series databases.

methodologies.name: decision-tree-architecture-style
methodologies.description: Provides a decision tree for choosing between Monolith, Modular Monolith, and Microservices grounded in 2025 industry data: under 10 developers must start with a Monolith, 10-50 must use a Modular Monolith unless bounded contexts and DevOps maturity are already strong, and only teams of 50+ with high DevOps maturity, clear bounded contexts, end-to-end service ownership, and a platform team should consider Microservices. Apply when starting a new project, evaluating a monolith-to-microservices migration, onboarding non-architects who need a defensible answer without a full ATAM cycle, or shortlisting options before heavier trade-off analysis. The tree explicitly references CNCF 2025 evidence that 42% of organizations who adopted microservices have consolidated back, plus headline cases like Amazon Prime Video's 90% cost cut by reverting to a monolith, to gate against premature distribution.

methodologies.name: decision-tree-build-vs-buy
methodologies.description: Walks a build vs buy decision through strategic importance (is this a competitive differentiator?) as the first gate, then 3-year TCO comparison, time-to-value, and integration complexity, with a vendor-evaluation scorecard for shortlisted commercial options. Apply when evaluating any new capability where both custom development and commercial solutions exist, reviewing an existing custom-built solution against newer commercial alternatives, justifying a build decision to stakeholders, or filtering vendors down to 2-3 candidates. A formal TCO is mandatory for any decision over $50k 3-year cost and must cover initial development or licensing, 3-year maintenance or subscription, integration effort, training, and the engineering opportunity cost diverted from core product — most build decisions undercount maintenance (15-20% of build cost per year) and security audit costs.

methodologies.name: decision-tree-cloud-provider
methodologies.description: Routes the AWS vs Azure vs GCP choice first by existing technology investments (Microsoft AD/Office 365 → Azure; Google Workspace/BigQuery → GCP; neutral → open), then by workload type, compliance certifications, data residency, and budget sensitivity, outputting a recommended provider, runner-up, and cost-optimization tips. Apply for greenfield projects with no cloud footprint, when adding a secondary provider for a specific workload (e.g., GCP for ML on top of an AWS-primary architecture), for compliance-gated decisions requiring a structured audit, or for cost-sensitive startups comparing free tiers and sustained-use discounts. The tree treats existing investments as the dominant factor because switching identity providers (AD vs Workspace) or analytics platforms (BigQuery vs Redshift) costs more than marginal cloud-service price differences, and migrations between hyperscalers typically take 12-18 months.

methodologies.name: decision-tree-process
methodologies.description: Defines a six-phase meta-process for architecture decisions: (1) problem definition with a single answerable question, explicit out-of-scope items, and reversibility assessment; (2) options generation; (3) trade-off analysis; (4) technical and stakeholder validation; (5) ADR documentation; (6) implementation planning with a review schedule. Apply to any significant architecture choice — style, technology, database, cloud provider, build vs buy — especially one-way doors that are hard or expensive to reverse, cross-team decisions needing alignment, or any choice where "why did we choose this?" will be asked in 12+ months. The phases exist because ad-hoc decisions suffer from scope creep, premature commitment, hidden trade-offs, stakeholder surprise, institutional amnesia, and drift; each phase closes one of those failure modes, and the domain-specific trees (architecture-style, tech-stack, cloud-provider, build-vs-buy) feed phase 2 as input.

methodologies.name: decision-tree-tech-stack
methodologies.description: Walks a tree to select frontend frameworks, backend languages, and mobile platforms by routing on application type first, then SEO requirements, performance needs, and team expertise, producing a recommended stack with runner-up and rationale. Apply when starting a new project or service, evaluating whether to adopt a new framework for an existing codebase, onboarding a team that needs a defensible documented choice, or filtering 5-10 candidates down to 2-3 for deeper comparison. Team expertise must carry at least 25% weight in the decision matrix and a team-familiar option scoring within 0.5 points of the top should win, because retraining and hiring friction are real measurable costs that 3-6 months of ramp-up rarely justifies for a small performance edge — the tree encodes 2025 market-share data so recommendations stay grounded in ecosystem reality.

methodologies.name: modular-monolith
methodologies.description: Modular monoliths combine monolith simplicity with microservice boundaries through strict module isolation and clear communication patterns. Use when scaling teams (10-50 devs) without distributed system complexity, enabling future extraction to microservices. Implements modules as bounded contexts with public APIs, separate schemas, and asynchronous event communication.

methodologies.name: monolith-architecture
methodologies.description: Traditional monolith architecture deploys all functionality as single unit, offering simplicity and performance for small teams and early-stage systems. Choose for teams <10 devs, MVPs, unclear domain boundaries, or limited DevOps expertise. Enables simple debugging, ACID transactions, and rapid iteration with the option to extract services later.

methodologies.name: patterns-overview
methodologies.description: Design patterns overview catalogs creational, structural, behavioral, architectural, and cloud-native patterns for solving recurring design problems. Reference when recognizing design needs, teaching patterns to teams, or selecting between pattern alternatives. Maps patterns to modern language features (lambdas replacing Strategy, reactive streams replacing Observer) and new AI-influenced patterns.

methodologies.name: performance-architecture
methodologies.description: Performance architecture designs systems to meet latency, throughput, and efficiency targets through optimization across client, edge, application, and data layers. Apply when defining SLOs (service level objectives), designing for scalability, or optimizing existing systems. Covers scaling patterns (horizontal/vertical), caching hierarchies, database optimization, and performance monitoring.

methodologies.name: quality-attributes
methodologies.description: Quality attributes framework (ISO 25010) defines measurable non-functional properties (performance, security, availability, maintainability) that determine system success. Document when establishing architecture requirements, evaluating design trade-offs, or assessing system fitness. Enables quality-driven architecture decisions with tactical patterns tied to each attribute.

methodologies.name: security-architecture
methodologies.description: Security architecture designs systems with defense in depth, Zero Trust principles, and modern authentication patterns. Apply when building systems handling sensitive data, requiring compliance, or exposed to external threats. Delivers comprehensive protection across network, identity, application, and data layers with measurable security controls.

methodologies.name: serverless-architecture-patterns
methodologies.description: Catalog of six core composition patterns for serverless workloads (API backend, event processing, saga/Step Functions orchestration, fan-out/fan-in, CQRS with event sourcing, edge computing) plus the most common anti-patterns (lambdaliths, nano-functions, synchronous chains, missing DLQ, missing idempotency). Apply when composing Lambda functions into multi-step workflows, building event-driven pipelines across SQS/SNS/EventBridge/Kinesis, or auditing existing serverless designs. Provides function-design rules, real-world reference architectures, and idempotency code examples.

methodologies.name: serverless-cold-start-optimization
methodologies.description: Diagnostic and mitigation guide for Lambda cold starts, covering runtime-specific impact (Java/.NET 1-5s vs Python/Node 100-500ms vs Go/Rust 50-150ms), code-level patterns (module-scope init, package size reduction), and infrastructure choices (ARM/Graviton, SnapStart, provisioned concurrency). Apply when cold-start rate exceeds 10% on user-facing functions, when p95 latency spikes trace to cold starts, or when evaluating provisioned-concurrency ROI. Ranks fixes by ROI to avoid defaulting to the most expensive option.

methodologies.name: serverless-cost-optimization
methodologies.description: FinOps framework for AWS Lambda workloads, decomposing cost into requests + GB-seconds + transfer and ranking optimization levers by savings: ARM/Graviton (20%), right-sizing via Power Tuning (10-50%), batch processing (30-70%), and Savings Plans/Managed Instances. Apply during architecture design, monthly cost reviews, anomaly investigation, or when evaluating Lambda-vs-Fargate migration at the 10-30M requests/month crossover. Includes per-function tagging, anomaly alerts, and SAM templates with batching plus partial-batch failure reporting.

methodologies.name: serverless-foundations
methodologies.description: Foundational reference for serverless computing: key characteristics (stateless, event-driven, pay-per-execution, scale-to-zero), FaaS provider constraints (AWS/Azure/GCP/Cloudflare/Vercel timeouts and memory limits), serverless-compatible databases (DynamoDB, PlanetScale, Neon, Upstash, Turso), and supporting services across the three major clouds. Apply when assessing whether serverless fits a use case, comparing providers, or onboarding architects to FaaS economics. Provides a fit-assessment decision matrix and explicit when-not-to-use cases (long-running jobs, steady high load, sub-50ms latency, GPU work).

methodologies.name: serverless-iac-and-templates
methodologies.description: Selection guide and copy-paste templates for serverless IaC frameworks: AWS SAM (AWS-native simplicity), Serverless Framework (multi-cloud plugins), SST (TypeScript full-stack DX), CDK (type-safe complex infra). Apply when scaffolding a new Lambda project, generating REST APIs, SQS processors, Step Functions workflows, or scheduled jobs, and when authoring least-privilege IAM. Production-ready templates default to ARM, X-Ray tracing, DynamoDB PITR, DLQs with maxReceiveCount=3, and ReportBatchItemFailures, plus a Powertools handler pattern for logging, tracing, metrics, and idempotency.

methodologies.name: structural-patterns
methodologies.description: Structural design patterns compose objects and classes into larger structures while maintaining loose coupling. Apply when integrating legacy systems, building tree hierarchies, adding behavior dynamically, or simplifying complex subsystems. Delivers flexible designs through adapter, bridge, composite, decorator, facade, proxy, and flyweight patterns.

methodologies.name: system-design-process
methodologies.description: System design process transforms requirements into actionable architecture through structured phases: understand, scope, design, validate, document. Use for new systems, technology decisions, or architectural reviews. Delivers architecture decisions, C4 diagrams, API contracts, and implementation plans aligned with business priorities.

methodologies.name: trade-off-build-vs-buy
methodologies.description: Structured framework for build-vs-buy-vs-hybrid capability decisions, combining strategic classification (Core/Context/Commodity), 3-year TCO comparison, vendor evaluation (financial stability, roadmap alignment, lock-in, data portability), and mandatory exit-strategy documentation. Apply when evaluating any capability with significant subscription or development investment (auth, billing, search, notifications, analytics). Counters the Forrester finding that 67% of failed implementations stem from incorrect build-vs-buy choices and the typical underestimation of build effort and operational complexity.

methodologies.name: trade-off-decision-matrix
methodologies.description: Construction technique for weighted decision matrices comparing 3-5 architecture options on a 1-5 scoring scale with sensitivity analysis to verify winner stability. Apply for technology selection, vendor comparison, or any moderate-impact technical choice where stakeholders contest criteria weights. Includes a standard 2-4 hour seven-phase checklist (context, options, criteria, evaluation, trade-offs, risks, documentation) and a 30-minute quick checklist for Type-2 reversible decisions. Flags the anti-pattern of accepting matrix winners when top-2 scores fall within 5%.

methodologies.name: trade-off-decision-methods
methodologies.description: Method-selection guide for architecture trade-off evaluation, scaling depth to decision reversibility: full ATAM (3-4 days, Type-1 irreversible), ATRAF for reference architectures, Mini-ATAM (half day), TARA (2-4 hours), and LAAAM (1 day). Apply when classifying a decision as Type 1 vs Type 2 to choose proportional analysis depth, building utility trees, or distinguishing sensitivity points from trade-off points. Mandates quantitative response measures on every quality-attribute scenario and prescribes diverge/converge/red-team subagent splits to counter LLM selection bias.

methodologies.name: trade-off-quality-attributes
methodologies.description: Reference catalog of quality-attribute conflict pairs (Performance vs Maintainability, Security vs Usability, Availability vs Consistency, Scalability vs Cost, etc.) grounded in ISO 25010, plus pre-tabulated trade-off profiles for architecture styles (monolith, modular monolith, microservices, serverless, event-driven), CAP theorem positions (CP/AP/CA), and communication patterns (sync/async/event-driven). Apply when building ATAM scenarios, choosing architecture style or storage strategy, or writing the Consequences section of an ADR. Mandates that every decision name the attribute it degrades.

methodologies.name: trade-off-stakeholder-communication
methodologies.description: Audience-specific communication framework for architecture trade-offs across executives, product, engineering, and operations, with mandatory six-element decision documentation (context, options, criteria/weights, trade-offs, risks/mitigations, rationale). Apply when writing ADR Consequences sections, presenting decisions to non-technical stakeholders, or running pre-commitment reviews of Type-1 decisions. Counters stakeholder surprise — the costliest failure mode where trade-offs are discovered in production — by requiring summaries to preserve key risks even when complexity is omitted.

methodologies.name: trade-off-technical-debt
methodologies.description: Decision framework for speed-vs-quality trade-offs based on Fowler's debt quadrant (deliberate/inadvertent x prudent/reckless), with a 15-20% debt budget, mandatory debt registry, and explicit repayment triggers (next feature in area, Nth bug, calendar deadline).
