Critical for brand credibility and marketing before launch.

methodologies.name: niche-evaluation
methodologies.description: Niche evaluation determines if a market segment is viable by assessing market size (TAM/SAM/SOM), competition positioning, audience accessibility, monetization potential, and personal fit. The scoring formula weights each factor and provides decision thresholds from 4.0+ (excellent, proceed) to <3.0 (reconsider). Use to prioritize between multiple ideas and avoid niches too broad or small.

methodologies.name: pricing-research
methodologies.description: Pricing research determines optimal pricing through value analysis, competitor benchmarking, and willingness-to-pay testing using van Westendorp methodology. It guides model selection (subscription vs one-time vs usage-based), tier design with 2-3x price jumps, and validation through pre-launch pages and A/B testing. Essential to avoid leaving money on the table or pricing yourself out of market.

domains.description: Research methodologies and user insights generation through qualitative and quantitative research methods. Covers user interviews, surveys, ethnographic research, data analysis, and insight synthesis. Use this when you need deep user understanding, validating product hypotheses, generating customer insights, discovering pain points, or building user empathy.

methodologies.name: feature-discovery
methodologies.description: Feature discovery systematically identifies, prioritizes, and validates potential product capabilities through customer research, usage analytics, competitive analysis, and market trends. It applies the Kano model to categorize features and RICE scoring for prioritization, with validation methods (prototype, fake door, wizard of Oz) before building. Use to build features users actually want, not guesses.

methodologies.name: idea-generation
methodologies.description: Idea generation discovers business opportunities by combining skills, market needs, and interests using seven frameworks: skills inventory, pain point mining, job substitution, productized services, unbundling, market stacking, and personal problems. Score each idea on market size, personal fit, competition, monetization, and MVP speed to identify highest-potential opportunities.

methodologies.name: jobs-to-be-done
methodologies.description: Jobs to Be Done (JTBD) focuses on the progress customers seek rather than their stated desires, using the formula: When [situation] I want to [action] So I can [outcome]. It identifies functional, emotional, and social jobs; maps the forces of progress (push, pull, habit, fear); and reveals true competition beyond obvious alternatives. Use to understand real customer motivations and identify unexpected competitors.

methodologies.name: niche-evaluation
methodologies.description: Niche evaluation determines market viability by assessing market size, competition, audience accessibility, monetization, and personal fit using a weighted scoring formula. It defines niches precisely using [Product] for [Audience] who [Problem] format and maps competitors on quality vs number matrix. Thresholds guide decisions from 4.0+ (excellent) to <3.0 (reconsider).

methodologies.name: pain-point-research
methodologies.description: Pain point research systematically discovers problems through targeted mining of Reddit, Twitter, reviews, Q&A sites, and communities using Tier 1-4 source hierarchy. It categorizes findings (time waste, money loss, complexity, etc.) and scores each pain on frequency, severity, reach, spending, and alternative solutions. Essential to identify real opportunities and validate that problems are worth solving.

methodologies.name: pricing-research
methodologies.description: Pricing research determines optimal price points through value-based analysis (time saved × wage = maximum price), competitor benchmarking matrices, and van Westendorp willingness-to-pay testing. It guides model selection, tier design with clear differentiation, and validation via pre-launch pages and A/B testing. Prevents underpricing that leaves revenue on the table or overpricing that loses customers.

methodologies.name: problem-validation
methodologies.description: Problem validation uses evidence-based hierarchy from strongest (users paid for solution) to weakest (users said they have problem), focusing on commitment signals and red flags. The mom test methodology asks about past behaviors not hypotheticals and uses the vision-framing-weakness-pedestal-ask opening framework. Essential to confirm problems exist before building solutions and investing time.

methodologies.name: problem-validation-2026
methodologies.description: Problem validation in 2026 emphasizes evidence hierarchy from actual payment through commitment to stated preference, using mom test questions that focus on past behavior not hypotheticals. Interview openings follow a structured framework: vision, framing, weakness, pedestal, ask. Commitment signals (time, reputation, money) matter more than compliments or hypotheticals.

methodologies.name: success-metrics-definition
methodologies.description: Success metrics definition identifies what truly matters using metric types (input vs output, vanity vs actionable), establishes a North Star metric, breaks down into 3-5 primary KPIs using AARRR framework, and sets data-backed targets. The hierarchy puts business goals at top, metrics at middle, and guardrails at bottom. Use to focus teams on what moves the business needle.

methodologies.name: use-case-mapping
methodologies.description: Use case mapping documents specific ways users interact with your product through actors, goals, preconditions, main flows, alternatives, and postconditions. It identifies actor types (primary, secondary, external), maps goal levels (summary, user, subfunction), and diagrams dependencies and relationships. Essential to ensure products support complete user workflows and catch edge cases.

methodologies.name: user-interviews
methodologies.description: User interviews are structured conversations with target customers using mom test principles: talk about their life not your idea, ask about specific past events not hypotheticals, listen more than you talk. The process includes recruiting participants, preparing scripts (warm-up, context, problem exploration, solutions, wrap-up), conducting interviews, and synthesizing patterns. Run 10+ interviews to identify reliable patterns.

methodologies.name: value-proposition-design
methodologies.description: Value proposition design creates clear promises of value through the canvas framework mapping customer jobs, pains, and gains against product offerings, pain relievers, and gain creators. It uses the formula: For [target] Who [need] [product] is [category] That [benefit] Unlike [alternative] We [differentiator]. Apply to ensure your offering actually solves meaningful problems your customers care about.

domains.description: Specification-Driven Development orchestrating the full workflow from specification through execution. Covers constitution and standards, roadmapping, specifications, design documents, implementation planning, task creation, quality gates, and reflexion learning. Use this when establishing development standards, planning major features, ensuring quality across implementation, or building team knowledge systems.

methodologies.name: api-first-development
methodologies.description: API-first development designs the API contract (OpenAPI specification) before writing code, making the specification the single source of truth for code generation, testing, documentation, and SDKs. It enables parallel development, reduces LLM hallucinations, provides living documentation, and supports multi-agent systems. Use versioning strategies and semantic versioning for sustainable API evolution.

methodologies.name: architecture-decision-records
methodologies.description: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) capture important architectural decisions with context and consequences, providing historical reasoning for design choices. ADRs have lifecycle (proposed, accepted, deprecated, superseded), stay immutable once accepted, use sequential numbering in version control, and integrate with agile sprints. Write ADRs for large-impact decisions using Nygard or MADR formats.

methodologies.name: backlog-grooming-roadmapping
methodologies.description: Backlog grooming systematically manages potential work and prioritization through weekly sessions that validate ideas, re-score based on new data, refine upcoming items, and clean archives. Prioritization frameworks (RICE, MoSCoW, value vs effort) guide decisions. Roadmap types (now-next-later, quarterly, outcome-based) communicate strategy to different audiences. LLMs help with thought clarification but humans own final decisions.

methodologies.name: code-review-cycle
methodologies.description: Code review cycle integrates human expertise with AI assistance through self-review, AI pre-screening, peer review, and architectural review. AI excels at style, patterns, and common issues while humans own business logic, security implications, and architecture. The workflow moves from author's self-review through automated checks, AI review, human review, iterations, and finally reflexion learning. Multi-model strategies use different LLMs for different strengths.

methodologies.name: design-docs-big-tech
methodologies.description: Comprehensive comparison of design document practices across major tech companies (Google, Amazon, Uber, Spotify, Netflix, Microsoft). Learn how leading organizations use design docs for early issue identification, consensus building, and knowledge sharing. Document before coding to catch design flaws when changes are cheap, and apply company-specific patterns (6-pagers, RFCs, ERDs) based on your team's needs.

methodologies.name: design-docs-patterns
methodologies.description: Lightweight vs heavyweight design doc approaches scaled to your problem size and team structure. Understand when to write informal 1-2 page docs vs formal 5-10+ page documents, how to integrate with Architecture Decision Records (ADRs), and effective review processes. Use the spectrum of formality—from quick RFCs to full ERDs—and learn LLM-assisted drafting techniques.

methodologies.name: key-trends-summary
methodologies.description: Survey of major 2025-2026 trends reshaping software development: Specification-Driven Development emergence, living documentation practices, ADR adoption across AWS/GCP/Azure, LLM-first workflows, platform engineering at scale, and observability-driven development. Understand how the development bottleneck shifted from implementation to specification, and how to structure work for AI-assisted coding.

methodologies.name: living-documentation
methodologies.description: Treat documentation as living artifacts that evolve with code via docs-as-code philosophy. Generate API docs from OpenAPI specs, code comments, and test scenarios rather than manual updates. Understand Cyrille Martraire's principles (Reliable, Low-Effort, Collaborative, Insightful), set up CI/CD for documentation validation, and optimize docs for both human and LLM consumption.

methodologies.name: mistake-memory
methodologies.description: Capture and prevent recurring LLM coding errors through structured mistake documentation. Distinguish hallucination errors (API inventing), context errors (information fade), implementation errors (edge cases), and process errors (skipped validation). Use five-whys analysis to find root causes, implement prevention layers (warnings, quality gates, automated rules), and integrate mistakes into task context.

methodologies.name: pattern-memory
methodologies.description: Build an evolving knowledge base of successful solutions with pattern memory. Capture high-confidence patterns (0.8+ score) from completed tasks—error handling approaches, API patterns, testing strategies—with working examples and application contexts. Store in .aidocs/memory with confidence scoring, reuse patterns across similar tasks, and let patterns feed into agent context before execution.

methodologies.name: quality-gates-confidence
methodologies.description: Establish systematic checkpoints (L1 through L6) to validate LLM-generated code before proceeding: syntax/format, unit tests, integration tests, code review, staging validation, and production gates. Calculate confidence scores combining automated checks (40%), coverage (20%), risk mitigation (20%), and traceability (20%). Use as pass/fail criteria for phase transitions.

methodologies.name: reflexion-learning
methodologies.description: Apply the Reflexion paper's verbal reinforcement learning to development: agents attempt tasks, receive feedback, reflect verbally, and store reflections in episodic memory. Map to PDCA cycle—Plan (load memory), Do (execute), Check (evaluate), Act (update memory)—enabling learning without weight updates. Integrate pattern and mistake memory with quality gates for continuous improvement.

methodologies.name: sdd-workflow-overview
methodologies.description: Complete end-to-end SDD workflow from constitution through done: specification (WHAT), design (HOW), test-plan (verification), implementation-plan (tasks), execution (parallel waves), and review (independent validation). Understand why specifications reduce LLM hallucinations by anchoring output to structured requirements, and when to skip SDD (< 2 hour tasks) versus 15-minute lightweight planning.

methodologies.name: task-creation-parallelization
methodologies.description: Decompose features into LLM-executable tasks following the 100k token rule—each task fits within context limits. Apply INVEST criteria (independent, negotiable, valuable, estimable, small, testable) and wave-based execution for 1.8-3.5x speedup. Manage context budgets (15% docs, 10% dependencies, 25% research, 40% implementation, 10% testing) and propagate patterns between task sessions.

methodologies.name: templates
methodologies.description: Copy-paste ready templates for all SDD documents: constitution (standards), spec (requirements), design (architecture), test-plan (verification), implementation-plan (breakdown), and memory files. Understand lifecycle progression and token budgets per document. Customize for your project, co-locate with code in git, and use YAML frontmatter for machine-readable metadata.

methodologies.name: workflows
methodologies.description: Step-by-step workflows for each SDD phase with decision points and state transitions. Choose from five workflow patterns: vertical with brainstorming branches, parallel preparation with checkpoints, spec-driven parallel development, async parallel spec generation, or multi-perspective planning. Manage confidence thresholds (70-95%) for phase transitions and integrate memory files for learning.

methodologies.name: writing-design-documents
methodologies.description: Answer 'HOW are we building this?' by bridging specification and implementation. Use Google-style structure (context, goals, overview, details, alternatives, cross-cutting) or LLM-optimized SDD format (architectural decisions, file structure, APIs, dependencies, security, performance). Document trade-offs explicitly using decision matrices, and structure for LLM code generation with explicit patterns and type definitions.

methodologies.name: writing-implementation-plans
methodologies.description: Transform architectural decisions into ordered, actionable tasks optimized for LLM execution. Apply the 100k token rule with token budget guidelines (simple ~25k, normal ~50k, complex ~80k). Build dependency graphs to identify parallel execution waves, estimate complexity using file/dependency/research factors, and validate INVEST criteria for each task.

methodologies.name: writing-specifications
methodologies.description: Define WHAT to build and WHY with precision for LLM agent execution. Use minimal viable spec (problem, solution, requirements, acceptance criteria, out-of-scope) or full spec structure with personas, user stories, SMART requirements, and Given-When-Then acceptance criteria. Provide six core areas (commands, testing, structure, style, workflow, boundaries) and use three-tier boundary system for agent autonomy.

methodologies.name: yaml-frontmatter
methodologies.description: Standardize metadata across SDD documentation using YAML frontmatter blocks (---). Enable machine-readable parsing by tools, SSGs (Jekyll, Hugo, Astro), and LLMs. Validate with schemas (Zod, JSON Schema) and store in version control. Include type, version, status, created, author fields for all constitution, spec, design, test-plan, implementation-plan, and task files.

domains.description: SDD planning phase creating specifications, design documents, implementation plans, and task files. Covers writing specifications and requirements, architectural design, breaking features into tasks, applying the 100k token rule, creating templates, and managing workflows. Use this when planning features before implementation, documenting technical approach, breaking work into manageable tasks, or establishing planning standards.

methodologies.name: api-first-development
methodologies.description: Design API contracts before implementation to enable parallel frontend/backend development. Use OpenAPI 3.1 as source of truth with design, validation, mocking, implementation, testing, and deployment phases. Define consistent endpoints, request/response schemas, error handling, and rate limiting upfront. Generate code, tests, and documentation from specs using Spectral linting and Prism mocking servers.

methodologies.name: architecture-decision-records
methodologies.description: Record important architectural decisions with context and consequences to prevent repeated discussions and preserve design rationale. Use ADR structure (title, status, context, decision, alternatives, consequences) stored in docs/adr/ alongside code. Maintain immutability—supersede rather than edit—with timestamps and cross-functional review. Helps teams understand 'why' decisions were made for onboarding and incident response.

methodologies.name: backlog-grooming-roadmapping
methodologies.description: Plan feature timeline and roadmap with success metrics, phases, and milestones. Organize features by priority (must/should/could/won't) and refine user stories into acceptance criteria. Track features through lifecycle (backlog → todo → in-progress → done) with regular grooming sessions. Use roadmap to manage stakeholder expectations and guide sprint planning.

methodologies.name: design-doc-advanced-patterns
methodologies.description: Master advanced design documentation patterns: handling large systems, cross-cutting concerns (security, observability), distributed tracing architecture, multi-tenant design. Learn when to create separate design docs vs consolidated docs, how to handle dependencies between design decisions, and techniques for documenting evolving systems.

methodologies.name: design-doc-examples
methodologies.description: Real-world design documentation examples from open source and public companies showing structure, trade-off analysis, and decision rationale. Study examples that successfully balance technical depth with readability for both humans and LLMs.

methodologies.name: design-doc-structure
methodologies.description: Establish consistent design document organization: reference documents, spec coverage matrix, architectural decisions (AD-X), file structure, data models, API contracts, dependencies, security, performance, and testing strategy. Structure for traceability from FR-X requirements through AD-X decisions to implementation tasks.

methodologies.name: design-doc-writing-process
methodologies.description: Step-by-step process for writing design documents: context loading (understand requirements), brainstorming alternatives, documenting decisions with rationale, soliciting feedback, and iterating. Use this process whether writing alone or with team collaboration.

methodologies.name: impl-plan-100k-rule
methodologies.description: Understand why 100k tokens is the optimal context limit for LLM task execution. Learn token estimation formula (context ~5-10k, files ~2-5k each, research ~10-30k, output ~5-20k) and use complexity factors (files, dependencies, research, testing, risk) to split tasks appropriately. Keep complex refactors and feature slices under limit.

methodologies.name: impl-plan-components
methodologies.description: Break down implementation plans into component parts: dependency analysis (DAG visualization), wave identification for parallel execution, INVEST validation, critical path calculation, and risk assessment. Understand task size indicators (too large if touching 5+ files or 3+ dependencies) and when tasks are too small to justify overhead.

methodologies.name: impl-plan-examples
methodologies.description: Provides real-world implementation plan examples ranging from simple features (3 tasks) to complex multi-tenant architectures (12 tasks) with dependency graphs, wave analysis, and critical path documentation. Solves the problem of unclear task organization by showing how to structure work into manageable, parallelizable units. Use this to understand task granularity, wave-based execution, and complexity estimation patterns. The practical value lies in concrete examples that demonstrate how to apply the 100k token rule and avoid common implementation plan mistakes.

methodologies.name: impl-plan-task-format
methodologies.description: Defines the standard format for individual tasks within implementation plans using INVEST criteria (Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable). Solves the problem of vague task definitions that fail during execution. Use when creating task files to ensure consistency and executability. Provides practical templates with dependency types (FS, SS, FF, SF), acceptance criteria guidance, and context estimation rules that enable agents to execute tasks reliably.

methodologies.name: spec-advanced-guidelines
methodologies.description: Prescribes how to write comprehensive, mission-critical specifications covering 14 sections from personas to NFRs to appendices. Solves the problem of incomplete specs that lead to scope creep and misaligned expectations. Use for complex features affecting multiple user types or with significant non-functional requirements. Delivers practical value by showing when full specs are needed versus minimal ones, how to measure traceability, and comprehensive acceptance criteria coverage strategies.

methodologies.name: spec-example-ecommerce-cart
methodologies.description: Full worked example of a shopping cart specification with complete sections including problem statement, personas, user stories, detailed functional/non-functional requirements, and acceptance criteria in Given-When-Then format. Solves the problem of not knowing what a complete, high-quality specification actually looks like. Use as a template reference when writing similar e-commerce or complex features. Provides practical value by demonstrating how to link requirements to user stories, organize acceptance criteria by scenario type, and document constraints.

methodologies.name: spec-examples-basic
methodologies.description: Provides a condensed authentication specification example showing the minimum viable sections for MVP features (problem statement, user stories, functional requirements, acceptance criteria, out of scope). Solves the problem of over-engineering simple features with unnecessary documentation. Use for straightforward CRUD operations or well-understood patterns where a minimal spec suffices. Delivers practical value by showing when to skip complex sections and how to stay concise while maintaining clarity.

methodologies.name: spec-requirements
methodologies.description: Covers SMART requirement definition (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound), user story mapping with backbones and walking skeletons, acceptance criteria in BDD format, and MoSCoW prioritization. Solves the problem of vague, unmeasurable requirements that create ambiguity between stakeholders. Use to formalize requirements gathering before design. Provides practical value through frameworks for problem analysis, use case modeling, and traceability that ensure every requirement serves a user need.

methodologies.name: spec-structure
methodologies.description: Defines the complete specification document structure (v2.0) with all sections, quality checklist, and common mistakes. Solves the problem of specifications missing critical sections or lacking clear organization. Use as a navigation guide and quality gate before design begins. Delivers practical value by providing a 14-section checklist, clarifying which sections are optional based on complexity, and showing how to validate specification completeness.

methodologies.name: task-creation-principles
methodologies.description: Explains the fundamental principles for decomposing work into proper tasks: right sizing (8-80 rule adapted to <100k tokens), INVEST criteria, clear boundaries (input/output), SMART criteria, and traceability to requirements and architecture decisions. Solves the problem of tasks that are too large to execute or lack necessary context for AI/human workers. Use when breaking down design documents into actionable work units. Provides practical value by establishing what makes a task good and how to validate quality before execution.

methodologies.name: task-creation-template-guide
methodologies.description: Provides enhanced task template (v2.0) with emphasis on context management including task dependency trees, related files, code dependencies, and estimated context budgets. Solves the problem of agents lacking sufficient context to execute tasks consistently with prior work. Use when creating individual TASK_*.md files to ensure critical context about completed dependencies is included. Delivers practical value by showing exactly what information agents need (summaries, patterns, key code snippets) from prior tasks.

methodologies.name: template-design
methodologies.description: Provides copy-paste design document template covering architecture decisions (context, options, rationale), components, data flow, data models, API endpoints, files, testing strategy, and FR coverage matrix. Solves the problem of inconsistent design documentation that varies in structure and completeness. Use after spec approval to ensure consistent design artifact quality. Delivers practical value by providing a proven checklist structure that bridges spec requirements to implementation plans.

methodologies.name: template-spec
methodologies.description: Provides copy-paste specification template with all required sections from reference documents through open questions and appendix. Solves the problem of starting from scratch when writing new features. Use as the baseline for every new feature specification. Delivers practical value by ensuring critical sections are never forgotten and providing consistent structure that stakeholders can rely on.

methodologies.name: template-task
methodologies.description: Provides copy-paste task file template showing metadata, SDD references, dependency tree, requirements coverage, acceptance criteria, technical approach, files to change, and context budgeting. Solves the problem of inconsistent task file quality and missing critical context sections. Use when generating TASK_*.md files to ensure standard quality. Delivers practical value by providing explicit sections for dependency trees and estimated context.

methodologies.name: templates
methodologies.description: Navigation hub for all SDD document templates (spec, design, task, constitution, implementation plan, roadmap, backlog item, confidence check, pattern/mistake records). Solves the problem of not knowing which template to use for which artifact. Use to locate appropriate templates for each phase of SDD workflow. Delivers practical value by consolidating all templates in one place with clear use cases and also providing token estimation guides.

methodologies.name: workflow-design-phase
methodologies.description: Detailed workflow guide for design phase covering design document writing (research → decisions → approach → testing), implementation plan creation (complexity analysis → 100k rule → wave analysis), task creation (dependency detection → task review), and parallelization analysis. Solves the problem of unclear process between spec approval and execution readiness. Use to navigate the entire design phase systematically. Delivers practical value through 4-pass code review patterns and parallelization speedup calculations (typical 1.8x-3.5x).

methodologies.name: workflow-spec-phase
methodologies.description: Detailed workflow guide for specification phase covering constitution writing (existing or new project modes), specification writing (Socratic dialogue, five whys, alternatives exploration), backlog grooming, and roadmapping. Solves the problem of chaotic requirements gathering with conflicting stakeholder expectations. Use to conduct requirements workshops and definition of ready gates. Delivers practical value through Socratic dialogue techniques and structured brainstorming patterns.

methodologies.name: workflows
methodologies.description: Navigation hub for all three SDD workflow phases (spec, design, execution) with quick reference matrix of which files/tools to use for specific goals. Solves the problem of not knowing which workflow document to follow. Use to understand the complete SDD lifecycle and find specific phase guidance. Delivers practical value by showing quality gate levels (L1-L6) and confidence thresholds (90%+) that indicate readiness to proceed.

methodologies.name: writing-implementation-plans
methodologies.description: Covers how to write implementation plans that bridge design documents to executable tasks, including work breakdown structure (WBS), dependency graphing, wave-based execution, task granularity, risk assessment, and rollout strategies. Solves the problem of designs that cannot be executed because task dependencies are unclear or context budgets are exceeded. Use after design approval to structure implementation work. Delivers practical value by detailing the 100k token budget rule and wave-based parallelization.

methodologies.name: writing-specifications
methodologies.description: Overview of specification methodology split into five focused documents covering structure, requirements (SMART), examples, and advanced guidelines for complex features. Solves the problem of incomplete or vague specifications that lead to misaligned development. Use to understand the complete spec writing process from problem analysis through acceptance criteria. Delivers practical value by showing how specs answer WHAT and WHY (not HOW) and providing examples at multiple complexity levels.

domains.description: Accessibility compliance and inclusive design ensuring products work for users with disabilities. Covers WCAG standards, assistive technology compatibility, accessible component design, testing, and remediation. Use this when building accessible products, ensuring WCAG AA or AAA compliance, testing with real users with disabilities, or auditing existing products for accessibility issues.

methodologies.name: flexibility-efficiency
methodologies.description: Applies Usability Heuristic #7 by designing for both novices and experts through keyboard shortcuts, customization, multiple paths to tasks, and accelerators like autocomplete and templates. Solves the problem of power users being slowed by beginner-oriented interfaces while novices are overwhelmed by expert-only designs. Use to balance accessibility for new users with efficiency for frequent users. Delivers practical value by showing how to layer features (visible → discoverable → muscle memory) and measure efficiency improvements.

methodologies.name: help-documentation
methodologies.description: Applies Usability Heuristic #10 by creating contextual, task-focused help (inline hints, tooltips, guided tours, searchable FAQs) organized by user goals rather than system architecture. Solves the problem of help that is hidden, outdated, too technical, or hard to find when users need it. Use to establish help hierarchies that reduce support costs and improve self-service. Delivers practical value through templates for help content and audits that identify gaps.

methodologies.name: match-real-world
methodologies.description: Applies Usability Heuristic #2 by using familiar language, following real-world conventions, organizing information logically for users (not the system), and using meaningful metaphors. Solves the problem of unfamiliar jargon and system-centric interfaces that confuse users and increase learning curves. Use to audit and improve labeling, error messages, and information architecture. Delivers practical value through language audit checklists and research methods for discovering actual user terminology.

methodologies.name: recognition-over-recall
methodologies.description: Applies Usability Heuristic #6 by making options visible (not hidden), providing contextual information, using recognition aids (thumbnails, labels, suggestions), and reducing memory load. Solves the problem of users forgetting information across interface sections or struggling to recall command syntax and hidden options. Use to minimize cognitive burden, especially for occasional users, older adults, and mobile contexts. Delivers practical value by showing how recognition-based design helps multiple accessibility groups and improves measurable metrics like error rates.

methodologies.name: visibility-of-system-status
methodologies.description: Applies Usability Heuristic #1 by providing immediate feedback for every action with appropriate loading indicators, progress bars, success confirmations, and error states matching response time expectations. Solves the problem of unresponsive-feeling interfaces that cause double-click submissions and task abandonment due to uncertainty. Use for any interface where operations take more than 100ms or have network dependencies. Delivers practical value through response time guidance (0.1s/1s/10s thresholds) and accessibility considerations for dynamic updates.

domains.description: UI design, wireframing, prototyping, design systems, and design tokens for web, mobile, spatial, and voice interfaces. Covers wireframing and prototyping, design tokens and theming, component libraries, AI-enhanced design workflows, voice UI design, and spatial computing interfaces. Use this when designing new features or products, building design systems, creating interactive prototypes, or implementing design tokens for scalable theming.

methodologies.name: design-tokens-fundamentals
methodologies.description: Covers design tokens (named design values for colors, spacing, typography) at three levels (global/primitive, semantic/alias, component) stored in platform-agnostic formats that bridge design and development. Solves the problem of inconsistent design implementation across platforms and difficulty maintaining design systems at scale. Use to establish single source of truth for design decisions enabling easy theming and updates. Delivers practical value by showing token structure, benefits (consistency, scalability, collaboration), and W3C standard foundations.

methodologies.name: prototyping
methodologies.description: Prototyping creates interactive representations of designs before development to answer specific research questions. By testing designs at varying fidelity levels (paper, clickable, high-fidelity), teams can validate interactions and catch usability issues early. The value delivered is faster iteration cycles, stakeholder alignment, and reduced development risks through early user feedback on flows and functionality.

methodologies.name: spatial-ux-fundamentals
methodologies.description: Spatial UX design addresses the unique challenges of 3D and spatial interfaces where 2D patterns do not translate. It covers scale, reach, sight-lines, and environmental adaptation across near, mid, and far fields. Understanding these fundamentals prevents poor ergonomics and spatial disorientation in XR and spatial computing applications.

methodologies.name: tailwind-design-tokens
methodologies.description: Integrating design tokens with Tailwind CSS creates a bridge between design systems and implementation. This methodology ensures consistent spacing, colors, and typography across components while maintaining flexibility through CSS variables. The practical benefit is eliminating design drift, enabling rapid prototyping, and reducing maintenance overhead when design decisions need to change.

methodologies.name: voice-ui-basics
methodologies.description: Voice UI design creates interfaces controlled by speech rather than visual interaction, solving unique challenges of conversational interfaces. Designing natural dialogues, handling errors gracefully, and confirming actions prevents the frustration and high error rates common in poorly designed voice features. When applied correctly, voice removes friction for hands-busy tasks and accessibility use cases.

methodologies.name: wireframing
methodologies.description: Wireframing focuses on layout and structure before visual design, preventing expensive changes downstream. By exploring multiple layouts and getting stakeholder alignment early, teams avoid designing in circles. The deliverable is clear information hierarchy, tested navigation, and documented behavior that serves as a blueprint for visual design and development.

domains.description: User research and discovery through interviews, usability testing, and behavioral studies to understand user needs and validate designs. Covers research planning, interview techniques, usability testing, observation methods, and insight synthesis. Use this when validating design assumptions, discovering user pain points, testing prototypes with real users, or generating design direction from user research.

methodologies.name: feature-discovery
methodologies.description: Feature discovery systematically identifies which capabilities to build by combining customer research, analytics, competitive analysis, and market trends. It solves the problem of building features nobody uses by prioritizing based on opportunity scoring and validation. The outcome is a ranked backlog where high-opportunity features have evidence of demand and problem-solution fit.

methodologies.name: jobs-to-be-done
methodologies.description: Jobs to Be Done shifts thinking from features to underlying motivations by asking what job users are trying to accomplish in specific situations. This framework reveals the real competition and why customers switch solutions. Understanding functional, emotional, and social jobs enables positioning that resonates and building features users actually hire your product to do.

methodologies.name: pain-point-research
methodologies.description: Pain point research systematically discovers and categorizes problems that cause frustration or waste time for your target audience. By mining complaints from Reddit, reviews, support tickets, and forums, then scoring by frequency and severity, you uncover high-value opportunities. The result is a prioritized list of solvable problems where demand is demonstrable.

methodologies.name: problem-validation
methodologies.description: Problem validation uses evidence-based techniques to confirm that a problem is both real and worth solving before building. The Mom Test approach of asking about past behavior rather than future intent, combined with commitment signals, distinguishes strong problems from weak ones. Validated problems have weekly+ frequency, high intensity, and demonstrated willingness to pay.

methodologies.name: problem-validation-2026
methodologies.description: Modern problem validation recognizes that startup viability requires continuous, ongoing validation rather than a one-time gate. Using evidence hierarchy from paid commitments down to stated needs, and applying 2025 Mom Test improvements, teams can fail fast and cheaply. The goal is rapid discovery of what is wrong with the idea, enabling quick pivots before large investment.

methodologies.name: success-metrics-definition
methodologies.description: Defining success metrics establishes what winning looks like across the product lifecycle. By creating a North Star metric and breaking it into primary KPIs across AARRR stages (acquisition, activation, retention, revenue, referral), teams focus efforts on what matters. The outcome is actionable targets that drive product decisions and measure progress toward business goals.

methodologies.name: use-case-mapping
methodologies.description: Use case mapping documents how different actors interact with your product to achieve specific goals. By identifying primary flows, alternatives, and edge cases, teams ensure no critical paths are missed and understand system behavior from the user perspective. The deliverable enables both designers and developers to build consistent experiences.

methodologies.name: user-validation-methods
methodologies.description: User validation methods span persona building, JTBD analysis, problem validation, and pain point mining to comprehensively understand market fit. By combining multiple validation sources, teams build confidence in problem-solution fit before heavy investment. The outcome is evidence-backed positioning and feature priorities grounded in actual user needs.

methodologies.name: validation-methods
methodologies.description: Validation methods reference comprehensive techniques for testing problem existence, niche viability, and market potential. Using validation hierarchies that rank paid commitments above stated preferences, teams assess whether pursuing an idea is worthwhile. The result is objectivity in go/no-go decisions and risk mitigation through continuous validation.

methodologies.name: value-proposition-design
methodologies.description: Value proposition design uses the Strategyzer canvas to map customer jobs, pains, and gains against your products and solutions, revealing fit. By understanding which pains your product relieves and gains it creates, you communicate clear reasons to buy. The outcome is positioning that resonates with customers and differentiates from alternatives.

domains.description: UX research focusing on user experience, satisfaction, and behavioral patterns across the full product lifecycle. Covers discovery research, usability testing, analytics, A/B testing, and experience optimization. Use this when improving product experience, understanding why users leave, optimizing key user flows, or tracking experience metrics over time.

methodologies.name: ab-testing
methodologies.description: A/B testing validates design improvements through statistical comparison of control and variant performance. By forming hypothesis, calculating sample size, and running tests to statistical significance, teams measure real impact rather than guessing. The discipline prevents shipping changes that seem good but hurt metrics and funds only improvements with proven value.

methodologies.name: card-sorting
methodologies.description: Card sorting reveals how users naturally group information and what labels they expect. By running open, closed, or hybrid sorts with 15-30 participants, teams discover mental models that guide information architecture. When user groupings differ from organizational logic, fixing IA based on card sort results dramatically improves findability.

methodologies.name: competitive-analysis
methodologies.description: Competitive analysis systematically examines what competitors do well, poorly, and missing to understand industry standards and opportunities. By analyzing 3-5 direct competitors, you identify table-stakes features, best practices to adopt, and gaps to differentiate. The output guides feature prioritization and positions your product relative to alternatives.

methodologies.name: consistency-standards
methodologies.description: Consistency across UI, terminology, and interaction patterns reduces learning curve and prevents errors from violated expectations. Following Nielsen's fourth heuristic and building a design system eliminates decision fatigue and variation. The benefit is reduced user confusion, faster task completion, and maintainability when design changes are needed.

methodologies.name: content-audit-process
methodologies.description: Content audits inventory and evaluate every piece of content against quality criteria, identifying what to keep, update, consolidate, or remove. By using automated crawlers plus manual review and pairing with analytics, teams make data-informed decisions. The result is removing outdated content, consolidating duplicates, and focusing effort on high-impact pieces.

methodologies.name: design-critique
methodologies.description: Design critique creates structured feedback conversations connected to goals rather than opinions. By setting context, asking clarifying questions before judging, and connecting feedback to principles, teams improve design quality without bruising egos. The outcome is psychological safety for designers to share work early and collective improvement of design thinking.

methodologies.name: error-prevention
methodologies.description: Error prevention stops mistakes before they happen using constraints, smart defaults, inline validation, and confirmation dialogs. By constraining input options, validating in real-time, and confirming destructive actions, you eliminate wasted user effort and support costs. Prevention is more humane and cheaper than recovery.

methodologies.name: error-recovery
methodologies.description: Error recovery provides clear, helpful messages in plain language that explain what went wrong, why, and how to fix it. Using human language instead of codes, placing errors inline, and offering next steps prevents user abandonment. The heuristic transforms errors from frustration points into opportunities to guide users toward success.

methodologies.name: heuristic-evaluation
methodologies.description: Heuristic evaluation is a quick usability inspection method where UX experts assess an interface against Nielsen's 10 usability principles, identifying obvious problems before expensive user testing. Use this early in design or before testing users to find 75% of issues with just 3-5 evaluators. It's fast and cost-effective, complementing but not replacing user testing.

methodologies.name: ia-framework
methodologies.description: Information architecture designs how users find content by organizing, labeling, and structuring navigation systems. Apply this when building or redesigning sites and applications to ensure users locate information intuitively rather than through brute-force search. Well-structured IA reduces support costs, increases discoverability, and scales as content grows.

methodologies.name: ia-templates
methodologies.description: IA templates provide reusable structures for documenting organizational schemes, sitemaps, and taxonomies for any information-rich product. Use these templates to standardize IA documentation across projects, ensure consistency in content organization, and communicate structure clearly to stakeholders and development teams. They save time while establishing governance for evolving content.

methodologies.name: journey-mapping
methodologies.description: Journey mapping visualizes the complete user experience across stages, touchpoints, emotions, and pain points. Use this when you need to understand the end-to-end customer experience, identify where friction occurs between channels, or align cross-functional teams around shared insights. It reveals systemic opportunities that single-screen optimization misses.

methodologies.name: mobile-ux-patterns
methodologies.description: Mobile UX patterns are proven design solutions for common mobile challenges like navigation, forms, gestures, and loading states, documented with specifications and usage guidelines. Apply these patterns to maintain consistency, leverage user expectations from other apps, and reduce design decisions for platform-specific constraints. They accelerate design while ensuring accessibility and usability.

methodologies.name: usability-testing
methodologies.description: Usability testing observes real users attempting tasks to validate whether a design works as intended, measuring effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction. Conduct testing early with 5-8 participants per user segment to discover actionable problems before launch and quantify design improvements. Testing reveals what users actually struggle with, not what designers assumed.

methodologies.name: user-control-freedom
methodologies.description: This heuristic ensures users can undo actions, easily exit unwanted processes, and recover from mistakes without feeling trapped. Apply by providing undo buttons, cancel operations, emergency exits in modals, and soft deletes instead of permanent removal. Giving users control reduces frustration, increases confidence in exploration, and lowers support burden.

methodologies.name: user-interviews
methodologies.description: User interviews are one-on-one conversations that uncover user needs, behaviors, motivations, and pain points through open-ended exploration. Conduct these in discovery and validation phases to replace assumptions with real understanding of user problems. Interviews generate insights no quantitative data can provide while building empathy within teams.

domains.description: Combined UX and UI design covering user research, information architecture, interaction design, and visual design. Bridges user research insights through wireframes and prototypes to high-fidelity visual design and design systems. Use this when designing products end-to-end, when UX and UI are tightly integrated in your process, or when one person needs to own both research and visual design.

methodologies.name: ab-testing
methodologies.description: A/B testing compares two design variations to measure which performs better on a specific metric like click-through rate or conversion. Use when you have enough traffic, clear metrics, and want to validate that design changes actually improve outcomes. It moves design decisions from opinion to data, providing confidence before rolling out changes.

methodologies.name: aesthetic-minimalist
methodologies.description: Minimalist design removes clutter by keeping only essential content, using white space, and applying visual hierarchy so important information stands out. Apply when the interface feels overwhelming, when many features rarely get used, or when mobile space is constrained. A clean interface reduces cognitive load, increases task completion speed, and improves perceived quality.

methodologies.name: card-sorting
methodologies.description: Card sorting asks users to organize content labels into categories that feel natural to them, revealing how they mentally group information. Use this to validate navigation structure, choose category names, or decide how to organize product features before building. It ensures your structure matches user mental models rather than internal org charts.

methodologies.name: competitive-analysis
methodologies.description: Competitive analysis systematically examines how competitors solve similar problems, revealing industry standards, best practices, and gaps to differentiate. Conduct this to understand user expectations (what features do most offer), avoid learning from competitor mistakes, and find opportunities where no one excels. It prevents reinventing solutions and sets realistic parity baselines.

methodologies.name: consistency-standards
methodologies.description: Consistency standards ensure the same action, term, or visual treatment works identically across the product and aligns with platform conventions. Maintain consistency through design systems, shared component libraries, and terminology guides so users transfer learning between sections. Consistency reduces cognitive load, increases perceived quality, and prevents user errors from misexpectations.

methodologies.name: content-audit
methodologies.description: Content audits systematically inventory what exists, evaluate quality against criteria, and decide what to keep, update, or remove. Perform audits before redesigns, when migrating content, or when you suspect duplicates and outdated pages. They reveal content gaps, quality issues, and governance problems while providing data for prioritizing content improvements.

methodologies.name: design-critique
methodologies.description: Design critique is a structured discussion where feedback is given against defined goals rather than personal preference, improving design quality through iterative discussion. Hold critiques at multiple design stages to catch issues early, align teams on direction, and build culture of learning. Effective critique balances positivity with specificity, making teams sharper without breeding defensiveness.

methodologies.name: error-prevention
methodologies.description: Error prevention stops problems before they occur through constraints (limiting valid inputs), smart defaults, real-time validation, and confirmation for destructive actions. Apply this throughout forms and critical paths to reduce user frustration, lower data quality issues, and decrease support tickets. Prevention is cheaper and kinder than recovery.

methodologies.name: error-recovery
methodologies.description: Error messages must explain plainly what went wrong, why it happened, and how users can fix it—delivered near the problem location. Use human language, avoid codes or blame, and always provide a recovery path or next step. Good error messages reduce support costs while helping users continue rather than abandon.

methodologies.name: flexibility-efficiency
methodologies.description: Flexibility serves both novices and experts through multiple paths to the same goal—defaults and wizards for beginners, shortcuts and customization for power users. Implement keyboard shortcuts, batch operations, templates, and settings that beginners ignore but experts appreciate. This design pattern keeps experienced users fast while maintaining approachability.

methodologies.name: help-documentation
methodologies.description: Help and documentation should be contextual, task-focused, searchable, and concise—appearing where users need it with concrete steps, not technical jargon. Provide inline hints, tooltips, guided tours, and knowledge bases covering the most common questions revealed by support tickets. Good help reduces support burden while making the product more self-service.

methodologies.name: heuristic-evaluation
methodologies.description: Heuristic evaluation is a quick usability inspection where experts assess an interface against established principles like Nielsen's 10 heuristics, finding obvious problems before user testing. Use this early in design cycles or when user testing budget is limited—3-5 evaluators find ~75% of issues. It's faster and cheaper than user testing but complements rather than replaces it.

methodologies.name: information-architecture
methodologies.description: Information architecture organizes content and navigation systems so users intuitively find what they need through logical grouping, clear labels, and predictable structure. Use this method when building information-rich products like websites or knowledge bases to prevent users from relying entirely on search. Good IA increases scannability and reduces support questions about findability.

methodologies.name: journey-mapping
methodologies.description: Journey mapping visualizes the complete user experience across stages, emotions, and pain points to identify end-to-end opportunities. Use this in product strategy and redesign planning to move beyond point solutions and align teams around shared understanding of user experience. It surfaces systemic problems and transformation opportunities.

methodologies.name: match-real-world
methodologies.description: This heuristic requires interfaces to use familiar language, real-world concepts, and conventions rather than internal jargon or system logic. Apply by conducting user interviews to learn how they describe tasks, using those words consistently in labels and messages, and following industry standards. Users learn faster when the interface speaks their language.

methodologies.name: mobile-ux
methodologies.description: Mobile UX prioritizes the mobile context—small screens, touch input, variable connectivity, and split attention—by focusing on essentials and designing from mobile-first. Use when designing for mobile-first audiences or when testing reveals mobile abandonment, designing touch targets 44-48pt, and simplifying flows to 1-2 steps per screen. Mobile-first design improves desktop experiences while ensuring mobile viability.

methodologies.name: prototyping
methodologies.description: Prototyping creates interactive mockups at varying fidelity—from paper sketches to near-final designs—to test ideas with users before expensive development. Use low-fidelity prototypes for concept testing, medium for flow validation, and high-fidelity for detailed interaction feedback. Prototyping reduces the cost of failure and surfaces misalignments between design intent and user understanding.

methodologies.name: recognition-over-recall
methodologies.description: Reduce user cognitive load by making interface options, actions, and navigation visible rather than hidden. This heuristic solves the problem of users having to memorize information or recall where features are located, which slows them down and increases errors. Apply it when designing menus, search interfaces, toolbars, or any multi-step task where users need to reference information. The value delivered is faster task completion, fewer mistakes, and improved user confidence—especially for occasional users or those in high-stress situations.

methodologies.name: tailwind-design-tokens
methodologies.description: Establish systematic design token integration with Tailwind CSS to ensure consistent styling across components and projects. This methodology solves the problem of CSS frameworks becoming fragmented without a unified design system and makes it harder to maintain consistency at scale. Apply it when setting up or refactoring a design system, defining colors, spacing, typography, and other theme values as CSS variables. The value delivered is maintainability, consistency across products, and the ability to support design system evolution without rewriting component styles.

methodologies.name: usability-testing
methodologies.description: Validate design decisions with real users before and after launch to reveal what works, what confuses, and where users struggle. This methodology solves the problem of launching products with undiscovered usability issues, poor user experience, and costly post-launch redesigns based on assumptions rather than evidence. Apply it in discovery phases to understand problems, during design to validate concepts, and after development to catch issues before release. The value delivered is early problem identification, reduced development costs, higher user adoption, and data-driven design improvements.

methodologies.name: user-control-freedom
methodologies.description: Give users clear escape routes, undo capabilities, and the ability to recover from mistakes to prevent them from feeling trapped. This heuristic solves the frustration and abandonment that occurs when users cannot easily reverse actions, exit unwanted flows, or restore defaults. Apply it to all user-triggerable actions—especially destructive ones—by providing undo buttons, cancel options, and emergency exits like X buttons on modals or Escape key support. The value delivered is increased user confidence, exploration without fear, and reduced support burden from users who made mistakes.

methodologies.name: user-interviews
methodologies.description: Conduct one-on-one conversations with target users to understand their needs, behaviors, pain points, and motivations instead of relying on assumptions. This methodology solves the gap between what designers think users want and what users actually need, preventing wasted development effort on unused features. Apply it early in discovery to understand the problem space, during design to validate concepts, and post-launch to understand usage patterns and issues. The value delivered is deep qualitative insights, reduced design risk, and the confidence that you are solving real problems users face.

methodologies.name: visibility-of-system-status
methodologies.description: Provide immediate and appropriate feedback for every user action so they always know what the system is doing and whether their input was received. This heuristic solves user confusion and repeated actions (like clicking submit twice) that occur when there is no feedback, causing users to distrust the system. Apply it to all clickable elements—use loading spinners for short waits, progress bars for long operations, and success/error messages to confirm outcomes. The value delivered is reduced user anxiety, fewer accidental duplicate submissions, higher task completion rates, and fewer support inquiries about whether actions worked.

methodologies.name: wireframing
methodologies.description: Create low-fidelity structural representations focused on layout, content hierarchy, and functionality before moving to visual design. This methodology solves premature focus on aesthetics that distracts from usability, expensive design changes late in the process, and unclear information architecture. Apply it early in design—sketch ideas on paper first, then create medium-fidelity wireframes to align stakeholders and validate structure before investing in visual design. The value delivered is faster iteration, cheaper changes, clearer stakeholder alignment, and early detection of structural problems.

domains.description: Core business analysis practices including BA planning and stakeholder analysis, requirements elicitation techniques, requirements lifecycle management (traceability, prioritization, validation), strategy analysis (current/future state, gap analysis), solution evaluation, and modern practices (agile frameworks, AI-enabled BA, data-driven requirements).

methodologies.name: agile-ba-frameworks
methodologies.description: Business analysts struggle to integrate their skills into agile ceremonies where BA role isn't explicitly defined. This methodology maps BA competencies to Scrum phases (backlog refinement, sprint planning, review) and SAFe levels (team, program, solution, portfolio), showing how BAs create value through user story elaboration, dependency analysis, and solution validation in iterative delivery.

methodologies.name: ba-governance
methodologies.description: Requirements approval and change control processes often lack clear decision authority. This methodology establishes governance through decision authority matrices, change control processes, prioritization rules, and approval sign-offs. Apply when defining how requirements will be managed, who can approve changes, and what audit trail to maintain for compliance or project continuity.

methodologies.name: ba-planning
methodologies.description: Business analysis happens ad-hoc without structured approach, causing inconsistent analysis, missed stakeholders, and unclear deliverables. BA planning defines the approach (plan-driven, change-driven, or hybrid), identifies stakeholders, selects elicitation techniques, specifies deliverables, and establishes governance. Apply at initiative start to align stakeholders on how analysis will be conducted and what will be produced.

methodologies.name: ba-requirements-mgmt
methodologies.description: Requirements degrade over time as projects evolve. This methodology manages version control, attribute tracking (status, priority, owner), quality monitoring through periodic reviews, and change impact analysis covering scope, effort, risk, and stakeholder impact. Apply when you need to maintain requirement accuracy, assess changes, and organize requirements into coherent structures with clear dependencies.

methodologies.name: ba-strategic-partnership
methodologies.description: BAs are often seen as order-takers documenting requirements rather than strategic partners. This methodology evolves BAs toward innovation leadership by identifying opportunities, quantifying value, facilitating design thinking, running experiments, and measuring outcomes. Apply when transforming BA role from requirements documentation to business value creation and enterprise-level strategy contribution.

methodologies.name: ba-trends-summary
methodologies.description: BA practices are rapidly evolving with AI, data analytics, and agile transformation. The top 6 trends shaping 2026 are AI-enabled decision making, outcome-driven strategy, responsible innovation, operational resilience, digital trust, and sustainability integration. Reference this summary when assessing BA skill priorities and understanding critical success factors like data quality and stakeholder alignment.

methodologies.name: data-driven-requirements
methodologies.description: Requirements based on stakeholder opinion rather than evidence lead to misdirected effort. This methodology applies data analytics to requirements engineering through statistical analysis, data visualization, A/B testing, and predictive analytics. Apply when you need to validate assumptions with evidence, prioritize features by value impact, and define success metrics before building—using dashboards and SQL queries to ground decisions.

methodologies.name: elicitation-techniques
methodologies.description: Elicitation techniques solve the problem where stakeholders struggle to articulate their needs clearly. When gathering requirements, you use interviews, workshops, observation, and prototyping to uncover both stated and unstated requirements. The value is discovering complete, accurate requirements early, preventing misunderstandings and costly rework.

methodologies.name: modern-ba-framework
methodologies.description: The Business Analysis Framework from 2015 needs modern application to remain relevant in AI and agile environments. When applying traditional BA practices today, you interpret framework concepts through cloud-native, digital transformation, and AI lenses using the updated Business Analysis Standard. This delivers contemporary guidance without abandoning proven BA fundamentals.

methodologies.name: process-mining-automation
methodologies.description: Process mining and RPA candidates are typically identified through subjective stakeholder input rather than data-driven analysis. When evaluating automation opportunities, you extract event logs from systems to discover actual process flows and assess automation readiness using quantitative criteria. This reduces RPA failure rates by ensuring processes are truly suitable for automation.

methodologies.name: requirements-documentation
methodologies.description: Requirements exist only in people's heads, creating misunderstandings between teams and no basis for testing. When documenting requirements, you create a shared reference organized by hierarchy (business → stakeholder → functional → non-functional) with clear, unambiguous statements and acceptance criteria. This enables testing, change control, and alignment across the project.

methodologies.name: requirements-lifecycle
methodologies.description: Requirements exist in multiple versions with no clarity on which is current or why changes were made. When managing requirements over time, you establish states (Draft → Proposed → Approved → Implemented → Verified) with version control and change requests. This prevents implementation mismatches and creates an audit trail for regulatory and compliance needs.

methodologies.name: requirements-prioritization
methodologies.description: All requirements appear equally important, causing resource conflicts and delayed ROI. When choosing what to build first, you apply frameworks like MoSCoW, RICE, or Value-Effort matrices to rank requirements consistently against business and technical criteria. This focuses the team on delivering value early and managing stakeholder expectations.

methodologies.name: requirements-traceability
methodologies.description: Changes to business goals leave unclear impact on requirements and tests. When managing requirement dependencies, you create bidirectional links from business needs through design to test cases, enabling impact analysis and gap identification. This ensures nothing is missed, changes are controlled, and every component has justification.

methodologies.name: requirements-validation
methodologies.description: Requirements are documented but never confirmed as correct, leading to expensive rework after development. When validating requirements, you conduct walkthroughs, inspections, or prototype reviews with stakeholders to find gaps before development starts. This ensures the team builds the right thing, eliminating surprises and reducing change requests.

methodologies.name: solution-assessment
methodologies.description: Solutions are built but nobody evaluates whether they meet business needs or deliver expected value. When assessing a solution, you compare it against requirements, measure business metrics, gather user feedback, and document limitations and recommendations. This closes the feedback loop, captures lessons learned, and guides improvements.

methodologies.name: stakeholder-analysis
methodologies.description: You gather requirements from the wrong people, missing critical perspectives and unresolved conflicts. When identifying stakeholders, you map all affected parties by influence and impact, understand their needs, assess their attitudes, and plan engagement strategies. This prevents late surprises, reduces resistance, and builds solution adoption.

methodologies.name: strategy-analysis
methodologies.description: Projects start without understanding why they matter to the business. When analyzing strategy, you define the business need, assess current state, describe desired future state, identify gaps, and recommend change approaches. This ensures initiatives align with organizational goals and deliver measurable business value.

methodologies.name: strategy-basics
methodologies.description: Organizations cannot articulate what success looks like or how to get there. When defining strategy, you assess current capabilities, articulate a compelling vision, define measurable goals, and plan the transition while identifying risks and constraints. This provides direction and prevents false starts.

methodologies.name: strategy-methods
methodologies.description: Multiple solution options exist but stakeholders lack a structured way to compare them. When evaluating options, you define criteria, weight them by importance, score each option, and identify risks and assumptions. This removes bias and creates a documented rationale that stakeholders understand and support.

domains.description: Requirements analysis and design modeling techniques: use case modeling, user story mapping for agile development, business process analysis with BPMN, data modeling and ERD, decision tables and business rules, system interface requirements, and acceptance criteria. Covers both traditional and agile modeling approaches.

methodologies.name: acceptance-criteria
methodologies.description: Developers and testers disagree on whether work is complete because "done" is undefined. When writing acceptance criteria, you define specific conditions using Given-When-Then scenarios or rule-based formats that are testable and clear. This enables objective acceptance and becomes the basis for test cases.

methodologies.name: business-process-analysis
methodologies.description: Process documentation relies on subjective input, actual execution differs from documented processes, and inefficiencies remain hidden. When analyzing processes, you document current flows, map value-adding versus non-value steps, identify bottlenecks and pain points, and design improved futures. This reveals improvement opportunities and supports system requirements.

methodologies.name: data-analysis
methodologies.description: Data exists in multiple systems with conflicting definitions, poor quality, and unclear requirements. When analyzing data, you identify business data needs, create a data dictionary, model relationships, assess quality, and define business rules. This ensures data integration works and systems have correct data foundations.

methodologies.name: decision-analysis
methodologies.description: Decisions are made based on gut feeling without evaluating all options or criteria consistently. When analyzing decisions, you identify alternatives, define weighted criteria, score options quantitatively, and document the rationale and risks. This ensures informed choices supported by stakeholders.

methodologies.name: interface-analysis
methodologies.description: Systems are built in isolation; integration issues surface during development when changes are expensive. When analyzing interfaces, you identify all connection points, specify data formats and protocols, define error handling, and document security requirements. This prevents integration surprises and enables parallel development.

methodologies.name: use-case-modeling
methodologies.description: Requirements are written as features without showing how users will actually use the system. When modeling use cases, you identify actors and their goals, document main flows and alternatives, and specify preconditions and business rules. This provides context for testing and guides development.

methodologies.name: user-story-mapping
methodologies.description: User stories exist as a flat list with no journey context, making release planning difficult. When mapping stories, you arrange them along a user journey (horizontal) and priority (vertical), then slice them into releases. This shows the complete user experience and enables sensible release planning.

domains.description: Orchestrator for business analysis coordinating two sub-skills: ba-core (planning, elicitation, requirements, strategy, evaluation) and ba-modeling (use cases, processes, data, decisions, interfaces, acceptance criteria). Covers 6 BABOK knowledge areas and 28 total methodologies for full business analysis practice.

methodologies.name: acceptance-criteria
methodologies.description: Developers and testers disagree on whether work is complete because "done" is undefined. When writing acceptance criteria, you define specific conditions using Given-When-Then scenarios or rule-based formats that are testable and clear. This enables objective acceptance and becomes the basis for test cases.

methodologies.name: agile-ba-frameworks
methodologies.description: Business analysts struggle to find their role in agile and scaled agile frameworks. When working in agile, you map BA competencies to agile ceremonies, refine backlog items, write acceptance criteria, and facilitate stakeholder collaboration. This ensures BA practices support iterative delivery without creating bottlenecks.

methodologies.name: ba-planning
methodologies.description: Business analysis happens ad-hoc without structure or clear approach. When planning BA work, you define the analysis approach, identify stakeholders, plan elicitation activities, and establish governance for approvals and change control. This ensures consistent, structured analysis that meets stakeholder needs.

methodologies.name: ba-strategic-partnership
methodologies.description: BAs are seen as order-takers documenting requirements rather than strategic partners creating value. When partnering strategically, you identify innovation opportunities, quantify business value, facilitate design thinking, and measure outcomes. This elevates BA from documentation to business transformation leadership.

methodologies.name: ba-trends-summary
methodologies.description: BA practices from 2015 are no longer sufficient for modern, AI-driven organizations. When understanding 2025-2026 trends, you focus on AI-enabled decision making, outcome-driven strategy, responsible innovation, operational resilience, digital trust, and sustainability integration. This ensures BA practices remain relevant and deliver modern value.

methodologies.name: business-process-analysis
methodologies.description: Business process analysis examines how work flows through an organization to understand current operations, identify improvement opportunities, and design future state processes. Use this when processes are unclear, inconsistent, or inefficient and you need to map actual workflows with stakeholders before making changes. Value includes visible bottlenecks, quantified waste, and a foundation for process improvement initiatives.

methodologies.name: data-analysis
methodologies.description: Data analysis in the BA context examines what data the business needs, how it is structured, where it comes from, and what quality requirements apply. Apply this when building systems that handle data, integrating systems with different data definitions, or designing data-driven solutions. The value is a complete data dictionary, validated quality standards, and elimination of inconsistent data definitions across systems.

methodologies.name: data-driven-requirements
methodologies.description: Data-driven requirements engineering uses evidence and analytics to validate assumptions, prioritize features, and measure success rather than relying on stakeholder opinions or gut feel. Use this in organizations with good data access, when stakeholder priorities conflict, or when business impact must be quantified. Value delivered includes statistical validation of feature choices, A/B test designs, and ROI-backed prioritization.

methodologies.name: decision-analysis
methodologies.description: Decision analysis provides structured approaches to evaluate options and make informed choices by removing bias and ensuring all relevant factors are considered. Apply this when multiple paths forward exist, stakeholders disagree, or the decision is complex with trade-offs. The value is documented decision rationale, agreed-upon evaluation criteria, and decisions that withstand later scrutiny.

methodologies.name: elicitation-techniques
methodologies.description: Elicitation is the process of drawing out information from stakeholders about their needs, wants, and constraints using techniques beyond simple questions. Use this when stakeholders cannot articulate what they need, when critical requirements are missing, or when different stakeholders give contradictory information. Value includes uncovering unstated requirements, validating understanding, and gaining stakeholder buy-in.

methodologies.name: interface-analysis
methodologies.description: Interface analysis identifies and documents the boundaries and connections between the solution and external systems, users, components, and external parties. Use this when systems need to communicate, when integration points are unclear, or when building new components that connect to existing infrastructure. The value is undefined integration issues discovered early, shared data element definitions, and clear handoff points.
