methodologies.name: knowledge-areas-detail
methodologies.description: This document provides detailed overview of the 6 Business Analysis Knowledge Areas (BA Planning, Elicitation, Requirements Lifecycle, Strategy Analysis, Requirements Analysis, Solution Evaluation) with specific tasks, outputs, and methodologies for each. Reference this when structuring a BA approach, assigning work to analysts, or understanding how BA activities relate to each other.

methodologies.name: knowledge-areas-overview
methodologies.description: The BA Framework Knowledge Areas Overview introduces the 6 core knowledge areas that organize BA work into coherent structure and provides templates for assessing BA coverage on projects. Use this to understand the full scope of BA responsibilities, select appropriate knowledge areas for your project type, or create a BA approach document.

methodologies.name: methodologies-detail
methodologies.description: This reference document contains detailed methodology content for governance frameworks, communication planning, elicitation preparation, requirements maintenance, change impact analysis, current state analysis, future state definition, risk analysis, change strategy, requirements architecture, solution options analysis, and solution limitation assessment. Reference this for deep-dive content on BA frameworks not covered in individual methodology files.

methodologies.name: modern-ba-framework
methodologies.description: This document explains how Business Analysis Framework applies in modern contexts (2025-2026) including agile, AI, digital transformation, cloud, and microservices environments. Use this when traditional BA practices feel disconnected from your organizational reality, when applying BA Framework to scaled agile or DevOps, or when your team questions framework relevance.

methodologies.name: process-mining-automation
methodologies.description: Process mining reconstructs actual process flows from system data rather than relying on documentation or stakeholder descriptions, enabling data-driven automation opportunity identification. Apply this when RPA initiatives need strong business cases, when processes are complex and poorly documented, or when discovering what people actually do versus what they document. Value includes discovering hidden steps, bottlenecks, and automatable patterns with evidence.

methodologies.name: requirements-documentation
methodologies.description: Requirements documentation formalizes requirements in standard formats to ensure clarity, traceability, and testability instead of leaving them in people's heads or scattered across documents. Use this on any project where different teams (dev, test, business) need to understand requirements consistently. Value includes preventing misunderstanding, providing basis for testing, and enabling change control.

methodologies.name: requirements-lifecycle
methodologies.description: Requirements lifecycle management ensures requirements remain accurate, traceable, and managed through their evolution from identification to implementation and beyond. Apply this when requirements change frequently, when you need to understand what changed and why, or when you cannot reconstruct how a requirement evolved. The value is audit trails, version control, and clear decision records for all changes.

methodologies.name: requirements-prioritization
methodologies.description: Requirements prioritization ranks requirements by importance using structured methods (MoSCoW, RICE, Kano, value-effort matrix) instead of letting the loudest voice or highest authority decide. Use this when resources are limited, when you cannot deliver everything immediately, or when stakeholders disagree on importance. Value includes focusing team effort on highest-value work, managing stakeholder expectations, and delivering ROI faster.

methodologies.name: requirements-traceability
methodologies.description: Requirements traceability links requirements to their origins and downstream artifacts to answer where requirements came from, what they affect, and whether every business need is covered. Apply this when change impact is unclear, when test coverage gaps exist, or when you cannot explain why specific functionality exists. Value includes impact analysis, coverage verification, and justification for every component.

methodologies.name: requirements-validation
methodologies.description: Requirements validation confirms that requirements are correct, complete, and understood before development begins rather than discovering misunderstandings after significant investment. Use this before committing to implementation, when stakeholders have not reviewed requirements, or when requirements are complex. The value is prevention of expensive rework and confirmation that the right thing is being built.

methodologies.name: solution-assessment
methodologies.description: Solution assessment evaluates whether a solution meets business needs and delivers expected value both during development and after deployment. Apply this when solutions have issues, when business value is unclear, or when you need to improve future initiatives. Value includes understanding what worked, what did not, lessons captured, and improvement recommendations.

methodologies.name: stakeholder-analysis
methodologies.description: Stakeholder analysis identifies all affected parties, understands their needs and concerns, and develops strategies for working with them productively. Use this at project start when you might miss important perspectives, when there is resistance, or when stakeholder groups have conflicting needs. The value is complete requirements coverage, resolved conflicts early, and successful adoption.

methodologies.name: strategy-analysis-business-need
methodologies.description: Frames the business need before any solution is proposed so that downstream initiatives connect to corporate strategic goals and survive steering reviews instead of being rejected as advocacy. Apply it when a sponsor hands the BA a vague mandate, in annual or quarterly portfolio planning where candidate initiatives compete for budget, before RFP or business-case work that will gate seven-figure spend, on M&A integration plans, on regulatory-driven change (SOX, GDPR, DORA, NIS2), and after any leadership strategy refresh. Produce a need statement under 30 words in the canonical form (organization needs to accomplish outcome because driver; if not addressed, consequence), with every driver carrying a primary-source verbatim quote stamped retrieved_at, a falsifiable quantified consequence of inaction, a named decision-maker who can commit resources, and explicit traces to corporate goal IDs that exist in the catalog rather than agent-invented ones. The practical value is a sponsor-signed gate that authorizes current-state work and gives the BA defensible standing under audit; the most common failure mode it prevents is a solution dressed as a need (`we need a new CRM`) that hard-codes a choice before the gap analysis has been done. Skip it for single-team low-blast-radius work, executive-locked directives, incident post-mortems, unproven product discovery, pure NPV/IRR financial decisions, and operational hot-fixes with a known patch.

methodologies.name: strategy-analysis-change-strategy
methodologies.description: Determines how to close the prioritized gaps from gap analysis by generating and evaluating multiple credible options instead of coronating a preferred choice, which is the third most common failure mode of strategy analysis. Apply it after a signed gap analysis exists, on initiatives whose business case will gate significant spend, in portfolio planning where options across multiple initiatives compete for the same budget, on M&A Day-1/100/365 plans, and on regulatory-driven change where auditors must see the chosen path justified. Generate three to five substantive options (build, buy, partner, modify, combination) plus a scored status-quo baseline whose risk score quantifies the cost of inaction, randomize option order to defeat the agent's first/last bias, and profile each against a weighted matrix (Strategic Fit 25%, Cost 20%, Time-to-Value 20%, Risk 15%, Capability 20%). Run a pre-mortem (`in 12 months we regret picking this — why?`) before sign-off so risks challenge rather than rationalize the choice, and schedule 90-day and 365-day reviews so the strategy becomes a learning loop instead of rotting. The practical value is leadership seeing what happens if they defer, decision-analysis selecting on the matrix rather than preference, and traceability from need through gap to chosen path. Skip it before gap analysis is signed, when the decision-maker has publicly committed, or for single-option technical fixes with negligible blast radius.

methodologies.name: strategy-analysis-current-state
methodologies.description: Inventories where the organization is today across six areas — business processes, organization structure, technology landscape, people and skills, data and information, and external factors — so that gap deltas have a measured baseline rather than anecdote. Apply it after the business need is sponsor-signed, on digital transformations where current state spans process/tech/people/data simultaneously, on M&A integration before Day-1/100/365 gap analysis, on regulatory-driven change where auditors will demand evidence not assertions, and as the home for SWOT and PESTLE so findings feed gap analysis instead of standing alone. Cover all six areas (any skip is documented as a known gap with owner and resolution plan) and produce a stakeholder coverage matrix whose rows are areas, columns are stakeholder roles, and cells record interviewed yes/no with evidence links — empty rows block sign-off. Force per-cell `relevance_to_us` on SWOT so generic items (`AI is disrupting industries`) are rejected, stamp every PESTLE source `retrieved_at` with primary-source citations and refuse evidence older than 90 days. The practical value is a representative baseline rather than the loudest-voice view, defensible evidence under audit, and capability mapping that establishes what the organization can actually do today. Skip it before need sign-off, on single-area tactical changes (scope to the affected area only), and in early product discovery where opportunity-solution-trees fit better.

methodologies.name: strategy-analysis-future-state
methodologies.description: Defines the desired post-change outcome with falsifiable, measurable goals so gap deltas can be computed, change strategy options can be evaluated, and the 90-day review can verify whether the future state was actually achieved. Apply it after current-state assessment is signed, when the business need has been framed as an outcome the future state must operationalize, in portfolio planning so initiatives are comparable, and on regulatory-driven change where the regulator's requirements become the future state to translate into measurable compliance goals. Every goal carries metric, baseline, baseline_source, target, target_unit and target_date (ISO, within 3 years), and any solution-shaped wording (`implement chatbot`, `deploy ERP`) is recast as an outcome (`self-serve resolution rate >= 40%`); a measurability validator wired as a pre-commit hook rejects goals lacking required fields, with target_date in the past, beyond 3 years, or containing solution verbs. Lock the future state in a workshop with the exec sponsor before gap analysis starts so scope cannot mutate mid-project and rationale does not rot. The practical value is preventing the most reliable failure mode — aspirations coded as future states (`zero defects`, `seamless experience`) — that produce huge unmeasurable gaps and collapsed prioritization. Skip it before current-state assessment, before the need is signed off, and during early product discovery where lean canvas and JTBD experiments must run first.

methodologies.name: strategy-analysis-gap-analysis
methodologies.description: Identifies and measures what must change by joining current-state metrics with future-state targets, producing a structured table where every gap row carries area, current value, future value, numeric `gap_size` with unit, priority, evidence URL, and a `blocks` list for the dependency DAG. Apply it after both current-state assessment is signed and the future state is locked, on initiatives requiring a change strategy where the gap list is the prioritized input to option evaluation, in portfolio planning across many initiatives so blocking and shared gaps surface, and on regulatory-driven change where each row's evidence URL is the regulator's citation. Reject prose-only gaps (`our reporting is slow`) and force percentile-rank prioritization across all gaps (top quartile H, middle half M, bottom quartile L) instead of free H/M/L because agents cluster around three or all-H, and validate the dependency DAG manually so sequencing follows structure rather than political visibility. The practical value is measurable gap deltas that allow weighted scoring in the change-strategy matrix, structural rather than popularity-weighted prioritization, and machine-checkable closure tracking. Skip it before the future state is locked, before current-state assessment is complete, and on single-area tactical changes where a two-line ADR is sufficient.

methodologies.name: use-case-modeling
methodologies.description: Use case modeling describes sequences of actions a system performs to provide value to actors, capturing who does what with the system and why in a way that describes context and flows. Use this when understanding how users interact with systems, when edge cases and exceptions need clear handling, or when tests must cover complete scenarios. The value is complete coverage of user goals, documented alternative flows, and clear test cases.

methodologies.name: user-story-mapping
methodologies.description: User story mapping arranges stories along user journeys (horizontal) and priority (vertical) to show complete user experience and enable release planning instead of a flat disconnected list. Use this in agile projects when determining what goes into each release, when user experience is fragmented, or when release scope is unclear. Value includes walking skeleton approach, complete end-to-end experiences in early releases, and better release planning.

domains.description: Orchestrates HR and recruiting from talent acquisition through onboarding and retention. Covers recruitment funnel optimization, structured interviewing with STAR framework, job description writing, employer branding and EVP development, onboarding with 30-60-90 day plans, and retention/DEI compliance.

methodologies.name: 30-60-90-day-plan
methodologies.description: The 30-60-90 day plan structures new hire onboarding into learning, contributing, and executing phases with clear milestones, activities, and success criteria instead of undefined ramp-up periods. Use this for all new hires to accelerate productivity, provide clear expectations, and enable course correction. Value includes faster time to full productivity, improved retention, and early identification of cultural or capability mismatches.

methodologies.name: employee-value-proposition
methodologies.description: Employee Value Proposition (EVP) defines the unique combination of compensation, benefits, career, culture, and purpose that you offer employees instead of competing primarily on salary. Use this when recruiting is difficult, when you want to attract a specific talent type, or when candidates choose competitors. Value includes differentiated employer brand, higher offer acceptance rates, and stronger talent attraction.

methodologies.name: employer-branding
methodologies.description: Employer branding encompasses EVP development, careers page optimization, employee testimonials, Glassdoor management, and content strategy to create a compelling narrative about why people should work for your company. Apply this when your employer brand lags competitors, when recruiting metrics are weak, or when you want to attract specific talent profiles. The value is measurable improvements in application rates, offer acceptance, and employee referrals.

methodologies.name: interview-methods
methodologies.description: Interview methods include structured interview design, behavioral (STAR) questions, technical assessments, culture fit evaluation, and reference checks to assess candidates consistently and predict job performance. Use this to reduce hiring bias, improve interview validity, and make more predictable hiring decisions. Value includes higher quality hires, fewer mismatches, and documented evaluation rationale.

methodologies.name: onboarding
methodologies.description: New hire onboarding frameworks covering 30-60-90 day plans, buddy programs, and day-one orientation. Structures the first 90 days to reduce turnover and accelerate productivity. Use when bringing team members into an organization to ensure consistent setup and clear progression from learning to independence.

methodologies.name: onboarding-30-day
methodologies.description: Framework for the critical first 30 days when new hires absorb company knowledge and build relationships. Addresses overwhelm without direction by providing structured learning activities, stakeholder meetings, and clear success criteria. Apply during weeks 1-4 to establish foundation and measure readiness for contribution phase.

methodologies.name: onboarding-60-90-day
methodologies.description: Two-phase progression for days 31-90: contribution phase (days 31-60) for applying knowledge and initial impact, execution phase (days 61-90) for independent work and measurable results. Use to transition from learning to productivity and ensure new hires reach performance standards within 90 days.

methodologies.name: recruiting-process
methodologies.description: Full-cycle talent acquisition from requisition through onboarding, including sourcing strategies, screening, structured interviews, offer management, and candidate experience. Improves hiring quality and time-to-fill by systematizing each stage. Apply to any new opening or recruitment initiative to reduce bottlenecks and bad hires.

methodologies.name: recruitment-funnel-optimization
methodologies.description: Analyzes and improves conversion rates at each recruiting stage: awareness, application, screening, interview, offer, onboarding. Identifies where candidates drop and provides targeted optimizations. Use quarterly to track funnel health and benchmark performance against benchmarks.

methodologies.name: retention-compliance
methodologies.description: Strategies to keep top talent and ensure legal hiring practices through stay interviews, exit analysis, talent reviews, and DEI practices. Prevents costly turnover and protects against discrimination claims. Apply continuously to retain high performers and proactively address flight risks.

methodologies.name: star-interview-examples
methodologies.description: Real examples of strong, weak, and red-flag STAR responses with interviewer probing techniques and training scenarios. Helps calibrate assessment quality by showing what excellent evidence looks like. Use during interviewer training and coaching to improve consistency and predictiveness of assessments.

methodologies.name: star-interview-framework
methodologies.description: Structured behavioral interviewing method using Situation-Task-Action-Result questions to predict job performance from past behavior. Replaces vague interviews with data-driven assessment and reduces bias. Apply to all interviews to gather specific examples and evaluate actual competencies.

methodologies.name: star-interview-method
methodologies.description: Teaching guide for the STAR technique with question bank by competency, follow-up strategies, and common pitfalls. Enables consistent evaluation across candidates when combined with scorecards. Use for interviewer training and to build custom question banks for your competency model.

methodologies.name: structured-interview-design
methodologies.description: End-to-end process to design consistent interview loops with standardized questions, behavioral anchors, and scoring rubrics. 2x more predictive than unstructured interviews and reduces hiring bias. Use once per role to document interview flow, then reuse for all future openings.

domains.description: Enterprise web backend development in Java Spring Boot, C# ASP.NET Core, PHP Laravel, and Ruby on Rails. Covers framework-specific patterns, ORM design (JPA/EF/Eloquent/ActiveRecord), testing frameworks, background job processing, and enterprise application architecture patterns.

methodologies.name: csharp-aspnet-core
methodologies.description: Clean architecture for ASP.NET Core APIs with controller, service, and repository patterns. Separates concerns and enables testability through dependency injection. Apply when building production APIs in C# to achieve scalability and maintainability.

methodologies.name: csharp-background-services
methodologies.description: Hosted background services and timed workers for async processing in ASP.NET Core using BackgroundService base class and Channel-based queues. Offloads long-running work without blocking requests. Use for email, notifications, batch jobs, and scheduled cleanup tasks.

methodologies.name: csharp-dotnet
methodologies.description: Comprehensive .NET backend patterns spanning ASP.NET Core controllers, Entity Framework repositories, xUnit testing, and background services. Provides reference implementations for production-grade C# applications. Use as a complete architecture guide when building enterprise systems in .NET.

methodologies.name: csharp-dotnet-patterns
methodologies.description: Rich domain models, CQRS/MediatR architecture, value objects, and clean architecture layers for complex .NET applications. Prevents anemic models and supports business logic integrity. Apply when complexity demands sophisticated architecture beyond basic CRUD.

methodologies.name: csharp-entity-framework
methodologies.description: Entity configuration, relationships, repository patterns, and query optimization for Entity Framework Core. Enables efficient database access through lazy loading, eager loading, and custom queries. Use for all data access layers to avoid N+1 queries and maintain schema consistency.

methodologies.name: csharp-xunit-testing
methodologies.description: Unit and integration testing for .NET with Moq, mocking, and WebApplicationFactory. Structures controller tests, service tests, and API tests. Apply to all production code to maintain quality gates and regression prevention.

methodologies.name: decomposition-laravel
methodologies.description: Action classes, DTOs, form requests, and resources to break apart fat Laravel controllers and models into focused, LLM-friendly files of 40-100 lines. Improves code maintainability and enables easier AI-assisted development. Apply when refactoring legacy monoliths or starting new Laravel projects.

methodologies.name: decomposition-rails
methodologies.description: Service objects, query objects, and concerns to decompose Rails fat controllers and models into single-purpose classes. Each file stays under 100 lines for LLM compatibility while maintaining Rails conventions. Use when building complex Rails applications or teaching Rails patterns.

methodologies.name: java-jpa-hibernate
methodologies.description: Entity definitions, repository queries with JOIN FETCH, and pagination for JPA/Hibernate in Spring Boot. Handles relationships, constraints, and performance optimization. Apply to all Spring Boot projects to access the database efficiently.

methodologies.name: java-junit-testing
methodologies.description: Controller tests with MockMvc, service tests with Mockito, and integration tests for Spring Boot. Covers testing patterns for different layers. Use to structure comprehensive test suites matching your Spring Boot architecture.

methodologies.name: java-spring
methodologies.description: Controller, service, and repository layers with dependency injection, transactional boundaries, and async configuration. Foundation for all Spring Boot REST APIs. Apply as the base pattern for any Spring-based backend.

methodologies.name: java-spring-async
methodologies.description: CompletableFuture, thread pools, and @Async methods to process work asynchronously in Spring Boot. Prevents blocking on I/O-heavy operations like email or external API calls. Use for notifications, bulk operations, and background tasks.

methodologies.name: java-spring-boot
methodologies.description: Complete Spring Boot application with clean architecture: controllers, services, repositories, entities, and DTOs. Includes exception handling, specifications for complex queries, and testing patterns. Use as the reference for structuring production Spring Boot applications.

methodologies.name: java-spring-boot-patterns
methodologies.description: Advanced patterns for Spring Boot including domain-driven design, event sourcing, specification-based queries, and global exception handling. Supports complex business logic without anemic models. Apply when standard CRUD patterns become insufficient.

methodologies.name: laravel-patterns
methodologies.description: Models with relationships, services for business logic, repositories for queries, form requests for validation, and resources for JSON responses. Keeps Laravel applications organized at scale. Use when building multi-feature Laravel applications to avoid monolithic controllers.

methodologies.name: php-eloquent
methodologies.description: Eloquent ORM provides an elegant and fluent interface for database interactions in PHP applications. Use it when building Laravel projects that need efficient query composition, relationship management, and data manipulation with minimal raw SQL. The pattern delivers cleaner code, built-in query optimization through eager loading, and seamless model-to-database mapping that reduces development time while maintaining type safety.

methodologies.name: php-laravel
methodologies.description: Laravel is a comprehensive PHP framework for building production-grade applications with clean architecture patterns. Apply it when developing web APIs and web applications that require structured routing, middleware chains, and integrated services for authentication and data validation. It provides service containers, dependency injection, and extensive ecosystem support that accelerates development from concept to deployment.

methodologies.name: php-laravel-patterns
methodologies.description: Rails-inspired patterns in Laravel standardize application structure through service objects, repositories, and form requests. Use when designing scalable Laravel applications that need consistent separation of concerns between controllers, business logic, and data access layers. These patterns improve testability, code reuse, and team collaboration by establishing clear contracts for how different components interact.

methodologies.name: php-phpunit-testing
methodologies.description: PHPUnit with Laravel's testing helpers provides comprehensive test coverage for applications from unit tests through feature tests. Use when implementing test-driven development requiring database isolation, HTTP client mocking, and assertion chains for validation logic. The framework supplies factories, seeders, and refresh mechanisms that make test setup simple while maintaining test independence.

methodologies.name: ruby-activerecord
methodologies.description: ActiveRecord transforms database tables into Ruby objects with query composition and relationship management. Apply when building Rails applications needing fluent query builders, automatic N+1 prevention through eager loading, and declarative validations. Query objects add an extra layer of abstraction for complex filtering logic, keeping models focused on business rules rather than query construction.

methodologies.name: ruby-rails
methodologies.description: Rails provides an opinionated framework for rapid web application development with convention-over-configuration. Use when building monolithic web applications and APIs that benefit from integrated routing, ORM, and middleware ecosystem. Rails' patterns including service objects, serializers, and controllers with before_action filters create predictable structure that teams can navigate quickly.

methodologies.name: ruby-rails-patterns
methodologies.description: Service objects in Rails encapsulate complex business logic outside model and controller layers, promoting single responsibility and testability. Apply when Rails operations grow beyond simple CRUD to require multi-step transactions, external service calls, or result objects with success/failure states. This pattern keeps controllers thin while centralizing business rules in testable, reusable classes.

methodologies.name: ruby-rspec-testing
methodologies.description: RSpec provides a behavior-driven testing framework for Rails with expressive syntax and powerful matchers. Use when writing model specs, service specs, and request specs that read like specifications of intended behavior. The framework's fixtures, stubbing, and transactional cleanup make tests fast and focused, enabling teams to confidently refactor with comprehensive test coverage.

methodologies.name: ruby-sidekiq-jobs
methodologies.description: Sidekiq processes background jobs asynchronously using Redis for reliable task execution with automatic retries and error handling. Apply when Rails applications need to defer long-running operations, batch process data, or schedule recurring tasks. The framework handles job queuing, worker scaling, and failure recovery transparently, allowing developers to focus on job logic.

domains.description: Systems-level backend development in Go and Rust with focus on performance, concurrency, and infrastructure. Includes microservices with HTTP APIs and gRPC, goroutine/async concurrency patterns, error handling, database design and SQL optimization, caching strategies, and message queue patterns.

methodologies.name: caching-http-headers
methodologies.description: Pushes caching to the highest-leverage layers (browser L1 and CDN L3 edge) so eligible responses never reach the origin, eliminating up to 90% of traffic and reducing latency to near-zero through correct `Cache-Control`, `ETag`, `Vary` and `Surrogate-Key`/`Cache-Tag` headers. Apply it on read-heavy public API endpoints (catalog, articles, pricing) that change predictably, on content-hashed static assets that should be `immutable, max-age=1y`, on personalized API responses that need browser caching but must never leak across users, and any endpoint where origin volume or TTFB is a concern. Set explicit `public, max-age=N, stale-while-revalidate=M` for shared content, `private, max-age=0, must-revalidate` for user-specific data, and `no-store` for sensitive responses; generate ETags from content hashes (not modification timestamps) so 304 responses work correctly across multi-server deployments; declare every dimension in `Vary` (especially `Accept-Encoding`) but never `Authorization` (use `private` instead) and never `*` on public endpoints. Wire CDN tag-based purges into the write path immediately after the database write so invalidations are selective and instantaneous instead of waiting for TTL or flushing the entire cache. Skip it for real-time feeds, non-idempotent POST/PUT/DELETE responses, bodies containing tokens or session cookies, and endpoints without a CDN purge API. The single most dangerous mistake it prevents is `Cache-Control: public` on user-specific data, which is a security vulnerability rather than a caching mistake.

methodologies.name: caching-in-memory
methodologies.description: Eliminates the 1-5ms Redis round-trip on hot paths called thousands of times per second by serving from a process-local L1 cache layered above Redis (L2) and the database (L3), keeping latency sub-millisecond for the dominant fraction of reads. Apply it for static or semi-static config loaded at startup (`WarmableCache.warm()` before traffic arrives), permission and role tables hit on every authenticated request, feature flags polled per request with bounded staleness acceptable, expensive pure computations with bounded inputs (`lru_cache`), and hot product or article data in read-heavy single-process-per-machine APIs. Choose the right primitive: `lru_cache(maxsize=1000)` for deterministic pure functions only (never on I/O or time-dependent code), `cachetools.TTLCache` wrapped with a module-level `threading.Lock` via `cached(cache, lock=...)` for thread-safe TTL data, and a `WarmableCache` with double-checked locking to prevent concurrent duplicate loads on miss. Cap cache memory at under 20% of process RAM, store anything larger than 1KB in Redis instead, and on writes invalidate top-down (L1 cache_clear then Redis delete), accepting per-process staleness or wiring a Redis pub/sub signal across workers. Skip it for user session data, anything needing cross-process invalidation without a coordination channel, high-cardinality keys that evict hot entries, large values, or multi-worker deployments where each worker serves different traffic.

methodologies.name: caching-invalidation
methodologies.description: Solves the hardest part of caching by codifying four complementary strategies — TTL-based, event-based, tag-based and version-based — so freshness budgets are met without flushing the whole cache or letting stale data leak into user-visible bugs. Apply TTL universally as a safety net (every Redis key uses `setex`/`SET EX`, never raw `SET`, with 10-20% random jitter to avoid synchronized expiry stampedes), event-based when you control the write path and need a freshness budget under five minutes, tag-based when entities have group membership (products in a category, articles by author) and an entire group must invalidate on one event, and version-based for deploy-time schema changes that need atomic obsolescence of all cached instances via an `INCR`-bumped `{entity}:v{version}:{id}` key namespace. Maintain a write-path audit table mapping mutations to expected cache-key deletions so a reviewer agent can reject PRs with empty invalidation lists, give every tag SET a TTL equal to its longest member to prevent orphaned tag accumulation, and use `SCAN` rather than `KEYS *` for any pattern iteration because `KEYS` is O(N) and blocks the Redis single-thread loop. The practical value is bounded staleness with deterministic invalidation paths instead of relying on TTL alone, plus safe schema migrations across deploys. Skip event-based without a write-path audit, tag-based without TTL on tag sets, version-based as the sole strategy, and `KEYS *` for bulk invalidation in production hot paths.

methodologies.name: caching-stampede-prevention
methodologies.description: Prevents the cache stampede (thundering herd) failure mode where a popular key expires and many concurrent requests simultaneously reload it from the origin, cascading into database overload and outages. Apply it on any cache key with more than around ten concurrent requests per second backed by an expensive origin call, after cache flushes or cold starts, and on materialized views that take seconds to compute. Three patterns are provided: a Redis distributed lock using atomic SET NX EX with a Lua check-and-delete release, probabilistic early refresh (XFetch with a tunable beta) that proactively rebuilds before TTL, and request coalescing for in-process L1 caches with a bounded coalescing window. The practical value is a stampede-safe wrapper, jittered TTLs, and explicit fallbacks that degrade gracefully instead of amplifying the incident.

methodologies.name: caching-write-patterns
methodologies.description: Solves the most common root cause of cache-related incidents: the wrong write pattern applied to the wrong entity. Use it when picking a strategy per entity type based on read-to-write ratio, consistency budget, and write throughput. It contrasts cache-aside (lazy load with explicit invalidation, default for read-heavy data with bounded staleness), write-through (synchronous DB plus cache for read-after-write consistency at higher write latency), and write-behind (async DB flush for high-throughput counters with a persistent recovery queue). Production code shows a typed key_builder requirement, mandatory setex with TTL plus jitter, exact-key DELETE rather than KEYS patterns, and the L1 to L5 cache flow that locates each pattern in the multi-level cache hierarchy.

methodologies.name: database-design
methodologies.description: Proper schema design establishes data integrity, query performance, and maintainability through normalization, indexing, and constraint enforcement. Apply when starting new projects or adding features requiring schema changes, using entity-relationship modeling and understanding access patterns. Strong database design prevents data anomalies, enables efficient queries, and provides foundation for scaling as data volume grows.

methodologies.name: error-handling
methodologies.description: Standardized error responses following RFC 7807 provide consistent API behavior with actionable information for clients. Use when designing REST APIs requiring structured error formats including type, status, and validation details. Consistent error handling improves client implementation, simplifies debugging with trace IDs, and enables monitoring systems to reliably detect and respond to failures.

methodologies.name: go-backend
methodologies.description: Go's standard library and frameworks like Gin provide efficient HTTP server patterns with built-in concurrency support. Apply when building microservices, APIs, or high-throughput systems where performance and simplicity matter. Go's approach to error handling and interface design encourages explicit error checking and loose coupling, resulting in maintainable applications at scale.

methodologies.name: go-channels
methodologies.description: Go channels enable safe communication between goroutines through send/receive operations with built-in synchronization. Use when building data pipelines, fan-out/fan-in patterns, or coordinating multiple concurrent operations. Channels eliminate manual locking complexity, support elegant cancellation through context, and enable select-based multiplexing for handling multiple inputs.

methodologies.name: go-concurrency-patterns
methodologies.description: Worker pools, rate limiting, and error groups standardize concurrent operation patterns in Go applications. Apply when processing work at scale, limiting resource consumption, or coordinating groups of goroutines with proper error propagation. These patterns prevent goroutine leaks, manage throughput, and simplify error handling across concurrent operations.

methodologies.name: go-error-handling
methodologies.description: Go's explicit error returns and error wrapping with context enable robust error handling that produces actionable messages. Use when developing any Go application requiring proper error propagation, recovery, and reporting. Go's approach forces developers to consider failure cases at each step, while sentinel errors and custom error types provide structure for testing and client error handling.

methodologies.name: go-error-handling-patterns
methodologies.description: Advanced Go error patterns including error wrapping, checking, and custom types enable sophisticated error handling strategies. Apply when building libraries or applications requiring rich error information for clients and logging systems. Patterns like error wrapping preserve error chains for debugging while allowing clients to check error types safely.

methodologies.name: go-goroutines
methodologies.description: Goroutines are lightweight concurrency primitives that enable processing thousands of concurrent operations efficiently. Use when building IO-bound services, background job processors, or systems requiring parallel computation. Goroutines with proper context handling, channel communication, and WaitGroup synchronization enable safe concurrent code without manual thread management.

methodologies.name: go-http-handlers
methodologies.description: Gin and Echo provide routing, middleware, and request binding for building REST APIs and web servers efficiently. Apply when creating Go HTTP services requiring structured routing, graceful error handling, and standard middleware like logging and CORS. These frameworks reduce boilerplate while maintaining Go's philosophy of explicit, fast code.

methodologies.name: go-layout-agentic-workflow
methodologies.description: Drives Go service scaffolding by AI agents through a four-stage pipeline (layout, code-gen, wiring, review) with bounded prompt contracts so layout violations are caught before code reaches review. Apply it when an agent is generating a multi-resource Go service, when several agents share the work across stages, or when onboarding agents to an existing standard-layout codebase. The methodology supplies prompt templates per stage, a dedicated go-layout-review agent that flags handler-to-repository imports skipping the service layer, persistent stack decisions in .aidocs/product_docs/go-stack.md to stop per-PR re-litigation of tool choice, and a catalogue of typical agent gotchas (pkg/utils muscle memory, gin.Context leaking into services, global DB handles, missing error wrapping, context not propagated to goroutines).

methodologies.name: go-layout-directory-structure
methodologies.description: Solves the per-PR bikeshedding over Go package placement by adopting the community-converged golang-standards/project-layout: cmd/ for binaries, internal/ for private app code, pkg/ only when there is a real external consumer, and migrations/ for schema files. Apply it on greenfield services, mid-size services with multiple binaries sharing a domain core, monorepos that publish reusable libraries, onboarding-heavy teams, and refactors of single-package apps that grew past about 3k LOC. Skip it for tiny CLIs under 500 LOC, library-only modules, hyper-optimised single-package code, or teams already converged on a different convention. The value is a navigability contract: an unfamiliar developer or agent finds handlers, models, and the migration runner without reading docs, because the layout maps directly to Go's import resolution.

methodologies.name: go-layout-layer-rules
methodologies.description: Makes the Go standard layout load-bearing by mechanically enforcing dependency direction the compiler does not check: handler imports the service interface, service imports the repository interface, and the repository implementation provides it. Apply it on any team or multi-agent Go service where shortcut handler-to-repository imports would otherwise accumulate, and on services large enough that one violated import boundary materially increases coupling. The rules pin interfaces at the consumer side (service.UserRepository in internal/service, not internal/repository), forbid the web framework from leaking into services, ban database/sql in domain models, mandate context.Context as the first parameter and %w error wrapping at every layer crossing, and outlaw package-global state. Depguard inside golangci-lint is the automated CI gate that turns these conventions into a build break.

methodologies.name: go-layout-toolchain
methodologies.description: Pairs each slot of the Go standard layout (cmd/, internal/handler, internal/repository, migrations/) to a vetted toolchain so agents stop mixing incompatible choices like GORM in a sqlc-designed repo or duplicate logger inits. Apply it when starting a new service, onboarding an agent to an existing one, or reviewing a PR for layer-appropriate tooling. It catalogues CLI tools (go, golangci-lint, depguard, gosec, gofumpt, mockgen, sqlc, air, delve, pprof, goreleaser, cobra, koanf, migrate), CI services (GitHub Actions setup-go, SonarCloud, Renovate, Sentry, OpenTelemetry), and testing infrastructure (testcontainers-go), each mapped to its layout slot. The practical value is a single reference that prevents tool collisions per layer and lets agents pick the right binary instead of inventing combinations.

methodologies.name: go-project-structure
methodologies.description: Standard Go project layout separates entry points in cmd/, private code in internal/, and public libraries in pkg/. Use when organizing Go projects to maintain clarity and prevent unintended external dependencies. Go's internal package mechanism enforces architecture boundaries without additional tools, while cmd/ structure naturally supports multiple binaries.

methodologies.name: mq-broker-implementations
methodologies.description: Provides production-ready Python implementations for the four most common message brokers — RabbitMQ via pika, Redis Streams via redis-py, Celery as a task-queue abstraction, and AWS SQS via boto3 — covering queue declaration, persistent publishing, explicit ack/nack consumers, and DLQ wiring. Apply it when picking a broker (RabbitMQ for routing topology and publisher confirms, Redis Streams for lightweight event sourcing on existing Redis, Celery for retry-and-workflow primitives over a backend, SQS for managed AWS queues with first-class redrive). It documents broker-specific reliability semantics — heartbeats and prefetch_count for RabbitMQ, delivery_mode=2 for persistence, dead-letter-exchange arguments — so the wrong idiom (auto-ack on RabbitMQ, missing acks=all on Kafka) does not silently drop messages under failure.

methodologies.name: mq-idempotent-consumers
methodologies.description: Solves the duplicate-message problem inherent in at-least-once delivery and the phantom-event problem when a publish happens inside a DB transaction that later rolls back. Apply it on any consumer with side-effects (DB write, external API, email send), DLQ replay flows, saga steps that may be triggered by multiple events, and producers that must publish only iff the DB transaction commits. The pattern requires a stable idempotency_key in the message body (not the broker envelope), Redis-based deduplication via atomic reserve-before-execute (SET NX with TTL), ack-after-commit ordering, and the transactional outbox pattern to make publish and commit atomic. It is cheaper, more portable, and more reliable than vendor-specific exactly-once configurations.

methodologies.name: mq-patterns
methodologies.description: Catalogues the four foundational message-queue topologies — point-to-point work queue, publish/subscribe fan-out, request/reply with correlation IDs, and dead letter queue for poison messages — and shows when to compose them. Apply them to decouple services so failures isolate, run async background jobs (image resize, ETL, ML inference), level traffic spikes that downstream cannot absorb synchronously, and build event-driven architectures with multiple independent consumers per event. Avoid them for sub-50 ms request/response paths, multi-service strong-consistency transactions (use SAGA or 2PC and understand the trade-offs), pure log shipping, cache invalidation chains (use Redis pub/sub), and tiny single-process apps where a goroutine plus channel suffices. The value is choosing the right shape upfront so ordering, fan-out, and reply semantics do not introduce subtle correctness bugs later.

methodologies.name: mq-reliability
methodologies.description: Closes the four reliability gaps that cause silent message loss and retry storms: publisher confirms (RabbitMQ confirm_delivery, Kafka acks=all) so 'published' means durably stored rather than just buffered in TCP; DLQ with alerting so poison messages are isolated rather than piled up unnoticed; exponential backoff with jitter so retry waves do not DOS the broker after a hiccup; and backpressure via prefetch limits so slow consumers do not starve siblings or OOM the broker. Apply it on any business-critical queue, any retry policy, any DLQ-routing flow, and any schema that evolves. Skip it for fire-and-forget logging or ephemeral dashboards where at-most-once delivery is correct. The practical artefacts include schema versioning rules and replay runbooks for production incidents.

methodologies.name: nosql-cassandra-patterns
methodologies.description: Models Cassandra and DynamoDB wide-column schemas around query patterns rather than entities, since these stores have no joins and no query planner. Apply it on write-heavy time-series workloads (IoT, metrics, logs, events), horizontally scalable storage where Postgres or MongoDB cannot sustain the rate, multi-region active-active deployments, and known stable access patterns. It enforces partition keys with high cardinality (thousands of distinct values, even write distribution, partitions under 100 MB and ~100k rows) by combining monotonically increasing values with a high-cardinality prefix, time-bucketing time-series partitions (sensor_id + date), choosing clustering order for sort, and creating duplicate tables or materialized views per access pattern. It explicitly bans ALLOW FILTERING and warns that LWT BATCH is not equivalent to ACID transactions.

methodologies.name: nosql-mongodb-patterns
methodologies.description: Designs MongoDB schemas around access patterns instead of entity diagrams, picking embedding for 1:few relationships always read together with a bounded child count below ~100, and referencing for 1:many, many:many, or independently queried children. Apply it on hierarchical or nested data, high-volume document workloads with evolving schemas, time-series via the bucket pattern, full-text search co-located with documents, and session or catalog systems with heterogeneous attributes. The methodology requires writing the access-pattern list (query shape, filters, sort, QPS, latency budget) before choosing embedding vs referencing, indexing every query field, adding a schemaVersion field for evolution, and capping arrays explicitly. It avoids the 16 MB document size cap and unbounded embedded arrays — the most common failure mode — through the bucket pattern, TTL indexes, and explicit caps.

methodologies.name: nosql-neo4j-patterns
methodologies.description: Uses Neo4j when the value is in the connections — social networks, recommendation engines, fraud rings, knowledge graphs, path-finding — where relational JOINs at depth >2 grow exponentially and graph traversal stays O(1) per hop. Apply it for friends-of-friends queries, collaborative filtering ('users who bought X also bought Y'), connected-account fraud detection, ontology and entity resolution, and shortest/all-paths problems. Avoid it on tabular or document-centric data, multi-million-write-per-second key-value workloads, large analytical aggregations (use a warehouse), and dense graphs at depth >5. It mandates labeled nodes, typed directed relationships with properties on the edge (not the node), bounded hop depth using *..N syntax with a LIMIT clause, and EXPLAIN/PROFILE on representative samples before deploying any traversal — preventing the unbounded-traversal failure that exhausts JVM heap and crashes the instance.

methodologies.name: nosql-redis-patterns
methodologies.description: Treats Redis as an in-memory data structure server rather than a plain key-value cache, mapping each access pattern to the correct type: String with SETEX for atomic cached values, Hash for objects with field-level HINCRBY, List for FIFO queues via LPUSH/BRPOP, Set for unique membership and set operations, Sorted Set for leaderboards and sliding-window rate limiting, Pub/Sub for real-time fan-out, and Streams with consumer groups for durable replayable events. Apply it on caching layers, session storage with automatic TTL, leaderboards and ranking, distributed rate limiting, lightweight task queues, real-time notifications, and ordered event streams. Avoid it as a primary durable store, for large binaries, complex relational queries, or replayable streams where Kafka is the right tool. The most-emphasised guard is consistent key namespacing plus per-key EXPIRE so keys never accumulate silently until maxmemory eviction breaks the application.

methodologies.name: rust-backend
methodologies.description: Rust's type system and async runtime enable building performant, memory-safe backend systems without garbage collection. Use when developing systems requiring maximum performance, safety guarantees, or situations where runtime failures are costly. Rust's compile-time checks prevent entire categories of bugs while providing efficient abstractions for concurrency and error handling.

methodologies.name: rust-error-handling
methodologies.description: Use Rust's Result and Option types for explicit error handling that avoids exceptions and null pointer issues. Apply this when building systems that require safe, predictable error propagation across synchronous and async code. Delivers confidence in error contracts and eliminates runtime surprises through compile-time verification.

methodologies.name: rust-http-handlers
methodologies.description: Implement type-safe HTTP request handlers using frameworks like Axum with extractors and middleware for routing, validation, and serialization. Apply when building RESTful APIs that need compile-time type checking and zero-cost abstractions. Delivers clean separation between HTTP concerns and business logic.

methodologies.name: rust-ownership
methodologies.description: Leverage Rust's ownership system to achieve memory safety without garbage collection through move semantics, borrowing, and lifetime tracking. Apply when writing performance-critical systems that must prevent data races and memory leaks at compile time. Delivers safe concurrent code and predictable resource management.

methodologies.name: rust-project-structure
methodologies.description: Organize Rust web applications using modular architecture with separate directories for handlers, services, models, and middleware aligned to the framework (Actix/Axum). Apply when maintaining projects beyond proof-of-concept scale to ensure team cohesion and code discoverability. Delivers consistency and reduces onboarding friction.

methodologies.name: rust-testing-antipatterns
methodologies.description: Catalogues the failure modes AI agents reproduce most often when generating Rust tests so reviewers and lint scripts can catch them before merge: Utc::now() injected into both production and assertion paths (passes at noon, fails at midnight); tokio::time::sleep used to 'give a task time' (flaky in CI); #[tokio::test] flavor mismatch where current_thread is the default but the unit calls spawn_blocking; shared lazy_static or once_cell state across parallel tests; hardcoded 127.0.0.1:8080 ports causing 'address already in use'; hallucinated mockall predicates that fail to compile; and flake quarantine misuse. Apply it when reviewing any AI-authored Rust test PR, in pre-commit via the rust-test-lint.sh script, during test-suite audits, and when onboarding a new agent. Skip it for production code outside #[test] and benches/.

methodologies.name: rust-testing-ci-toolchain
methodologies.description: Replaces the limitations of cargo test in CI with cargo nextest for parallel per-process execution and per-test timeouts, cargo llvm-cov for fast accurate LCOV/HTML coverage feeds, and cargo +nightly miri for undefined-behaviour detection in unsafe blocks and FFI that the borrow checker cannot see. Apply it on Rust projects with more than a handful of integration tests, multi-crate workspaces, codebases with unsafe or FFI, projects uploading coverage to Codecov or Coveralls, and library crates needing cargo test --doc on every PR. Skip it for trivial scripts, criterion benchmarks, and cross-compile targets without representative host runs. Mandatory practices include a .config/nextest.toml with fail-fast=false and retries=1 on the ci profile, soft (not gated) coverage reporting, miri on a separate non-blocking job, and tool versions pinned via taiki-e/install-action plus Renovate.

methodologies.name: rust-testing-integration
methodologies.description: Drives integration tests for Axum, Actix, or Tonic services through tower::ServiceExt::oneshot — calling the real router in-process with no network socket — and uses testcontainers-rs to spin up a real Postgres per test or a shared container with per-test schema isolation (CREATE SCHEMA test_<uuid> + DROP CASCADE). Apply it for services where you need confidence that the router, extractors, middleware, serialization, and DB round-trip work end-to-end; for handlers reading or writing a database where mocks would just assert mock setup; and for pagination, filtering, and ordering logic that requires real SQL. Skip it for pure business logic without I/O (use mockall) and for hot-path benchmarks (use criterion). The rules forbid hardcoded ports (bind :0 + local_addr()), forbid pointing tests at shared dev or staging databases, and require running migrations inside the test setup before any assertions.

methodologies.name: rust-testing-property
methodologies.description: Replaces brittle hand-written example tables with proptest (or quickcheck), generating hundreds of random inputs to verify invariants — encode/decode identity, sorted-output monotonicity, non-negative results — and automatically shrinking failures to a minimal reproducer. Apply it on parsers, codecs, serialization round-trips, math utilities, and any pure function whose output must satisfy a property for all valid inputs. Skip it for tests that need precise control over a single specific input, for I/O- or DB-driven integration tests where proptest's generation model does not compose well, and for criterion benchmarks. The methodology requires at least one proptest! block per crate of parsers/codecs/serialization, persisting failing seeds via FileFailurePersistence::WithSource('regression') so a CI failure becomes a free regression test, and using approx::assert_relative_eq! for f64 comparisons rather than assert_eq!.

methodologies.name: rust-testing-unit
methodologies.description: Structures Rust unit tests as colocated #[cfg(test)] mod tests blocks for access to private items, with mockall::mock! providing trait mocks and #[tokio::test] driving async tests under explicitly chosen runtime flavors. Apply it on Axum/Actix/Tonic services needing fast feedback, async-heavy code where tokio::time::pause controls virtual time deterministically, service methods depending on cheaply mockable traits (DB, HTTP client, clock), and library crates where doc-tests should run on every PR. Skip it for trivial scripts, code dominated by mock setup (where the test asserts the mock and not behaviour), pure serde round-trips (use proptest), and unsuitable cross-compile targets. Mandatory rules: never mix colocated and tests/ for the same surface, always specify the tokio flavor explicitly to surface Send/Sync bugs, never use sleep to wait for tasks, and use expect with descriptive messages instead of unwrap so the next agent has a starting point on failure.

methodologies.name: rust-tokio-async
methodologies.description: Use Tokio for efficient concurrent I/O operations with async/await syntax, handling CPU-bound work via spawn_blocking and managing concurrency with semaphores. Apply when processing multiple requests simultaneously without thread overhead or when coordinating across service boundaries. Delivers scalable throughput with minimal resource consumption.

methodologies.name: sql-optimization
methodologies.description: Analyze and improve database queries using EXPLAIN output, strategic indexing, query rewriting, and pagination patterns to reduce latency and resource usage. Apply when users report slow response times or database becomes a bottleneck under production load. Delivers lower costs and faster application response times.

domains.description: Domain-Driven Design for maintainable enterprise software architecture. Covers focusing on core business domain, establishing shared language between technical and domain experts, and designing systems that reflect business logic clearly. Essential for large-scale enterprise applications with complex business rules and evolving requirements. Use when building systems with non-trivial business logic or managing technical complexity in large teams.

methodologies.name: clean-architecture
methodologies.description: Structure applications in concentric layers (entities, use cases, adapters, frameworks) with inward-pointing dependencies to isolate business logic from infrastructure concerns. Apply when building systems expected to last years and adapt to changing requirements without major rewrites. Delivers testability, maintainability, and technology independence.

methodologies.name: cqrs-pattern
methodologies.description: Separate read and write operations into distinct models optimized for their specific access patterns, using events to synchronize state between models. Apply when read/write ratios are imbalanced or complex domain logic demands independent scaling of different operations. Delivers performance improvements and simplified reasoning about state changes.

methodologies.name: domain-driven-design
methodologies.description: Design systems around core business concepts using ubiquitous language, bounded contexts, aggregates, and value objects to align code structure with domain understanding. Apply when collaborating closely with domain experts or building large systems with multiple teams owning different subdomains. Delivers code that reflects business intent and resists entropy.

methodologies.name: event-sourcing-basics
methodologies.description: Store application state as an immutable sequence of events rather than current state, rebuilding state by replaying events to recover point-in-time state and audit trail. Apply when regulatory requirements mandate complete change history or when debugging requires understanding how state evolved. Delivers compliance, auditability, and temporal queries.

methodologies.name: event-sourcing-implementation
methodologies.description: Implement event stores with optimistic concurrency control, projections for read models, and snapshots for performance using databases like PostgreSQL or specialized event stores. Apply when building systems with Event Sourcing architecture that require reliable persistence and projection consistency. Delivers scalable event persistence and playback capability.

methodologies.name: microservices-design
methodologies.description: Decompose applications into loosely coupled services organized by business capability, each with independent deployment and data ownership. Apply when teams exceed 30 developers, system scale requires independent feature scaling, or technology diversity matters. Delivers team autonomy, targeted scalability, and faster innovation cycles.

domains.description: Enterprise system architecture patterns and infrastructure design. Covers service mesh architecture, service discovery, load balancing, and zero-trust security through mutual TLS. Includes design patterns for large distributed systems, microservices topology, and infrastructure-as-code approaches. Apply when architecting complex systems with multiple services or designing for high-scale enterprise deployments.

methodologies.name: cloud-architecture
methodologies.description: Design applications for cloud environments using elasticity, redundancy, and automated scaling across the six pillars of well-architected frameworks. Apply when evaluating cloud provider choices (AWS/Azure/GCP) or architecting systems for global reach and resilience. Delivers cost efficiency, high availability, and operational automation.

methodologies.name: container-orchestration
methodologies.description: Use Kubernetes to automate container deployment, scaling, and networking through declarative configuration, self-healing, and rolling updates. Apply when managing complex microservices deployments at scale or needing zero-downtime updates and automatic failure recovery. Delivers operational consistency and resource efficiency.

methodologies.name: data-pipeline-design
methodologies.description: Design ingestion, transformation, and storage systems using medallion architecture (bronze/silver/gold layers) with orchestration tools like Airflow or Dagster. Apply when building systems that must reliably process large data volumes or maintain data quality across transformation stages. Delivers data reliability and analytics readiness.

methodologies.name: distributed-patterns
methodologies.description: Apply Saga, CQRS, Event Sourcing, and circuit breaker patterns to solve distributed transaction, consistency, and failure scenarios across service boundaries. Apply when coordinating work across multiple services or preventing cascading failures in microservice architectures. Delivers system resilience and clear failure semantics.

methodologies.name: event-driven-architecture
methodologies.description: Build systems using asynchronous event-driven communication where services publish events to brokers and subscribe to relevant events independently. Apply when reducing temporal coupling between services or supporting fan-out scenarios where multiple consumers react to events. Delivers loose coupling, scalability, and audit trails.

methodologies.name: microservices-architecture
methodologies.description: Organize large applications into independently deployable services aligned to business capabilities with decentralized data ownership and API-driven communication. Apply at scale (30+ developers, 10+ services) when team autonomy and independent scaling matter more than operational simplicity. Delivers velocity and fault isolation at the cost of distributed systems complexity.

methodologies.name: observability-architecture
methodologies.description: Implement three pillars of observability—metrics (what happened), logs (why it happened), traces (where it happened)—with correlated instrumentation across services. Apply when troubleshooting production issues requires understanding request flows and system state without predefined dashboards. Delivers faster incident detection and resolution.

methodologies.name: quality-attributes-analysis
methodologies.description: Analyze non-functional requirements using quality attribute scenarios, utility trees, and ATAM to make explicit the architectural trade-offs between competing goals. Apply during design phases when evaluating architectural alternatives or when stakeholders disagree on priorities. Delivers shared understanding and principled architectural decisions.

methodologies.name: reliability-architecture
methodologies.description: Design systems using SLIs, SLOs, SLAs, circuit breakers, retries, and chaos engineering to maintain availability targets and recover quickly from failures. Apply when availability requirements exceed 99.9% or when downtime directly impacts revenue. Delivers predictable service quality and rapid problem detection.

methodologies.name: service-mesh
methodologies.description: Deploy a service mesh (Istio, Linkerd, Cilium) to handle service-to-service communication, mTLS encryption, traffic management, and observability at the infrastructure layer. Apply at 10+ microservices scale when zero-trust security and uniform observability justify operational overhead. Delivers transparent security and centralized traffic control.

domains.description: Enterprise Java development with Spring Boot and layered architecture. Covers project setup, layered architecture patterns, dependency injection, REST API design, and Spring ecosystem best practices. Includes frameworks for robust, testable, and maintainable Java applications. Use when building enterprise applications, microservices, or REST APIs that require strong architectural foundations.

methodologies.name: api-gateway-patterns
methodologies.description: Use API Gateway to centralize authentication, rate limiting, request routing, and response transformation across backend services without duplicating concerns in each service. Apply when exposing multiple services under a unified API or managing versioning, deprecation, and client access control. Delivers consistent API behavior and reduced service coupling.

methodologies.name: api-monitoring-alerting
methodologies.description: Replaces noisy threshold alerts with SLO-anchored multi-window multi-burn-rate alert rules so on-call pages fire only when the error budget is genuinely at risk, following Google SRE Workbook chapter 5. Apply it on any production API with defined SLOs wired to PagerDuty/Opsgenie/Squadcast, when establishing SLIs and error budgets ahead of a launch, when migrating from threshold rules to burn-rate rules, and when onboarding a new microservice into Prometheus + Alertmanager. Skip it for pre-PMF prototypes, internal tools used by under ten people, and any service without a defined SLO (the SLO is the prerequisite). Every alert must carry a for: clause of at least 2 minutes, a severity label of page or ticket, and a runbook_url annotation — enforced in CI via promtool check rules plus a grep gate. Page-level rules combine 1h fast burn with 5m confirmation; ticket-level rules combine multi-day windows for slow erosion.

methodologies.name: api-monitoring-health-checks
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of cascading restart loops and false-healthy signals caused by conflating liveness and readiness probes in orchestrated APIs. Apply when shipping any service to Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, ECS, or any platform that distinguishes liveness from readiness, and when wiring observability for the first time before a launch or scale event. The methodology splits responsibilities into a /healthz endpoint that only confirms the process is alive (no downstream calls) and a /readyz endpoint that probes database, cache, and external dependencies and returns 503 with named failures, so traffic is gated without restarting healthy pods. It also flags a recurring agent failure mode of inventing vendor health URLs like https://api.stripe.com/health and prescribes using the documented status contract instead. The practical value is fewer self-inflicted outages, accurate signals to the load balancer, and clean SLO targets (99.9% availability, p95 under 500ms) with sane alert thresholds.

methodologies.name: api-monitoring-logging
methodologies.description: Addresses the dual problem of unsearchable logs and disconnected traces in production APIs by mandating structured JSON logging with a request_id, OpenTelemetry trace_id and span_id, and a redaction layer for secrets. Use it when migrating from print/unstructured loggers to a real aggregation platform (Loki, Datadog, CloudWatch), instrumenting OTel traces, or onboarding a service with PII or PCI exposure. The pattern includes a middleware that binds request scope to structlog, returns X-Request-ID to clients for support lookups, propagates W3C traceparent into Celery and queue workers, and forces an explicit head or tail sampling decision before deploy to keep span volume from exploding at 1000 RPS. It explicitly rejects logger.info(request.json()) which routinely leaks passwords, tokens, and PAN into multi-year retention windows. The value is queryable structured logs that join 1:1 to traces, controlled cost, and clean separation between safe metadata and sensitive fields.

methodologies.name: api-monitoring-metrics
methodologies.description: Prevents the most common Prometheus production incident, cardinality explosion from labels like user_id or raw path, while delivering the RED (Rate, Errors, Duration) and USE (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) views that operators actually need. Apply when adding production observability to a new or existing API, building Grafana dashboards before a launch, or migrating from legacy APM SDKs to OpenTelemetry. The methodology pins labels to method, route template, and status class only, requires histogram buckets tuned to the service's actual latency profile (or native histograms on Prometheus 2.40+), and runs the metrics middleware before authentication so 401 and 429 are counted. It includes a concrete FastAPI implementation, a cardinality lint check, and a load-test gate ensuring middleware overhead stays under one millisecond per request. The payoff is bounded time series, accurate p99 estimates that trigger SLO burn-rate alerts on time, and dashboards that survive scale.

methodologies.name: clean-architecture
methodologies.description: Clean Architecture separates concerns into concentric layers with dependencies pointing inward, keeping business logic independent of frameworks and delivery mechanisms. Use when building complex, long-lived enterprise systems that may need infrastructure changes while maintaining high testability and team collaboration. This approach isolates domain rules from technical details, making the codebase easier to reason about, test, and evolve as requirements change.

methodologies.name: continuous-delivery
methodologies.description: Continuous Delivery automates the entire software release pipeline—from building and testing to staging and production—ensuring code is always in a deployable state. Apply this when teams need frequent releases, rapid iteration, or want to reduce deployment risk through automated verification at every stage. The practice separates deployment (pushing to production) from release (making features visible), enabling faster feedback loops and easier rollback.

methodologies.name: cqrs-pattern
methodologies.description: Command Query Responsibility Segregation separates read and write operations into different models, optimizing each path independently for its specific access patterns and performance needs. Use when systems have vastly different read and write volumes, require audit trails, or work well with event sourcing and eventual consistency. This pattern enables scaling queries independently from commands and works naturally with event-driven architectures.

methodologies.name: csharp-aspnet-core
methodologies.description: ASP.NET Core provides a modern, cross-platform framework for building web APIs and cloud-native applications with built-in dependency injection and async/await patterns throughout. Apply when building enterprise applications, microservices, or REST/gRPC APIs that require high performance and robust transaction management. The framework's middleware pipeline and configuration flexibility support clean layered architectures from startup.

methodologies.name: csharp-background-services
methodologies.description: Background Services in ASP.NET Core enable processing long-running tasks outside the request-response cycle using hosted services and channel-based queuing. Use when building features like order processing, scheduled cleanups, or email sending that shouldn't block HTTP endpoints. This approach ensures graceful shutdown, cancellation token support, and dependency injection for background work.

methodologies.name: csharp-dotnet
methodologies.description: C# and .NET provide a mature, feature-rich platform for enterprise development with strong typing, LINQ, async/await, and a comprehensive standard library. Apply for building scalable backend systems, cloud applications, or cross-platform CLI tools where performance, safety, and developer productivity are priorities. The ecosystem includes excellent ORM frameworks, testing libraries, and deployment tooling.

methodologies.name: csharp-dotnet-patterns
methodologies.description: .NET Patterns cover architectural approaches like CQRS, event sourcing, and domain-driven design implemented with C# idioms and framework capabilities. Use when designing complex domain models, decoupled services, or systems requiring rich event histories and temporal queries. These patterns work synergistically with .NET's strong type system and async support.

methodologies.name: csharp-entity-framework
methodologies.description: Entity Framework Core is a modern ORM enabling database access through LINQ queries against strongly-typed domain models, with support for migrations and complex relationships. Apply when building data-intensive applications where you want to avoid manual SQL while maintaining query performance and type safety. The fluent configuration API and shadow properties support sophisticated domain models.

methodologies.name: csharp-xunit-testing
methodologies.description: xUnit is a modern testing framework for .NET with clean syntax, excellent async support, and flexible test organization via traits and custom attributes. Use when writing unit tests, integration tests, or even acceptance tests for .NET applications where readability and composability matter. The framework integrates deeply with CI/CD pipelines and provides detailed failure diagnostics.

methodologies.name: ddd-aggregates
methodologies.description: Replaces anemic data classes plus services with proper Aggregate roots that enforce business invariants from the inside, eliminating the bypass paths that scatter rules across service code. Use it for any domain object with lifecycle state and invariants (Order, Subscription, Booking), when multiple entities must change atomically, when state transitions need to record domain events, or when complex domains need a stable cluster API for multiple teams. The pattern requires named command methods (place, cancel, ship) instead of public setters, identity-only references between Aggregates, one Aggregate per DB transaction with eventual cross-Aggregate consistency via events, and one failing unit test per invariant before implementation. It also flags AI-agent failure modes such as DDD-washing folder names while keeping anemic models, and traversing object graphs across Aggregate boundaries. The value is rules that cannot be bypassed by construction, clean transaction scope, and domain code that doubles as living documentation.

methodologies.name: ddd-anti-corruption-layer
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of foreign vocabulary and external SDK types leaking into the domain model when integrating with payment gateways, shipping carriers, legacy systems, or other Bounded Contexts. Apply it whenever a domain depends on an external API or another team's model and you need protection from versioning, breaking changes, and terminology mismatch. The ACL puts a domain-owned interface (InventoryChecker, PaymentGateway) in the domain layer using only domain types, and concentrates all imports of the external SDK into a single adapter in the infrastructure layer that translates field names, error types, and failure modes (e.g., availableUnits to quantity, ExternalApiError to InventoryUnavailableError, with a documented fail-safe default for non-critical integrations). It also catches typical agent failures: skipping the interface, propagating raw exceptions, and stuffing business logic into adapters. The practical payoff is that external API churn touches only the adapter, domain unit tests use mock clients, and Ubiquitous Language stays uncontaminated.

methodologies.name: ddd-domain-events
methodologies.description: Decouples cross-Aggregate reactions from primary state changes by raising frozen, identifiable Domain Events inside Aggregate command methods and dispatching them only after the DB transaction commits. Use it when a state transition triggers downstream work in other Bounded Contexts (order placed → billing, shipping, notifications), when audit trails are required, or when adopting Event Sourcing.
