The methodology requires immutable events with event_id and occurred_at for idempotency, collection via aggregate.collect_events() after commit (never before), and the Outbox pattern for production systems with Kafka, RabbitMQ, or SNS so the event row and the state change land in the same DB transaction and a relay publishes them. This eliminates the dual-write window where events leak for failed transactions or get lost when the process crashes after commit. The value is loosely coupled handlers, no ghost events, and exactly-once-effective downstream reactions.

methodologies.name: ddd-repositories
methodologies.description: Inverts the dependency between the domain and persistence so domain logic never imports SQLAlchemy, Django ORM, or JPA types, and unit tests run without a database. Apply it whenever an Aggregate must be persisted and reloaded, when the domain must remain ORM-agnostic, or when write-side and read-side models diverge in a CQRS system. The pattern places an abstract Repository interface in the domain layer with identity-based methods (find_by_id, save, delete) using only domain types, and an infrastructure implementation that performs explicit translation between persistence models and Aggregates via _to_entity / _to_model. Collection queries by arbitrary criteria are deliberately excluded — those belong in Read Models or Query Services to avoid blurring the write boundary or bypassing Aggregate invariants. The value is fast in-memory fakes for unit tests, clean ORM swap paths, and a domain that depends on what it needs rather than how it is stored.

methodologies.name: ddd-value-objects
methodologies.description: Eliminates a class of bugs caused by primitive obsession — passing a price where a quantity is expected, mixing currencies, storing emails without validation — by replacing raw strings, ints, and Decimals with immutable, self-validating domain types like Money, Email, Address, and PhoneNumber. Use it for any primitive with validation rules or context-dependent meaning, for compound values that always travel together, and to consolidate inline validation scattered across the codebase. Each Value Object validates its invariants in the constructor or __post_init__ so illegal states are unrepresentable, is enforced immutable by the type system (frozen dataclass, record, readonly struct), and returns new instances from operations rather than mutating. Equality is structural by attributes, which makes them safe to share, hash, and compare. The practical value is fail-fast construction, no aliasing surprises, and code that documents domain meaning in its types.

methodologies.name: event-sourcing-agentic
methodologies.description: Provides a structured five-stage agentic pipeline (aggregate design → code-gen → projection → tests → anti-pattern review) for generating event-sourcing scaffolding without the typical AI failure modes. Use it when scaffolding a new event-sourced aggregate from a bounded context spec, generating projection and repository code from a locked design, or running anti-pattern review on event-sourcing PRs. It enumerates the recurring agent gotchas — CRUD-shaped Updated/Changed events, state mutation outside apply handlers, missing expected_version on save, hallucinated event-store APIs, and treating snapshots as truth — and pins each to a lint rule or prompt constraint. The methodology persists the event catalog at .aidocs/product_docs/event-catalog.md and binds one event/projection/replay-test triple per SDD task using the faion-sdd-executor-agent. The value is correct event-sourcing scaffolds on the first pass instead of post-hoc cleanup of CRUD-shaped code generated by unguided agents.

methodologies.name: event-sourcing-aggregate
methodologies.description: Defines the apply/replay split that makes event-sourced aggregates testable and historically reconstructable: command methods validate invariants and emit events, while state mutation lives only inside apply() handlers. Apply it when implementing an event-sourced domain model with business invariants, building a CQRS write side, or designing repositories that load events, replay, and execute commands. The pattern mandates optimistic concurrency via save(stream_id, events, expected_version) using the version loaded at command start to detect concurrent writes, and a collect_pending_events() method on the aggregate that the repository calls after persistence so outbox publication and saga triggers see only committed events. It explicitly forbids cross-aggregate mutation in a single command handler — that work belongs to a saga or process manager. The value is given/when/then test simplicity, deterministic replay from any historical point, and protection against silent ghost writes under concurrency.

methodologies.name: event-sourcing-fundamentals
methodologies.description: Establishes the core principles of Event Sourcing — events as the immutable append-only source of truth, current state derived by replay, ordered application via stream version — and the explicit decision criteria for when to adopt it. Use it for compliance-heavy domains (banking, ledger, healthcare) needing complete audit trails, temporal queries, ML feature stores, undo/what-if workflows, or CQRS with multiple read models that must stay in sync from one truth. It is equally explicit about when not to use it: CRUD data with no audit need, reporting-first products, pre-PMF MVPs, teams without DDD experience (where the event log degenerates into CDC of CRUD), and GDPR contexts where crypto-shredding is unacceptable. Events must be named in past tense as business actions (OrderPlaced, ItemAdded) — never CRUD-shaped Updated snapshots — and must never be mutated or deleted. The value is a clear go/no-go framework that prevents the over-engineering trap of speculative event sourcing.

methodologies.name: event-sourcing-projections
methodologies.description: Turns the write-optimized event store into queryable, denormalized read models by maintaining purpose-built projections that apply events as idempotent UPSERTs keyed on (stream_id, position). Apply it for any query that cannot be answered by loading a single aggregate (list views, cross-entity joins, analytics), read-heavy surfaces where replay would be too slow, and CQRS systems with multiple consumer shapes (mobile, admin, reporting). Each projection tracks its last processed position in a checkpoint table so duplicates and restarts do not corrupt the read model, must contain no business logic or side effects, and must be fully rebuildable from offset 0 with a tested rebuild command exercised at least quarterly. Real-time read-your-writes flows need a separate strategy because projection lag is intrinsic. The value is fast denormalized queries, schema migrations via wipe-and-replay, and read models that stay disposable rather than becoming a parallel source of truth.

methodologies.name: event-sourcing-snapshots
methodologies.description: Cuts aggregate-load latency for long event streams by periodically caching derived state, while preserving correctness by treating snapshots strictly as a performance cache rather than a source of truth. Use it only after profiling confirms that replay is the bottleneck — typically aggregates with more than 50–100 events per instance, multi-year accounts, or high-frequency order streams. The methodology stores (stream_id, snapshot_version, serialized_state) every N events (recommended 50–100), loads via fetch-latest-snapshot then replay-only-newer-events, and mandates a tested rebuild path from event offset 0 without any snapshot present. The hard correctness rule is that any event class field addition, removal, or rename forces invalidation and rebuild of all affected snapshots before deploy, since old snapshots encode the old shape and combining them with new events silently produces wrong state. The value is bounded command latency without sacrificing replay determinism or schema-evolution safety.

methodologies.name: event-sourcing-versioning
methodologies.description: Solves the most underestimated operational challenges in event sourcing: schema evolution after consumers exist, and GDPR right-to-erasure on an immutable log. Apply it from day one to any event class consumed beyond its origin (projections, sagas, downstream microservices), to any system processing personal data, and when a schema registry like Apicurio or Confluent is in play. Each published event carries an event_version (default 1); any rename, removal, or semantic change bumps the version and registers an upcaster that transforms old shapes to new. A committed .event-catalog.json plus a CI lint gate makes breaking changes machine-detectable instead of relying on PR review. For PII, the only architecturally sound options are crypto-shredding (per-subject encryption with key drop on erasure) or PII externalization (opaque IDs in events, mutable side table). The value is consumer compatibility through schema change and a defensible answer to GDPR Article 17.

methodologies.name: java-jpa-hibernate
methodologies.description: JPA with Hibernate provides an object-relational mapping framework that translates domain objects into database tables while handling query generation and relationship loading. Apply when building Java applications that interact with relational databases, especially those with complex entity graphs or polymorphic structures. The framework eliminates boilerplate SQL while preserving flexibility through custom queries and lazy loading strategies.

methodologies.name: java-junit-testing
methodologies.description: JUnit is the standard testing framework for Java, providing annotations, assertions, and fixtures for unit and integration testing with excellent IDE and build-tool support. Use when writing tests for Java applications where you want expressive test methods, parameterized testing, and clean test organization. The framework's extension model supports custom test lifecycle management and conditional execution.

methodologies.name: java-spring
methodologies.description: Spring Framework provides comprehensive dependency injection, aspect-oriented programming, and integration with persistence, messaging, and web layers for building enterprise Java applications. Apply when building backend services, microservices, or APIs where you want to separate cross-cutting concerns and declaratively configure component interaction. Spring's annotation-driven approach reduces boilerplate while enabling powerful metaprogramming.

methodologies.name: java-spring-async
methodologies.description: Spring's async capabilities enable non-blocking, concurrent processing through @Async methods, CompletableFuture composition, and configurable thread pool management. Use when background tasks, external API calls, or CPU-intensive operations shouldn't block the main request thread, or when orchestrating multiple concurrent operations. This approach significantly improves throughput under load.

methodologies.name: java-spring-boot
methodologies.description: Spring Boot provides opinionated auto-configuration and embedded application servers, dramatically reducing setup overhead while maintaining Spring's power and flexibility. Apply when building production-ready microservices, REST APIs, or standalone applications where you want convention over configuration. The framework includes built-in actuator endpoints, health checks, and metrics for operational observability.

methodologies.name: java-spring-boot-patterns
methodologies.description: Spring Boot Patterns combine clean architecture, domain-driven design, and Spring idioms—such as repositories, specifications, and event publishing—for maintainable enterprise systems. Use when structuring layered applications with separation of concerns, complex query requirements, or event-driven domains. These patterns leverage Spring's ecosystem while keeping business logic decoupled from infrastructure.

methodologies.name: laravel-patterns
methodologies.description: Laravel Patterns apply clean architecture and modern PHP practices using Laravel's service container, Eloquent ORM, and middleware pipeline to build maintainable full-stack applications. Use when building web applications or APIs where you want fast development cycles, elegant syntax, and integrated tooling for testing, migrations, and queuing. Laravel's conventions reduce decisions while its services allow flexibility.

methodologies.name: microservices-circuit-breaker
methodologies.description: Prevents cascading failures by wrapping every outbound dependency call in a three-state machine — CLOSED (normal), OPEN (fast-fail without calling), HALF_OPEN (limited recovery probes) — turning slow failures from connection-pool exhaustion into immediate CircuitOpenError responses. Apply it to every outbound HTTP or gRPC call between microservices, calls to third-party APIs without availability SLOs, and message consumers whose handlers call downstream services. Each breaker requires explicit configuration of failure_threshold, recovery_timeout, and half_open_max_calls so failure policy is auditable rather than implicit. After the recovery_timeout, a small number of probe calls determine whether the circuit closes or reopens, enabling automatic recovery without operator intervention. Skip it for in-process domain calls, idempotent writes to a service's primary DB (use retries with backoff), and flows where every failure must be surfaced immediately. The value is bounded blast radius when one dependency degrades and clean recovery without manual restarts.

methodologies.name: microservices-inter-service-comm
methodologies.description: Disambiguates the synchronous-versus-asynchronous decision that drives most distributed-monolith failures: HTTP/gRPC for queries that need an immediate single-hop response, message bus for mutations that span services or events that fan out, and never sync chains deeper than two hops inside a request handler. Apply it when designing new service-to-service interactions, breaking up a monolith, or refactoring chains that have started to couple availability across services. Every synchronous client must declare an explicit timeout (e.g., httpx timeout=10.0) so a slow dependency cannot stall callers and exhaust pools, and service addresses must come from discovery (Consul, Kubernetes DNS, ECS) rather than hardcoded config so services scale, move, and restart without consumer reconfiguration. Async messaging is preferred for mutations and events; sync is allowed only when proven necessary and the dependency is one hop away. The value is decoupled availability, predictable failure modes, and chains that survive partial outages.

methodologies.name: microservices-observability
methodologies.description: Treats distributed tracing as the primary debugging interface and a schema registry as the contract gate that keeps event payloads from becoming silent breaking-change vectors. Apply it to any production microservices system where 'which service caused this error?' is a real question, any system with more than two services exchanging events, and CI pipelines that need automated boundary-violation detection. Every inbound request opens a span and every outbound HTTP call or bus publish propagates the W3C traceparent header — a service that drops the header breaks tracing for everything downstream. OpenTelemetry SDK plus a single shared tracing helper avoids per-service drift, trace IDs are injected into every WARN/ERROR log line, and event classes register in Apicurio, Confluent, or Buf with BACKWARD compatibility enforced as a CI gate (with 90-day deprecation windows). A boundary lint script catches sync chains, missing timeouts, and shared-DB imports before merge. The value is mean-time-to-diagnose measured in minutes instead of hours.

methodologies.name: microservices-saga-pattern
methodologies.description: Replaces unavailable distributed ACID transactions with a sequence of local transactions whose failures trigger paired compensation steps in reverse order, achieving eventual all-or-nothing semantics across services without 2PC. Apply it for multi-service workflows requiring all-or-nothing semantics (create order → reserve inventory → process payment), long-running business processes where holding locks is impractical, and workflows orchestrated by Temporal, Cadence, or Camunda. The hard rule is that every step must have a paired idempotent compensation transaction (half-compensated sagas leave inventory reserved with payment refunded and no order cancellation, blocking items indefinitely), saga state must be persisted and observable through STARTED/PROCESSING/COMPLETED/COMPENSATING/FAILED transitions logged with saga ID and step name, and compensations must tolerate retry because orchestrators crash mid-flow. Skip it for single-service operations, sub-millisecond latency budgets, and flows where the domain has no meaningful compensation (you cannot unsend an email). The value is data integrity in distributed workflows without distributed locks.

methodologies.name: microservices-service-boundaries
methodologies.description: Forces service boundaries to be drawn along bounded contexts and data ownership rather than layers, convenience, or team politics, which is what determines whether a microservices effort delivers independent deployability or recreates the monolith with extra latency. Apply it when designing new services, breaking up a monolith with clearly load-bearing seams, supporting three-or-more teams that map cleanly to bounded contexts, or needing blast-radius isolation for PCI/PHI/regional compliance. Each service owns its tables (no cross-service SQL, no shared ORM models, no infrastructure imports across boundaries), exposes one API surface, has high internal cohesion, and assumes every outbound call will fail. Explicitly avoid microservices for solo or two-person teams, pre-PMF products with unstable domain boundaries, sub-millisecond synchronous workloads, and 'microservices for resume' situations where a modular monolith delivers most of the benefit. The value is clean independent deployability and the absence of hidden monoliths formed by shared databases.

methodologies.name: php-eloquent
methodologies.description: Eloquent is Laravel's expressive ORM, providing query builders, eager loading, relationships, and accessors/mutators for database interaction through elegant PHP syntax. Use when building Laravel applications requiring complex queries, relationships, or denormalized data without hand-writing SQL. The query builder API is composable, making complex queries maintainable.

methodologies.name: php-laravel
methodologies.description: Laravel is a modern PHP framework emphasizing developer happiness, rapid development, and full-stack capabilities from routing through database access and queue processing. Apply when building web applications or APIs where you want expressive, maintainable code with excellent testing and deployment tooling. The extensive ecosystem provides packages for most common tasks.

methodologies.name: php-laravel-patterns
methodologies.description: Laravel Patterns extend the framework with repositories, service layers, DTOs, and form requests to organize business logic and maintain clean separation of concerns. Use when scaling Laravel applications beyond simple CRUD, or when teams need consistent patterns for handling complex domains and validation rules. These patterns prevent controllers from becoming bloated and make testing easier.

methodologies.name: php-laravel-queues
methodologies.description: Laravel Queues enable asynchronous job processing through multiple backends (database, Redis, SQS), with retry logic, rate limiting, and batch processing capabilities. Use when tasks like sending emails, processing uploads, or heavy computations shouldn't block HTTP responses, or when you need to handle traffic spikes gracefully. The job system includes middleware for concurrency control and failure handling.

methodologies.name: php-phpunit-testing
methodologies.description: PHPUnit is PHP's standard testing framework, providing assertions, test fixtures, mocking, and code coverage analysis for unit and integration testing of PHP applications. Use when testing Laravel or other PHP applications where you need expressive test syntax, isolated test execution, and detailed failure reporting. The framework integrates with IDEs and CI systems for fast feedback loops.

methodologies.name: ruby-activerecord
methodologies.description: ActiveRecord is Ruby on Rails' ORM providing query objects, scopes, and associations that make database interaction intuitive while maintaining SQL power when needed. Use when building Rails applications or standalone projects where you want readable, composable queries without sacrificing performance or flexibility. The pattern naturally leads to domain-driven models with business logic.

methodologies.name: ruby-rails
methodologies.description: Establish Rails applications with structured, scalable patterns using service objects for business logic encapsulation, query objects for efficient database access, and proper transaction handling. Apply this when building production Rails applications that need to grow beyond simple CRUD operations—service objects separate concerns while maintaining transactions, and query objects provide a clean abstraction for complex database interactions. The value is code that scales with team size, remains testable, and accommodates changing business requirements without database query sprawl.

methodologies.name: ruby-rails-patterns
methodologies.description: Leverage Rails architectural patterns—service objects, query objects, and callback systems—to structure applications for maintainability and scalability. Use when designing backend systems that handle multiple responsibilities (user creation, emails, auditing) in a single flow while keeping code DRY and testable. Service objects provide explicit transaction boundaries, query objects create reusable query logic, and scopes enable semantic database access, reducing the likelihood of N+1 bugs and making the codebase easier to navigate as it grows.

methodologies.name: ruby-rspec-testing
methodologies.description: Write comprehensive, maintainable test suites using RSpec with proper test structure—model specs for validations and associations, service specs for business logic, and context blocks for grouping related test cases. Implement this when building Rails applications requiring confidence in correctness; model specs verify data integrity, service specs test the actual business logic in isolation, and proper setup with factories ensures tests are fast and non-flaky. The value delivered is early detection of regressions, refactoring confidence, and clear documentation of system behavior through well-written tests.

methodologies.name: ruby-sidekiq-jobs
methodologies.description: Process long-running tasks asynchronously using Sidekiq with proper job structure, retry strategies, and error handling to prevent blocking requests and improve user experience. Use this when handling orders, sending emails, exporting data, or any operation that doesn't need to complete immediately—Sidekiq enqueues jobs, workers process them in background, and exponential backoff prevents hammering external services during failures. The benefit is responsive applications that don't timeout, graceful degradation when services are down, and the ability to retry failures with intelligent backoff strategies.

domains.description: CI/CD pipeline management covering GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, and GitOps with ArgoCD. Includes observability (Prometheus, Grafana, ELK Stack, AIOps), security (secrets management, SSL/TLS, security as code, nginx, load balancing), backup strategies, FinOps cost optimization, and DORA metrics.

methodologies.name: aiops
methodologies.description: Automate IT operations through AI-powered anomaly detection, automated remediation, and predictive analytics to reduce manual incident response and achieve proactive system healing. Apply AIOps when managing complex infrastructure at scale where manual monitoring isn't feasible—multi-signal anomaly detection prevents alert fatigue, auto-remediation (with human approval gates) restores services in seconds, and predictive analytics prevent incidents before user impact. The delivery is faster incident resolution (MTTR improvements of 50%+), fewer escalations, and cultural shift from reactive firefighting to proactive system management.

methodologies.name: argocd-gitops
methodologies.description: Implement declarative infrastructure and application deployments using Git as the single source of truth with ArgoCD continuously reconciling cluster state. Deploy this when managing Kubernetes clusters where consistency and auditability matter—Git diffs become deployment diffs, drift detection auto-corrects configuration deviations, and rollbacks are just Git reverts. The value is reliable deployments, complete audit trails, self-healing clusters, and the ability to review all infrastructure changes as code diffs before apply.

methodologies.name: azure-app-gateway-waf
methodologies.description: Deploys Azure Application Gateway v2 (WAF_v2 SKU) as a regional Layer 7 load balancer with integrated OWASP 3.2 web application firewall, SSL termination, and Key Vault certificate integration, removing the need for backends (AKS, App Service, VMs) to handle TLS or WAF themselves. Apply it for regional Layer 7 fronting, SSL offload with Key Vault-managed certificates, OWASP Top 10 protection on internet-facing or corporate-facing apps, URL path-based routing, and chaining with Front Door Premium via Private Link for global plus regional WAF layering. Critical operational rules: the dedicated subnet needs a /26 minimum (recommended /24) with a 2025-required delegation to Microsoft.Network/applicationGateways or Terraform fails at apply, WAF must start in Detection mode for one week to surface false positives via ApplicationGatewayFirewallLog before switching to Prevention, and certificates must be referenced by key_vault_secret_id with the gateway's managed identity granted get and list on Key Vault (direct PFX uploads cause silent renewal drift). Skip for pure TCP/UDP, global multi-region (use Front Door), and cost-sensitive POCs. The value is one auditable Terraform-defined edge that handles TLS, WAF, and routing without per-app reinvention.

methodologies.name: azure-compute
methodologies.description: Select and configure appropriate Azure compute services (VMs, VMSS, AKS, Container Apps, App Service) based on workload requirements, scaling needs, and operational complexity. Use this framework when architecting Azure infrastructure to make informed decisions—VMs offer full OS control but require management, AKS provides Kubernetes sophistication for complex microservices, Container Apps reduces operational overhead with serverless scaling to zero, and App Service simplifies deployment for standard web apps. The value is right-sized infrastructure that balances cost, control, and operational burden against actual workload needs.

methodologies.name: azure-front-door
methodologies.description: Azure Front Door Premium addresses global Layer 7 traffic distribution, edge CDN caching, DDoS mitigation, and outer-tier WAF in a single managed service, removing the need for DNS-based load balancing or Traffic Manager workarounds. Apply it for multi-region applications that need health-aware failover, for the Front Door + Application Gateway two-tier enterprise pattern, and whenever origins must remain private (Premium tier with Private Link keeps traffic on the Azure backbone). The methodology delivers Terraform recipes for origin groups, routes, managed certificates, WAF policies (DefaultRuleSet 2.1 + BotManagerRuleSet 1.0) and the critical NSG lock-down to the AzureFrontDoor.Backend service tag that prevents WAF bypass. It also flags the 5-15 minute global config propagation so deploy pipelines budget for it and avoids Front Door in dev/test where iteration speed matters more than edge presence.

methodologies.name: azure-nat-gateway
methodologies.description: Azure NAT Gateway provides predictable, zone-redundant outbound internet connectivity for private subnets, replacing the older Load Balancer outbound rules whose silent SNAT port exhaustion produces random timeouts that look like application bugs. Use it whenever a private subnet pulls container images, hits external APIs, or must present a stable egress IP for third-party allowlisting, and on AKS node pools that need bootstrap egress; skip it on Virtual WAN spokes or subnets already routed through Azure Firewall to avoid competing default routes. The methodology covers SNAT capacity sizing (each public IP gives ~64,000 ports, add one IP per 50,000 expected concurrent connections, max 16 IPs), the mandatory Standard SKU + Static allocation + zone-redundant zones ["1","2","3"] combination, and idle timeout tuning between 4 and 15 minutes. It also enforces SNATConnectionCount and DroppedPackets metric alerts because exhaustion is otherwise undiagnosable in production.

methodologies.name: azure-nsg-rules
methodologies.description: Azure Network Security Groups provide stateful 5-tuple subnet and NIC-level filtering and are the primary east-west traffic gate in Azure, but Azure's invisible default rules allow VNet-to-VNet and outbound internet — making explicit deny-by-default essential. Apply this methodology to every production subnet (except GatewaySubnet and AzureFirewallSubnet), to enforce zero-trust between web/app/data tiers, and whenever HIPAA/PCI/SOC2 requires segmentation evidence. It mandates an explicit DenyAllInbound rule at priority 4096, exact source/destination CIDRs or service tags on every allow rule, version-2 flow logs with Traffic Analytics shipped to Log Analytics for 30+ days, and a deterministic naming convention (`{Allow|Deny}{Direction}{Protocol}{Description}`). Concrete Terraform examples cover three-tier topologies and warn about regional-only service tags, missing UDP/443 for HTTP/3, and the inline-vs-association NSG attachment conflict that produces flapping connectivity.

methodologies.name: azure-private-link
methodologies.description: Azure Private Link replaces public PaaS endpoints with a private NIC injected into your VNet, eliminating the data-exfiltration risk that Service Endpoints leave open and keeping all traffic on the Azure backbone. Apply it for Storage, Key Vault, SQL, PostgreSQL, ACR, and App Service in production and any environment under HIPAA/PCI/SOC2 that demands private connectivity, and skip it in dev/test where Private DNS complexity costs more than it saves. The most critical rule is that every Private Endpoint requires a matching `privatelink.*` Private DNS zone linked to every consumer VNet with `registration_enabled = false`; without the link, DNS silently resolves to the public IP and the connection works but defeats the entire isolation model. The methodology supplies the full zone-name table (blob, file, vault, postgres, azurecr.io, etc.) and Terraform patterns covering `public_network_access_enabled = false`, the Private Endpoint resource, and validation steps from inside the VNet.

methodologies.name: azure-vnet-hub-spoke
methodologies.description: VNets are Azure's mandatory isolation boundary and the hub-spoke topology centralises shared services (Firewall, Bastion, DNS, VPN/ExpressRoute Gateway) so workload spokes can be added without duplicating infrastructure or policy. Apply it when designing a landing zone, when multiple teams share networking primitives, or when on-prem connectivity and east-west inspection are required, and avoid the overhead in single-team flat-VNet workloads or throwaway POCs. The methodology insists on a non-overlapping CIDR plan written down before any Terraform — typically /16 per VNet and /24 per subnet with 50% headroom and dedicated ranges for AKS pod CIDRs — and on subnet delegations for App Gateway, AKS, and PostgreSQL Flex (App Gateway v2 deploys fail without delegation since 2025). It also calls out the non-transitive nature of VNet peering, which forces a UDR through Azure Firewall to allow spoke-to-spoke traffic that diagrams routinely assume already works.

methodologies.name: backup-basics
methodologies.description: Establish foundational backup strategies using the 3-2-1 rule (three copies, two media types, one offsite) to ensure data protection, business continuity, and compliance with recovery objectives. Implement this when protecting critical data systems—identify RPO/RTO needs, choose backup types (full/incremental/snapshot), and distribute copies across storage systems and locations to withstand single-point failures. The value is measurable disaster recovery capability, compliance adherence (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2), and confidence that data can be recovered within acceptable time/data-loss thresholds.

methodologies.name: backup-cloud-aws
methodologies.description: Cloud-native AWS backup centralises RDS, DynamoDB, EBS, EFS, S3, and FSx snapshots through AWS Backup vaults and plans, with cross-region copy rules and Vault Lock to satisfy immutable-retention compliance, and pairs them with S3 lifecycle rules that tier objects through Standard, Standard-IA, Glacier, and Deep Archive for 70-90% storage savings on long retention. Apply it to consolidate fragmented per-service backup scripts into Terraform-managed plans, to enable ransomware-resistant Object Lock, and to tier existing backup buckets that have never left Standard; do not use it for on-prem workloads (use Restic/Borg) or Kubernetes state (use Velero, which understands K8s resources beyond the underlying EBS). The methodology codifies the minimum viable backup-bucket configuration: versioning enabled, all four public-access blocks set, KMS server-side encryption, and at least one transition rule after 30 days, all expressible as Terraform IaC.

methodologies.name: backup-database-mysql-mongo
methodologies.description: This methodology covers the two MySQL/MariaDB backup paths (mysqldump for portable logical exports and Percona XtraBackup for hot physical backups of InnoDB) plus MongoDB backups via mongodump with oplog capture for replica-set point-in-time consistency. Apply it when picking a backup strategy for a database that cannot tolerate write locking, when migrating between environments via portable SQL, when MySQL volumes exceed 50 GB and mysqldump windows become unacceptable, or when a MongoDB replica set needs cross-collection consistency; skip it for fully-managed RDS/Atlas where the provider already runs automated backups. The rules enforce `--single-transaction --routines --triggers` on InnoDB mysqldump runs (omitting routines/triggers silently produces backups that fail application startup) and `mongodump --oplog` on replica sets, because without the oplog a busy dump captures collections at different timestamps and restore is silently inconsistent.

methodologies.name: backup-database-postgres
methodologies.description: PostgreSQL has two non-interchangeable backup surfaces — logical (pg_dump/pg_dumpall) and physical (pg_basebackup, pgBackRest) — and only physical backups combined with WAL archiving enable point-in-time recovery, the only remedy against logical corruption that replicates to standbys instantly. Apply this methodology when building backup pipelines with offsite copies to S3, implementing PITR, migrating databases between environments via custom-format dumps, or scaling to large volumes where pgBackRest's parallel and incremental backups become essential; managed PaaS like RDS/Cloud SQL already covers most use cases unless cross-account copies are needed. The single most important rule is to verify every dump with `pg_restore -l` before uploading because more than 50% of "successful" backups fail at restore time; the integrity check costs under a second and catches truncations from disk-full or interrupted streams that otherwise upload silently.

methodologies.name: backup-filesystem-restic
methodologies.description: Restic and BorgBackup deliver encrypted, deduplicated, integrity-checked filesystem backups to cloud object storage (S3, B2, Wasabi) or SSH targets, solving the three problems that plain rsync ignores: encryption at source, chunk-level dedup, and built-in repository verification. Apply them to back up `/etc`, `/home`, `/var/www`, application data, and database export files to offsite storage, especially in multi-server fleets that share a central Borg or Restic REST server; do not use them for Kubernetes PVs (use Velero) or to back up live database files (run pg_dump/XtraBackup/mongodump first). The methodology mandates loading `RESTIC_PASSWORD` from a secrets manager (never CLI or repo) because a lost passphrase makes the repository permanently unreadable, and weekly `restic check --read-data-subset=5%` runs to detect silent pack-file corruption before a restore is needed; systemd timers and forget+prune retention close the loop.

methodologies.name: backup-kubernetes-velero
methodologies.description: Velero captures both Kubernetes resource state (Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, PVCs) and underlying volume data through a single Schedule CRD, using CSI VolumeSnapshots when the storage driver supports them and Kopia as the file-system backup mover otherwise. Apply it to clusters running stateful workloads that need namespace or cluster-level restore, to multi-namespace apps, to DR drills that restore into a different cluster or region, and to live cluster-to-cluster migrations; skip it for pure stateless workloads (rebuild from Git/CI/CD) and for operator-managed databases where the operator's native tool is more accurate. The methodology emphasises the Velero v1.15+ breaking change: `--default-volumes-to-restic` is deprecated and must be replaced with `--default-volumes-to-fs-backup` plus the Kopia data mover and `--use-node-agent`, a trap that older training data and many tutorials still get wrong.

methodologies.name: backup-verification-dr
methodologies.description: Backup verification, monitoring, and DR runbooks address the documented reality that more than 50% of "successful" backups fail at restore time — exit-code-zero only proves the write phase, not the recoverability of the data. Apply this methodology to every production backup pipeline as a non-optional layer: schedule restore-to-isolated-target probes with quantifiable integrity signals (table count, row count, schema diff, file hash, restic check), wire Prometheus alerts for backup age, size deltas, and duration spikes (a 10x size jump or near-zero size never shows up as a job failure), and run quarterly full DR drills to keep RTO/RPO honest. Verification must run on its own schedule and a failure must page on its own — never restore into the production target — so corruption, truncation, and logical drift are caught before the crisis rather than during it.

methodologies.name: cicd-cert-rotation-pipeline
methodologies.description: This methodology treats certificate rotation as a declarative IaC pipeline rather than manual toil, covering cert-manager CRDs for Kubernetes, Terraform-managed AWS ACM and GCP Certificate Manager for cloud-issued certs, and Traefik with the ACME resolver for Docker Compose stacks. Apply it whenever you need automated issuance and rotation as part of an infrastructure apply step, are migrating to short-lived certificates (LE 6-day, upcoming 45-day), or are setting up a new Ingress/Gateway API; skip it for service-mesh mTLS (Linkerd/Istio/Cilium handle their own identity rotation) and for bare-metal hosts that should use cicd-tls-renewal-automation instead. The non-negotiable rule for cert-manager is `privateKey.rotationPolicy: Always`, plus exact `secretName` parity between Certificate spec and Ingress reference because a mismatch produces no error and silently keeps the old cert; private keys never enter Git — load via SOPS, Vault, or External Secrets.

methodologies.name: cicd-mtls-deployment
methodologies.description: Mutual TLS authenticates services at the transport layer using internal-CA-issued client certificates, and this methodology covers the two characteristic failure modes at scale — shared long-lived certs that require mass rotation on compromise, and CRL/OCSP revocation that is operationally expensive and often disabled. Apply it for service-to-service auth in zero-trust architectures, for nginx/HAProxy mTLS termination that forwards client identity to backends, and for programmatic short-lived cert issuance from CI/CD using step-ca or Vault PKI; do not hand-roll on top of Linkerd/Istio/Cilium meshes which already auto-rotate per-pod identities, and do not use mTLS for browser-facing client auth where cert distribution is impractical. The core rule is that internal CAs must issue 24h-7d certs over an ACME or API endpoint so expiry replaces revocation as the security boundary, eliminating the soft-fail OCSP problem entirely.

methodologies.name: cicd-tls-renewal-automation
methodologies.description: Automated TLS renewal addresses the number-one cause of TLS outages: certificates that expire because a manual step in the renewal loop was skipped, throttled by ACME rate limits, or broken by a DNS-01 path mismatch. Apply this methodology for any public web/API endpoint, when adopting short-lived (LE 6-day or 45-day) certs where automation is structurally mandatory, when configuring post-renewal nginx/apache/traefik reload hooks, and when wiring ACME Renewal Information (ARI) checks into a CI/CD pipeline. The methodology helps select the right ACME client per environment — certbot for nginx/apache on Ubuntu/Debian, acme.sh for DNS-01 wildcards or minimal-dependency setups, lego for Go pipelines, step-ca for self-hosted CAs — and forbids mixing clients on the same host because competing renewals corrupt the cert store. Operational posture: alerts at 21 days, pages at 7 days, manual touch only in emergencies.

methodologies.name: cicd-tls-validation-gate
methodologies.description: TLS validation gates run testssl.sh, sslyze, and SSL Labs against staging endpoints in CI as a binary pass/fail check before promoting to production, catching cipher regressions (CBC, RC4, 3DES copied from old guides), broken chains, weak protocols, OCSP failures, and HSTS misconfigurations that can permanently lock out a preloaded subdomain. Apply it on every deploy that provisions or modifies TLS configuration, after each cert rotation as the completion criterion, on every nginx/HAProxy/Envoy upgrade to confirm config survived, on new domain launches, and quarterly to catch protocol drift; do not use it for self-signed dev environments or as a pre-commit hook because it requires a live endpoint. The mandatory gate is `testssl.sh --severity HIGH --jsonfile output.json` returning no HIGH/CRITICAL findings, and Certificate Transparency log monitoring detects rogue issuance outside the pipeline entirely.

methodologies.name: docker-optimization
methodologies.description: Reduce Docker image size, build time, and attack surface by selecting appropriate base images (Distroless/Chainguard for security, Alpine for minimalism), using multi-stage builds, and enabling BuildKit cache mounts. Apply when containerizing applications where image size directly impacts pull time, storage cost, and security exposure—slim/Alpine base images cut sizes 70-80%, multi-stage builds eliminate build artifacts from production, and BuildKit cache mounts speed incremental builds. The benefit is faster container pulls, smaller registry storage, reduced CVE surface area, and improved build pipelines that scale better under load.

methodologies.name: dora-metrics
methodologies.description: Measure and improve software delivery performance using DORA metrics (deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, MTTR) to establish baselines and drive continuous improvement. Implement this when optimizing team velocity and reliability—DORA metrics correlate strongly with organizational performance, showing that frequent small deployments with low change failure rates predict business outcomes. The value is data-driven insights into pipeline bottlenecks, visibility into whether improvements actually work, and alignment between engineering improvements and business outcomes rather than vanity metrics.

methodologies.name: elk-stack-logging
methodologies.description: Aggregate, search, and visualize logs at scale using Elasticsearch for storage, Logstash/Beats for collection, and Kibana for exploration to enable troubleshooting, security analysis, and compliance. Deploy this when centralizing logs from many sources—applications write to stdout, Beats collect and ship, Elasticsearch indexes for fast search, Kibana provides dashboards and alerting. The value is rapid root-cause analysis (correlate logs across services), security incident investigation (audit trails), and operational dashboards that make system behavior transparent.

methodologies.name: fco-commitment-pricing
methodologies.description: Reserved Instances and Savings Plans trade flexibility for 60-75% discounts versus on-demand by pre-committing to 1-3 year capacity, and this methodology shows when and how to do it without the over-commitment trap. Apply it after rightsizing (always rightsize first) for production databases, app servers, and analytics clusters running 24/7 with at least 6 months of stable usage, and especially when on-demand spend exceeds $5,000/month — the typical 1-year RI break-even is 7-9 months. The core rule is to start at 70-80% of minimum observed baseline and prefer Convertible RIs for flexibility (Standard RIs are locked and can only be sold via the Marketplace); for AWS, Compute Savings Plans express commitment as dollars per hour and apply across EC2/Lambda/Fargate. Avoid commitments on bursty workloads, on systems planned for decommission inside the term, and on architectures that are still actively evolving.

methodologies.name: fco-cost-allocation
methodologies.description: Cost allocation makes cloud spend visible per team, service, environment, and cost centre via a mandatory tag taxonomy enforced at resource creation time — without it, the "tragedy of the commons" stalls optimization in politics because no one feels responsible for the shared bill. Apply it in any multi-team account, before rolling out chargeback or showback, when monthly spend exceeds $50,000 with no team-level breakdown, and when wiring budget alerts that need to notify the right owner; skip the overhead for single-owner accounts and short-lived R&D scratch accounts (use TTL instead). The rule is that all resources must carry environment, team, service, and cost-center tags, with creation blocked via AWS SCP, Azure Policy, or GCP Organization Policy when any required tag is absent — retroactive tagging tops out at 60-70% coverage and warning-only enforcement at 40-60%, while creation-time blocking achieves 98%+. Real outcome: a 300-person engineering org cut total spend by 20% in 6 months purely from visibility.

methodologies.name: fco-rightsizing
methodologies.description: Rightsizing matches instance sizes to actual workload demand, attacking the largest single source of cloud waste — overprovisioned compute running at 10-15% utilization for months because someone upgraded "just in case." Apply it to any environment with 4+ weeks of monitoring data that has not been rightsized this quarter, after lift-and-shift migrations (commonly oversized due to conservatism), and always before purchasing Reserved Instances so the commitment lands on the correct size; skip instances with spiky CPU above 70% peaks or latency-sensitive services that need 5-10% headroom validated by load testing. CPU/memory rightsizing typically yields 20-40% savings, Graviton/ARM migration adds 20-40% on top with latency improvements, and AMD migration adds 10-20% versus Intel. The mandatory discipline is 4 weeks of data plus 95th-percentile peak (not just averages), non-prod first with a 48-hour validation window, then prod.

methodologies.name: fco-spot-instances
methodologies.description: Spot/Preemptible instances expose unused cloud capacity at 70-90% discounts in exchange for a 2-minute reclaim notice, making them the right answer for fault-tolerant workloads — CI/CD, batch and ETL, ML training with checkpointing, rendering, and stateless scale-out — but a wrong answer for production databases and any service that cannot survive sudden node loss. Apply this methodology to convert long-running compute that already tolerates restart, especially ML training where a documented case dropped a $120k/month bill to $38k/month (68%) just by adding S3 checkpointing and running 85% on Spot. Four architecture requirements are all mandatory or none of the savings holds: a graceful 2-minute shutdown handler, application checkpointing for jobs longer than 10 minutes, automatic on-demand fallback after 10 minutes of unavailability, and instance diversification across at least 3 instance families and 2 AZs to survive concurrent capacity claims.

methodologies.name: fco-waste-elimination
methodologies.description: Waste elimination removes idle and orphaned resources that generate cost with zero value — unattached EBS volumes, orphaned snapshots, stopped instances, unassociated Elastic IPs, idle load balancers — and schedules dev/staging/QA environments to stop outside business hours for 65-70% non-prod savings. Apply it as the first FinOps action in any environment because the risk is near zero and the ROI is immediate, before any quarterly FinOps review so analysis lands on real spend, and on any environment that has not been audited in the past 30 days. The methodology supplies AWS CLI recipes to find each waste category and warns to always run a dry-run audit before automated deletion and to define explicit exceptions (on-call, demo, overnight jobs) before turning on schedules. A documented case cut combined non-prod from $90k/month to $28.2k/month (69%) with no engineering effort beyond initial setup.

methodologies.name: finops-agentic-workflow
methodologies.description: Solves the slow cadence of monthly FinOps reviews by running a continuous loop of AI agents that ingest billing data, audit tags, hunt idle resources, and recommend Reserved Instance or Savings Plan adjustments. Apply it once cloud spend exceeds roughly $10k/month, multiple teams ship independently, or commitment renewals require analysis of 30-90 day usage patterns that humans find tedious. The practical value is catching anomalies within hours instead of next invoice while keeping safety: detection agents stay read-only, remediation agents run only on human-approved tickets, and irreversible actions like termination, RI/SP changes, or instance-family swaps always pass through a human-in-loop checkpoint. It also catalogs the specific failure modes (CPU-only idle classification, spot recommendations without checkpointing, anomaly detection on under 60 days of history) that agents otherwise reproduce by default.

methodologies.name: finops-ai-ml-costs
methodologies.description: Addresses the unique cost shape of AI/ML workloads, where GPU compute runs 5-10x more than standard compute and instance-hour dashboards mask the real driver — token economics and per-request cost. Apply it to any GPU workload, training above $10k/month, inference with repetitive queries, or LLM API spend that is growing with no per-model tracking. The practical levers are spot/preemptible training with SIGTERM-driven checkpointing every 10 minutes (70-90% discount, less than 10 minutes of lost work on interruption), Redis-based deterministic-key response caching aiming for 20-60% hit rate, INT8/FP16 quantization for ~2x throughput at half the cost, and request-batching to saturate GPUs. It also requires tracking cost per training run, per 1k input/output tokens, per experiment, and cache hit rate, plus AI-specific alerts separate from general infra anomaly detection because token counts vary 10x per request.

methodologies.name: finops-cost-visibility
methodologies.description: Solves the foundational problem that no FinOps optimization works reliably without 95%+ tag coverage and trustworthy cost allocation — without it, every report is an estimate dressed as a fact. Apply it as the first phase of any FinOps practice, when tag compliance is below 95%, when no per-team or per-product breakdown exists, or before introducing chargeback. The practical work is enforcing four mandatory tag keys (project, environment, owner, cost-center) at resource creation via SCP/Organization Policy/Azure Policy rather than documentation alone, configuring cost allocation tags and detailed billing exports (CUR to Athena, BigQuery export), and standing up two dashboard tiers — executive KPIs plus per-team service breakdowns with absolute-dollar plus relative-percent anomaly alerts. Crucially, it sequences showback (visibility) before chargeback (financial consequence) so engineering teams can validate attribution accuracy before money moves.

methodologies.name: finops-framework
methodologies.description: Frames FinOps as a cultural practice, not a tool — bringing financial accountability to cloud spending through cross-functional collaboration between Engineering, Finance, and Product, structured as INFORM (visibility) → OPTIMIZE (efficiency) → OPERATE (governance). Apply it when cloud bills exceed $5k/month with 20%+ MoM growth and no per-team visibility, when commitment renewals need data-driven sizing, or in pre-IPO phases requiring credible unit economics ($/customer, $/transaction, $/inference). The practical value comes from market-grounded targets: 32% of $723.4B annual cloud spend is wasted, 17% average budget overrun, and only 23% of organizations describe themselves as highly efficient. The framework defines team composition, weekly/monthly/quarterly/annual review cadence, the canonical KPIs (cost per unit of work, GPU utilization, waste %, allocation accuracy, SP coverage 70-80%), and tool selection across native (Cost Optimization Hub, FinOps Hub, Cost Management) and third-party (CloudZero, Kubecost, Apptio, Infracost) options.

methodologies.name: finops-governance
methodologies.description: Solves the decay problem where one-time optimization erodes within weeks as teams ship new workloads — the OPERATE phase makes cost hygiene structural through automated policies, tiered budget alerts, scheduled dev/test shutdown, and Infracost-style cost gates in CI/CD. Apply it once INFORM is complete, when teams use IaC (Terraform/Pulumi/CloudFormation), when dev/test environments idle overnight, when budget overruns surface only on the monthly invoice, or when engineering decisions are made without a cost signal. The practical wins include 60-70% reduction on dev/test compute via timezone-aware auto-shutdown (the single highest-ROI FinOps automation), tiered budget alerts at 50%/75% forecast and 90%/100% actual routed to Slack on-call (not email), approval workflows for resources above per-environment thresholds ($500-$2k dev, $5k+ prod), and Infracost PR comments that block deploys exceeding 10-20% baseline increase without explicit justification.

methodologies.name: gha-caching-artifacts
methodologies.description: Solves the wall-clock waste of reinstalling dependencies on every CI run (30-120 seconds on most stacks dropping to under 5 seconds with a hit) and the conflation between caches and artifacts that produces stale dependency bugs or storage bill surprises. Apply it on any job that installs npm/pip/go/cargo/bundler dependencies, on Docker builds (use type=gha cache-from/cache-to with mode=max), and whenever build outputs need to flow between jobs (use artifacts, not cache). The practical rules are non-negotiable: cache keys must include hashFiles('**/lockfile') with restore-keys for partial hits, set retention-days: 7 on intermediate artifacts (reserve the 90-day default for releases), use actions/cache@v4 not v3 since the Feb 2025 service rewrite silently breaks v3, and embed matrix variables in artifact names to avoid overwrites. A cache miss is recoverable; an artifact-download failure is fatal — they are not interchangeable.

methodologies.name: gha-deployment-patterns
methodologies.description: Solves the typical CD failure pattern where ad-hoc deploy steps inlined into one monolithic workflow ship straight to production with no environment protection, race on concurrent deploys, and copy inconsistent security across repos. Apply it for any push-to-main that auto-deploys to staging with optional production approval, organizations with multiple service repos sharing a deploy target, release pipelines publishing npm/PyPI plus Docker on a tag, or workflows targeting more than two environments. The practical structure stages CD as build → staging (auto) → production (manual approval) backed by GitHub Environments with required reviewers, extracts shared deploy logic into reusable workflows (workflow_call) with explicit secrets (never secrets: inherit) and explicit timeout-minutes (15-30 tests, 60 builds, 120 deploys), and enforces cancel-in-progress: false on production concurrency groups so a new commit never kills an in-flight rollout. It also covers canary (10% traffic, metric gate, full rollout), blue-green, release-on-tag changelog generation, and SSH/Vercel deploy targets.

methodologies.name: gha-matrix-builds
methodologies.description: Solves cross-platform and cross-version regressions that ship undetected when CI tests only one OS/version combination, plus the inverse problem where the default fail-fast: true cancels every other axis as soon as one combination fails. Apply it to libraries that must support multiple language versions (Python 3.10/3.11/3.12, Node 18/20/22), multi-OS apps (Linux/Windows/macOS), monorepos with differing runtime requirements, and any project where a per-platform failure should not suppress others. The practical rules are setting fail-fast: false on every test matrix, using exclude to prune invalid combinations and include to attach extra variables (e.g., coverage: true) to one canonical run, embedding matrix variables in artifact names to avoid collisions, omitting max-parallel unless runner quota is constrained, and never using matrix for deploy or release jobs (those should target one specific artifact, not fan out). It also covers the build/combine pattern with merge-multiple: true downstream.

methodologies.name: gha-security-hardening
methodologies.description: Solves the supply-chain and credential-exposure failure modes that the March 2025 Tj-actions/changed-files incident made impossible to ignore — moveable tags executing attacker code with full GITHUB_TOKEN, pull_request_target leaking every secret to malicious fork PRs, and long-lived AWS/GCP/Azure keys persisting until manually rotated. Apply it on any workflow running on PRs or pushes to shared branches, anything that authenticates to a cloud provider, anything using third-party Marketplace actions, and any repo with required status checks. The practical mandates: SHA-pin every uses: to a 40-char commit (with a version comment) and let Renovate keep them current, set permissions: at job-level with minimum scopes (never write-all), replace long-lived cloud creds with OIDC (id-token: write plus configure-credentials), forbid pull_request_target on workflows that check out PR code, and avoid ${{ github.event.* }} interpolation in run: shells (use env: to dodge shell injection). Ship actionlint plus a custom gha-audit.py as required PR checks; the doc also catalogs the eight LLM-specific failure patterns agents reproduce when copying pre-2025 docs.

methodologies.name: gha-workflow-structure
methodologies.description: Solves the structural mistakes agents most commonly make in GHA — five sequential jobs that should be parallel, missing or excessive needs: edges, and concurrency groups that either pile up redundant PR builds or cancel in-flight production deploys. Apply it when structuring a new workflow, diagnosing slow CI, adding an environment with scoped secrets and approvals, or setting up scheduled maintenance. The practical pattern is to model the job DAG (job, needs[], trigger, runs-on, permissions, timeout) before writing YAML, use group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }} with cancel-in-progress: true for CI but group: deploy-${{ inputs.environment }} with cancel-in-progress: false for deploys, attach workflow_dispatch to every schedule for manual re-runs during incidents, run cron at low-traffic UTC, and use dorny/paths-filter for monorepos so unchanged packages are not rebuilt. It also covers GitHub-side prerequisites — branch protection, environments with reviewers, secrets, and Actions permissions restricting which actions can be used.

methodologies.name: github-actions-basics
methodologies.description: Set up CI/CD pipelines directly in GitHub using workflows triggered by pushes, pull requests, and schedules with matrix builds for multi-platform testing and integrated GitHub integration. Implement this when GitHub is your repository host and you need lightweight, integrated CI/CD without external infrastructure—workflows run on GitHub's free runners (public repos) or paid runners (private), matrix strategies test across OS/language versions in parallel, and built-in GitHub integration enables PR checks and release automation. The value is reduced operational overhead (no CI server to maintain), faster setup time, and native integration with GitHub issues, PRs, and releases.

methodologies.name: gitlab-cicd
methodologies.description: Define CI/CD pipelines in GitLab using .gitlab-ci.yml with stages, jobs, rules, and runners to enable complex workflows with integrated DevSecOps, container registry, and Kubernetes deployment capabilities. Apply when using GitLab for projects requiring sophisticated pipeline control—DAG pipelines with needs-based ordering enable parallelism, built-in SAST/DAST scanning add security, integrated container registry simplifies deployments, and review apps enable per-MR preview environments. The delivery is comprehensive CI/CD with security scanning built-in, efficient parallel execution, and reduced context-switching between tools.

methodologies.name: gitops-core-principles
methodologies.description: Solves manual-deployment inconsistency, configuration drift, and unaudited cluster changes by treating Git as the single source of truth — the four OpenGitOps principles plus pull-based reconciliation by an in-cluster operator that self-heals drift without distributing cluster credentials to external CI. Apply it when migrating Kubernetes/Helm/Kustomize workloads off kubectl-apply pipelines, managing 10+ clusters with PR-based environment promotion, working in compliance-heavy environments needing every cluster change tied to a Git commit, or separating CI from CD cleanly. The practical guidance covers the push vs pull vs hybrid decision, an ArgoCD vs Flux matrix anchored in 2025-2026 market data (ArgoCD ~60% share with built-in UI and lower learning curve, Flux ~25% with stronger multi-tenancy and lower footprint), a five-phase adoption path from assessment to multi-cluster ApplicationSets to self-service IDP, agent model routing per task type, and a catalog of LLM-specific gotchas (deprecated apiVersions, :latest tags, cross-env contamination, accidental SOPS commits).

methodologies.name: gitops-flux
methodologies.description: Provides Flux CD setup and operation as the lightweight, modular GitOps operator alternative to ArgoCD — CRD-driven (GitRepository, Kustomization, HelmRelease, ImagePolicy) with each controller (source/kustomize/helm/image/notification) deployable and sized independently. Apply it for platform-engineering teams that prefer CLI-first workflows, multi-tenant platforms needing namespace-scoped controllers, existing Flux deployments being maintained after the 2024 Weaveworks shutdown moved Flux fully to CNCF governance, resource-constrained clusters where ArgoCD's footprint is prohibitive, or Helm-heavy clusters using ImagePolicy + ImageUpdateAutomation for automated tag bumps. The practical wins are bootstrapping via flux CLI which commits its own manifests to Git, configuring webhooks from the Git provider for sub-second sync (instead of waiting on the poll interval), using dependsOn and prune: true on Kustomizations, setting ignoreDifferences for HPA-managed replicas to avoid reconciliation fights, and pinning exact Helm chart semver in production. Image automation is safe for dev only — production must go through PRs.

methodologies.name: gitops-progressive-delivery
methodologies.description: Extends GitOps from atomic replace-all-pods deployments to automated canary and blue-green rollouts with metric-driven rollback — Flagger or Argo Rollouts shifting traffic incrementally (10% steps to 50% max), analyzing success rate and latency at each step, and rolling back without human intervention when thresholds are breached. Apply it for high-traffic production services where bad deploys impact many users before detection, services with reliable Prometheus metrics that can serve as rollback signals, GitOps-adopted teams ready to add automated rollback, and blue-green switchovers needing zero-downtime control. Flagger integrates natively with ArgoCD/Flux but requires a traffic management layer (Istio, Linkerd, NGINX, Contour, App Mesh); Argo Rollouts is standalone with NGINX/Traefik/ALB/Istio support. The practical rules require at least two analysis metrics (success rate above 99% AND p99 latency below threshold — single metric misses regressions), conservative progressDeadlineSeconds of at least 3x interval x steps, synthetic load via webhook during analysis to make low-QPS windows statistically meaningful, and skipping it entirely for schema migrations that require both versions to share a database state.

methodologies.name: gitops-repository-structure
methodologies.description: Solves the unmanageability of GitOps config repos as environments and teams grow — specifically the merge-conflict and invisible-promotion-diff problems that branch-per-environment generates during hotfixes. Apply it when bootstrapping a new GitOps config repo, migrating from branches to a maintainable structure, onboarding a new team or app into an existing platform, or defining the dev → staging → prod promotion strategy. The practical pattern is folder-per-environment monorepo with Kustomize base/overlays (or Helm value files), separating the app repo from the config repo (or maintaining a clean /deploy boundary inside a monorepo), pinning exact Helm chart versions in every production overlay, validating manifests in CI on every PR with kustomize build/helm template + kubeconform + conftest/Kyverno, and structuring promotion as auto-PRs from CI for dev with human-gated PRs into prod overlays. The doc includes both single-team and multi-tenant tenant directory layouts, plus diff-reviewer and PR-author agent prompts that constrain working directories to prevent cross-env contamination.

methodologies.name: gitops-secrets-security
methodologies.description: Solves the unique GitOps secret-exposure problem — the cluster operator reads from Git directly, so any plaintext secret committed becomes visible to everyone with repo read access plus historical access. Apply it on any GitOps setup (it is mandatory, not optional), choosing between SOPS for in-place YAML encryption with age/AWS KMS/GCP KMS keys, Sealed Secrets for cluster-scoped asymmetric encryption without external KMS, and External Secrets Operator for pulling from Vault/AWS Secrets Manager/GCP SM/Azure Key Vault without ever putting secret data in Git. The practical security posture also covers RBAC least-privilege on the operator service account (no cluster-admin), egress network policies restricting traffic to Git provider + registry + secret stores, audit-log alerts on ClusterRoleBinding creation and wildcard RBAC, CODEOWNERS enforcement for any RBAC-touching PR, per-environment SOPS keys (so staging keys cannot decrypt prod), and a catalog of agent-specific risks (PR-description leakage, accidental SOPS decryption, kubectl secret dumps, helm template --debug output, auto-merge bots combined with auto-sync). A drift-aware safe-sync helper script refuses replica decreases and RBAC widening before applying.

methodologies.name: grafana-basics
methodologies.description: Create operational dashboards and alerts in Grafana by connecting data sources (Prometheus, Loki, Elasticsearch), selecting appropriate visualizations, and leveraging variables for filtering to provide real-time system visibility. Use this when building observability for operations teams—time series panels track metrics over time, stat panels show current KPIs, heatmaps reveal latency distributions, and Loki panels aggregate logs all on one dashboard. The value is at-a-glance system health visibility, reduced mean-time-to-detect (problems visible before users complain), and the ability to correlate metrics/logs for rapid root-cause analysis.

methodologies.name: grafana-setup
methodologies.description: Deploy, provision, and scale Grafana for high availability using Kubernetes or Docker, configure data sources and dashboards as code, and implement dashboard-as-code patterns with Grafonnet or Terraform. Apply this when Grafana moves from dev/test to production—Kubernetes deployments enable HA with shared PostgreSQL backend, provisioning ensures consistent dashboards across environments, and dashboard-as-code prevents manual configuration drift. The value is production-grade observability infrastructure that survives failures, version-controlled dashboards that can be code-reviewed, and the ability to rapidly spin up new dashboards via templates.

methodologies.name: jenkins-basics
methodologies.description: Build CI/CD pipelines with Jenkins using Declarative Pipeline syntax, shared libraries for code reuse, and multi-branch/organization folders for auto-discovery of repositories and branches. Use this when managing complex pipelines with custom logic or integrating with legacy systems—Declarative pipelines offer structure with built-in error handling, shared libraries eliminate duplication across jobs, and multi-branch pipelines auto-discover and build all branches/PRs. The value is powerful, customizable CI/CD that adapts to complex business requirements, enterprise features (RBAC, audit logging), and integration with mature plugin ecosystem.

methodologies.name: jenkins-pipeline-patterns
methodologies.description: Implement advanced Jenkins pipeline patterns using shared libraries for DRY code, parallel stages for concurrent execution, matrix builds for multi-configuration testing, and proper error handling with post sections. Apply when Jenkins pipelines become complex—shared libraries centralize common logic, parallel stages reduce build time through concurrent execution, and matrix builds test across OS/language combinations without duplication. The delivery is maintainable, fast pipelines where changes to common logic update all consumers, build times decrease through parallelism, and cross-platform testing happens automatically.

methodologies.name: lb-algorithms
methodologies.description: Picks the correct load-balancing algorithm against real traffic shape rather than the LLM-default of round-robin-is-fairest, distinguishing static algorithms (round-robin, weighted RR, IP hash, random) that ignore real-time backend state from dynamic ones (least connections, weighted least-conn, least response time, resource-based) that adapt. Apply it before writing any LB config, when reviewing an existing config that exhibits uneven utilization or high p99, when autoscaling triggers redistribution problems, or when traffic mixes fast and slow endpoints. The practical decision rule: round-robin only if backends are equal-capacity AND request time is uniform, never IP hash with autoscaling or NAT/VPN clients (sticky property breaks on every scale event and one corporate IP becomes a thundering herd), default to least-connections for variable-cost workloads (WebSocket, DB proxies, mixed API costs), and measure p50/p99 distribution first — if they differ by more than 2x choose dynamic, within 20% round-robin is acceptable. Weight reflects capacity in heterogeneous fleets; revisit when traffic doubles or fleet composition changes.

methodologies.name: lb-cloud-terraform
methodologies.description: Provides Terraform modules for AWS ALB/NLB and GCP Global HTTP(S) Load Balancers — the standard IaC vehicle since it gives state-driven drift detection, plan/apply safety, and modular reuse across environments. Apply it when provisioning a new cloud LB on AWS or GCP, migrating from self-hosted HAProxy/Nginx to a managed cloud LB, attaching WAF/Shield/Cloud Armor to existing infrastructure, or setting up NLB for TCP/UDP databases or Redis. The practical templates cover ALB with target groups, HTTPS listener pinned to ssl_policy = ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06, HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect, security groups, S3 access logs with 90-day lifecycle expiry, and mandatory enable_deletion_protection in non-ephemeral environments. NLB templates handle TCP-only protocols (no path routing, no WAF). The GCP side ties together global anycast IP, managed SSL certificates, backend service with CDN policy, URL map with host/path matchers, and HTTPS proxy. The doc also flags silent quota walls agents hit at scale (100 target groups per ALB, 100 rules per listener, 50 backend services per URL map) and excludes Kubernetes — use AWS LB Controller or GKE Ingress instead.

methodologies.name: lb-haproxy-production
methodologies.description: Hardens HAProxy for production: global tuning (maxconn 50000, nbthread per cores, cpu-map), TLS 1.2+/1.3 with Mozilla intermediate cipher profile, stick-table-based stateful rate limiting (100 req/10s per IP without external Redis), path-based ACL routing to separate backends, deep HTTP health checks with expect status, security headers (HSTS, X-Frame-Options, etc.), and active-passive HA via keepalived VRRP for sub-3-second VIP failover. Apply it when standing up HAProxy in front of bare-metal/VM fleets, as a Kubernetes Ingress controller, when implementing rate limiting without external state, when routing TCP (MySQL, Redis with PING/PONG checks) alongside HTTP from one process, or when keepalived-based VIP failover is required. The practical hardening rules: restrict admin socket to mode 660, disable or auth-protect the stats page in production, and never include RC4/3DES/MD5 in cipher lists or set TLS below 1.2. A preflight script validates configs (haproxy -c, nginx -t, kubeconform), runs testssl.sh, and oha load tests against /healthz before swap.

methodologies.name: lb-health-checks
methodologies.description: Solves the number-one footgun in LB configuration — health-check tuning that flaps services in and out (too aggressive) or keeps dead backends in rotation (too lax) — and the silent failure where a 200 OK is returned even when all dependencies are dead. Apply it when implementing any new backend behind an LB, adding Kubernetes liveness/readiness probes, debugging flapping services, or hardening an existing service to accurately reflect dependency health. The practical separation: /health/live for liveness (process alive, failure restarts the pod), /health/ready for readiness (deep dependency probe of DB/cache/queues, failure removes from LB pool without restart), and /health for basic checks. Reference implementations in Python Flask, Node.js Express, and Go include context-bounded readiness probes (5-second timeout) so a hanging DB connection cannot indefinitely block the endpoint. Tune intervals to 10-30s with healthy_threshold 2 and unhealthy_threshold 3 (HAProxy) or 2 (cloud LBs); return 503 not 500 on dependency failure so the LB removes the backend without triggering error-rate alerts.

methodologies.name: lb-high-availability
methodologies.description: Designs HA load-balancer topologies that eliminate hidden SPOFs — agents commonly draft "HA" setups with one LB instance, single-AZ backends, or 60-second DNS TTLs that violate a 99.99% SLA. Apply it when designing any service with availability SLA at or above 99.9%, reviewing an architecture for hidden SPOFs, planning zero-downtime deployments, adding multi-region failover, or doing post-incident review of an LB outage. The practical mandates are minimum 2 LB instances (active-active or active-passive via keepalived/VRRP for sub-3-second VIP failover on-prem, or AWS ALB across 2+ subnets in different AZs for managed HA), backends spread across at least 2 AZs with at least 2 healthy instances per zone, and prefer anycast (AWS Global Accelerator, Cloudflare LB, GCP Global LB) over DNS-based GSLB (Route 53, NS1, Akamai GTM) for SLAs at or above 99.99% — DNS TTLs are honored unevenly by corporate/carrier resolvers, so real failover is 30 seconds to 5 minutes. Zero-downtime requires SIGTERM-driven readiness probes that flip to 503 immediately so the LB drains traffic within one health-check interval before the deregistration_delay window stops the process.

methodologies.name: lb-kubernetes-ingress
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of safely routing all HTTP(S) cluster traffic through a small, controller-specific Ingress fleet whose annotation namespaces and resource shape directly determine availability. Apply when deploying or tuning an Nginx, HAProxy, Traefik, or Envoy Gateway controller (and the Ingress or Gateway API resources behind it) for production, including separate controllers per traffic class, MetalLB-fronted bare-metal clusters, or migrations toward Gateway API. The methodology delivers a hardened controller deployment template (3+ replicas, minAvailable: 2 PodDisruptionBudget, hostname anti-affinity, AZ topology spread, explicit CPU/memory requests and limits) plus per-Ingress production patterns for cert-manager TLS, force-ssl-redirect, rate limiting, CORS, body size, timeouts, and security headers. It also calls out the recurring failure modes that LLM agents introduce, namely mixing nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io and haproxy.org annotation prefixes and under-provisioning CPU on TLS- and regex-heavy controllers.

methodologies.name: lb-layer-selection
methodologies.description: Addresses the first architectural choice in any load balancer design: whether to operate at L4 (transport, TCP/UDP by IP and port) or L7 (application, HTTP headers, URL, cookies), and how to combine the two. Use it at the start of an infrastructure design, when reviewing an agent proposal that mixes the two layers, when traffic is purely TCP/UDP, or when content routing, SSL termination, header manipulation, A/B testing, or API-gateway features are required. The value is a concrete decision rule, side-by-side HAProxy examples for TCP database proxies and HTTP path routing, and an explicit cloud LB mapping (ALB for L7, NLB for L4, Global Accelerator for anycast failover instead of DNS GSLB). It also names the SSL termination strategies (terminate, pass-through, re-encrypt) that must be locked in at design time, and the agent gotchas around DNS-based GSLB SLOs and forgetting to size the LB tier itself for TLS- or regex-heavy L7 work.

methodologies.name: lb-monitoring
methodologies.description: Solves the blind-spot problem where backend failures, latency spikes, and pool exhaustion are detected only after users complain because no one is watching the LB layer where every request actually flows.
