Apply it when standing up observability for a new HAProxy or Nginx production deployment, retrofitting Prometheus scraping onto an LB that lacks monitoring, defining SLIs and SLOs for a load-balanced service, or diagnosing flapping backends through correlated logs. The methodology delivers ready-to-paste Prometheus scrape configs for haproxy-exporter (port 9101) and nginx-prometheus-exporter (port 9113), and a mandatory four-alert baseline: BackendDown (critical, for: 1m), HighErrorRate above 5% (warning, for: 5m), HighLatency p99 above 2s (warning, for: 5m), and ConnectionPoolExhausted above 90% (warning, for: 5m). It also enumerates the metrics that matter (request rate, error rate, p50/p95/p99 latency, active connections, backend health, pool saturation, certificate expiry) and requires centralized log aggregation to ELK or Loki with X-Request-ID and X-Forwarded-For propagation.

methodologies.name: lb-nginx-production
methodologies.description: Covers the case where Nginx fills both the web server and load balancer roles and needs hardening for production traffic instead of staying at default tutorial values. Use it when serving static content alongside upstream proxying, caching upstream responses, splitting /api, /ws, and /admin paths into different pools, proxying WebSockets, or replacing a more complex HAProxy setup for HTTP-only workloads. The methodology delivers a full nginx.conf template covering worker_processes auto with worker_rlimit_nofile 65535, upstream zones with keepalive (and the proxy_http_version 1.1 plus empty Connection header that actually activates it), Mozilla intermediate TLS 1.2/1.3 ciphers with ssl_stapling, limit_req_zone and limit_conn_zone rate limiting, HSTS and X-Frame-Options security headers, WebSocket upgrade blocks, and proxy_next_upstream retry on failure. It also flags the common mistakes: ssl_prefer_server_ciphers must be off for TLS 1.3, worker_connections must match ulimit -n, and overlapping location prefixes need explicit precedence with = or ^~ modifiers.

methodologies.name: lb-session-persistence
methodologies.description: Tackles sticky sessions, an anti-pattern that often masks a missing shared session store and quietly breaks autoscaling, NAT/VPN clients, and graceful backend replacement. Apply when reviewing or designing persistence for apps that keep state in local memory, long-lived WebSocket connections, in-process shopping carts and login wizards, legacy code that cannot be refactored, or when auditing whether existing stickiness can be removed by adding Redis. The methodology insists on first asking whether session state can move to Redis or Memcached (eliminating stickiness entirely), and when stickiness is unavoidable it favors LB-inserted cookie persistence over source-IP hashing because IP hash collapses corporate NAT and mobile-carrier traffic onto one backend and reshuffles on every scale event. It supplies HAProxy and Nginx Ingress cookie-affinity templates with mandatory Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite flags, plus a WebSocket pattern with timeout tunnel 1h instead of standard HTTP read/write timeouts.

methodologies.name: lb-technology-selection
methodologies.description: Solves the upstream architectural choice of which load balancer to deploy: HAProxy, Nginx, AWS or GCP or Azure managed LBs, or a Kubernetes Ingress controller. Use it at the start of a new service fleet, when migrating from single-instance to load-balanced architecture, when evaluating self-hosted versus managed cloud LBs, or when picking an Ingress controller for a new cluster. The methodology delivers a concise decision matrix (HAProxy for raw TCP throughput, Nginx for web-server-plus-LB combos, ALB or NLB for AWS auto-scaling, Gateway API for multi-cloud futures), the combined HAProxy-L4-in-front-of-Nginx-Ingress-L7 pattern for bare-metal clusters, an agentic workflow that drives changes through IaC and validation tools (haproxy -c -f, nginx -t, kubectl ingress-nginx, vegeta, testssl.sh), and a services catalog tagging each LB by agent-friendliness. It also captures the LLM gotchas (blind worker_connections=100000, mismatched Ingress annotation prefixes, 2018-vintage cipher copy-paste, shallow /healthz endpoints) and rules out replacing managed cloud LBs prematurely or papering over architectural smells with sticky sessions.

methodologies.name: load-balancing-concepts
methodologies.description: Understand load balancing fundamentals including algorithm selection (round-robin, least-connections, weighted), health checks, session persistence, and L4 vs L7 trade-offs to design appropriate traffic distribution. Study this to make informed load balancer selection—L4 provides maximum throughput for simple TCP/UDP routing, L7 enables content-based routing and SSL termination for HTTP/HTTPS, health checks prevent routing to failed servers, and session persistence ensures request consistency. The value is properly selected load balancing matching actual workload characteristics (HTTP vs raw TCP, stateful vs stateless), avoided performance bottlenecks, and high availability through intelligent traffic distribution.

methodologies.name: nginx-configuration
methodologies.description: Nginx configuration covers reverse proxy setup, SSL/TLS termination, and security hardening for modern web applications. Use it when setting up high-performance web servers, implementing reverse proxies for backend services, or optimizing web application performance with caching and compression. It provides standards-based patterns for TLS 1.2/1.3, security headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options), and load balancing strategies that can handle 400K-500K requests/sec in clustered deployments.

methodologies.name: platform-engineering
methodologies.description: Platform engineering reduces developer cognitive load by providing self-service infrastructure provisioning and golden paths for common deployment patterns. Use it when teams waste 40%+ time on infrastructure rather than features or when tool fragmentation prevents developers from shipping quickly. It delivers 2-3x faster environment setup, 40-50% reduced cognitive load, and enables 2.3x higher developer activity through well-designed Internal Developer Platforms.

methodologies.name: security-container-scanning
methodologies.description: Addresses the reality that 87% of production container images carry at least one major vulnerability and that the OS, runtime, and application dependencies bundled in every layer must be scanned before they reach a registry. Apply it on every project that builds and pushes images, when generating SBOMs for FedRAMP, SOC2, or NIST SSDF compliance, when signing images for admission control verification, or when scanning Dockerfiles, Kubernetes manifests, and Terraform in the same pipeline. The methodology mandates running Trivy or Grype during the build step, exiting non-zero on CRITICAL or HIGH findings before any push, generating a CycloneDX (or SPDX, but never both) SBOM as an immutable per-build artifact, and signing images with Sigstore cosign keyless OIDC so no key material lives in CI secrets. It also provides ready-made GitHub Actions workflows, a trivy.yaml config with ignore-unfixed and a .trivyignore template that requires expiry dates, and warns against floating @master scanner tags that silently change CI behavior.

methodologies.name: security-dast
methodologies.description: Covers Dynamic Application Security Testing, the runtime complement to SAST that finds auth bypasses, session flaws, SSRF, missing security headers, and injection vectors which only appear in a running application. Use it as a post-deploy gate against staging on every main-branch deploy, on scheduled nightly or weekly full scans, for authenticated coverage of login-gated endpoints, and for template-based CVE and misconfiguration scanning with Nuclei. The methodology fences DAST into staging only (production URLs are explicitly forbidden in any CI configuration), requires a scan-scope file like .zap/rules.tsv that excludes payment processors, mail senders, and destructive endpoints, and pins Nuclei templates instead of floating to community HEAD. It includes ready-to-use OWASP ZAP baseline and Nuclei GitHub Actions with SARIF upload, a custom security-headers Nuclei template, and a tool-selection note matching ZAP to startups, Nuclei to template-based CI, and Burp Suite to manual pen testing.

methodologies.name: security-policy-as-code
methodologies.description: Closes the gap that lets workloads with privileged containers, root users, untrusted images, or no resource limits enter a Kubernetes cluster despite passing SAST and container scans. Apply it on multi-team clusters where app teams should not override security baselines, when compliance mandates baseline pod security, when enforcement extends beyond Kubernetes (Terraform, API gateways, CI gates via Conftest), or when verifying Sigstore cosign signatures at admission time. The methodology insists on deploying the policy engine in audit mode first, remediating existing violations, then switching to Enforce; every policy must have unit tests via kyverno test or conftest verify; and all policies live in Git and are applied through GitOps (ArgoCD, Flux), never via manual kubectl apply. It supplies baseline ClusterPolicies (no privileged, runAsNonRoot, no privilege escalation, drop ALL capabilities, read-only rootfs, required limits, trusted-registry allowlist, no :latest tag), Rego equivalents with Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplates, and a contract-test pattern where app PRs include fixture manifests to prevent policy-as-code drift.

methodologies.name: security-sast
methodologies.description: Addresses the cost of finding application-level vulnerabilities late by running Static Application Security Testing on the diff at PR time, which is the fastest feedback loop in the security pipeline. Apply it on any project shipping code to production, when wiring SAST as a PR check, when authoring custom rules for hardcoded secrets or SQL interpolation, or when generating SARIF for GitHub Advanced Security and GitLab Code Quality. The methodology gates merges only on CRITICAL and HIGH findings (exposing MEDIUM and LOW as informational at first, then tightening), pins scanner image digests and rule-pack versions instead of floating tags, and sets a baseline on first integration so legacy debt does not block new work. It supplies Semgrep and CodeQL GitHub Actions workflows, custom Semgrep rules for hardcoded secrets and API keys, a tool selection guide (CodeQL for GitHub, Semgrep for custom rules, Snyk Code for OSS-heavy repos, SonarQube for combined quality plus security, language-native tools like Bandit, gosec, Brakeman), and warns that auto-fix bots can regress functionality and that CVSS-only gating must be tempered with reachability evidence.

methodologies.name: security-supply-chain
methodologies.description: Defends against the dominant attack vector of the last three years (a 7x increase in supply chain attacks), which targets dependencies, build tooling, and CI secrets rather than the application's own code. Apply it on every project with external dependencies, on any repo where developers might commit secrets, on artifacts shipped externally or to regulated industries, and when standing up an automated security PR bot. The methodology requires Gitleaks or detect-secrets pre-commit hooks plus a CI scan with full git history (fetch-depth: 0), git filter-repo and credential rotation when a secret reaches main, committed lock files for every manifest (package-lock.json, poetry.lock, go.sum, Cargo.lock), an SCA scan (osv-scanner, Trivy FS, Snyk) in CI, automated dependency updates with auto-merge capped at minor and patch releases, CycloneDX SBOM generation per build, SLSA provenance attestation via cosign attest, and a license allowlist that denies GPL-3.0 and AGPL-3.0 by default for commercial code. It also lays out a four-level DevSecOps maturity ladder from foundation (CI gates) to optimized (AI-assisted triage with reachability and full SLSA Level 3).

domains.description: DevOps orchestrator coordinating infrastructure and CI/CD. Manages containerization with Docker, container orchestration (Kubernetes, Helm), infrastructure provisioning (Terraform, IaC), cloud platforms (AWS, GCP), CI/CD pipelines, monitoring, and operational excellence. Covers 58 total methodologies.

methodologies.name: aiops
methodologies.description: AIOps applies machine learning to automate IT operations including anomaly detection, root cause analysis, and incident management powered by LLMs. Use it to reduce alert fatigue, accelerate mean-time-to-recovery (MTTR) from hours to minutes, and enable self-healing systems that resolve incidents automatically. It combines observability data (metrics, logs, traces) with AI models to predict failures before they impact production and provide intelligent runbook automation.

methodologies.name: argocd-gitops
methodologies.description: ArgoCD implements GitOps for Kubernetes, using Git repositories as the single source of truth for application and infrastructure state with automatic synchronization. Deploy it when managing multi-cluster Kubernetes environments or needing declarative, auditable deployments that sync automatically when Git state changes. It provides drift detection, automatic rollback capabilities, and visual monitoring through a web UI, making Kubernetes deployments as reliable as code commits.

methodologies.name: aws-cfn-terraform-templates
methodologies.description: Provides battle-tested CloudFormation YAML and Terraform HCL starting points for the AWS infrastructure patterns that teams keep rebuilding from scratch and getting wrong. Use it when deploying a new dual-AZ VPC with public and private subnets, launching an ECS Fargate service behind an ALB, setting up a serverless Lambda plus API Gateway plus DynamoDB stack, provisioning RDS PostgreSQL with auto-generated Secrets Manager credentials, or building reusable Terraform VPC and ECS service modules. The templates encode the correct dependency ordering and the safety defaults that LLMs and hurried operators routinely omit: NAT Gateway depending on InternetGatewayAttachment, ECS service depending on the ALB Listener, RDS DeletionPolicy and UpdateReplacePolicy set to Snapshot with DeletionProtection enabled, Multi-AZ and Performance Insights on, gp3 storage encrypted, and SecretTargetAttachment linking the secret to the live RDS endpoint. Companion JSON snippets cover S3 lifecycle policies (Glacier transition, multipart abort), Lambda and ECS trust policies, S3 and DynamoDB access policies, and ECS task definitions with awslogs and Secrets Manager-sourced env vars. The included antipatterns warn against DeletionPolicy: Delete on production RDS and hardcoded passwords as plaintext CloudFormation parameters.

methodologies.name: aws-cli-compute
methodologies.description: Reference for the AWS CLI v2 commands that automate EC2, S3, and Lambda lifecycle operations correctly the first time, including the --query filters, JMESPath expressions, and credential scoping that LLMs frequently leave out. Apply it when scripting EC2 instance launch, stop, terminate, AMI capture, security group, key pair, or Elastic IP workflows; when managing S3 buckets, objects, sync operations, presigned URLs, versioning, lifecycle policies, and bucket policies; or when deploying, updating, invoking (sync, async, with --log-type Tail), versioning, layering, and aliasing Lambda functions for blue-green rollouts via routing-config weights. It also covers shared credential setup with named profiles, role assumption via source_profile, environment variable authentication, output formats (json, table, text, yaml), pagination via NextToken, and troubleshooting (--debug, sts get-caller-identity, simulate-principal-policy). Antipatterns explicitly call out using root account credentials for daily operations and hardcoding access keys in scripts or code.

methodologies.name: aws-cli-containers-iac
methodologies.description: Companion to the EC2 and S3 CLI reference, covering the multi-step command sequences for AWS database, container, IaC, IAM, and observability operations where ordering mistakes cause failed deployments. Apply it when managing RDS instances, snapshots, parameter groups, and Aurora cluster failovers; ECS clusters, task definitions, services, run-task one-shots, and execute-command exec; ECR login, repositories, and images; EKS clusters, node groups, and add-ons including kubeconfig updates; CloudFormation stacks with change sets, validation, and event extraction; IAM users, roles, policies, permission boundaries, and access keys; and CloudWatch log groups, streams, filter-log-events, live tail, metrics, alarms, dashboards, and Log Insights queries. The methodology also encodes the deployment patterns that string these together: ECS rolling deployment with services-stable wait, Lambda blue-green with weighted alias routing, cross-region disaster recovery copies for RDS snapshots and AMIs, and unused-resource sweeps (orphan EIPs, old snapshots, stopped instances, Cost Explorer monthly spend).

methodologies.name: aws-infra-llm-prompts
methodologies.description: Solves the problem that unstructured AWS design conversations with LLMs produce generic answers because the model is never forced to gather constraints up front. Use it when starting a new AWS architecture conversation, reviewing an existing architecture for security, cost, or reliability gaps, generating IAM policies, DR runbooks, scaling configurations, or workload migration plans through LLM assistance. The methodology delivers parameterized prompt templates for VPC architecture, ECS or EKS container architecture, serverless API design, IAM policy authoring, AWS security audit, secrets management strategy, AWS cost analysis, EC2 right-sizing, Reserved Instance and Savings Plans strategy, disaster recovery design (with explicit RTO and RPO inputs), high availability design, auto-scaling design, workload and database migration planning, and monitoring plus alerting design. Each template forces the agent to elicit environment, scale, compliance posture, and budget before producing concrete Terraform, CloudFormation, CLI commands, or runbook output, with iteration tips like 'Make this more cost-effective' and 'Convert to multi-region'.

methodologies.name: aws-well-architected-checklists
methodologies.description: Translates the abstract AWS Well-Architected Framework pillars into concrete pass-or-fail checklist items that can be wired into CI gates, pre-launch reviews, and quarterly audits. Apply it during pre-launch review of a new workload, on quarterly Well-Architected reviews of existing production environments, when bootstrapping a new AWS account baseline, during post-incident retrospectives to identify which items were missing, or in agent-driven audits where each item maps to a verifiable CLI check. The methodology covers all six pillars (Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability) plus a new-account baseline (root MFA, CloudTrail in all regions, Config, Security Hub, GuardDuty, billing alerts, VPC planning) and a pre-production gate (least-privilege IAM, encryption, load testing, scaling tests, multi-AZ failover verification, runbooks). Headline numbers like 72% savings from Savings Plans, 90% from Spot, and 40% from Graviton anchor the cost-optimization items, and a quick-reference IAM and shared-responsibility table sits alongside for architecture review.

methodologies.name: azure-architecture
methodologies.description: Azure architecture follows the Well-Architected Framework with five pillars (reliability, security, cost, operations, performance) and implements landing zones for enterprise cloud adoption. Use it to design Azure infrastructure that scales across subscriptions, implement governance through policies, and follow Microsoft's Cloud Adoption Framework. It covers landing zone design areas, management group hierarchy, and recommendations for hybrid cloud, identity management, and cost optimization.

methodologies.name: backup-strategies
methodologies.description: Backup strategies implement modern 3-2-1-1-0 rule (three copies, two media types, one offsite, one immutable, zero errors) to protect against ransomware and ensure zero-error recovery. Apply it for databases, filesystems, Kubernetes clusters, and cloud resources when data loss would impact operations or compliance. It provides RPO/RTO targets per criticality level, immutable backup implementation (S3 Object Lock, WORM), and tools for automated backup and recovery testing.

methodologies.name: devops-aws-monitoring-dr
methodologies.description: Addresses the twin failure modes of unobserved AWS systems that accumulate silent failures and untested DR runbooks that collapse at the worst possible moment. Apply it when setting up monitoring for a new production AWS workload, retrofitting observability onto an architecture without dashboards or alarms, designing or testing a disaster recovery runbook, or preparing for an architecture review that touches the Reliability and Operational Excellence pillars. The methodology requires per-tier CloudWatch dashboards (API request and error rate, Lambda duration and errors and throttles, DynamoDB consumed capacity, queue depth, Container Insights for ECS and EKS) on a 5-minute aggregation window, a tiered alarming model with critical pages, business-hours warnings, and digest informationals, mandatory ok_actions plus alarm_actions on KMS-encrypted SNS topics, runbook links in alarm descriptions, and X-Ray Active tracing on every Lambda and API Gateway stage. DR strategy is selected by documented RTO and RPO (backup/restore for RTO over 24h, pilot light for 1-24h, warm standby for 15min-1h, active-active for under 15min) and tested at least quarterly because untested DR is no DR.

methodologies.name: devops-aws-serverless-api
methodologies.description: Codifies the serverless API pattern on AWS that delivers 60-80% cost reduction versus always-on containers for variable traffic by combining API Gateway HTTP API, Lambda on arm64 Graviton, DynamoDB single-table on-demand, EventBridge, SQS with DLQ, and Step Functions. Use it for REST or GraphQL APIs with spiky traffic, event-driven async processing under 15 minutes, workloads that should scale to zero overnight, greenfield services where container orchestration overhead is unjustified, or as the standard glue layer for Bedrock RAG pipelines. The methodology mandates Lambda architectures=["arm64"] with python3.12 or nodejs22.x and tracing_mode="Active", HTTP API v2 with explicit CORS allow_origins (never *), custom domain plus ACM certificate, default route throttling limits, DynamoDB billing_mode="PAY_PER_REQUEST" with point_in_time_recovery and a declared TTL attribute, SQS queues with visibility_timeout at least 6x the Lambda timeout and a DLQ at maxReceiveCount=3, EventBridge rules using source plus detail-type filters (no wildcards), and CloudFront fronting private S3 with Origin Access Control instead of legacy OAI.

methodologies.name: devops-aws-service-selection
methodologies.description: Solves the highest-leverage decision a DevOps engineer makes at project kickoff: choosing the right compute and database services from the 200+ AWS catalog before a wrong default creates compounding migration debt. Apply it when starting a new AWS project, reviewing an existing architecture for cost or operational gaps, mapping legacy on-premises components to AWS equivalents, or running a Well-Architected Review. The methodology forces a documented evaluation against all six pillars (Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability) before the first production deployment, and prescribes priority-ordered compute selection: tasks under 15 minutes go to Lambda on arm64, container workloads default to Fargate, EKS only when node-level control or service mesh is genuinely required, batch processing uses AWS Batch with Spot, ML inference picks Inferentia2 or Graviton, and EC2 is reserved for legacy or OS-specific cases. Database choice follows access patterns (Aurora Serverless v2 for relational, DynamoDB for key-value, ElastiCache Serverless for cache, Neptune for graph, DocumentDB for Mongo-compatible) rather than engine familiarity, and a model-tier guide assigns Haiku to mechanical CLI runs, Sonnet to debugging and review, and Opus to multi-region or microservice-monitoring design.

methodologies.name: devops-aws-terraform-cicd
methodologies.description: Establishes the production-ready Terraform project layout and GitHub Actions pipeline that prevents cross-environment blast-radius, concurrent state corruption, and long-lived AWS credentials in CI secrets. Use it when starting a new AWS project with Terraform, adding CI/CD to an existing Terraform codebase, setting up isolated dev, staging, and prod environments, or enforcing cost allocation tagging and provider version pinning across a team. The methodology requires per-environment directories under infrastructure/environments/ with their own main.tf, variables.tf, and terraform.tfvars; an S3 backend with per-environment state keys, encryption, and DynamoDB locking; provider default_tags injecting Project, Environment, and ManagedBy="terraform" everywhere; pinned aws provider ~> 5.0; per-service KMS keys with rotation and a 30-day deletion window; and sensitive=true on outputs like database endpoints. The GitHub Actions workflow uses aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials with role-to-assume (OIDC, no static keys), runs a matrix plan across all three environments on PRs, and applies sequentially with max-parallel: 1 after merge to main, gated by a required-reviewer GitHub Environment for production.

methodologies.name: devops-aws-three-tier
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of building a secure, highly available production baseline on AWS by separating workloads into three tiers: a public-subnet ALB, private-subnet ECS Fargate or EKS application pods, and isolated database subnets running Aurora Serverless v2. Apply it when designing new cloud-native web apps, migrating on-prem three-tier systems, or meeting compliance isolation requirements (PCI, HIPAA), spanning at least two AZs per tier with one NAT Gateway per AZ in production. The pattern delivers least-privilege networking through security groups that reference each other by ID instead of CIDR, eliminates lateral-movement paths between tiers, and bundles enforced defaults like KMS encryption, IAM database auth, deletion protection, TLS 1.3 ALB policies, and 30-day backups so the Terraform stack ships secure by construction.

methodologies.name: devops-elk-architecture
methodologies.description: Solves the production logging problem of a single flat Elasticsearch node collapsing under load by partitioning the cluster into dedicated master, data-hot, data-warm, ingest, coordinating, and ML node roles with hot-warm-cold storage tiers. Apply it when centralizing logs across services, building SIEM or compliance retention (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2), or planning capacity for ELK/EFK/Elastic Stack deployments via Docker Compose or ECK on Kubernetes. The methodology delivers concrete sizing rules (heap at 50% of RAM capped at 32 GB to keep CompressedOops, 20-40 GB target shard size, max 20 shards per GB heap), a capacity formula that multiplies daily volume by retention, replicas, 1.15 index overhead and 1.20 disk headroom, and clear guidance on when to choose Loki, OpenSearch, or Datadog instead.

methodologies.name: devops-elk-beats-collection
methodologies.description: Solves log collection from heterogeneous sources by deploying Filebeat as a lightweight host agent or Kubernetes DaemonSet that tail-follows files, handles rotation and truncation, parses multiline stack traces and JSON, and ships to Elasticsearch or Logstash. Apply it when collecting logs from Linux/Windows hosts whose code cannot be modified, when every container's stdout/stderr must be centralized without per-app instrumentation, and when Autodiscover should automatically configure inputs as pods come and go. It delivers field-flattening conventions (fields_under_root with service and environment), processor pipelines for host/cloud/Kubernetes metadata enrichment and fingerprint deduplication, and ECK-managed Beat manifests with hostNetwork, daemonSet volumes, and TLS-secured bulk outputs to Elasticsearch.

methodologies.name: devops-elk-index-management
methodologies.description: Solves the operational risk of manual Elasticsearch index housekeeping (accidental deletes, disk-full red clusters, sprawling shard counts, dual text+keyword auto-mappings) by combining three primitives: index templates that lock in shards, replicas, refresh interval and explicit mappings before the first document arrives; ILM policies that automate hot-rollover-warm-cold-delete transitions; and rollover aliases that decouple the write target from the physical index name. Apply it on any production cluster receiving time-series logs with a defined retention period or when migrating from daily-named indices to size-based rollover. The pattern eliminates manual cleanup, controls storage cost by shifting aging data to cheaper tiers, and prevents the most common mis-mapping bugs that double fielddata memory.

methodologies.name: devops-elk-logstash-pipeline
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of turning unstructured log lines into queryable, compliant, structured documents by configuring Logstash input/filter/output pipelines with grok and JSON parsing, date normalization, GeoIP and useragent enrichment, mutate-gsub PII masking, multi-pipeline isolation, and dead-letter queues. Apply it when log sources mix multiple formats, when GDPR or PCI-DSS demands sensitive data never reach the index, when heavy parsing must be isolated from the write path, or when streams need conditional routing to different indices and retention policies. It delivers concrete .conf templates with Beats input over TLS, conditional JSON parsing, dedicated pipelines, and tuning guidance for high-throughput ingestion including Kafka buffering options when a single node saturates.

methodologies.name: devops-elk-queries-alerting
methodologies.description: Solves the operational gap of logs being collected but never consulted in time, by combining Kibana Query Language for fast field-indexed filtering, Elasticsearch DSL aggregations (terms, percentiles, date-histograms) for analytics, and Kibana alerting rules and Watcher for automated notifications on error spikes, missing heartbeats, or P99 latency breaches. Apply it when building operational dashboards across services, debugging incidents by service/time/error filters, running ad-hoc top-N analyses, or wiring proactive alerts that reduce MTTR. The methodology favors keyword-field KQL over slow regex on raw message, shows when Prometheus or pre-aggregated rollup indices are a better fit, and delivers practical query patterns that translate directly into dashboards and alert rules.

methodologies.name: devops-lb-algorithms
methodologies.description: Solves the upfront architectural decision of which load balancer type and algorithm to use, covering L4 transport-level routing for raw throughput, L7 application-level routing for HTTP content-based decisions, DNS for geographic distribution, and Global LB for cross-region failover. Apply it when scaling horizontally, removing single-server SPOFs, supporting zero-downtime deploys with traffic draining, or routing geographically distributed traffic. It compares stateless round robin, weighted, least-connections, IP hash, and consistent hashing with concrete trade-offs (session stickiness, server heterogeneity, cache locality), so the wrong choice does not produce uneven load, broken sessions, or extra latency, and it explicitly steers single-instance and shared-state workloads away from load balancing.

methodologies.name: devops-lb-haproxy
methodologies.description: Solves the need for fine-grained, self-hosted L4/L7 load balancing using HAProxy and Nginx for environments where managed cloud LBs are unavailable, too costly, or insufficiently controllable. Apply it for Docker Compose and bare-metal setups, when ACL-based path routing, sticky sessions, stick-table rate limiting, or protocol-aware health checks for MySQL/PostgreSQL/Redis are required, and when migrating between cloud and on-prem with consistent config. The methodology delivers production frontend/backend separation with HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects, ACL routing into multiple backends with independent algorithms, explicit timeout tuning, and L4 database proxying, while warning that a single HAProxy instance is itself a SPOF and must always be paired active-passive or active-active.

methodologies.name: devops-lb-health-checks
methodologies.description: Solves the cascading-failure risk of load balancers forwarding traffic to crashed or overloaded backends by designing TCP, HTTP, gRPC, and custom health checks that distinguish liveness (process alive) from readiness (can serve traffic) and verify real downstream dependencies instead of returning blanket 200s. Apply it on every production load-balanced service, on services with external DB/cache/upstream dependencies that fail independently, on Kubernetes deployments needing liveness and readiness probes, and during blue-green or rolling rollouts where new instances must pass before receiving traffic. It delivers tuning guidance for fast detection without flapping plus implementation examples in Go, Python/FastAPI, and Node.js so each runtime exposes a check that reflects actual service health.

methodologies.name: devops-lb-high-availability
methodologies.description: Solves the paradox that the load balancer itself is a SPOF that negates the HA of the backend pool by selecting an HA topology — active-passive with VRRP/keepalived, active-active with floating VIP or anycast, or DNS failover — and then choosing the right cloud LB product (AWS ALB/NLB/GLB, GCP HTTPS LB, Azure Front Door) when managed services are preferred. Apply it for any production service with an SLA above 99%, multi-AZ backend pools, or global apps that need cross-region failover or latency-based routing. The methodology delivers Terraform and CloudFormation templates, explicit comparison tables across cloud providers, and explicit warnings against active-active without session sync or sticky sessions, which would otherwise break stateful applications.

methodologies.name: devops-lb-kubernetes
methodologies.description: Solves the Kubernetes-specific load balancing problem where pods are ephemeral by using the layered model of ClusterIP/NodePort/LoadBalancer/ExternalName Services for L4 stable virtual IPs, Ingress controllers (Nginx, Traefik, AWS ALB Controller) for L7 host/path routing and TLS termination, and readiness/liveness probes that gate traffic during rolling deploys and trigger restarts on deadlocks. Apply it for any cluster-hosted service that needs stable network access, when multiple HTTP services should share one external IP to avoid one cloud LB per service, or when cloud-specific features (WAF, GCP NEG) need controller annotations. It explicitly steers raw TCP/UDP workloads to LoadBalancer Services with NLB annotations and warns against NodePort in production due to firewall-unfriendly port ranges.

methodologies.name: devops-lb-ssl-tls
methodologies.description: Solves where and how to handle TLS in the request path by choosing among LB termination (decrypt and forward HTTP), re-encryption (decrypt and re-encrypt to backends for end-to-end compliance), and passthrough (forward encrypted traffic untouched), with TLS 1.3 preferred and TLS 1.2 the 2025-2026 minimum. Apply it on any public-facing HTTP service, when PCI-DSS or HIPAA require end-to-end encryption, when gRPC or WebSocket traffic must own its TLS session, or when one LB serves multiple certificates via SNI. The methodology delivers cipher policy, HSTS, OCSP stapling, and certificate-rotation guidance, centralizes cert management to remove per-backend handshake CPU cost, and warns that passthrough disables L7 routing and that LBs without HSM or vault-backed keys must not terminate TLS.

methodologies.name: devops-platform-backstage
methodologies.description: Solves the developer-portal problem of fragmented service discovery, scattered runbooks, and manual scaffolding by deploying Backstage — the CNCF-donated Spotify portal — as a unified surface combining service catalog, TechDocs, and a golden-path Software Templates scaffolder, extended through 300+ community plugins. Apply it when an engineering org needs unified service discovery and self-service workflows, when teams waste time hunting ownership and dependencies, or when a plugin-extensible portal is preferred to building a custom web app. The methodology insists Backstage be treated as a product with dedicated engineering, mandates a complete catalog-info.yaml (apiVersion, kind, owner, lifecycle, type, dependsOn, techdocs-ref) at every service repo root to prevent orphaned entries, and cites Spotify's outcomes — 280 teams, 2,000+ services, onboarding cut from 60 to 20 days, 2.3x GitHub activity — as the empirical baseline.

methodologies.name: devops-platform-crossplane
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of forcing every developer to learn cloud-provider IaC by using Crossplane to expose simple Kubernetes-native CRD claims (Database, Cache, Network) backed by platform-authored CompositeResourceDefinitions and Compositions that reconcile the full multi-resource AWS/GCP/Azure stack with security defaults baked in. Apply it when a platform team is building self-service infrastructure abstractions on top of an existing Kubernetes cluster, when infrastructure should ride the same GitOps pipeline (ArgoCD/Flux) as application deployments, or when one developer-facing API must target different cloud backends per environment. The methodology mandates non-overridable security defaults — storageEncrypted true, publiclyAccessible false, deleteProtection patterns — at the Composition layer, achieving Netflix Wall-E-style near-100% compliance because insecure choices are impossible to request, not merely policy-forbidden.

methodologies.name: devops-platform-golden-paths
methodologies.description: Solves the inconsistency and waste of every team independently re-solving CI/CD, observability, secret management, and security wiring by designing opinionated-but-optional templates — golden paths — for the most common developer tasks (new service, database, API gateway) that bundle best practices into a one-form-submit action. Apply it when creating new microservices/APIs/workers/frontends in orgs with established patterns, provisioning standard databases and gateways, or for any infrastructure setup repeated more than five times a month across teams. The methodology requires every golden path to publish a clear contract — what is provided, what the developer supplies, and the explicit escape hatch for deviations — citing Spotify's 89% voluntary adoption and Netflix Wall-E's near-100% security compliance as evidence that making the right way the easy way beats mandates.

methodologies.name: devops-platform-idp-core
methodologies.description: Solves the systemic problem that developers spend 40%+ of their time on infrastructure rather than features by building an Internal Developer Platform — a self-service layer comprising Self-Service Portal, Golden Paths, Service Catalog, Environment Management, Policy Enforcement, and Developer Documentation — that absorbs cloud complexity behind a curated interface. Apply it in engineering orgs above ~20 developers where infra requests bottleneck delivery, where multiple teams keep reinventing the same patterns, when onboarding takes more than a week, or when DevOps ticket queues exceed 30% repetitive requests. The methodology cites concrete outcomes (40% lower DevOps ticket volume, 40-50% cognitive load reduction, 60% faster onboarding) and the Google Shift Down strategy, and it explicitly excludes small teams and pre-PMF startups where the platform investment delays product iteration.

methodologies.name: devops-platform-policy-finops
methodologies.description: Solves two compounding governance gaps — security/compliance drift and runaway cloud cost — by embedding OPA/Gatekeeper admission policies (required team and app labels, mandatory CPU/memory limits, runAsNonRoot, CIS benchmarks) directly into the platform and integrating FinOps so cost visibility per team/service plus pre-deployment cost estimates reach developers at deploy time, not weeks later via finance. Apply it on multi-tenant Kubernetes clusters with recurring resource or security misconfiguration, in SOC2/HIPAA/PCI environments, or when cloud spend grows faster than usage. The methodology insists golden paths are established first and policy enforced second, mandates a 2-week dry-run period before switching ConstraintTemplates from audit to enforce, and applies the Google Shift Down principle so insecure or wasteful configurations are rejected at API server admission rather than caught in retrospective review.

methodologies.name: docker
methodologies.description: Docker containerization packages applications with dependencies into portable, isolated units for consistent dev-to-prod environments. Use it when building microservices, implementing CI/CD pipelines, or ensuring environment consistency across teams. It covers image optimization techniques (multi-stage builds, Alpine/slim bases), layer caching strategies, health checks, security patterns (non-root users, minimal images), and networking/volume management.

methodologies.name: docker-image-optimization
methodologies.description: Solves slow deploys, oversized registries, and bloated attack surfaces by stacking four independent levers — multi-stage builds, strategic layer ordering with cache mounts, .dockerignore, and minimal base image variants (slim, alpine, distroless) — to compress a typical 900 MB Python image down to 50-120 MB. Apply it on every production image, on CI pipelines where build time exceeds two minutes, on any image above 300 MB, in repos polluted with node_modules/__pycache__/.venv/target, and on security-sensitive workloads. The methodology mandates that the final stage never use a full base image (it may appear only in build stages), provides a comparison table of size ratios across Python/Node/Java/Go family variants, and explicitly carves out exceptions for dev images using bind mounts and runtimes that genuinely need build tools at runtime.

methodologies.name: docker-language-templates
methodologies.description: Solves the recurring problem of engineers writing production Dockerfiles from scratch and missing the right base-image variant, dependency-cache pattern, non-root user creation, or HEALTHCHECK invocation by providing copy-paste Dockerfile and Docker Compose templates for Python (Django, FastAPI), Node.js (Next.js, NestJS), Go, Rust, Java (Spring), and static sites. Apply it when bootstrapping a new service, auditing an existing Dockerfile line-by-line against a reference, or onboarding junior engineers who need canonical examples encoding team standards. Every template uses multi-stage builds, non-root users, HEALTHCHECK instructions, and pinned base images, and is meant to be combined with the optimization and security-hardening methodologies on top; unusual runtimes (GPU, FUSE, privileged sockets) start from the generic template and document the specialization.

methodologies.name: docker-llm-prompts
methodologies.description: Solves the prompt-engineering tax of asking an LLM to generate or audit Dockerfiles ad hoc by providing parameterized templates scoped to specific tasks: producing a production Dockerfile from language/framework/entry-point/port inputs, optimizing an existing one, running a security audit with severity ratings, debugging build or runtime failures, generating CI build/test/scan/push steps, and migrating Docker Compose to Kubernetes. Apply it for languages or frameworks not covered by the language templates, for quick audits without rereading the full optimization or security methodologies, when integrating Docker into CI/CD, or when designing multi-container architectures. The narrow per-task scope keeps the model focused on a single deliverable with the right checklist embedded, and outputs must always be validated against the docker-image-optimization and docker-security-hardening checklists before deployment.

methodologies.name: docker-security-hardening
methodologies.description: Solves the problem that the Docker default baseline (root user, all capabilities, writable filesystem, no seccomp) makes container breakout cheap and supply-chain CVEs invisible. Apply on every production image and runtime: create a non-root user with USER and --chown, drop all capabilities and re-add only what is needed, run with --read-only plus tmpfs for /tmp, set --security-opt no-new-privileges, inject secrets at runtime via Docker Secrets or Vault rather than ENV/ARG, and add a Trivy or Docker Scout scan that fails the build on HIGH or CRITICAL CVEs and emits an SBOM. The combined layered controls turn container breakout into a chain of unlikely steps, force timely base image refresh, and create a cryptographic chain via image signing that registry compromise cannot forge.

methodologies.name: dora-metrics
methodologies.description: DORA metrics (Deployment Frequency, Lead Time, Change Failure Rate, Time to Restore, Reliability) measure software delivery performance with five levels from low to elite. Use them to quantify engineering team productivity, identify bottlenecks, and guide improvement investments across deployment, testing, and monitoring. Elite teams deploy multiple times daily with <1 hour lead time and <15% failure rates, proving that throughput and stability are not trade-offs.

methodologies.name: finops
methodologies.description: FinOps (Financial Operations) optimizes cloud spending through visibility, continuous optimization, and operational practices, addressing $200B+ annual waste across cloud platforms. Implement it when cloud bills exceed expectations, to establish cloud cost ownership across teams, or to reduce spending while maintaining or improving performance. It delivers 10-20x ROI through rightsizing, commitment discounts, spot instances, and automated cost controls.

methodologies.name: finops-devops-cost-alerts
methodologies.description: Addresses retrospective cloud billing where overspend is only seen after the month closes by shifting cost feedback to near-real-time. Set up budget alerts at 50/75/90/100% of monthly budget per account, subscription, or project (with forecasted alerts at 100% and escalation at 90%+), enable native anomaly detection (AWS Cost Anomaly Detection, Azure Cost Management, GCP threshold alerts) at roughly 20% above a 7-day moving average for services over $500/month, and investigate within a 4-hour window using a structured runbook. Layer in reusable LLM prompts for monthly bill analysis, rightsizing review, Savings Plan recommendations, anomaly investigation, executive summaries, and team reports to compress days of pivot-table work into hours and run a daily/weekly/monthly/quarterly review cadence that keeps FinOps proactive instead of reactive.

methodologies.name: finops-devops-cost-commitments
methodologies.description: Solves the on-demand premium problem by mapping workload shape to the right commitment instrument: Reserved Instances for stable instance-family workloads (up to 75% off), Compute and EC2 Instance Savings Plans for flexible baseline (up to 72%), and Spot for fault-tolerant batch, CI runners, and ML training (70-90%). Apply only after at least 30 days of stable usage data and after rightsizing, sizing Savings Plan commitments against the 5th-percentile hourly spend rather than monthly averages and ramping coverage from 50-60% toward a 70-80% target. Spot requires checkpointing every 30 minutes, multi-AZ diversification across 3+ instance pools, and exclusion of stateful services; weekly RI/SP utilization monitoring with an 80% floor catches over-commitment before it becomes stranded spend.

methodologies.name: finops-devops-cost-kubernetes
methodologies.description: Targets Kubernetes clusters running uniform over-provisioned node pools at roughly 25% CPU and 40% memory utilization, where 75% of node spend is wasted. Replace single pools with workload-specific node pools (CPU-intensive c2/c3 or c5/c6, memory-intensive n2-highmem or r5/r6, general e2 or m5/t3) plus Cluster Autoscaler, add a Spot/preemptible pool with min=0 and a NoSchedule taint for batch and CI workloads, deploy VPA in Recommendation mode for 7 days before enabling Auto on stateless deployments, and after 30 days of autoscaling buy Committed Use Discounts or Reserved Node Groups on 80% of the observed stable baseline. The compounded levers raise utilization to roughly 55% CPU and 65% memory and cut a benchmark 500-node GKE cluster from $120k to $62k per month — about 48% savings.

methodologies.name: finops-devops-cost-rightsizing
methodologies.description: Tackles the 32% average cloud waste driven by over-allocation, lifted-and-shifted instance types, and idle resources that were never decommissioned. Use it whenever Compute Optimizer, Azure Advisor, or GCP Active Assist show CPU below 20% or memory below 30% over a 14-day window, when dev/staging RDS still runs Multi-AZ, or before any RI/Savings Plan commitment locks the wasteful baseline in. Rightsize non-production first with 20% headroom above peak (not average), enable autoscaling before downsizing variable workloads, consider Graviton or Ampere ARM for eligible web/container/batch workloads (20-40% better price-performance), schedule dev environments to office hours, run a monthly waste scan for unattached volumes, idle IPs, idle LBs, orphaned snapshots, and unused NAT Gateways with a 5-day approval window, and remove Multi-AZ from non-prod databases. Together these are the highest-ROI, lowest-risk first actions in any FinOps engagement.

methodologies.name: finops-devops-cost-tagging
methodologies.description: Solves the unallocated-spend problem where below-90% tag compliance pushes 10%+ of the bill into shared pools and makes per-team optimization invisible. Define a minimal four-tag mandatory schema (project, environment, owner, cost-center) with regex validation, enforce it at resource-creation time via AWS SCPs, Azure Policy at Management Group scope, and GCP organization label constraints rather than retroactive audits, and add Terraform module defaults so untagged resources cannot be created in IaC. Roll out in audit mode first, tag the top 20 untagged resources by cost, communicate two weeks before flipping to deny, and pair with billing exports (AWS CUR, Azure Cost Management, GCP BigQuery) for a unified multi-cloud dashboard. The target is 95%+ compliance, which is the prerequisite for every downstream FinOps action — rightsizing, commitments, chargeback — to actually attribute correctly.

methodologies.name: gcp
methodologies.description: Google Cloud Platform operations cover compute (GKE Autopilot, Cloud Run, Compute Engine), storage, databases, identity (Workload Identity Federation), and networking. Use it when building on GCP, optimizing GKE deployments, or implementing serverless architecture with Cloud Run. It provides cost optimization strategies (spot VMs, committed discounts), modern IAM patterns eliminating service account keys, and GKE best practices for simplified operations.

methodologies.name: gcp-gke-architecture
methodologies.description: Addresses the two highest-impact, irreversible GKE decisions — cluster mode (Autopilot vs Standard) and topology (zonal/regional/multi-cluster) — plus the node-pool strategy that determines bin-packing efficiency. Default to Autopilot regional for new clusters; use Standard only when GPU, privileged containers, or OS-level node config is required, and zonal only for dev/test below 99.9% SLA. For Standard, run a general on-demand pool, a Spot pool with min=0 and NoSchedule taints for batch (saving 70-90%), and a GPU or high-memory pool when workload demands it. Apply the supplied Terraform for a private regional cluster with Workload Identity, Binary Authorization, Dataplane V2, Managed Prometheus, network policy, shielded nodes, and weekly maintenance windows so production deployments inherit GKE best practice instead of reinventing it.

methodologies.name: gcp-landing-zone
methodologies.description: Establishes the governance layer once for a new GCP organization so subsequent workloads inherit security, IAM, networking, and FinOps controls automatically, instead of accumulating ad-hoc projects with overlapping CIDRs, missing audit logs, and primitive Owner/Editor grants. Apply when bootstrapping a new org, migrating from another cloud, or formalizing multi-environment project structure: build the folder hierarchy (Production/Staging/Development/Shared Infrastructure), configure Cloud Identity with SSO and MFA, disable service-account key creation, require OS Login, deny external VM IPs, restrict resource locations, enable uniform bucket access, and provision Shared VPC, Cloud NAT, Private Service Connect, billing exports, and budgets via the supplied Terraform module. The six-phase checklist (Planning, Identity, Hierarchy, Network, Security, Operations) gates production rollout and removes weeks of inconsistency from later workload onboarding.

methodologies.name: gcp-network-architecture
methodologies.description: Solves the IP-overlap and network-governance problems that block multi-project growth on GCP: poor planning leads to CIDR conflicts, undersized GKE pod ranges that cap cluster scale, exhausted VPC peering quotas, and uncontrolled firewall changes by service teams. Apply Shared VPC for any environment with more than one project, allocate a /16 per environment with /20 primary subnets, /18-/20 GKE pod secondary range, /24 services range, and /28 master CIDR, replace VPC peering for Google APIs with Private Service Connect (single internal IP, no peering quota or route leakage), use Cloud NAT for private egress, and enforce a hierarchical firewall policy with a base deny-all and allow rules by service tag (IAP, internal, health-checks). Centralized network admin in the host project plus VPC Flow Logs gives a single place to audit while service projects deploy workloads without the ability to modify firewall rules or create rogue peerings.

methodologies.name: gcp-storage-cdn
methodologies.description: Addresses two costly defaults: Cloud Storage objects sitting in STANDARD indefinitely (3-5x more expensive than COLDLINE) and global users hitting the origin directly with no edge caching. Pick the storage class by access frequency or use Autoclass when patterns are unknown, set lifecycle rules at bucket creation to transition to NEARLINE after 30 days, COLDLINE after 90, and ARCHIVE after 365, and limit object versions to 3-5. Front static content with a backend bucket plus Cloud CDN using CACHE_ALL_STATIC, default TTL 1h, max 1d, negative caching, and serve-while-stale for high availability during origin outages, then attach a managed SSL certificate, URL map, HTTPS proxy, and an HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect. The supplied Terraform delivers a complete static-hosting stack for SPAs, Gatsby/Hugo/Next.js builds, or backup archives.

methodologies.name: github-actions-cicd
methodologies.description: GitHub Actions integrates CI/CD directly into GitHub with workflows triggered by push, pull request, or schedule events, supporting matrix builds and reusable workflows. Use it for GitHub-hosted projects needing free CI/CD, multi-platform builds, or tight GitHub integration (issues, PRs, releases). It provides OIDC for credentialless cloud authentication, nested reusable workflows (up to 10 levels), and extensive marketplace actions reducing configuration effort.

methodologies.name: gitlab-cicd
methodologies.description: GitLab CI/CD uses `.gitlab-ci.yml` to define pipelines with stages, jobs, and rules, supporting DAG execution via `needs:` and complex monorepo workflows. Deploy it for GitLab-hosted projects, complex pipelines with parent-child relationships, or organizations needing integrated DevSecOps. It provides built-in container registry, environment tracking with automatic deployment URLs, review apps for safe changes, and automated security scanning.

methodologies.name: gitops
methodologies.description: GitOps uses Git repositories as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure, with agents (ArgoCD, Flux) continuously reconciling cluster state. Implement it for multi-cluster Kubernetes management, compliance-critical deployments, or when audit trails matter. It enables pull-based deployments (pull from clusters rather than push from CI), automatic drift detection, policy-driven RBAC, and progressive delivery patterns (canary, blue-green).

methodologies.name: grafana-dashboards
methodologies.description: Grafana dashboards visualize metrics from Prometheus, Loki, Tempo, and other sources with interactive queries and alerting. Use them to monitor system health, create runbooks for on-call teams, and provide real-time observability to stakeholders. It supports dashboard templating with variables, recording rules for faster queries, and alert annotations showing incident states on graphs for rapid incident response.

methodologies.name: helm-charts
methodologies.description: Helm packages Kubernetes manifests into reusable charts with templating, versioning, and dependency management. Use it for templating complex Kubernetes deployments, managing application releases, or distributing reusable configurations across teams. It enables version control of infrastructure, chart repositories for discovery, and umbrella charts that orchestrate multiple sub-charts for complete application stacks.

methodologies.name: jenkins-pipelines
methodologies.description: Advanced Jenkins pipeline patterns including declarative and scripted syntax, shared libraries, and Blue Ocean UI. Use declarative pipelines by default for CI/CD workflows, switching to scripted only for complex dynamic logic. Shared libraries centralize reusable code in global functions or classes, reducing duplication across teams and enabling consistent CI/CD practices.

methodologies.name: kubernetes
methodologies.description: Production-grade Kubernetes operations covering deployments, services, scaling, security, and resource management for versions 1.28+. Apply deployment strategies (rolling updates, blue-green, canary) appropriate to your workload, manage resources via requests and limits, and implement security contexts for least-privilege container access. Use Helm for package management and ArgoCD for GitOps-driven deployments.

methodologies.name: kubernetes-deployment
methodologies.description: Kubernetes deployment strategies—recreate, rolling update, blue-green, canary—each with distinct trade-offs between downtime, risk, and complexity. Choose based on your tolerance for disruption: rolling updates suit standard production services, blue-green enables instant rollback for critical systems, and canary with metric analysis minimizes blast radius for high-traffic platforms.

methodologies.name: nginx-configuration
methodologies.description: High-performance HTTP server and reverse proxy supporting web serving, SSL/TLS termination, load balancing, and caching. Optimize with sendfile, tcp_nopush, and gzip compression; harden security via strong ciphers, HSTS headers, and version hiding; and use upstream blocks with load balancing algorithms for distributing traffic across backend pools.

methodologies.name: prometheus-monitoring
methodologies.description: Open-source time-series monitoring using pull-based metrics collection, PromQL querying, and Alertmanager for alert routing and grouping. Implement best practices like bounded labels (no user IDs), recording rules for expensive queries, and alert rules that focus on symptoms rather than causes. Use Prometheus Operator for Kubernetes or federation for multi-cluster scenarios.

methodologies.name: secrets-management
methodologies.description: Secure handling of passwords, API keys, and certificates through dynamic, just-in-time credentials instead of static, long-lived secrets. Choose a tool (Vault for multi-cloud, AWS Secrets Manager for AWS-only, External Secrets Operator for Kubernetes) and implement automatic rotation, audit logging, and zero local secret storage via injection at runtime.

methodologies.name: security-as-code
methodologies.description: Embedding security controls into every phase of the software delivery lifecycle through policy as code (OPA, Kyverno), vulnerability scanning (Trivy, Checkov), and compliance automation. Shift from reactive security checkpoints to proactive rules enforced in CI/CD and admission control, enabling faster, safer deployments while maintaining compliance.

methodologies.name: ssl-tls-setup
methodologies.description: Configuration of TLS 1.2+ with strong cipher suites, certificate automation (Let's Encrypt ACME), and best practices including HSTS, CAA records, and certificate transparency monitoring. Automation is mandatory as certificate validity periods shrink: 45-day certs by 2028 require fully automated renewal, discovery, and monitoring infrastructure.

methodologies.name: terraform
methodologies.description: Infrastructure as Code using HCL syntax for declaring cloud resources, managing state in remote backends (S3, Terraform Cloud), and applying changes via plan-apply workflow. Organize code into modules for reusability, use remote state locking to prevent concurrent modifications, and implement security scanning (tfsec, Checkov) to catch policy violations before deployment.

methodologies.name: terraform-iac
methodologies.description: Terraform-based infrastructure provisioning with emphasis on remote state management, module composition, and testing frameworks (Terraform test, Terratest) for infrastructure validation. Separate environments into distinct directories or workspaces, use input validation blocks, and deploy via CI/CD with plan approval gates to maintain safety and auditability.

domains.description: Infrastructure provisioning and management: Docker containerization and Compose, Kubernetes deployments and Helm charts, Terraform and infrastructure-as-code patterns, AWS cloud services (EC2, ECS, Lambda, S3, networking), and GCP (Compute, Cloud Run, storage, networking). Handles modern infrastructure automation.

methodologies.name: aws-architecture-services
methodologies.description: Service selection guidance and integration patterns for AWS architecture, covering serverless vs containers decision (Lambda for variable workloads, ECS/EKS for steady-state), event-driven architecture with EventBridge, and Well-Architected framework application. 78% of teams now run hybrid architectures, choosing services per workload characteristics rather than all-or-nothing approaches.

methodologies.name: aws-cli-setup
methodologies.description: Provides the operational baseline for AWS CLI access on workstations and CI runners so authentication errors and accidental cross-account operations stop happening. Install AWS CLI v2 (v1 lacks SSO and IMDSv2), configure one named profile per account/environment, never overwrite the default to point at production, and use ~/.aws/config role_arn + source_profile chains for cross-account assume-role rather than copying long-lived production keys to laptops. In CI, inject short-lived STS credentials via AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, AWS_SESSION_TOKEN, and AWS_DEFAULT_REGION environment variables (which take precedence over the credentials file) instead of writing files to disk. EC2, ECS, and Lambda should use IAM instance/task/execution roles entirely — no CLI credentials file at all.

methodologies.name: aws-cost-optimization
methodologies.description: Addresses the four largest controllable AWS cost drivers — compute on-demand premium, storage tier drift, NAT Gateway and data-transfer leakage, and over-provisioned instance types — anchored on 30-day rolling Cost Explorer windows rather than misleading same-day data. Cover stable baseline with 1-year no-upfront Compute Savings Plans first (most flexible), layer EC2 Instance SPs or Regional RIs for the deeper-discount portion, leave 20-30% on demand for burst, and use Spot only for fault-tolerant batch, CI runners, or ML training. Mandate S3 lifecycle policies at bucket creation with Intelligent Tiering for variable-access data and Glacier for compliance archives; eliminate NAT processing charges with free Gateway endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB plus Interface endpoints for ECR and CloudWatch Logs in EKS/ECS VPCs; pick a DR tier (backup and restore, pilot light, warm standby, multi-site active-active) sized to actual RTO/RPO instead of paying for the highest tier by default.

methodologies.name: aws-ec2-ecs
methodologies.description: AWS compute services including EC2 instance types (general, compute, memory optimized), ECS Fargate task sizing, and best practices for security (IAM roles, non-root users, read-only filesystems), cost optimization (Graviton instances, Spot), and resilience (multi-AZ spread, health checks, auto-scaling). Right-size tasks to avoid over-provisioning and use Fargate Spot for fault-tolerant batch workloads.

methodologies.name: aws-ec2-provisioning
methodologies.description: Replaces ad-hoc EC2 launches and SSH-key sprawl with a reproducible launch-template + Auto Scaling Group pattern that satisfies modern security and compliance baselines. Every instance enforces IMDSv2 (HttpTokens=required, hop-limit 2) to block the SSRF credential-theft path that caused Capital One, mounts encrypted gp3 EBS root volumes, and attaches an IAM instance profile with AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore so Session Manager replaces inbound port 22 entirely. ASGs sit behind ELB health checks across at least two AZs with a target-tracking policy at 70% CPU and instance refresh at 75% min-healthy for zero-downtime AMI rollouts. Security groups are role-based per tier (ALB, app, database) and reference each other by group ID with descriptions, never CIDR for inter-tier traffic, and never 0.0.0.0/0 on 22 or 3389.

methodologies.name: aws-iam-practical-patterns
methodologies.description: Targets the most common root cause of AWS security incidents — overly permissive IAM policies — with concrete patterns instead of slogans. Write application policies that scope every Action to a minimum set and every Resource to specific ARNs (no Action: "*" or Resource: "*"), with Condition blocks on aws:ResourceTag, MFA, or IP. Replace EC2 access keys with instance roles that attach AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore plus least-privilege inline policies, and require cross-account AssumeRole trust policies to enforce aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent and a unique sts:ExternalId for third-party tools, scoping the principal to a specific role rather than account root. When delegating IAM role creation, attach a permission boundary that explicitly denies iam:* mutations and organizations:* — the only mechanism that prevents a delegated admin from creating a backdoor AdministratorAccess role.

methodologies.name: aws-iam-security-foundations
methodologies.description: Establishes the four-layer AWS security baseline — identity, encryption, detection, and network — required for every production environment because each layer addresses a failure mode the others cannot. Make IAM Identity Center the only human entry point with federated MFA and 1-8 hour session limits, eliminate long-lived access keys, and use IRSA (OIDC + service-account-scoped IAM roles) for EKS pod workloads instead of instance-profile sharing. Enforce S3 default encryption, account-level EBS encryption, RDS encryption at cluster creation, and customer-managed KMS keys with rotation for confidential data; store all credentials in Secrets Manager (never env vars or Parameter Store standard) with TLS 1.2 minimum on ALBs. Enable GuardDuty organization-wide with S3, Kubernetes audit log, and EBS malware protection, plus Security Hub with CIS Foundations and AWS Foundational Security Best Practices to surface drift, anomalies, and misconfiguration in one dashboard.

methodologies.name: aws-infra-prompt-library
methodologies.description: Provides parameterized LLM prompt templates for recurring AWS infrastructure work so generated Terraform or CloudFormation is usable on the first pass instead of requiring heavy editing. Apply when generating a three-tier VPC, role-based security groups, IAM roles (including IRSA for EKS), or Auto Scaling configuration; auditing IAM policies and security groups for least-privilege and exposure violations; debugging connectivity, IAM permission-denied errors, or EC2 instance issues; analyzing cost and planning Reserved capacity; producing architecture documentation, runbooks, and change requests; planning workload or database migrations; and preparing CIS, SOC2, PCI-DSS, or HIPAA compliance evidence. Each prompt names the inputs (region, environment, current config snippet) and the required output format, which is the difference between a generic example and ready-to-merge IaC.

methodologies.name: aws-lambda
methodologies.description: Serverless function management covering cold start optimization (SnapStart, provisioned concurrency, code optimization), idempotency for reliable retries, and event source mapping (SQS, Kinesis, DynamoDB Streams) with batch configuration. Initialize SDK clients outside handlers for reuse, use layers for shared dependencies, and monitor duration/concurrency to stay within service limits.

methodologies.name: aws-monitoring-observability
methodologies.description: Addresses the gap where failures are discovered by customers because the baseline observability stack was never set up. Provision CloudWatch dashboards and alarms for the minimum viable production set — EKS Container Insights pod CPU/memory, RDS CPU at 80% and DatabaseConnections at 80% of max, ALB 5xx counts, Lambda errors and duration P99, SQS dead-letter queue age — wired to a KMS-encrypted SNS topic that fans out to PagerDuty, OpsGenie, or Slack. Pair with X-Ray for distributed tracing, an organization-level CloudTrail with all-region coverage, KMS encryption, log-file validation, and an Object Lock S3 bucket in a log-archive account that workload accounts cannot delete from, and AWS Config plus Security Hub for drift, CIS, and AWS Foundational standards in one dashboard. All resources are Terraform-managed with reusable module variables so dashboards and alarms scale across environments without copy-paste.

methodologies.name: aws-multi-account-landing-zone
methodologies.description: Solves the blast-radius and governance problem of a single shared AWS account where one workload's IAM mistake or quota event affects every workload and audits cannot prove environment isolation. Bootstrap an Organizations structure with a workload-free management account (Organizations, billing, IAM Identity Center only), an OU hierarchy with Security, Infrastructure, Workloads (nested Prod/Non-Prod), Sandbox, Policy Staging, and Suspended OUs, and SCPs applied at OU level — never on individual accounts — to deny root-user actions, require S3 encryption, enforce IMDSv2, and restrict regions. Layer Control Tower for the packaged baseline, Account Factory for Terraform when account lifecycle should run through PRs, and Landing Zone Accelerator (pinned, not always-latest) for compliance framework support; centralize CloudTrail in a log-archive account and apply Tag Policies for project, environment, and cost-center attribution. The result is hard isolation, OU-level inheritance for new accounts, and auditable evidence for GDPR/HIPAA/PCI/SOC2.

methodologies.name: aws-networking
methodologies.description: VPC architecture with public/private/data tiers, security groups and NACLs for traffic control, and Transit Gateway for multi-VPC connectivity. Implement zero-trust networking by restricting traffic to necessary paths, using VPC endpoints for private AWS service access, and enabling VPC Flow Logs for auditing. New services like VPC Lattice simplify cross-VPC service communication.

methodologies.name: aws-s3-storage
methodologies.description: S3 bucket configuration for secure object storage including bucket policies for HTTPS enforcement, encryption options (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS), lifecycle policies for cost optimization, and cross-region replication for disaster recovery. Enable versioning with MFA delete, use Intelligent-Tiering for unknown access patterns, and block public access at account and bucket levels.

methodologies.name: aws-vpc-design
methodologies.description: Solves the lateral-movement risk of flat VPCs and the surprise NAT/data-transfer bills that dominate AWS networking costs. Design every production VPC as a minimum /16 with three subnet tiers (public, private, database) across at least three AZs — load balancers and NAT only in public, workloads only in private, RDS/ElastiCache only in database — using the terraform-aws-modules/vpc module. Run one NAT Gateway per AZ in production (single NAT acceptable in dev/staging) and enable VPC Flow Logs to S3, not CloudWatch, with REJECT or ALL capture for security forensics. Always enable free Gateway endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB, and add Interface endpoints for ECR (api+dkr), CloudWatch Logs, and Secrets Manager when ECS/EKS workloads pull images or fetch credentials at scale; tag subnets with kubernetes.io/role/elb and internal-elb up front so the AWS Load Balancer Controller works whenever EKS arrives.

methodologies.name: aws-vpc-three-tier
methodologies.description: Provides the executable, CLI-reproducible blueprint for building a production three-tier VPC from scratch when the higher-level design needs to be turned into actual resources. Create a /16 VPC with DNS enabled, an Internet Gateway, and 9 subnets (3 AZs x public/private/database with /24 each); deploy per-AZ NAT Gateways in public subnets to remove the AZ-crossing data-transfer charge and the single point of failure; attach a free S3 Gateway endpoint to all private route tables and Interface endpoints for ECR (api+dkr) and Secrets Manager to keep image pulls and credential fetches off the public internet; expose workloads through an internet-facing ALB with HTTPS using ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06, an ACM certificate, and an HTTP-to-HTTPS 301 redirect; turn on VPC Flow Logs with at least 30-day retention; and wire multi-VPC environments through a Transit Gateway in a shared-services account instead of building O(n²) peering.

methodologies.name: aws-well-architected-framework
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of ad hoc, inconsistent AWS architecture reviews by structuring assessments around the six Well-Architected pillars (Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability) so cross-team findings share a common vocabulary. Apply it before major releases or audits, during quarterly hygiene reviews, on pre-migration assessments, after multi-pillar incidents, or when inheriting an undocumented AWS environment. Reviews run as a read-only pipeline (Organizations, CloudTrail, Config, GuardDuty, Security Hub) that scores each pillar 1-5 with concrete evidence (ARNs, policy snippets) and emits remediation as Terraform diffs for human approval. The practical value is auditable, prioritized remediation plans split cleanly across FinOps, security, and SRE owners with each finding backed by reproducible evidence rather than opinion.

methodologies.name: cloud-run-autoscaling
methodologies.description: Addresses Cloud Run instance behavior under variable load by tuning the three primary levers — concurrency per instance, min/max instances, and billing mode — to balance cost, latency, and throughput. Use it when sizing a production service, eliminating cold-start latency, switching between request-based and instance-based billing, or troubleshooting latency spikes. Concurrency should be 1-10 for CPU-bound work and 80-1000 for I/O-bound services, min-instances >= 1 (with --cpu-boost) eliminates cold starts, and instance-based billing (--no-cpu-throttling) is mandatory for background workers, sidecars, or queue consumers that need CPU between HTTP requests. The practical value is preventing two failure modes: under-concurrency that forces wasteful scale-out and request-based billing that silently starves background goroutines.

methodologies.name: cloud-run-deployment
methodologies.description: Covers the full deployment surface of Cloud Run services — Dockerfile patterns (Python, Go distroless, Node), required container behaviour, gcloud production flags, Terraform google_cloud_run_v2_service usage, and Cloud Build CI/CD wiring. Apply it when shipping a new stateless HTTP service, building a Cloud Run target Dockerfile, configuring health probes, or migrating from Cloud Functions to a container runtime. Containers must read PORT, start in under 10 seconds, handle SIGTERM, pull images from Artifact Registry (not deprecated gcr.io), inject secrets via Secret Manager, and run under a dedicated service account with restricted ingress. The practical value is repeatable production deployments where cold starts are reliable rather than flaky and credentials never leak into images.

methodologies.name: cloud-run-jobs
methodologies.description: Solves run-to-completion batch workloads on Cloud Run by orchestrating up to 10,000 parallel tasks with 168-hour timeouts and automatic retries, replacing ad hoc cron-on-VM patterns. Use it for ETL pipelines, report generation, data exports, ML training preparation, database migrations, backfills, or scheduled tasks too complex for Cloud Functions. The canonical pattern partitions work via CLOUD_RUN_TASK_INDEX and CLOUD_RUN_TASK_COUNT (last task handles remainder), enforces task idempotency so retries are safe, sets explicit task-timeout, and uses Cloud Scheduler with an OIDC-authenticated POST to the v1 jobs:run endpoint for cron triggering. The practical value is parallel batch execution without external orchestration, with checkpointing to GCS for tasks longer than 30 minutes and timezone-aware scheduling.

methodologies.name: cloud-run-monitoring
methodologies.description: Closes the observability gap on Cloud Run by combining structured JSON logs to stdout, the built-in Cloud Monitoring metrics (request count by response_code_class, latency percentiles, instance count, CPU/memory), Cloud Trace propagation via X-Cloud-Trace-Context, and BigQuery billing analysis for cost attribution. Apply it when standing up production logging, defining SLOs, building dashboards and alerting policies, debugging latency or scaling issues, or diagnosing failed Job tasks. Logs must include severity, message, and a logging.googleapis.com/trace field for request correlation, with alerting on 5xx rate and p99 latency configured before launch. The practical value is field-based filtering and log-based metrics that make incidents debuggable at scale where unstructured text would not be searchable.

methodologies.name: cloud-run-traffic-management
