methodologies.description: Decouples Cloud Run deployment from release by using --no-traffic + --tag to ship a new revision to a stable preview URL, then shifting traffic via update-traffic for blue-green or canary rollouts. Use it for any zero-downtime production release, pre-release validation against a tagged URL, gradual canary stages (5% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100%), instant rollbacks via --to-revisions, and A/B testing across revisions. Blue-green flips 100% in one atomic call (safer for schema or contract changes), canary monitors each stage for at least 10 minutes against error-rate and p99-latency thresholds, and never run canaries longer than 24 hours. The practical value is rollback measured in seconds rather than rebuild cycles, with the previous revision retained warm to absorb traffic again.

methodologies.name: cloud-run-vpc-access
methodologies.description: Connects Cloud Run services to private VPC resources (Cloud SQL, AlloyDB, Memorystore, on-prem via Interconnect) via Direct VPC egress instead of legacy Serverless VPC Access connectors, and adds multi-container sidecars for Cloud SQL Proxy and OpenTelemetry collectors. Apply it when reaching private databases, restricting ingress to internal load balancers, migrating from connectors, or adding observability/security sidecars. Direct VPC egress requires a /26+ subnet, eliminates the 200 Mbps connector bottleneck, and scales linearly per instance; multi-container deployments need the run.googleapis.com/container-dependencies annotation, sidecar startup probes, and instance-based billing (cpu_idle=false) so sidecars are not CPU-starved between requests. The practical value is higher throughput, lower cost, and tighter ingress controls (internal / internal-and-cloud-load-balancing / all) layered with Cloud Armor.

methodologies.name: docker
methodologies.description: Container development and deployment emphasizing immutable images, layer caching optimization, and security by default (non-root users, read-only filesystems, dropped capabilities). Use multi-stage builds to separate build and runtime stages, scan with Docker Scout or Trivy before pushing, and leverage BuildKit features like secret mounts and cache persistence for efficient builds.

methodologies.name: gce-autoscaling
methodologies.description: Configures GCE autoscalers for Managed Instance Groups across five signal types (CPU, load-balancing utilization, custom Cloud Monitoring metrics, schedules, predictive ML forecast) with scale-in controls that prevent flapping. Use it for any MIG-backed service with variable traffic, HTTP frontends behind GCLB, queue-draining workers, predictable business-hour patterns, or apps with initialization longer than 2 minutes that benefit from predictive pre-warming. Production rules: cooldown_period > VM startup + warmup, target CPU 60% (never 80%+), scale_in_control time_window_sec >= 600 with max 20% reduction per window, min_replicas >= 1 zonal / >= 3 regional, and max_replicas capped at expected peak load divided by 60% utilization plus a 20% margin. The practical value is fast, right-sized capacity changes that avoid both runaway scaling and flapping loops during brief traffic dips.

methodologies.name: gce-instance-templates
methodologies.description: Treats GCE instance templates as the immutable, versioned source of truth for VM configuration so Managed Instance Groups produce identical replicas, autoscaling works, and rolling updates are safe. Apply it when creating any MIG, deploying multi-zone identical fleets, rolling out app version updates, running canary deployments via dual templates, or building custom baked images to cut startup time. Templates must use image families (e.g. ubuntu-os-cloud/ubuntu-2404-lts-amd64) instead of pinned versions so OS patches arrive automatically, carry a versioned name (date or semver), bind a dedicated service account with cloud-platform scope (never the default Compute SA), and include lifecycle { create_before_destroy = true } in Terraform so MIG updates do not break. The practical value is reproducible VM provisioning with zero-downtime rolling updates and least-privilege blast-radius defaults.

methodologies.name: gce-managed-instance-groups
methodologies.description: Operates Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) for self-healing, zero-downtime VM fleets that integrate with Google Cloud Load Balancing — the foundation of any stateless production service on GCE. Use it for stateless services needing more than one VM, autoscaling APIs, batch worker fleets, custom GKE node pools, and blue-green/canary deploys via rolling-action start-update with --canary-version. Production MIGs MUST be regional with EVEN distribution across at least 3 zones, configure autohealing with HTTP health checks and an initial_delay_sec exceeding full startup + warmup, and use update_policy type=PROACTIVE with max_unavailable_fixed=0 and max_surge_fixed>=1 so capacity never drops during updates. The practical value is automatic recreation of failed VMs, surviving a zone outage at 66% capacity instead of 0%, and updates that complete deterministically rather than waiting for opportunistic recreation.

methodologies.name: gce-spot-vms
methodologies.description: Uses Spot VMs (up to 91% discount) for fault-tolerant workloads via the modern provisioning_model = SPOT (replacing legacy preemptible = true with its 24-hour limit), combined with shutdown scripts that flush state to GCS within the 30-second preemption window. Apply it for batch pipelines with checkpointing, dev/test environments, GKE burst capacity via --spot node pools with taints, stateless microservices in autohealing MIGs, and CI/CD build agents — never for single-instance databases or 100% production fleets without a standard VM floor. Required configuration: provisioning_model = SPOT, instance_termination_action = STOP or DELETE, automatic_restart = false, shutdown-script that finishes in 30s, regional MIG spanning at least 2 zones, and a 30% standard VM baseline for SLO-bearing services. The practical value is 60-91% compute savings while maintaining capacity through zone-level preemption events via multi-zone placement and standard-VM floors.

methodologies.name: gcp-arch-patterns
methodologies.description: Production GCP patterns for GKE clusters, Cloud SQL high availability, CDN with Cloud Storage, and microservices architectures. Deploy regional GKE for production HA, use direct VPC egress instead of connectors for better throughput, and combine Cloud Run for event-driven workloads with GKE for long-running services in a hybrid strategy.

methodologies.name: gcp-billing-cost
methodologies.description: Builds the cost-control foundation for any GCP project: budget alerts at multiple thresholds (50%, 90%, 100%), BigQuery billing exports for chargeback queries, and a mandatory label taxonomy (env, team, cost_center, managed_by) enforced through org policy. Use it when setting up a new organization or project, implementing chargeback by team or cost center, configuring alerts before launching production workloads, or running cost-optimization audits with the Recommender API. The console UI cannot filter by labels or aggregate across folders, so BigQuery exports queried via Looker Studio dashboards are mandatory at any scale beyond a single team. The practical value is preventing surprise overspend through early alerts and producing per-team chargeback reports that finance can rely on, with committed-use discount analysis driven by the same exports.

methodologies.name: gcp-cloud-run-serverless
methodologies.description: Captures production-grade Cloud Run and Cloud Functions Gen2 patterns in a single security-first checklist: dedicated per-service service accounts (never the default compute SA with editor permissions), Secret Manager for all credentials, VPC connector with --vpc-egress=all-traffic for private resource access, --min-instances=1 to eliminate cold starts, --execution-environment=gen2, and tag-based blue-green traffic splitting. Apply it when deploying containerized stateless APIs, setting up Gen2 functions for HTTP or Pub/Sub triggers, configuring canary releases, or auditing serverless security posture. The methodology bundles security, deployment, and setup checklists that translate directly into gcloud flags. The practical value is preventing the most common production mistakes — broad default-SA permissions, plaintext secrets, public ingress, and big-bang rollouts — through opinionated defaults that map one-to-one to deploy commands.

methodologies.name: gcp-compute-gke
methodologies.description: Hardens Compute Engine and GKE clusters by replacing insecure defaults: Shielded VMs with secure boot/vTPM/integrity monitoring, OS Login instead of SSH keys, no public IPs (IAP for SSH), and either GKE Autopilot (recommended) or Standard with Workload Identity, private nodes, network policy, Dataplane V2 (Cilium), shielded nodes, and master-authorized-networks. Apply it when provisioning VMs for stateful or legacy workloads, building regional MIGs with autoscaling, creating new GKE clusters, or hardening existing Standard clusters. Autopilot enforces Google's pod security baseline by default and removes node management overhead; Standard mode demands explicit hardening flags but offers DaemonSet and host-network access. The practical value is closing distinct attack surfaces (rootkit, key leakage, internet exposure, lateral movement) through compounding controls rather than relying on any single layer.

methodologies.name: gcp-iam-design
methodologies.description: Designs least-privilege IAM at every level of the GCP hierarchy and eliminates service-account-key-file risk via Workload Identity Federation for GKE workloads. Apply it when designing role assignments for new orgs/folders/projects, setting up service accounts for GKE pods that need GCP APIs, auditing existing bindings for over-permissive roles, or implementing break-glass procedures for emergency org admin access. Hard rules: never grant basic roles (Owner/Editor/Viewer) in production, never use the default compute SA, never share service accounts across workloads, and never mount JSON key files in pods — bind KSA to GSA via roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser instead. The practical value is dramatically reduced blast radius when credentials leak, plus auditable per-workload activity logs and IAM conditions that scope bindings to specific resources or time windows.

methodologies.name: gcp-llm-prompts
methodologies.description: Provides parameterized LLM prompt templates for GCP infrastructure tasks — Terraform generation (VPC, firewall, service accounts, Cloud Run, GKE), security audits, IAM reviews, connectivity and Cloud Run troubleshooting, cost optimization, migration planning, runbook authoring, and compliance evidence collection. Use it when delegating GCP work to an agent that needs explicit context (project, region, environment, constraints) to produce least-privilege, production-ready Terraform or gcloud output rather than insecure defaults like public IPs and primitive roles. Each prompt encodes required security constraints (no SA keys, no basic roles, Secret Manager for credentials, VPC connector for private access) as part of its structure plus a standard set of placeholder variables. The practical value is consistent agent output that does not require post-hoc security cleanup, with prompts grouped by lifecycle phase from generation through troubleshooting and audit.

methodologies.name: gcp-networking
methodologies.description: Design and manage Google Cloud Platform VPC networks, subnets, and connectivity. Covers VPC modes, firewall rules, Cloud NAT, and load balancing strategies to ensure secure, scalable network infrastructure with proper isolation and traffic control.

methodologies.name: gcp-networking-vpc
methodologies.description: Replaces the permissive default VPC with a custom four-tier network (public, private application, GKE with secondary ranges for pods/services, database) hardened by least-privilege firewall rules, IAP-only SSH (35.235.240.0/20), Cloud NAT for private outbound, a global HTTPS load balancer with Google-managed SSL, and Cloud Armor WAF with OWASP rules and rate limiting. Apply it when bootstrapping a new project, replacing the default VPC, setting up a public HTTPS endpoint, layering WAF protection, or auditing firewall rules for accidental public exposure. All firewall rules MUST use network tags rather than CIDR allowlists, an explicit deny-all-ingress at priority 65534 keeps the implicit deny visible in flow logs, and Private Google Access plus VPC Flow Logs cover GCP API access and traffic monitoring. The practical value is defense in depth where Cloud Armor blocks layer-7 attacks at the edge before they reach VPC firewalls or IAM.

methodologies.name: gcp-org-policies
methodologies.description: Establishes a security baseline through GCP organization policies — coarse guardrails at org or folder level that override project settings regardless of IAM permissions. Apply them at minimum on every new organization: gcp.resourceLocations (data residency), compute.vmExternalIpAccess (deny external IPs with DMZ folder exception), iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation (force Workload Identity), compute.requireOsLogin (centralized SSH via IAM), and storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess (no ACL-based misconfigurations). Use them for new org bootstrapping, hardening existing orgs where teams created resources without constraints, meeting GDPR/HIPAA/SOC2 residency requirements, and enforcing label taxonomy via custom constraints (custom.requireProjectLabels). The practical value is preventing the most common GCP misconfigurations by construction — IAM controls who can do what, org policies control what configurations are even possible — with folder-level policies offering targeted exception patterns.

methodologies.name: gcp-overview-cli
methodologies.description: Covers the GCP service catalogue, gcloud CLI installation, authentication modes (interactive, service-account key, ADC), named configurations per environment, CI/CD deployment patterns (Cloud Build -> Artifact Registry -> Cloud Run / GKE), cross-region disaster recovery (Cloud SQL replicas, snapshots, multi-region buckets), and cost optimization via the Recommender API and BigQuery billing exports. Use it when onboarding a new project or developer, establishing repeatable build/push/deploy pipelines, choosing the right compute tier (Compute Engine vs GKE Autopilot vs Cloud Run vs Cloud Functions Gen2), or running weekly idle-resource and IAM-overgrant audits. Named gcloud configurations (one per environment) and CLOUDSDK_* environment variables prevent accidental cross-environment commands in CI. The practical value is consistent CLI ergonomics, correct service selection that avoids migration debt, and automated waste detection through Recommender rather than manual cost spelunking.

methodologies.name: gcp-resource-hierarchy
methodologies.description: Designs the four-level GCP tree (Organization -> Folder -> Project -> Resource) so IAM, org policies, and cost attribution flow downward correctly from day one, avoiding costly later restructuring. Apply it when bootstrapping a new organization, migrating standalone projects into an org, planning multi-environment deployments, or auditing an existing hierarchy for policy-inheritance issues. Hard rules: keep folder nesting at 3 levels or fewer, separate dev/staging/prod into different projects (not folders inside one project) since projects are the billing and API-enablement boundary, follow a deterministic naming pattern ({type}-{env}-{app}-{component}), and require env/team/cost_center/managed_by labels enforced via custom org-policy constraint. Reference patterns are provided for enterprise, startup, and multi-team layouts. The practical value is a hierarchy that stays auditable as the organization scales, with bootstrap and shared-services folders separated from environment folders for clean delegation.

methodologies.name: gcp-security-iam
methodologies.description: Provides a GCP security framework that solves the customer side of Google Cloud's shared responsibility model: IAM least privilege, keyless authentication via Workload Identity Federation, perimeter-based data exfiltration prevention with VPC Service Controls, CMEK encryption, and audit logging. Apply it when bootstrapping a new GCP project, removing service account key files from CI/CD or GKE workloads, adding VPC-SC perimeters around Cloud Storage or BigQuery, or running quarterly IAM reviews. The practical value is concrete: WIF eliminates leaked-key incidents in GitHub Actions and pods, IAM conditions and Recommender drive permissions to actual usage, and VPC-SC dry-run validation prevents the silent-outage failure mode that plagues premature enforcement. Includes ready-to-use IAM, data security, and monitoring/auditing checklists tied to 2025-2026 GCP capabilities.

methodologies.name: gcp-storage
methodologies.description: Configure and optimize Google Cloud Storage for different access patterns and cost tiers. Addresses storage classes, lifecycle policies, integrations with compute services, and recent innovations like Rapid Storage and Anywhere Cache to balance performance and expense.

methodologies.name: gcp-terraform-templates
methodologies.description: Delivers a production-ready Terraform HCL module set for GCP so teams stop hand-copying gcloud commands and missing critical flags like deletion_protection, enable_private_endpoint, or workload_pool. Modules cover a four-tier VPC with Cloud NAT, least-privilege firewall rules, per-workload service accounts, Cloud Run v2 with probes and secret bindings, private GKE Autopilot with Workload Identity, and Cloud SQL PostgreSQL with private IP, PITR, and Secret Manager integration, plus a complete root main.tf composing them all. Use it when bootstrapping a new GCP project, adding a Cloud Run/GKE/Cloud SQL component to an existing root, or standardizing service account and IAM patterns across teams. The value is encoded best practices (Shielded VMs, CMEK, regional vs zonal sizing per environment) plus reproducibility through pinned provider constraints and GCS state with locking.

methodologies.name: helm-advanced
methodologies.description: Apply advanced Helm templating patterns including library charts, hooks, tests, and umbrella chart composition. Enables complex Kubernetes deployments with reusable components, lifecycle management, and production-ready multi-chart orchestration.

methodologies.name: helm-basics
methodologies.description: Master Helm package management for Kubernetes including chart structure, templating, releases, and repositories. Provides the foundation for deploying complex applications with configuration management, dependency handling, and standardized deployment workflows.

methodologies.name: iac-basics
methodologies.description: Understand Infrastructure as Code principles, tools comparison, and GitOps integration for declarative infrastructure. Covers idempotency, state management, testing strategies, and tool selection between Terraform, CloudFormation, and alternatives across cloud providers.

methodologies.name: iac-patterns-cicd
methodologies.description: Defines the canonical six-stage Terraform CI/CD pipeline (fmt, validate, tflint, security scan with checkov or tfsec, plan with PR comment, apply on merge) so teams replace ad-hoc terraform apply with a reviewable, audited contract. Apply it on any Terraform project with more than one contributor, multi-environment deployments, compliance-bound infrastructure, or production stacks needing approval gates and scheduled drift detection. Practical wins include OIDC-based credential-free authentication that swaps long-lived IAM keys for 15-minute tokens, GitHub Environments approval gates that block accidental production deploys, matrix plans that surface dev/staging/prod diffs in a single PR, and daily drift-detection jobs that alert when reality diverges from declared state without auto-remediating. Comes with a complete GitHub Actions workflow, a developer Makefile mirroring the CI stages, and a security checklist.

methodologies.name: iac-patterns-composition
methodologies.description: Covers the layer above individual modules: how environments, teams, and components share Terraform code while remaining independently deployable. Four patterns are mapped to organizational fit: Terramod for multi-environment composition with isolated state, Landing Zone for self-service compliant accounts, Service Catalog for vetted multi-resource templates, and Hub-Spoke for shared services consumed by isolated workload accounts. Apply it when designing state isolation boundaries, adding a second environment, onboarding multiple teams onto a standardized cloud baseline, or splitting a monolithic root module to reduce blast radius. Practical value is concrete directory layouts (environments/dev|staging|prod plus modules/ and shared/), Terragrunt root configs that generate provider and backend per child, and rules forcing per-environment state plus per-component splits where change rates differ — preventing the worst class of incident where a dev apply touches prod resources.

methodologies.name: iac-patterns-dry
methodologies.description: Eliminates the four most common classes of Terraform duplication that cause silent environment drift: scattered inline expressions, copy-pasted resource blocks, repeated nested blocks, and duplicated provider/backend files across environments. The fix per class: locals for naming, common tags, CIDR derivations, and environment conditionals; for_each on typed maps instead of count; dynamic blocks for ingress rules, lifecycle rules, and similar nested structures; and Terragrunt generate blocks that produce backend.tf and provider.tf from path-derived locals. Use it when you spot three or more near-identical resource blocks, are about to refactor toward a module extraction, or are setting up a third or later environment. The concrete value is fewer hidden divergences, safer additions and removals (for_each preserves resource identity by key), and a single-edit propagation model so a tag rename or region change doesn't require touching every file.

methodologies.name: iac-patterns-module-design
methodologies.description: Frames Terraform module authoring as a deliberate choice among five canonical patterns rather than ad-hoc accumulation: Base for single-resource encapsulation with locked security defaults, Wrapper for non-overridable org-policy enforcement on top of a community module, Facade for simplified stack interfaces hiding multi-module wiring, Factory for typed-map driven multi-resource creation via for_each, and Composite for co-deployed groups that still expose all outputs. Apply it before writing HCL for any new reusable module or when refactoring copy-pasted blocks into shared code. The practical value is that each pattern has clear decision criteria and matching design rules — single responsibility, semantic versioning with pinned constraints, dependency injection over internal data sources, validated inputs with sensible defaults, minimal documented outputs — backed by working examples (S3 base module, RDS wrapper enforcing org policy, web-application facade) and explicit antipatterns to avoid.

methodologies.name: iac-patterns-testing
methodologies.description: Defines a four-level testing pyramid for Terraform that maps each test class to the failure mode it actually catches: static analysis (fmt, validate, tflint, checkov/tfsec) for structural and policy errors in seconds, native terraform test with .tftest.hcl files and mock providers for plan-only contract validation, Terratest in Go for integration verification against real cloud resources with deferred Destroy, and OPA/Conftest or Sentinel for policy-as-code compliance checks against plan JSON. Use it for any reusable module before publishing, security-sensitive modules creating IAM or networks, modules consumed by multiple teams, or pre-merge CI gates. The practical value is calibrated cost-versus-confidence: cheap static gates run on every PR, plan-only unit tests validate validation rules and outputs without cloud credentials, integration tests catch IAM propagation and eventual-consistency bugs only visible after apply, and policy tests turn compliance wikis into executable rules.

methodologies.name: k8s-basics
methodologies.description: Learn Kubernetes fundamentals including core concepts, resources, namespaces, and kubectl operations. Establishes essential knowledge for container orchestration with focus on resource management, security, and production best practices.

methodologies.name: k8s-canary-progressive
methodologies.description: Replaces the rolling update's all-or-nothing exposure with traffic-controlled progressive delivery, limiting blast radius when a new version has bugs. Three implementation tiers are covered: manual canary using two Deployments behind a shared Service selector with replica-ratio weighting, Nginx Ingress canary annotations for percentage-based or header-based routing (the latter ideal for QA testing in production), and Argo Rollouts as the 2025-2026 standard with step-based promotion, AnalysisTemplate queries against Prometheus or Datadog, and automatic rollback when success conditions fail. Apply it to risky production releases, features needing real-traffic validation, regulated workloads, or GitOps environments wanting automated promotion. The concrete value is a worked Argo Rollouts manifest with 10/30/50/80/100% steps gated by a Prometheus success-rate query plus blue-green configuration, command cheat sheets, and a canary readiness checklist.

methodologies.name: k8s-deployment-workloads
methodologies.description: Establishes the production baseline for Kubernetes workloads: choose Deployment for stateless services and StatefulSet only when stable network identity, per-pod persistent storage, or ordered startup is required, then make every container set CPU/memory requests and limits, all three health probes (startup, liveness, readiness), pod anti-affinity, and topology spread constraints. Apply it when writing a new manifest, deciding workload type, auditing existing services for missing probes or limits, or setting up scheduling constraints. Practical value comes from the workload-selection table (PostgreSQL/Redis/Elasticsearch/Kafka as StatefulSet; web/worker/frontend as Deployment), probe-timing guidelines that prevent slow-start restart loops and hide behind separate /health/live and /health/ready endpoints, the rule that forbids latest tags and missing limits (the most common cause of cluster instability), and ready-to-copy YAML for a 3-replica PostgreSQL StatefulSet with headless Service and anti-affinity.

methodologies.name: k8s-limitrange
methodologies.description: Solves two Kubernetes namespace-level problems with a single resource: developer-omitted resources blocks (which silently produce BestEffort QoS and destabilize the cluster under memory pressure) and individual containers requesting too much or too little (which cause unschedulable pods or immediate OOMKill). A Container-type LimitRange supplies admission-time defaults plus min/max bounds and an optional maxLimitRequestRatio cap on overcommit, with separate Pod-type and PersistentVolumeClaim-type limits available. Apply it to every namespace in a production or shared cluster, when developers frequently forget resource specs, when a container is being OOMKilled at startup, or when one container is monopolizing a node. The practical value is calibrated profiles for development, staging, and production (defaults, max, ratios), the rule that defaultRequest stays below default to produce Burstable rather than accidental Guaranteed QoS, and explicit antipatterns including identical values across environments and oversized defaults that drain ResourceQuota.

methodologies.name: k8s-resource-quota
methodologies.description: Pairs with LimitRange to give Kubernetes namespaces aggregate compute, storage, and object-count caps so a runaway team or scaling bug cannot consume the entire cluster. Quotas govern requests.cpu/memory and limits.cpu/memory plus pod, service, secret, configmap, and PVC counts, with scopeSelector enabling separate caps for BestEffort batch versus Guaranteed/Burstable production pods or for PriorityClass-based reservations. Apply it to every shared, production, staging, or team namespace, after a scaling incident, when implementing chargeback, or when a namespace needs object-sprawl limits. Practical value includes the rule that both requests.* and limits.* must be set together to avoid accounting mismatches, object-count caps that block infinite-retry pod explosions which compute caps alone miss, sizing proportional to team need with 15-20% cluster headroom, and the recommendation to start generous and tune down based on 30-day kube_resourcequota observations rather than guesses.

methodologies.name: k8s-resource-requests-limits
methodologies.description: Gets per-container resources right by tying requests and limits to observed P50 and P99 metrics rather than guesses, since 2025 benchmarks show 99.94% of clusters are over-provisioned at just 10% average CPU utilization. The methodology covers units and fields (millicores, binary memory suffixes, never decimal), the QoS class system that controls eviction order (Guaranteed for critical stateful workloads via requests==limits, Burstable for standard variable workloads, BestEffort banned from production), right-sizing from VPA recommendations, and JVM-specific alignment of -Xmx with limits.memory. Apply it on every production container, when diagnosing repeated OOMKills or >10% CPU throttling, when pods fail to schedule with Insufficient resources, or when right-sizing with Goldilocks/Kubecost/CAST AI/VPA. Practical value: concrete YAML for Burstable APIs, Guaranteed databases, and JVM services, a Prometheus metric and alerting list, plus prompts for right-sizing from observed metrics.

methodologies.name: k8s-rolling-update
methodologies.description: Locks in zero-downtime deployments for Kubernetes Deployments by combining maxUnavailable: 0, maxSurge: 1, minReadySeconds 10-30s, and a meaningful readiness probe so new pods cannot be marked Available before they actually serve traffic. Compares Rolling Update against Recreate, Blue-Green, and Canary so teams pick the right tool, and includes pre/during/post update checklists, a full kubectl rollout command reference, and a documented rollback procedure with the triggers that should fire it (>1% error rate, >10% P99 latency, crash loops, broken functionality, security findings). Apply it for any image, config, or env-var update on a Deployment, when tuning rollout speed for high-replica services, when debugging a stuck rollout, or when documenting rollback. The concrete value is the explicit zero-downtime formula plus a fast-rollout variant for large Deployments and a bad example showing how missing minReadySeconds creates a brief outage with fast-crashing apps.

methodologies.name: k8s-scaling-availability
methodologies.description: Pairs HorizontalPodAutoscaler v2 (CPU, memory, custom or external metrics) with PodDisruptionBudget to keep production services healthy under both load changes and voluntary disruption. The HPA configuration uses asymmetric scale behavior — immediate scaleUp with stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0 and aggressive policies, conservative scaleDown over 300s with at most 10% or 2 pods removed per minute — to absorb spikes without thrashing. PDBs use minAvailable or maxUnavailable (never both) sized to replica count so kubectl drain proceeds one pod at a time without violating the budget or deadlocking rolling updates. Apply it when adding autoscaling, protecting a service during cluster maintenance or node drains, tuning scale-down conservatism, or wiring KEDA for queue-depth or HTTP-rate scaling. Concrete value: full HPA v2 manifest with combined CPU/memory targets and behavior block, a PDB sizing guide for 2/3/5+ replicas, and verification commands.

methodologies.name: k8s-security-hardening
methodologies.description: Implements five-layer defense-in-depth so a compromised container cannot pivot to the Kubernetes API, adjacent services, or cloud metadata: namespace Pod Security Standards labeled enforce: restricted, full container securityContext (runAsNonRoot, non-root UID, readOnlyRootFilesystem, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities drop ALL, seccompProfile RuntimeDefault), dedicated ServiceAccounts with automountServiceAccountToken: false and minimal Role/RoleBinding (never ClusterRole), secrets via secretKeyRef or external vault rather than ConfigMaps or plain env, and default-deny NetworkPolicy with explicit ingress, egress, and the often-forgotten DNS UDP 53 rule. Apply it to every production deployment, namespace setup, security audit, zero-trust rollout, or secrets review. Practical value is concrete YAML for each layer, antipatterns showing how secrets leak via ConfigMaps and kubectl describe, and a pre-production checklist tying together Kyverno/OPA Gatekeeper/Polaris/kubeconform tooling.

methodologies.name: terraform
methodologies.description: Manage multi-cloud infrastructure at scale using Terraform's declarative language, state management, and modules. Addresses remote backends, security, CI/CD integration, and workspace strategies for team collaboration.

methodologies.name: terraform-basics
methodologies.description: Master Terraform fundamentals including HCL syntax, providers, resources, and the plan-apply workflow. Establishes core competency for infrastructure provisioning with proper file organization and meta-argument usage.

methodologies.name: terraform-modules-composition
methodologies.description: Covers how root modules assemble child modules into a complete system through three patterns: flat composition wiring outputs to inputs explicitly (no sibling data-source lookups that create implicit ordering bugs), hierarchical composition that hides internal multi-module wiring behind an intermediate stack with a clean caller interface, and the factory pattern using for_each over typed maps to deploy N near-identical instances safely. Adds DRY techniques (locals for common tags merged into resource tags, environment config maps consolidating dev/staging/prod differences) and a workspace warning that production isolation belongs in separate directories with separate backends, not workspaces. Apply it when assembling VPC plus compute plus database, deploying microservice or per-team variants, or reducing copy-paste across module calls. Practical value: working examples of each pattern, a count-versus-for_each comparison showing how list reordering forces destruction/recreation, and an environment_config local that puts all environment differences in one block.

methodologies.name: terraform-modules-prompts
methodologies.description: Provides a structured LLM prompt library for AI-assisted Terraform module work, covering the full lifecycle: generation (from-scratch modules, converting inline resources, child modules for parent composition), review (security, best practices, cost/performance), refactoring (splitting mega-modules, adding validation, modernizing legacy code), troubleshooting (errors, state drift, circular dependencies), documentation (README and ADR), testing (native terraform test and Terratest), migration (inline-to-module with state preservation, version-to-version), composition design, and cost optimization. Apply it when generating an unfamiliar resource type, reviewing a module against security or best-practice baselines, or producing documentation and tests at scale. The practical value is that each prompt is fill-in-the-blank with required context fields (resource type, provider, features, constraints, paste-target sections) so output captures validation rules, sensitive markings, and version constraints rather than the generic boilerplate unstructured prompts produce.

methodologies.name: terraform-modules-security
methodologies.description: Sets the minimum quality bar for production-grade Terraform modules across four areas: secrets management (no hardcoded credentials in .tf or .tfvars, secrets sourced from Vault or cloud secret managers, sensitive flag on variables and outputs, .gitignore covering tfstate and sensitive tfvars, encrypted state backend), IAM least-privilege (no wildcard actions without justification, role-based not user-based, resource-level ARNs not wildcards), network security (no 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on non-LB security groups, databases in private subnets only), and a layered testing/CI strategy (terraform fmt + validate + tflint + Checkov/tfsec + plan + gated apply). Apply it when authoring or reviewing modules touching IAM, networking, encryption, or secrets, or when setting up a CI pipeline. Practical value: a complete GitHub Actions workflow with environment-gated production apply, a terraform test that exercises a CIDR validation block, and a code-review red-flag checklist (mega-modules, provider blocks in children, hardcoded account IDs).

methodologies.name: terraform-modules-structure
methodologies.description: Defines the canonical Terraform module layout — main.tf for resources and module blocks only, variables.tf with descriptions/types/defaults/validation, outputs.tf with descriptions and sensitive flagging, versions.tf for required_version and required_providers, optional locals.tf and data.tf — plus the design principles that prevent technical debt: single responsibility, no mega-modules over 500 lines, explicit input/output contracts instead of implicit cross-module references, sensible defaults with validation, and never embedding provider blocks in child modules. Distinguishes Root, Child, and Published module roles and recommends a project layout separating environments/, modules/, and global/. Apply it when authoring a new reusable module, refactoring a flat root that grew beyond one concern, onboarding engineers or agents to module conventions, or publishing to a registry. Practical value is predictable module navigation in O(1), antipatterns showing why mixed-responsibility modules become unmaintainable, and worked examples of validated variables and sensitivity-marked outputs.

methodologies.name: terraform-modules-versioning
methodologies.description: Prevents the silent-breakage failure mode where an unpinned module source picks up a breaking change overnight and surfaces during the next production apply. Mandates Semantic Versioning with Git tags, the pessimistic constraint operator (~>) on every registry/GitHub source so consumers get safe patch and minor updates while major bumps stay manual, and a maintained CHANGELOG.md categorized as Added/Changed/Breaking Changes/Fixed/Deprecated/Removed with migration instructions for every major release. Apply it when a module has more than one consumer, when upgrading dependencies, when releasing a new version, or when choosing a constraint operator for a new module call. Practical value: a SemVer decision table mapping common change types to the correct version bump, examples covering registry constraints, GitHub ref pins, and local paths (which must omit the version argument), a sample CHANGELOG with a migration block, and a release checklist that catches the common omissions which break registry sync or leave consumers without upgrade guidance.

methodologies.name: terraform-state
methodologies.description: Manage Terraform state securely including remote backends, locking, encryption, and import operations. Prevents concurrent modifications and data loss through proper state handling, versioning, and access control.

domains.description: Conversion rate optimization specializing in landing page design, CRO tactics, funnel analysis and optimization, free trial conversion, and product-led growth mechanics. Covers funnel frameworks, landing page psychology, A/B testing for conversions, PLG implementation, and onboarding flow design.

methodologies.name: funnel-basics-examples
methodologies.description: Analyze real-world funnel optimization examples across SaaS, e-commerce, mobile, and B2B with benchmarks. Demonstrates industry-specific drop-off patterns and proven tactics that deliver 50-180% conversion improvements.

methodologies.name: funnel-basics-framework
methodologies.description: Systematically identify and eliminate conversion friction through funnel mapping, diagnosis, and A/B testing. Provides structured approach to diagnose drop-offs, prioritize with ICE scoring, and iterate toward measurable improvements.

methodologies.name: funnel-tactics-advanced
methodologies.description: Apply advanced techniques including personalization, retargeting, and industry-specific optimizations. Addresses sophisticated funnel problems with multi-channel strategies, exit intent tactics, and performance measurement frameworks.

methodologies.name: funnel-tactics-basics
methodologies.description: Execute practical funnel optimizations starting with quick wins like form field reduction and button contrast. Provides immediate, high-impact tactics for landing pages, signup forms, checkout flows, and onboarding.

methodologies.name: growth-conversion-optimization
methodologies.description: Improve visitor-to-customer conversion through systematic testing and research-driven optimization. Combines analysis, hypothesis generation, and A/B testing to achieve 1-2% conversion improvements that double effective marketing spend.

methodologies.name: onboarding-flows
methodologies.description: Design user onboarding that minimizes friction and accelerates activation through templates and progressive disclosure. Reduces abandonment and increases time-to-value by revealing features contextually and celebrating early wins.

methodologies.name: plg-basics
methodologies.description: Understand product-led growth models including freemium, free trial, and open core alternatives. Enables customer acquisition through product experience rather than sales teams, reducing CAC and improving scalability.

methodologies.name: plg-implementation-guide
methodologies.description: Implement product-led growth by defining aha moments, optimizing onboarding, and building upgrade triggers. Provides playbooks for freemium-to-paid, trial-to-paid, and expansion workflows with self-serve purchasing.

methodologies.name: plg-metrics
methodologies.description: Track PLG success through activation rate, free-to-paid conversion, and product-qualified leads scoring. Enables data-driven PLG optimization with cohort analysis and natural growth rate measurement.

methodologies.name: plg-optimization-tactics
methodologies.description: Optimize each PLG stage through activation friction reduction, free tier strategy, and expansion playbooks. Delivers tactical improvements in onboarding, checkout design, and seat expansion with A/B testing guidance.

domains.description: Data-driven growth marketing with focus on experimentation and metrics. Covers AARRR framework (Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Revenue, Referral), analytics setup (Google Analytics, Plausible), A/B and multivariate testing, cohort analysis, viral loops and referral mechanics, retention strategies, and growth operations automation.

methodologies.name: aarrr-pirate-metrics
methodologies.description: AARRR breaks growth into five measurable stages: Acquisition (how users find you), Activation (experiencing core value), Retention (returning users), Revenue (monetization), and Referral (word-of-mouth). Instead of optimizing every metric at once, identify which stage is your bottleneck—typically activation is broken long before you should scale acquisition—then focus all effort on moving that one lever. Track weekly dashboards showing the conversion rate at each stage so the entire team sees which metric matters and aligns efforts.

methodologies.name: ab-testing-basics
methodologies.description: A/B testing removes guesswork by randomly splitting users between two versions and measuring which performs better using statistical significance rather than intuition. Define a clear hypothesis (if-then-because), choose one measurable outcome, run until you reach the required sample size without peeking early, then check if the difference is real or random chance. This process works for landing pages, email subject lines, button colors, or onboarding flows—anything users can encounter in two variants.

methodologies.name: ab-testing-setup
methodologies.description: Sample size calculation ensures your test isn't wasting traffic on underpowered experiments or running forever collecting unnecessary data. Input your baseline conversion rate and the minimum improvement worth detecting, and the formula tells you how many users per variant you need to detect that difference with 80% confidence. Account for your daily traffic to estimate test duration, then commit to running to completion without early stopping, which invalidates statistical results.

methodologies.name: activation-framework
methodologies.description: Activation is the moment a new user receives core value and sees why your product matters—the 'Aha moment.' Map your entire activation funnel step-by-step (signup → email verification → onboarding → first meaningful action), identify where users drop off most, then reduce friction at bottlenecks through UX simplification, better guidance, or progressive onboarding. Define activation as a user action that correlates with long-term retention, not vanity metrics like 'created profile,' because only then can you measure and improve what matters.

methodologies.name: activation-metrics
methodologies.description: Identify activation by finding the user action that strongest predicts retention: compare Day-30 retention for users who completed onboarding versus those who didn't, or analyzed feature adoption patterns, choosing the behavior that shows the highest correlation. Measure what percentage of signups reach this activation event within 24 hours (Day 0) and within 7 days (Day 7), tracking improvements weekly as you reduce activation barriers. Good B2B SaaS targets 50%+ Day-0 activation; consumer apps target 60%+ when onboarding is frictionless.

methodologies.name: activation-tactics
methodologies.description: Seven proven tactics reduce activation friction: progressive onboarding (teach while doing, not upfront setup), templates/presets (start with a winning example), progress checklists (visible completion), better empty states (clear next step), faster time-to-value (reduce steps from 10 to 3), targeted emails (re-engage inactive users), and in-app guidance (tooltips show what's next). Run A/B tests on each tactic against your baseline to measure which moves the activation rate most, typically expecting 10-30% lifts from well-executed changes.

methodologies.name: cohort-implementation
methodologies.description: Build cohort retention analysis using SQL (window functions, CTEs for dates-since-signup) or Python/pandas to group users and calculate retention percentages at retention points (D1, D7, D30). Create a materialized view or automated daily refresh that powers a dashboard showing current cohort performance and historical trends. Focus on weekly or monthly cohorts depending on your product cadence; daily cohorts become too noisy for most products, while quarterly cohorts miss short-term trends.

methodologies.name: conversion-tracking
methodologies.description: Set up conversion events in analytics platforms (GA4, Mixpanel) to mark moments when users achieve key outcomes: purchases, trial signups, feature completions, or milestone events. For multi-step processes like checkout, track each step (cart view → payment info → purchase) to identify where users drop, then use funnel analysis to calculate drop-off percentages. Implement server-side tracking for events you control (subscription renewals) and client-side for user actions (clicking buttons) so you capture 100% of conversions even if JavaScript fails.

methodologies.name: google-analytics
methodologies.description: Google Analytics 4 tracks website and app behavior through event-based measurement: install the gtag snippet, define what you want to measure (sign-ups, purchases, content consumed), and segment users by properties (plan type, traffic source, device). Use the Data API for programmatic access to build custom reports or dashboards; set up conversion events in Admin to measure them separately; create audiences for segmentation (returners, high-value users) to power downstream tools like email and ads.

methodologies.name: growth-loops
methodologies.description: A growth loop is a self-reinforcing cycle where each user action generates output that brings in new users, who then repeat the action: Dropbox's 'get 500MB per referral' loop meant users shared because they benefited, making others sign up, who then referred more. Types include viral loops (direct invites), content loops (created content attracts search traffic), paid loops (revenue funds ads for more revenue), and network effect loops (value increases with more users). Calculate the viral coefficient (K-factor) to see if your loop compounds (K > 1.0) or needs external fuel.

methodologies.name: growth-referral-programs
methodologies.description: Referral programs structure word-of-mouth by incentivizing existing customers to invite friends: decide whether both parties get rewards (double-sided, best), only the referrer, or only the referee; set reward size (20-50% of customer acquisition cost works); pick trigger points (post-purchase enthusiasm, after support wins); and make sharing frictionless (one-click link, email templates, QR code). Promote heavily via email, in-app, and website so customers remember they exist, and track which programs convert best to iterate on copy, offer, and placement.

methodologies.name: growth-viral-loops
methodologies.description: Design loops where output from one cycle seeds the next: users take actions that create shareable artifacts (posts, projects, reports), which reach others via social sharing or links, motivating them to join and take the same action. Implement the loop by choosing one naturally shareable moment, reducing friction to share (pre-filled messages, one-click), removing signup barriers for new visitors (view before joining), and measuring the viral coefficient (invites per user × conversion rate of invites). If K-factor < 0.3, the loop helps but isn't explosive; K > 1.0 drives exponential growth.

methodologies.name: north-star-metric
methodologies.description: Choose ONE metric that best represents customer value delivery and drives long-term revenue—for Slack it's 'daily active users' (engagement), for Airbnb 'nights booked' (core transaction), for Netflix 'hours watched' (consumption). North Star metrics avoid revenue (which lags) and vanity metrics (page views); correlate with retention/loyalty; and are actionable by team. Define input metrics (team-specific levers that feed the North Star), set quarterly targets, and review trends quarterly to ensure the metric stays valid as the product evolves.

methodologies.name: ops-churn-basics
methodologies.description: Measure and understand why customers leave: calculate monthly churn (lost customers ÷ starting customers) and watch for trends—5% monthly compounds to 46% annual churn. Segment churn by reason (not using product, price objection, found competitor) through exit surveys; identify leading indicators (no login 7+ days, feature usage drops) to catch at-risk customers before they cancel. Build a health score combining login frequency, feature usage, support sentiment, and account age so you can proactively intervene on customers scoring below 40 before they churn.

methodologies.name: ops-churn-prevention
methodologies.description: Intervene early when risk signals appear: send re-engagement emails at 7 days no-login, personal outreach at 14 days; offer a save deal (50% off 2 months, pause 30 days) when users attempt cancellation; and win-back churned users with new-feature announcements and discounts at 7/30/90-day intervals. Measure save offer conversion (typically 20-30% effective) and win-back conversions (recovering 10% of churned customers). Segment interventions by churn reason—price objectors get discounts, feature-gap complainers get trial upgrades—to address the actual problem.

methodologies.name: ops-metrics-basics
methodologies.description: Choose 5-10 essential metrics (North Star, leading indicators, financial) rather than tracking everything and paralyzed by analysis: MRR, churn rate, LTV, CAC, activation rate, retention (D7/D30), and profit margin. Set targets for each (e.g., 50% growth MoM, 5% churn target), create alerts when metrics drift 20%+ off track, and review daily (2 min pulse), weekly (30 min deep dive), and monthly (2 hours analysis). Build or use a dashboard tool (Looker Studio for solopreneurs) that auto-pulls data from Stripe/GA4 so metrics stay fresh without manual work.

methodologies.name: privacy-compliance
methodologies.description: Comply with GDPR, CCPA, and regional privacy laws when tracking user behavior: default analytics consent to denied, ask users explicitly before tracking, respect Do Not Sell signals, anonymize IPs, and delete data on request. Set up consent management that blocks GA4 until users opt-in, implement cookie banners, and use Measurement Protocol with consent flags. Audit your data retention (14 months is GA4 default) and document what data you collect, why, and how long you keep it—compliance avoids expensive fines and maintains user trust.

methodologies.name: retention-metrics
methodologies.description: Track D1/D7/D30 retention by cohort to see if recently-joined users are stickier than older cohorts, indicating product improvements. Segment by behavior (did they complete onboarding?) or feature (did they collaborate with teammates?) to find which actions predict the highest retention. Build engagement dashboards showing DAU/MAU ratio (ideal > 25%), session frequency, and session length to catch drops early. Use health scoring to flag at-risk users (low login frequency, feature usage declining) and proactively send re-engagement campaigns before they churn.

methodologies.name: retention-strategies
methodologies.description: Six loop patterns drive retention: content loops (Netflix, TikTok—new content brings returns), social loops (Facebook—friends' activity), progress loops (Duolingo—streaks and levels), stored value loops (Notion—data lock-in), workflow loops (Slack—team dependencies), and network effects (Figma—collaboration). Implement engagement hooks like streaks (loss aversion), achievements (celebration), variable rewards (unpredictable content), and commitment devices (scheduled content, public goals). For each loop, measure participation rate (what % use the feature), completion rate (do they finish?), and impact on retention to prioritize which loops to build.

methodologies.name: statistics-application
methodologies.description: Apply statistical tests in practice: collect sample data, calculate the p-value using the two-proportion z-test, and check if p < 0.05 to declare significance. Pre-calculate sample size needed (typically 5,000-50,000 per variant depending on baseline and minimum effect size), run the test to that sample without peeking early, then interpret confidence intervals. If the 95% CI for lift is [+0.2%, +0.8%] and excludes zero, the difference is significant; if it crosses zero, you need more data or different assumptions.

methodologies.name: statistics-basics
methodologies.description: A/B tests use the null hypothesis ('there's no difference') and p-values to quantify confidence: a p-value of 0.03 means 'if there were truly no difference, we'd see this result 3% of the time by chance.' Set significance level (alpha = 0.05, standard) before testing and power (80% is standard—if the true difference exists, you have 80% chance of detecting it). Understanding Type I error (false positive: declaring a difference when none exists) and Type II error (false negative: missing a real difference) protects against shipping false positives or killing winners prematurely.

methodologies.name: viral-loops
methodologies.description: Viral loops describe self-reinforcing user acquisition mechanisms where existing users bring new users automatically. There are five loop types—word of mouth, inherent virality, artificial incentives, content virality, and outbreak—each suited to different product categories. Choose your loop type based on product fit, design it into your core experience, and track the viral coefficient (K-factor) to measure and optimize loop effectiveness.

methodologies.name: viral-metrics
methodologies.description: Viral coefficient (K-factor) measures how many new users each existing user brings. It's calculated as K = invites per user × invite conversion rate. K > 1 means exponential growth; K < 0.5 means slow organic growth. Track both components separately to identify whether to improve invitation frequency or conversion rates, and benchmark against industry standards for your product type.

methodologies.name: viral-optimization
methodologies.description: Optimize viral loops by measuring current K-factor, identifying your loop type, then systematically testing improvements to invites and conversion separately. Increase invites through share moments and friction-free sharing; increase conversions through personalization, social proof, and one-click signup. A/B test each tactic and monitor weekly to catch declining performance early.

domains.description: Go-to-market strategy, product launches, and market positioning. Covers GTM planning, market positioning, pricing models, press coverage, partnership development, and customer success operations. Includes frameworks for product launches, competitive positioning, and revenue operations. Apply when planning product launches, entering new markets, building partnership ecosystems, or scaling customer success.

methodologies.name: growth-affiliate-marketing
methodologies.description: Affiliate marketing scales customer acquisition by leveraging others' audiences. Design a commission structure (percentage, flat fee, or tiered), recruit quality affiliates from blogs and newsletters, provide marketing assets, and track performance via unique links. This works best for products with high LTV that justify affiliate commissions while keeping CAC below 30% of lifetime value.

methodologies.name: growth-brand-positioning
methodologies.description: Brand positioning answers why customers should choose you over alternatives by owning a specific space in their mind. Define your target customer with ruthless specificity, understand competitive alternatives, identify your unique differentiator, choose your category strategy, then craft a positioning statement. Test with customers and commit for 12+ months—strong positioning guides product decisions and makes sales easier.

methodologies.name: growth-gtm-strategy
methodologies.description: Go-to-market strategy answers five questions: WHO (target customer), WHAT (value proposition), WHERE (channels), HOW (sales motion), and WHEN (phased launch). Start with a specific ICP, choose 2-3 primary channels to master before expanding, select a sales motion (self-serve, sales-assisted, or enterprise), and launch in phases from private beta through general availability.

methodologies.name: growth-influencer-marketing
methodologies.description: Find influencers in your niche (search hashtags, competitor collaborations, tools like Modash), vet them for authentic engagement rather than follower count, then propose mutually beneficial partnerships. Micro-influencers (10K-100K) offer best ROI for most brands. Structure compensation (gifting, flat fee, affiliate, or hybrid), provide clear creative guidelines, and measure conversions with unique codes and UTMs.

methodologies.name: growth-press-coverage
methodologies.description: Earn press coverage by identifying target journalists in your niche, researching their recent work, creating newsworthy story angles, then pitching with personalized emails under 150 words that highlight why it matters to their readers. Start with Tier 3 (niche blogs), build credibility through HARO responses, then approach higher-tier outlets. Leverage coverage by sharing everywhere and maintaining journalist relationships.

methodologies.name: ops-annual-planning-process
methodologies.description: Annual planning aligns yearly direction with daily work. Reflect on the past year, set a clear vision for 12 months ahead, identify 2-3 strategic priorities, break into quarterly milestones, then monthly and weekly action plans. Review progress monthly, reassess quarterly, and adjust as reality changes. This disciplines your business to compound effort toward meaningful goals instead of drifting.

methodologies.name: ops-annual-planning-templates
methodologies.description: Planning templates provide structure for annual goals (vision, theme, metrics by tier), quarterly breakdowns with initiatives, monthly plans with weekly tasks, and OKR frameworks. Use templates to document what success looks like, track progress over time, and communicate priorities to yourself and your team. Templates transform vague intentions into measurable action.

methodologies.name: ops-contractor-basics
methodologies.description: Delegate tasks that aren't your competitive advantage by hiring contractors. Identify what to delegate (specialized skills, time-consuming work, repetitive tasks), find quality candidates through platforms or referrals, screen with portfolios and test tasks, then evaluate for fit. Standard rates vary widely; calculate ROI by comparing contractor cost to the value of your freed time.

methodologies.name: ops-contractor-management
methodologies.description: Manage contractors effectively by setting clear expectations, documenting processes, maintaining regular communication, and giving constructive feedback using the situation-behavior-impact framework. Use async tools like Slack and Loom, weekly check-ins for coordination, and monthly reviews for performance evaluation. Good management reduces scope creep and ensures consistent quality work.

methodologies.name: ops-customer-success-basics
methodologies.description: Customer success proactively ensures customers achieve their goals with your product. Define what success means (feature adoption, completion rates, outcome delivery), create touchpoints throughout the customer journey, provide enabling resources (guides, templates, webinars), then expand with successful customers. This prevents churn before it happens and creates expansion opportunities.

methodologies.name: ops-customer-success-metrics
methodologies.description: Health scoring combines product usage, feature adoption, support sentiment, engagement, and payment status into a single score predicting churn risk. Establish thresholds (80+ healthy, 40-59 at-risk, <40 critical), automate alerts, track by cohort, and monitor leading indicators like declining usage that predict churn before cancellation happens.

methodologies.name: ops-financial-basics
methodologies.description: Understand your unit economics by tracking revenue and expenses, calculating LTV and CAC, then ensuring LTV > 3x CAC. Build a simple P&L model monthly, track key ratios (gross margin, net margin, payback period), and review trends. Sound financials let you make faster growth decisions and know when you can afford to invest in acquisition.

methodologies.name: ops-legal-basics
methodologies.description: Protect your business legally by choosing the right entity (LLC is ideal for most solopreneurs), creating essential policies (Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, Refund Policy), understanding key regulations (GDPR, CCPA, CAN-SPAM), protecting your IP through trademark registration, and using proper contracts for employees and partners.

methodologies.name: ops-legal-compliance
methodologies.description: Legal compliance covers business structure, policies, regulations, IP protection, and contracts. This umbrella breaks into two components: legal-basics (entity choice, policies, regulations) and legal-compliance-checklist (staged implementation from day 1 through ongoing maintenance). Follow both for comprehensive legal foundation.

methodologies.name: ops-legal-compliance-checklist
methodologies.description: Implement legal compliance in stages: Day 1 (form LLC, get EIN, basic policies), Month 1 (review policies, check GDPR), Year 1 (trademark, contractor templates), then ongoing updates as your business changes. Use this checklist to track progress and ensure nothing is missed during growth.

methodologies.name: ops-partnership-strategy
methodologies.description: Build partnerships by identifying complementary partners who share your audience, evaluating them on reach-relevance-trust-effort-risk, then making mutually beneficial proposals. Structure partnerships as affiliate programs, co-marketing campaigns, integrations, or reseller relationships. Start with specific asks, provide all needed assets, communicate regularly, and measure ROI to expand successful partnerships.

methodologies.name: ops-tax-basics
methodologies.description: Understand your tax situation: choose a tax-efficient structure (S-Corp saves ~$7K/year at $100K profit), track deductible expenses (home office, software, marketing), maximize tax-advantaged accounts (SEP-IRA up to ~$18K), then set aside 25-30% of profit for taxes. S-Corp election becomes worthwhile at $80K+ consistent profit.

methodologies.name: ops-tax-compliance
methodologies.description: Meet tax deadlines quarterly (April 15, June 15, Sept 15, Jan 15) by paying estimated taxes using the safe harbor method—pay 100% of last year's liability to avoid penalties. Keep records for 7 years, implement a bookkeeping system monthly, and consider hiring a CPA if your situation grows complex.

methodologies.name: ops-upselling-cross-selling
methodologies.description: Expand revenue from existing customers through upsells (higher tier of same product), cross-sells (complementary products), and seat expansion (more licenses). Trigger upsells when customers hit limits or adopt advanced features, offer at the right moment (after success, not during problems), use value-based framing (time saved, cost reduction, capability gain), and make upgrading friction-free.

domains.description: PPC Manager handles paid advertising campaigns across Google Ads, Meta Ads, LinkedIn, and other platforms. It covers campaign setup, audience targeting, ad creative optimization, bidding strategy, conversion tracking, and performance analysis. Use this domain when launching paid acquisition campaigns, optimizing ad spend, managing multiple ad platforms, or analyzing return on ad spend (ROAS). Core skills include understanding platform-specific mechanics, keyword research, audience segmentation, and attribution modeling across channels.

methodologies.name: ads-ab-testing-ads
methodologies.description: Test ad elements systematically using the scientific method: hypothesize what will work better, test one variable at a time with equal budget split, wait for statistical significance (95% minimum), then implement the winner at scale. Test high-impact elements first (offer, hook, creative type), avoid testing too many things, and document results to build institutional learning.

methodologies.name: ads-analytics-setup
methodologies.description: Set up analytics by installing GA4 plus ad platform pixels, configuring custom events for your business goals, marking conversions, and using consistent UTM parameters. Track the full funnel (signups, trials, purchases, feature adoption), create reports by source and campaign, and connect ad platform data back to revenue for accurate ROI calculation.

methodologies.name: ads-attribution-models
methodologies.description: Attribution models determine how credit is assigned across marketing touchpoints when users interact with multiple ads before converting. Understanding the difference between last-click, first-click, linear, time decay, and data-driven attribution models helps you allocate budget accurately across channels and avoid overvaluing some channels while undervaluing others. Use data-driven attribution or GA4 unified view as your source of truth while recognizing that platform-reported conversions naturally overlap.

methodologies.name: ads-budget-optimization
methodologies.description: Budget optimization reallocates spending from underperforming campaigns to winners based on efficiency metrics like CPA and ROAS. Use a structured framework to audit current performance, calculate efficiency ratios, apply the 70-20-10 rule (70% proven, 20% promising, 10% testing), and account for diminishing returns when scaling. Weekly optimization with 20-30% incremental increases typically delivers better results than large swings.

methodologies.name: ads-conversion-tracking
methodologies.description: Conversion tracking connects ad spend directly to business outcomes through pixel installation and event configuration across platforms. Set up both client-side pixels and server-side tracking (CAPI) to capture all conversion types, verify events are firing correctly, and define the right attribution window for your sales cycle. Proper tracking is the foundation for all optimization and ROI measurement.

methodologies.name: ads-google-campaign-setup
methodologies.description: Google Ads campaign setup requires choosing the right campaign type (Search, Display, Shopping, PMax), configuring conversion tracking first, setting campaign-level settings, organizing ad groups by theme with 10-20 related keywords, and adding extensions. Start with Search campaigns for beginners using Maximize Conversions bidding, then expand to other types once you understand the platform.

methodologies.name: ads-google-keywords
methodologies.description: Keyword strategy balances reach and relevance through intent-first research, qualification, and match type selection. Use broad to phrase to exact match progression, organize keywords by theme in ad groups, continuously add negatives from search terms reports, and improve Quality Scores through tighter ad group structure and better landing page relevance. Weekly optimization of keywords based on search terms data is critical.

methodologies.name: ads-google-reporting
methodologies.description: Reporting teaches you what metrics to monitor, how to diagnose performance issues, and what actions to take. Check daily spend pacing, do weekly full reviews with Search Terms analysis and bid adjustments, and monthly trend analysis with budget reallocation. Use the diagnostic framework: high CPA can stem from poor ads (CTR), poor landing page (CVR), poor audience (CPM), or audience fatigue (frequency).

methodologies.name: ads-linkedin-ads
methodologies.description: LinkedIn Ads reach professional decision-makers through precise targeting by job title, company size, and industry, but cost 5-15x more than social platforms. Use Lead Gen Forms or Website Conversions objectives, target narrow ICPs, create professional value-first creative, expect $30-100+ cost per lead, and measure to pipeline (not just form fills). ABM campaigns targeting specific companies perform best.

methodologies.name: ads-meta-campaign-setup
methodologies.description: Meta campaign setup follows a three-tier structure (Campaign → Ad Set → Ad) with Campaign Budget Optimization recommended for testing. Install the Meta Pixel before running ads, define your conversion event and target CPA, choose 3-5 audiences per campaign, create 3-5 ad variations per audience, and let Meta optimize during the learning phase (50+ conversions per week). Start with $20-50/day minimum.

methodologies.name: ads-meta-creative
methodologies.description: Meta creative stops the scroll in the first 3 seconds through attention-grabbing visuals and compelling hooks, then communicates value and drives action. Follow the AIDA framework (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action), use native-looking real photos over stock images, test hooks systematically before other variables, and refresh creatives every 2-4 weeks to prevent fatigue. Dynamic Creative automatically tests asset combinations.

methodologies.name: ads-meta-reporting
methodologies.description: Meta reporting reveals what's working through custom columns, breakdown analysis by device/time/placement/region, and diagnostic frameworks that connect high CPA to root causes. Set up daily spend checks, weekly full reviews with breakdown analysis, monthly trend reviews and budget reallocation. A rising CPA can signal audience fatigue (increasing frequency), creative fatigue (declining CTR), or landing page issues (declining CVR).

methodologies.name: ads-meta-targeting
methodologies.description: Meta targeting moves from broad cold audiences (interests) to warm custom audiences (website visitors, engagers) to hot audiences (cart abandoners) to lookalikes of best customers. Start with 1% lookalike of purchasers for scale, always exclude recent converters to prevent ad fatigue, and test audience types systematically with equal budgets. 500K-5M audience size is optimal; too narrow limits learning.

methodologies.name: ads-retargeting
methodologies.description: Retargeting shows ads to people who already know you, dramatically improving conversion rates and lowering CPA by 40-70% compared to cold traffic. Segment audiences by behavior and intent (all visitors, product viewers, cart abandoners), tailor messaging to each stage, set frequency caps to prevent annoyance, always exclude converters, and refresh creative every 2-3 weeks. Retargeting should be 20-30% of total ad spend.

methodologies.name: ads-twitter-ads
methodologies.description: Twitter/X Ads work for tech products, niche communities, and conversations reaching engaged audiences at $5-15 CPM. Use follower lookalikes of competitors for targeting, create native-looking valuable content (not overly promotional), write conversational copy with hooks and clear CTAs, and test 3+ ad variations. Engagement rate and follower acquisition often matter more than direct conversions.

methodologies.name: facebook-ads
methodologies.description: Facebook Ads API enables programmatic campaign creation, management, and optimization through structured campaign-adset-ad hierarchy. Set up conversion tracking via pixels, choose optimization goals matching your business objective, configure targeting with interests and behaviors, create responsive ads with multiple headlines and descriptions, and use bid strategies (Lowest Cost, Cost Cap, ROAS). Campaign Budget Optimization (CBO) simplifies multi-adset testing.

methodologies.name: google-ads-basics
methodologies.description: Google Ads API basics cover OAuth authentication with developer tokens, account hierarchy (Manager → Customer → Campaign → Ad Group → Ad), customer management, and campaign operations. Authenticate via YAML config or dictionary with credentials, list campaigns with metrics queries, and understand resource structures. Test tokens allow unlimited requests; standard access requires approval.

methodologies.name: google-ads-optimization
methodologies.description: Google Ads optimization covers bidding strategies (Manual CPC, Maximize Conversions, Target CPA, Target ROAS), conversion action creation with attribution windows, bid adjustments by device and location, and GA4 integration. Start with Maximize Conversions for data collection, switch to Target CPA after 30+ conversions, set lookback windows matching your sales cycle (30-90 days typical), and upload offline conversions for complete attribution.

methodologies.name: google-ads-reporting
methodologies.description: Google Ads API reporting provides patterns for querying campaign data, setting up automation, handling errors, and implementing best practices at scale. Use it to build dashboards, schedule reports, monitor performance metrics, and automate bidding or pausing decisions. The methodology delivers visibility into ad spend, conversion tracking, and the ability to scale account management across multiple campaigns and customers.

methodologies.name: google-display-ads
methodologies.description: Display campaigns reach users across Google's network of websites and apps through contextual, audience, demographic, and remarketing targeting. Create responsive display ads with 1-5 headlines, descriptions, images, logos, and business name that Google assembles automatically. Use placement targeting for specific websites, keyword targeting for contextual relevance, and audience targeting for specific user segments to control reach and relevance.

methodologies.name: google-pmax
methodologies.description: Performance Max campaigns use Google's AI to optimize across Search, Display, YouTube, Gmail, Discover, and Maps automatically. Provide maximum asset variety (5 headlines, 5 long headlines, 4+ descriptions, 3-5 images, optional videos), audience signals for guidance, and sufficient budget (3x target CPA minimum). Allow 4-6 weeks learning phase, replace underperforming assets weekly, and avoid pausing/restarting which disrupts optimization.

methodologies.name: google-search-ads
methodologies.description: Search campaigns show text ads when people search your keywords, offering high intent traffic through Search Network only (avoid Display). Use Responsive Search Ads with 15 headlines and 4 descriptions to test combinations, organize keywords by theme in ad groups with 10-20 keywords each, start with phrase match and graduate top performers to exact match, and review Search Terms weekly to add negatives and new keywords.

methodologies.name: google-shopping-ads
methodologies.description: Shopping campaigns showcase product listings from your Google Merchant Center feed with automatic ad generation. Link Merchant Center to your Ads account, create product partitions to organize by category/brand/condition, adjust bids by product performance, and review the Shopping performance report by product to identify winners and losers. Feed quality (images, titles, attributes) directly impacts ad appearance and performance.

methodologies.name: growth-paid-acquisition
methodologies.description: Paid acquisition accelerates growth through systematic testing and scaling when unit economics work. Calculate LTV and target CAC (LTV/3 for SaaS), choose one platform, test 3-5 audiences with 3-5 ad variations per audience, and scale winners while killing underperformers. Track conversions end-to-end, optimize weekly, and remember that paid channels compound over time as you learn what works.

methodologies.name: instagram-ads
methodologies.description: Instagram Ads reach visual audiences through Feed (1:1, 4:5), Stories (9:16), Reels (9:16, 15-90s), and Explore placements. Create platform-native content avoiding stock photos, add captions for sound-off viewing, test formats separately before combining, and monitor frequency to prevent fatigue. Shopping ads with product tags convert well for e-commerce; engagement and reach objectives work for awareness.

methodologies.name: meta-audience-targeting
methodologies.description: Meta audience targeting progresses from demographic and interest-based cold audiences through custom audiences (website visitors, customers) to lookalikes of best customers.
