Use 500K-5M audience size for balance, layer targeting with AND logic for precision (interests + behaviors + custom audiences), test 1% lookalikes for quality and 5-10% for scale, and always exclude existing customers from prospecting campaigns.

domains.description: SMM Manager orchestrates organic social media activities across platforms like Twitter, LinkedIn, Instagram, and Threads. It covers content strategy, community building, audience growth tactics, engagement optimization, and organic reach maximization. Use this domain when developing social media presence, launching platforms, building communities, or growing followers organically. Core skills include understanding platform algorithms, content calendars, community moderation, and engagement metrics.

methodologies.name: instagram-basics
methodologies.description: Instagram growth requires a multi-format strategy prioritizing Reels (80% of effort) over feeds, as the platform now rewards video content and discovery over engagement. Apply this when building brand presence on Instagram, especially for product launches or audience growth—Instagram's 2B+ monthly users and algorithm changes make this essential for organic reach in 2024+.

methodologies.name: threads-basics
methodologies.description: Threads is Meta's emerging Twitter alternative where early adopters gain significant reach with less competition. Use this methodology to cross-promote from Instagram and build a conversational audience through authentic engagement, hot takes, and quick tips that the platform's algorithm actively rewards.

domains.description: Agile PM specialist covers Scrum and Kanban methodologies, ceremonies (standups, planning, retros), PM tools (Jira, Linear, ClickUp, GitHub Projects), dashboards and reporting, team development, and hybrid delivery approaches. Use this when managing agile sprints, selecting PM tooling, implementing ceremonies, building dashboards, scaling across teams, or blending agile with waterfall practices. Includes 28 methodologies across tools, frameworks, and team management.

methodologies.name: agile-hybrid-approaches
methodologies.description: Many real-world projects don't fit pure waterfall or pure agile—hybrid approaches combine elements of both to match project characteristics. Apply this when requirements are partially known, stakeholder availability is mixed, or delivery requirements vary across phases, enabling teams to maintain flexibility while meeting governance needs.

methodologies.name: azure-devops-boards
methodologies.description: Azure DevOps Boards is Microsoft's enterprise project management tool supporting multiple process templates (Scrum, Agile, CMMI) with extensive customization and Analytics capabilities. Use this for large organizations already in the Microsoft ecosystem that need compliance, audit trails, and complex enterprise workflows with custom process control.

methodologies.name: gitlab-boards
methodologies.description: GitLab Issue Boards provide kanban-style project management integrated directly with GitLab's DevOps platform, eliminating tool switching for dev teams. Apply this when your team uses GitLab for CI/CD and source control, especially for organizations wanting unified DevOps and PM in a single platform.

methodologies.name: hybrid-delivery
methodologies.description: Hybrid delivery combines waterfall planning and governance with agile execution, following the principle 'Plan with Waterfall, Execute with Agile'. Use this pattern for projects with fixed requirements upfront but iterative development needs, hardware/software combinations, or regulated industries requiring stage gates.

methodologies.name: jira-workflow-management
methodologies.description: Jira is the industry-standard issue tracking tool for agile teams, offering highly customizable workflows, extensive automation, and a mature ecosystem of integrations. Apply this when scaling software development across teams that need detailed workflow configuration, sprint management, and comprehensive reporting.

methodologies.name: kanban-scaled-agile-ceremonies
methodologies.description: Kanban and SAFe provide alternative agile cadences for continuous-flow projects and enterprise scaling. Use Kanban ceremonies when your team works without sprints and needs flow optimization, or SAFe ceremonies (PI Planning, Scrum of Scrums) when scaling agile across multiple coordinated teams.

methodologies.name: performance-domains-overview
methodologies.description: Performance Domains are the core of modern Project Management Framework, focusing on outcomes rather than processes across eight interconnected areas (Stakeholder, Team, Development Approach, Planning, Project Work, Delivery, Measurement, Uncertainty). Apply this framework when you need holistic project health assessment rather than step-by-step process compliance.

methodologies.name: pm-framework-focus-areas
methodologies.description: PMBOK 8 replaced rigid Process Groups with five flexible Focus Areas (Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring & Controlling, Closing) that work across all delivery methods. Use this when you need a framework that supports both predictive and agile approaches without forcing one philosophy onto diverse project types.

methodologies.name: pm-tools-comparison
methodologies.description: Selecting the right PM tool requires systematic evaluation of features, usability, integrations, enterprise requirements, and total cost of ownership. Apply this methodology when choosing a new PM tool, conducting proof-of-concept evaluations, or deciding between competing solutions based on weighted criteria.

methodologies.name: pm-tools-overview
methodologies.description: PM tools range from simple kanban boards (Trello) to enterprise suites (Jira, Azure DevOps), with specialized options for developer teams (Linear, GitHub Projects) and non-technical groups (Asana, Monday). Use this reference when starting a new team or project without existing tooling to understand capabilities and find the right fit.

methodologies.name: predictive-analytics-pm
methodologies.description: Machine learning enables predictive capabilities in project management—forecasting schedule delays weeks in advance, detecting cost overruns early, and identifying risk patterns. Apply this when you have historical project data and want to proactively prevent common project failures rather than react to them.

methodologies.name: raci-matrix
methodologies.description: RACI (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) clarifies individual roles in every task, eliminating confusion about who owns decisions and who performs work. Apply this when a project has unclear responsibilities, multiple stakeholders need coordination, or teams are distributed and decisions slow down.

methodologies.name: reporting-basics
methodologies.description: Effective project reporting transforms raw data into actionable insights through metrics frameworks, dashboard types, and KPI selection tailored to different audiences. Apply this when setting up new project visibility systems, improving reporting, or creating stakeholder-specific dashboards for team, management, and executive visibility.

methodologies.name: scrum-ceremonies
methodologies.description: Scrum Ceremonies (Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Retrospective, Backlog Refinement) create the rhythm and structure for iterative delivery. Apply this when starting a Scrum team, remote teams need ceremony adaptation, or existing ceremonies need optimization for better team effectiveness.

methodologies.name: seven-performance-domains
methodologies.description: Project Management Framework 8 updated to seven integrated domains (Governance, Scope, Schedule, Finance, Stakeholders, Resources, Risk) with quality and communications now embedded throughout. Use this when understanding the latest PMBOK framework structure, which emphasizes value delivery and sustainability alongside traditional PM areas.

methodologies.name: six-core-principles
methodologies.description: Six guiding principles (Adopt Holistic View, Focus on Value, Embed Quality, Lead Accountably, Integrate Sustainability, Build Empowered Teams) anchor Project Management Framework 8. Apply these as a decision-making framework when prioritizing what matters in projects—moving from process compliance to outcome achievement.

methodologies.name: team-development
methodologies.description: Tuckman's Team Development Stages (Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing, Adjourning) explain how teams evolve, and PMs must adjust their leadership accordingly. Apply this when building new teams, managing conflict, developing skills, or understanding why team productivity changes—recognizing that high performance requires time and intentional development.

methodologies.name: tool-migration-basics
methodologies.description: PM tool migration involves assessment, field mapping, best practices planning, and training to minimize disruption and maximize success. Apply this before switching PM tools—conduct pre-migration audits, map fields precisely, plan for parallel runs, and involve stakeholder champions to reduce resistance.

methodologies.name: tool-migration-process
methodologies.description: This five-phase migration process (Planning, Preparation, Pilot, Full Migration, Cutover, Stabilization) with data migration scripts, rollback plans, and change management ensures smooth tool transitions. Apply this when executing the actual migration, moving data in waves, validating each step, and maintaining team productivity during the transition.

methodologies.name: value-stream-management
methodologies.description: Value Stream Management combines lean VSM, flow metrics, and DORA metrics to optimize end-to-end delivery rather than local team optimization. Apply this when teams are improving DevOps metrics but business value isn't increasing, revealing bottlenecks outside development that limit actual delivery.

domains.description: Traditional PM specialist covers PMBoK knowledge areas including stakeholder management, scope planning, schedule development, cost estimation, risk management, quality assurance, earned value management, and project closure. Use this for projects with fixed scope, detailed upfront planning requirements, large team coordination, compliance needs, or when applying PMBoK 7/8 frameworks. Includes 22 methodologies across planning, execution, monitoring, and closure.

methodologies.name: benefits-realization
methodologies.description: Benefits are measurable improvements resulting from project outcomes, and successful projects must track their realization post-delivery or risk investing in value that never materializes. Apply this when defining business cases, during project closure, and post-launch to prove ROI and learn what drives actual business value.

methodologies.name: change-control
methodologies.description: Change Control formalized the process of evaluating, approving, and implementing scope changes to prevent scope creep and maintain project baselines. Apply this in projects with fixed budgets or contracts, when stakeholders request additions, or when protecting schedule requires saying 'no' systematically.

methodologies.name: communications-management
methodologies.description: Communications Management ensures stakeholders receive the right information in the right format at the right frequency, preventing surprises and misalignment. Apply this by creating a communication plan early that maps stakeholder needs to channels and cadences, reducing meeting overload while keeping everyone informed.

methodologies.name: cost-estimation
methodologies.description: Cost estimation decomposes project expenses into direct, indirect, fixed, and variable costs, then applies three-point estimation and contingency reserves to create realistic budgets. Use this when planning financial resources for any project phase. It delivers predictable budgets that account for unknowns, preventing cost surprises and enabling better financial control throughout execution.

methodologies.name: earned-value-management
methodologies.description: Earned Value Management tracks project performance by comparing planned value, earned value, and actual cost, generating schedule and cost indices that predict final outcomes. Apply this to identify whether projects will finish on time and on budget before problems become critical. It provides objective performance data instead of subjective status, enabling data-driven corrective action.

methodologies.name: lessons-learned
methodologies.description: Lessons learned captures knowledge from project experiences—what worked, what failed, and what to improve—through structured sessions and documentation that get applied to future projects. Conduct these continuously during projects and especially at closeout. This breaks the cycle of repeating mistakes and builds organizational capability over time.

methodologies.name: pm-certification-alignment-2026
methodologies.description: This brief documents 2026 PM certification exam focus areas, emphasizing the shift from process/people (91%) to business environment (26%), including value delivery, governance, sustainability, and strategic alignment. Use this to understand certification scope changes and study priorities if pursuing updated credentials.

methodologies.name: pm-certification-changes-2026
methodologies.description: Starting July 1, 2026, new PM certifications reduce people/process weighting by 9% each and increase business environment by 18%, with new emphasis on benefits realization, strategic alignment, organizational change, and value stream mapping. Reference this when updating study plans or exam preparation strategies.

methodologies.name: procurement-management
methodologies.description: Procurement management moves from make-or-buy decisions through vendor selection, contract negotiation, and ongoing vendor oversight, using statements of work and evaluation matrices to ensure quality suppliers and clear deliverables. Apply this whenever external resources are needed—contractors, vendors, or third-party services. It reduces risk of bad partnerships and cost overruns through structured selection and management.

methodologies.name: project-closure
methodologies.description: Project closure formally accepts deliverables, releases resources, processes final payments, captures lessons learned, and transitions work to operations before disbanding the team. Execute this regardless of project success or failure. It provides legal closure, prevents resource waste, preserves knowledge, and gives team recognition rather than fading away.

methodologies.name: project-integration
methodologies.description: Project integration coordinates across all knowledge areas—scope, schedule, cost, quality, risks—through unified project plans and change control, preventing siloed decisions that optimize parts at the expense of the whole. Use continuously throughout the project to ensure changes in one area are reflected everywhere. It maintains project coherence and prevents cascading failures.

methodologies.name: quality-management
methodologies.description: Quality management defines standards, establishes processes to ensure those standards are met (assurance), and verifies that deliverables actually meet them (control), using definitions of done and checklists. Apply this from project start to prevent rework consuming 20-40% of project time. It costs less to build quality in than to test it in later.

methodologies.name: resource-management
methodologies.description: Resource management identifies skill needs, assesses availability, allocates people to work, and adjusts when demand exceeds capacity through leveling techniques or scope reduction. Use this during planning and continuously during execution to prevent overloading key people or leaving others idle. It ensures the right skills are available when needed without creating bottlenecks.

methodologies.name: risk-management
methodologies.description: Risk management identifies threats and opportunities, analyzes their probability and impact, plans responses (avoid, mitigate, transfer, accept), and monitors for triggers. Apply this to handle uncertainty—surprises still happen but you are not blindsided because response plans exist. This shifts projects from reactive crisis mode to proactive management.

methodologies.name: risk-register
methodologies.description: A risk register documents identified risks with probability, impact, scores, assigned owners, and response strategies in a living document reviewed regularly. Create this early and maintain it throughout the project as risks change. Without ownership and tracking, risks hide until they become full-blown crises.

methodologies.name: schedule-development
methodologies.description: Schedule development sequences activities, estimates durations, identifies dependencies and the critical path, then adds buffers where non-critical paths meet critical ones. Use this to establish realistic timelines based on actual dependencies rather than arbitrary deadlines. It reveals which activities truly control the project and where to focus management attention.

methodologies.name: scope-management
methodologies.description: Scope management collects requirements, defines what is included and excluded, creates a work breakdown structure, validates deliverables, and controls changes through formal processes. Apply this to prevent scope creep—changes that happen without approval, consuming budget and schedule. Clear scope with stakeholder sign-off creates the baseline against which changes are measured.

methodologies.name: stakeholder-engagement
methodologies.description: Stakeholder engagement identifies all people affected by or affecting the project, analyzes their power and interest, maps them on a power-interest grid, and plans tailored communication strategies. Use this to ensure key influencers are involved before they become blockers and that quiet stakeholders with hidden power are not overlooked.

methodologies.name: stakeholder-engagement-advanced
methodologies.description: Advanced stakeholder engagement assesses current engagement levels (unaware to leading), identifies gaps from desired levels, develops strategies to move stakeholders (address concerns, involve in decisions, empower), and tracks progress through interactions. Apply this when stakeholder resistance threatens the project or when you need to build champions who drive adoption.

methodologies.name: stakeholder-register
methodologies.description: A stakeholder register documents everyone affected by the project—their role, interests, influence, and impact—with engagement strategy for each. Create this early and update as the project evolves and new stakeholders appear. It prevents operating blind to hidden power and ensures communication matches each person's needs.

methodologies.name: wbs-creation
methodologies.description: WBS creation decomposes project scope hierarchically from major deliverables to work packages (8-80 hours), ensuring all work is captured and nothing is forgotten through the 100% rule. Build this collaboratively with the team to gain buy-in and discover dependencies early. It transforms overwhelming projects into manageable pieces.

methodologies.name: work-breakdown-structure
methodologies.description: A work breakdown structure is the project's backbone—a hierarchical decomposition of all deliverables from the project down to work packages that can be estimated, assigned, and measured. Use this to organize project scope, allocate budget and schedule, identify dependencies, and assign accountability. Every project needs one.

domains.description: Project Manager orchestrator coordinates both agile and traditional PM approaches, routing tasks to appropriate sub-skills. Use this as entry point for any PM need—it identifies whether your situation calls for Scrum ceremonies, waterfall planning, EVM tracking, team development, or hybrid approaches. Includes 50 combined methodologies across agile and traditional domains with integrated decision framework.

methodologies.name: agile-ceremonies-setup
methodologies.description: Agile ceremonies (sprint planning, daily standup, review, retrospective, refinement) provide structure and rhythm for iterative delivery through timeboxed events. Set these up when starting a Scrum team or transitioning from waterfall to agile. These ceremonies keep teams aligned, unblock progress daily, and create continuous improvement feedback loops.

methodologies.name: agile-hybrid-approaches
methodologies.description: Hybrid approaches combine predictive and agile elements (water-scrum-fall, agile discovery plus waterfall implementation) tailored to project characteristics like requirement clarity, stakeholder availability, and risk tolerance. Use this when pure agile or pure waterfall do not fit—many real projects need both. The key is intentional tailoring rather than unplanned compromise.

methodologies.name: azure-devops-boards
methodologies.description: Azure DevOps Boards is an enterprise PM tool supporting multiple process templates (Agile, Scrum, CMMI), custom workflows, advanced reporting, and CI/CD pipeline integration. Deploy this in Microsoft-heavy organizations or when compliance and audit trails are critical. It provides comprehensive tracking from planning through delivery with enterprise governance features.

methodologies.name: benefits-realization
methodologies.description: Benefits realization defines expected business value (revenue, efficiency, quality) from project outputs, assigns benefit owners, tracks progress post-delivery, and adjusts when adoption lags. Apply this to ensure projects deliver not just outputs but measurable business outcomes. Without benefits tracking, nobody knows whether investments actually paid off.

methodologies.name: change-control
methodologies.description: Change control establishes a process for requesting, analyzing impact, deciding on, and implementing changes to scope, schedule, or budget. Create this early and enforce it consistently; even small changes without analysis create scope creep. It prevents uncontrolled scope expansion while allowing legitimate changes to be made intentionally.

methodologies.name: communications-management
methodologies.description: Communications management identifies what information each stakeholder needs, how often, in what format, and creates communication plans with regular rhythms (standup, status reports, meetings). Use this to prevent misalignment where people work from outdated information or feel blindsided. Good communication is often as important as good execution for project success.

methodologies.name: cost-estimation
methodologies.description: Cost estimation breaks projects into detailed work packages and calculates total expenses through bottom-up labor analysis, resource costs, and contingency reserves. Apply this when planning project budgets to ensure realistic financial forecasting and identify hidden costs. Delivers accurate cost baselines that prevent budget overruns and inform make-or-buy decisions.

methodologies.name: cross-tool-migration
methodologies.description: Structured methodology for migrating from one project management tool to another, covering planning, preparation, pilot migration, full deployment, and stabilization. Use when outgrowing current tooling or consolidating across teams to minimize disruption. Delivers smooth transition with data integrity, team adoption, and lessons captured for future changes.

methodologies.name: earned-value-management
methodologies.description: Earned Value Management combines actual spending with planned progress to calculate performance indices that predict final cost and schedule. Apply continuously throughout projects to detect cost overruns and schedule slips weeks in advance. Delivers early warning system for corrective action and realistic forecasts of project completion.

methodologies.name: gitlab-boards
methodologies.description: GitLab's integrated issue tracking and kanban boards enable DevOps teams to manage work alongside CI/CD pipelines. Use when team leverages GitLab for both code and project management, requiring unified DevOps environment. Delivers seamless workflow from planning to deployment with automated issue closure and integrated CI/CD triggers.

methodologies.name: hybrid-delivery
methodologies.description: Hybrid approaches combine waterfall planning and governance with agile execution and iteration to fit projects with mixed characteristics. Apply when some aspects require upfront planning (hardware, compliance) while others need flexibility (software, creative work). Delivers best of both worlds: structured oversight with adaptive delivery.

methodologies.name: jira-workflow-management
methodologies.description: Jira provides customizable workflows, automation rules, and reporting for agile and traditional project tracking. Use when managing scaled agile teams requiring complex permissions, integrations, and governance across multiple projects. Delivers enterprise-grade visibility with flexible configuration for team processes.

methodologies.name: lessons-learned
methodologies.description: Structured process to capture knowledge throughout and after projects by documenting what worked, what failed, root causes, and recommendations. Conduct sessions at milestones and closure to prevent repeating mistakes and build institutional knowledge. Delivers continuous organizational learning and process improvements applied to future projects.

methodologies.name: performance-domains-overview
methodologies.description: Project Management Framework 7 defines eight interconnected performance domains (Stakeholder, Team, Development Approach, Planning, Project Work, Delivery, Measurement, Uncertainty) that focus on outcomes rather than processes. Use this framework to assess and balance all critical areas of project management. Delivers holistic view of project health and guidance on where to apply methodologies.

methodologies.name: pm-certification-alignment-2026
methodologies.description: Guide to exam topics and weight distribution for 2026 PM certification, emphasizing shift toward business environment (26% vs 8%) and value delivery over process compliance. Use to prepare for certification exam with focus on strategic business skills, governance, and sustainability. Delivers exam preparation strategy and study focus areas.

methodologies.name: pm-certification-changes-2026
methodologies.description: Summary of domain weight changes in 2026 certification exam with increased emphasis on business environment (26%), strategic alignment, and value delivery. Highlight new study focus areas including business case development, benefits realization, and organizational change management. Delivers quick reference for exam preparation priorities.

methodologies.name: pm-framework-focus-areas
methodologies.description: Project Management Framework 8 replaces Process Groups with five flexible Focus Areas (Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring & Controlling, Closing) distributed across Performance Domains. Use this flexible framework for both predictive and agile projects with non-prescriptive processes. Delivers adaptable methodology that fits context rather than forcing rigid process compliance.

methodologies.name: pm-tool-selection
methodologies.description: Structured evaluation framework comparing tools across core features, usability, integrations, enterprise capabilities, and cost. Use when selecting or replacing project management platforms through stakeholder interviews, requirements matrices, and POC evaluation. Delivers data-driven decision framework and TCO analysis to justify tool investment.

methodologies.name: predictive-analytics-pm
methodologies.description: Apply machine learning to project data for schedule forecasting, budget prediction, resource optimization, and risk detection. Use when historical project data is clean and accessible to identify patterns early and make proactive adjustments. Delivers AI-powered insights that complement human judgment and reduce overruns.

methodologies.name: procurement-management
methodologies.description: End-to-end vendor management from make-or-buy analysis through contract closure, including Statement of Work creation, vendor evaluation, and change management. Apply when outsourcing work to ensure clear deliverables, fair evaluation, and controlled risks. Delivers cost-effective vendor relationships and protected project interests through contracts.

methodologies.name: project-closure
methodologies.description: Formal closure process verifying deliverable acceptance, releasing resources, closing contracts, capturing lessons, and transitioning to operations. Conduct at project completion to ensure stakeholder sign-off, free up people for new work, and extract knowledge. Delivers defined endpoint, resource availability, and organizational learning.

methodologies.name: project-integration
methodologies.description: Project manager as integrator coordinating across all knowledge areas (scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, risk, communications, procurement) to maintain coherence and make trade-off decisions. Use throughout projects for change control, status monitoring, and decision-making. Delivers unified project view and prevents silos from sub-optimizing whole.

methodologies.name: quality-management
methodologies.description: Three-process quality approach (Plan, Assure, Control) that builds quality in, monitors processes, and verifies deliverables meet standards. Apply at project start to define metrics, throughout execution to maintain standards, and at delivery to confirm acceptance. Delivers reduced rework, customer satisfaction, and sustainable team morale.

methodologies.name: raci-matrix
methodologies.description: Assigns Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed roles to team members for each project task to clarify authority and accountability. Create at project kickoff to prevent confusion on decision authority and task ownership. Delivers explicit role clarity reducing bottlenecks and duplicate work.

methodologies.name: ref-pmbok
methodologies.description: Quick reference guide to Project Management Framework 7th and 8th editions covering performance domains, principles, estimation techniques, EVM formulas, and risk strategies. Use for rapid lookup of framework concepts, domain weights, and decision criteria. Delivers compressed reference material for framework guidance.

methodologies.name: resource-management
methodologies.description: Identify, acquire, and optimize human and physical resources by assessing needs, evaluating availability, creating allocation plans, and leveling conflicts. Apply from planning through execution to prevent bottlenecks and burnout. Delivers efficient resource utilization and team capability development.

methodologies.name: risk-management
methodologies.description: Systematic identification, analysis, and response planning for threats and opportunities including probability/impact assessment and response strategy selection. Conduct throughout project lifecycle with regular reviews and trigger monitoring. Delivers early warning system for surprises and prepared contingency plans.

methodologies.name: risk-register
methodologies.description: Living document tracking all identified risks with probability, impact, scores, response strategies, owners, and status updates. Establish at project start and review weekly to catch new risks and track effectiveness of responses. Delivers central repository of uncertainty management and accountability.

methodologies.name: schedule-development
methodologies.description: Convert work packages into activities, sequence dependencies, estimate durations, identify critical path, and add buffers to create realistic timeline. Build schedule during planning using multiple estimation techniques and dependency analysis. Delivers project duration forecast and visibility into schedule drivers.

methodologies.name: scope-management
methodologies.description: Collect requirements, define scope statement, create Work Breakdown Structure, validate deliverables, and control changes. Apply from initiation through closure to prevent scope creep and misaligned expectations. Delivers clear boundaries, controlled changes, and stakeholder agreement.

methodologies.name: seven-performance-domains
methodologies.description: Project Management Framework 8 reorganization into seven domains (Governance, Scope, Schedule, Finance, Stakeholders, Resources, Risk) with integrated quality and communications. Use this updated framework for projects requiring strong governance, value delivery focus, and sustainability considerations. Delivers modern PM approach emphasizing business outcomes and organizational alignment.

methodologies.name: six-core-principles
methodologies.description: Six Core Principles from PMBoK 8 guide ethical and effective project leadership: Adopt a holistic view of projects within organizational systems, focus on value delivery rather than just outputs, embed quality throughout rather than testing it in, lead with accountability and transparency, integrate sustainability considerations, and build empowered teams. These principles replace the traditional framework's activity-based approach with outcome-oriented thinking.

methodologies.name: stakeholder-engagement
methodologies.description: Stakeholder engagement identifies and manages all people affected by or affecting the project using a Power-Interest grid. This beginner methodology walks through identifying stakeholders, analyzing their influence and interest levels, planning engagement strategies, executing communication, and monitoring changes. Effective engagement prevents scope creep, manages expectations, and removes hidden blockers before they derail progress.

methodologies.name: stakeholder-engagement-advanced
methodologies.description: Advanced stakeholder engagement moves beyond identification to relationship building, focusing on moving stakeholders through engagement levels from unaware to leading champions. This intermediate methodology covers assessing current engagement levels, understanding stakeholder needs and motivations, developing tailored strategies for each stakeholder, executing engagement activities with tracking, and recognizing red flags when engagement is declining, enabling project managers to build sustainable support networks.

methodologies.name: stakeholder-register
methodologies.description: A stakeholder register centralizes information about everyone affected by the project, serving as the foundation for engagement planning. This beginner methodology documents each stakeholder's role, interest, influence, impact, and attitude using a Power-Interest grid, then defines communication strategies for each. Maintaining an up-to-date register prevents surprises and ensures no critical voices are ignored.

methodologies.name: team-development
methodologies.description: Team development applies Tuckman's model (Forming-Storming-Norming-Performing-Adjourning) to guide teams through maturity stages with appropriate PM interventions. This intermediate methodology includes building trust through transparency, developing skills through training and mentoring, and improving processes through retrospectives. Understanding the current stage prevents overreacting to storming conflict while recognizing when a team needs more autonomy.

methodologies.name: value-stream-management
methodologies.description: Value Stream Management (VSM) combined with DORA metrics provides end-to-end visibility from customer request through delivery, identifying bottlenecks outside traditional software teams. This methodology uses flow metrics like lead time and process time alongside DORA deployment metrics to measure true organizational effectiveness, revealing how AI productivity gains can evaporate without holistic optimization across all stages.

methodologies.name: wbs-creation
methodologies.description: Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) creation decomposes projects into manageable, deliverable-oriented pieces using hierarchical decomposition. This beginner methodology starts with major deliverables, decomposes to work packages measurable by the 8/80-hour rule, and applies the 100% rule to ensure complete scope capture. A WBS transforms overwhelming projects into estimable, assignable pieces while the WBS dictionary documents each element.

methodologies.name: work-breakdown-structure
methodologies.description: Work Breakdown Structure organizes project scope hierarchically from major deliverables down to work packages, applying the 100% rule to verify nothing is missed. This beginner methodology guides creation from deliverable identification through decomposition, validation, and dictionary documentation. WBS serves as the foundation for scheduling, budgeting, and team organization, preventing scope creep and ensuring clarity about what constitutes project completion.

domains.description: Product Manager orchestrator covers MVP/MLP planning, feature prioritization (RICE, MoSCoW frameworks), roadmap design, backlog management, product discovery, validation, OKR setting, and launch planning. Use this when defining products, prioritizing features, managing backlogs, planning launches, or iterating based on user feedback. Includes planning sub-skill (17 methodologies) for strategy and operations sub-skill (16 methodologies) for execution.

methodologies.name: blurred-roles-team-evolution
methodologies.description: Modern product teams operate as overlapping Venn diagrams rather than relay-race handoffs, requiring skill overlap across traditional boundaries. This methodology reflects 2026 reality where PMs understand design and data, designers track metrics and understand constraints, engineers participate in discovery, and data analysts join product decisions. The AI-era ratio of 2 PMs per engineer (vs. traditional 1:4-8) demands this integrated team structure for agility.

methodologies.name: competitive-positioning
methodologies.description: Competitive positioning defines unique market value using April Dunford's framework: identify competitive alternatives, discover unique attributes, translate to customer value, identify best-fit customers, define market category, and craft positioning statements. This intermediate methodology prevents generic 'we're like X but better' claims and feature wars by grounding positioning in what customers actually care about and why your solution fits their needs better than alternatives.

methodologies.name: continuous-discovery-habits
methodologies.description: Continuous discovery establishes an ongoing cadence of customer conversations using Teresa Torres' framework with product trios, opportunity-solution trees, and weekly customer touchpoints. This methodology embeds discovery as a 15-20% capacity allocation alongside delivery, with structured weekly rituals: interviews, feedback review, behavioral analysis, team syncs, and planning. Organizations like Spotify and Tesco use this framework to maintain learning velocity and avoid building features nobody wants.

methodologies.name: experimentation-at-scale
methodologies.description: Experimentation at scale moves beyond ad-hoc A/B testing to organizational learning infrastructure using feature flags, analytics, and warehouses for hypothesis-driven decisions. This methodology defines maturity levels from ad-hoc to cultural, establishes guardrail metrics beyond success metrics, uses sequential testing for faster decisions, and documents learnings for organization-wide access. At scale (500-100,000 annual experiments), companies like Microsoft and GoDaddy build competitive advantages through decision velocity.

methodologies.name: feedback-management
methodologies.description: Feedback management creates a systematic pipeline to collect, categorize, prioritize, and act on user feedback from multiple sources including support, reviews, surveys, and interviews. This beginner methodology centralizes feedback in one tool, establishes categorization taxonomy, aggregates patterns, connects to roadmap priorities using RICE, and closes the loop with users about decisions. Systematic feedback management prevents the loudest voices from dominating while ensuring users feel heard.

methodologies.name: learning-speed-competitive-moat
methodologies.description: In markets where AI enables rapid product clones, learning speed becomes the sustainable competitive advantage through faster signal collection, belief updates, and execution. This methodology automates market signal collection, conducts weekly strategy reviews instead of quarterly ones, reduces approval chains for decision speed, and ships experiments in days not weeks. Organizational learning infrastructure indexed by AI and decision logs tracking predictions vs. outcomes build defensibility no feature can match.

methodologies.name: methodologies-summary
methodologies.description: Summary reference for 33 product management methodologies spanning MVP/MLP planning, feature prioritization (RICE, MoSCoW), roadmaps, discovery and validation, business models, operations, and extended techniques. This methodology index provides quick selection guidance: use MVP for unvalidated ideas, MLP when MVP validates but needs retention, RICE for feature prioritization, continuous discovery for learning velocity, and stage-appropriate strategies for product lifecycle management.

methodologies.name: minimum-product-frameworks
methodologies.description: Nine minimum product frameworks solve different problems: MVP validates core assumptions, MLP adds emotional connection in crowded markets, MMP generates enterprise revenue, MAC differentiates on one feature, RAT tests the biggest risk, MDP exceeds expectations, MVA delivers business value, MFP proves technical feasibility, and SLC balances simplicity with polish. This methodology prevents the mistake of building generic MVPs in red-ocean markets where being 'minimum' means 'mediocre' compared to 'good enough' alternatives.

methodologies.name: mlp-planning
methodologies.description: MLP (Minimum Lovable Product) planning evolves MVP into a product users want to stick with and recommend by adding polish and delight. This intermediate methodology layers functionality, reliability, usability, and delight across core features, identifies delight opportunities at first impression and success moments, prioritizes Polish tasks, and audits features to ensure 4+ rating on all layers. MLP increases retention by 40% and NPS significantly by understanding that 'viable' without 'lovable' loses users to better UX.

methodologies.name: portfolio-strategy
methodologies.description: Portfolio strategy allocates product investments across three horizons: 70% to core products (stable revenue, 0-12 months), 20% to adjacent markets (new segments, 12-24 months), and 10% to transformational bets (breakthrough innovation, 24-36 months). This methodology adjusts allocations by economic condition, with growth economies supporting 60/25/15 allocation while recessions require 80/15/5 protection of core. Portfolio balance prevents over-betting on unproven innovation while defending against disruption.

methodologies.name: product-analytics
methodologies.description: Product analytics connects user behavior data to product decisions through tracking plans, dashboards, and regular analysis rituals. This intermediate methodology defines key questions before implementing tracking, standardizes event naming, builds role-specific dashboards, establishes daily-weekly-monthly-quarterly review cadences, and focuses on actionable metrics rather than vanity metrics. Moving from basic 'what happened' insights through advanced funnels and cohorts to predictive modeling enables data-driven prioritization.

methodologies.name: product-explainability
methodologies.description: Product explainability ensures products communicate clearly to both humans and AI systems in a world where discovery is AI-mediated. This 2026 methodology documents purpose through value propositions and metadata, capabilities through documentation and schema markup, limitations and constraints, and use cases with semantic tagging. Keeping knowledge bases aligned with actual products becomes essential as AI systems evaluate products without human intermediation in search, recommendations, and buying guidance.

methodologies.name: product-led-growth
methodologies.description: Product-led growth uses the product as the growth engine through self-serve onboarding, rapid time-to-value (aha moment <5 min), built-in virality, and expansion revenue without sales intervention. This methodology optimizes for 40%+ activation rates, tracks Product Qualified Leads weekly, targets 120%+ Net Revenue Retention, and personalizes onboarding by user segment. PLG companies achieve higher revenue per employee: Ahrefs reached $40M ARR with 40 people using pure PLG.

methodologies.name: product-lifecycle
methodologies.description: Product lifecycle management applies stage-appropriate strategies (introduction-growth-maturity-decline) with different investment levels, metrics, and team focus for each stage. This intermediate methodology helps avoid growth tactics in declining products or optimization investments in early launches, provides stage-specific KPIs, anticipates transitions, and plans exits. Recognizing the current stage prevents resource misallocation while building confidence in strategy decisions.

methodologies.name: product-operations
methodologies.description: Product operations dedicates people to process standardization, tool management, data infrastructure, enablement, and stakeholder communication so product managers focus on discovery and strategy. This 2026 methodology shows 96% of organizations now have Product Ops with 50% reporting directly to CPO, addressing the core problem that PMs spend 30-40% of time on operational tasks. Product Ops is evolving from tactical process management to strategic enablement leveraging AI for automated reporting and predictive analytics.

methodologies.name: release-planning
methodologies.description: Release planning bundles completed features into logical releases with clear goals, communication plans, and success metrics. This intermediate methodology defines release types (major quarterly, minor monthly, patch weekly, hotfix immediate), assesses readiness through checklists, coordinates stakeholder communication, and plans post-release monitoring. Structured releases prevent chaos, shipping on Fridays, monster releases, and communication failures while enabling teams to tell coherent product stories.

methodologies.name: stakeholder-management
methodologies.description: Stakeholder management identifies everyone affected by your product and keeps them appropriately engaged through systematic planning and communication. When stakeholder misalignment threatens delivery, use this to map power and interest levels, define engagement strategies, and resolve conflicts proactively. It delivers aligned teams, fewer surprises, and smoother approvals by clarifying decision authority upfront.

methodologies.name: technical-debt-management
methodologies.description: Technical debt management makes invisible productivity losses visible and prioritizes paydown systematically. Use it when team velocity slows mysteriously or fear prevents safe refactoring. This methodology quantifies impact, ranks debt by interest rate and alignment, allocates 15-20% sprint capacity to paydown, and prevents new debt through definition of done. It restores velocity by converting technical drag into managed work.

methodologies.name: workflows
methodologies.description: Product manager workflows define structured processes from idea to first task, including project bootstrap and most lovable product planning. Use these workflows to move from vision through tech stack selection, MVP scoping, backlog creation, and constitution writing in a predictable sequence. They compress setup time and ensure all stakeholders confirm direction before engineering begins.

domains.description: Product Operations handles day-to-day product management execution—prioritization frameworks, backlog grooming, product analytics, feedback management, technical debt tracking, and stakeholder coordination. Use when managing backlogs, analyzing metrics, collecting user feedback, or optimizing product processes. Focuses on continuous iteration and data-driven decision making.

methodologies.name: blurred-roles-team-evolution
methodologies.description: Blurred roles and team evolution addresses the shift from traditional handoff-based teams to overlapping skill sets required in 2026. Use this when your team feels disconnected or siloed. It redefines what PMs, designers, and engineers must understand beyond their role (design fluency, data depth, AI basics, distribution) and explains why modern teams need more PMs per engineer. It rebuilds alignment by clarifying the new operating model.

methodologies.name: experimentation-at-scale
methodologies.description: Experimentation at scale transforms ad-hoc testing into an organizational learning system with feature flags, statistical rigor, and hypothesis-driven culture. Apply this when test results aren't informing strategy or when you're making decisions without evidence. It defines maturity levels, tools, best practices, and guardrail metrics that enable 500+ experiments per year. It builds competitive advantage through decision velocity backed by data.

methodologies.name: learning-speed-competitive-moat
methodologies.description: Learning speed as competitive moat positions organizational agility as the primary defense against AI-enabled product cloning. Use when facing commoditization or when large competitors move faster. It emphasizes rapid signal collection, weekly belief updates, AI-powered market monitoring, and decision logs that track predictions versus outcomes. The methodology builds advantage not through features but through how fast your team notices changes and ships different answers.

methodologies.name: product-explainability
methodologies.description: Product explainability ensures your product communicates clearly to both people and AI systems that increasingly mediate discovery and purchase. Use when AI-powered recommendations don't mention you or when search results get your value proposition wrong. It keeps knowledge bases aligned with actual product, uses structured data for machine consumption, documents capabilities and limitations clearly. It maintains product visibility in an AI-mediated world.

methodologies.name: product-led-growth
methodologies.description: Product-led growth makes the product itself the primary acquisition engine through self-serve onboarding, fast time-to-value, and expansion via product usage rather than sales. Use when sales cost exceeds lifetime value or when you need to scale without hiring a sales team. It optimizes for activation rate, time-to-aha, product-qualified leads, and expansion revenue. It achieves 10x revenue per employee through product-centric motion.

methodologies.name: product-operations
methodologies.description: Product operations (Product Ops) frees product managers from operational overhead by standardizing workflows, managing tools, centralizing metrics, and enabling cross-functional alignment. Use when PMs spend 30-40% of time on admin instead of strategy or when teams can't find shared truth on metrics. It evolves from tactical process management to strategic enablement that uses AI automation and predictive analytics. It recovers PM capacity for discovery and strategy.

methodologies.name: stakeholder-management
methodologies.description: Stakeholder management at the product operations level keeps product decisions aligned across engineering, design, marketing, and executive teams through systematic engagement and communication planning. Use when conflicts between functions block progress or when key stakeholders surprise you late in a project. It maps stakeholder interest and power, defines engagement cadence and channels, escalates blockers quickly. It removes friction from cross-functional execution.

domains.description: Product Planning focuses on strategic planning activities including MVP scoping, roadmap design, OKR setting, discovery methods, user story mapping, and launch planning. Use when defining product vision, scoping MVPs, creating roadmaps, or planning product launches. Emphasizes aligning product strategy with business goals and user needs.

methodologies.name: competitive-positioning
methodologies.description: Competitive positioning defines how your product is uniquely valuable compared to alternatives by translating attributes into customer value and finding underserved segments. Use when you can't explain why someone should choose you or when you're racing on features. The methodology identifies competitive alternatives, maps unique attributes, translates to specific customer outcomes, and finds market gaps. It creates positioning that resonates instead of me-too claims.

methodologies.name: continuous-discovery-habits
methodologies.description: Continuous discovery habits embed weekly customer conversations and assumption testing into product rhythm rather than treating discovery as one-time kickoff. Use when product decisions feel disconnected from customer reality or when you're building what you think is right instead of what customers need. It establishes product trio weekly cadence, maps insights to opportunity trees, balances discovery (15-20%) with delivery (65-75%). It keeps product direction grounded as markets change.

methodologies.name: portfolio-strategy
methodologies.description: Portfolio strategy allocates capacity across three horizons: core (70%) for consistent revenue, adjacent (20%) for new markets, and transformational (10%) for innovation bets. Use when you lack strategic direction or when economic uncertainty requires balanced risk allocation. It shifts allocation based on market conditions (growth vs recession) and defines success metrics and timeframes for each horizon. It prevents all-or-nothing bets while protecting core business.

domains.description: Market Researcher handles market sizing (TAM/SAM/SOM), competitor analysis, competitive intelligence, pricing research, trend analysis, niche evaluation, business model planning, and idea generation. Use when evaluating market opportunities, analyzing competitors, pricing products, validating market size, or assessing niche viability. Includes 22 methodologies grounded in frameworks like Porter's Five Forces and business model canvas.

methodologies.name: business-model-planning
methodologies.description: Business model planning evaluates how products create, deliver, and capture value through business model canvas, revenue model analysis, and unit economics validation. Use when you're unsure monetization will work or when revenue model mismatches customer expectations. It maps value chain, identifies revenue opportunities, compares comparable models, calculates CAC and LTV, stress tests assumptions. It prevents building products with no sustainable path to profitability.

methodologies.name: business-model-research
methodologies.description: Business model research analyzes how to sustainably create and capture value by examining revenue model options, building business canvases, and calculating unit economics. Use when exploring business model alternatives or when current model isn't scaling. It covers five revenue models (subscription, one-time, transaction, advertising, marketplace), stress tests against competition and churn, validates LTV:CAC ratio. It ensures product economics work before scaling.

methodologies.name: competitive-intelligence
methodologies.description: Competitive intelligence monitors competitor moves continuously rather than point-in-time snapshots using platforms like Crayon, Klue, and AI-powered tools. Use to anticipate competitor moves before they impact your roadmap or to understand emerging competitive threats. It tracks pricing changes, feature launches, hiring patterns, customer reviews weekly. The methodology shifts from reactive description to predictive analysis, enabling preemptive strategy.

methodologies.name: competitive-intelligence-methods
methodologies.description: Competitive intelligence methods provide frameworks for competitor analysis and feature gap identification across direct, indirect, and substitute competitors. Use to find market whitespace or to understand why competitors haven't built features customers request. It maps competitors systematically, identifies feature gaps with evidence from reviews and forums, validates whether gaps are opportunities or intentional. It uncovers differentiation vectors competitors missed.

methodologies.name: competitor-analysis
methodologies.description: Competitor analysis maps the competitive landscape to find positioning gaps and learn from others' moves. Use when entering a crowded market or when you need to articulate differentiation. It identifies direct and indirect competitors, analyzes product, pricing, positioning across each, maps them on positioning matrices, and identifies gaps. It prevents copying competitors and reveals underserved segments.

methodologies.name: continuous-discovery
methodologies.description: Continuous discovery integrates ongoing user interviews and assumption testing into product workflow using the Teresa Torres framework. Use when discovery feels disconnected from delivery or when you're shipping based on assumptions rather than customer evidence. It establishes weekly interview and testing cadence, uses product trio model, maintains research repository. It ensures product evolution tracks customer needs as they change.

methodologies.name: distribution-channel-research
methodologies.description: Distribution channel research identifies and evaluates pathways to customers by testing channels against fit criteria and modeling economics. Use when product exists but nobody knows about it or when you're spreading resources across unproven channels. It maps organic, paid, sales-led, and viral channels, evaluates fit, calculates CAC for each, stress tests economics. It prioritizes 1-2 high-fit channels for initial focus before diversifying.

methodologies.name: market-analysis
methodologies.description: Market analysis provides frameworks for TAM/SAM/SOM sizing, trend analysis, and competitor intelligence to ground market opportunity. Use when assessing whether a market is worth pursuing or when setting realistic targets. It covers three sizing methods (top-down, bottom-up, competitor-based), trend assessment by stage and drivers, and competitive positioning mapping. It separates realistic opportunities from hype.

methodologies.name: market-research-tam-sam-som
methodologies.description: Market research (TAM/SAM/SOM) sizes total addressable market, serviceable market, and realistic capture targets using three calculation methods. Use when designing roadmap or fundraising to ground claims in data. It calculates TAM (theoretical 100% capture), SAM (your segment), and SOM (realistic 3-year target), validates estimates across methods, checks assumptions. It prevents both over-optimism and missed opportunities by grounding strategy in market reality.

methodologies.name: product-development-trends
methodologies.description: Product development trends synthesizes market shifts toward agile, data-driven, cross-functional development powered by AI-augmented ideation and continuous feedback. Use to understand how development methodology must evolve to stay competitive. It describes AI's role in gap identification, continuous feedback loops, rapid pivots, and the speed expectations (days not weeks). It positions organizations to move faster than traditional competitors.

methodologies.name: product-development-trends-2026
methodologies.description: Product development trends 2026 covers modern development combining agile speed, data-driven precision, and cross-functional breadth where AI augments ideation and humans decide significance. Use when planning product methodology for next year or when current process can't keep pace. It identifies key trends (AI ideation, continuous feedback, rapid pivots), speed requirements (turnaround in days), and research outputs needed (market reports, need assessments, validation studies). It prepares teams for accelerated development cadence.

methodologies.name: risk-assessment
methodologies.description: Risk assessment identifies and plans for potential business threats through structured categorization (market, product, team, financial, operational risks) and probability-impact analysis. Use this methodology when evaluating viability of a business idea, launching a new product, or planning major pivots. It delivers a risk register with mitigation strategies and contingency plans, enabling proactive management rather than reactive firefighting.

methodologies.name: trend-analysis
methodologies.description: Trend analysis identifies emerging market patterns and evaluates their viability for business opportunities by monitoring signal sources, assessing adoption curves, and analyzing STEEP factors. Apply this when deciding whether to enter a new market or product category to avoid chasing fads while capturing real opportunities. The methodology delivers trend strength scores and timing assessments, showing exactly when markets are ready for entry.

domains.description: Researcher orchestrates both market and user research, coordinating market intelligence (TAM, competitors, pricing) with user insights (personas, pain points, validation). Use as entry point for any research need—it routes to market researcher for business intelligence or user researcher for user understanding. Includes 43 combined methodologies with integrated research workflows.

methodologies.name: agent-invocation
methodologies.description: Agent invocation provides reference patterns for orchestrating research agents across nine modes (ideas, market, competitors, personas, validation, niche, pricing, names, domains) with specific prompts and expected outputs. Use this when building research workflows or triggering subagent research within larger systems. It ensures consistent agent selection, proper mode usage, and sequential execution to prevent rate limiting and maintain data quality.

methodologies.name: audience-segmentation
methodologies.description: Audience segmentation divides markets into distinct groups with different needs using demographic, behavioral, psychographic, and needs-based criteria, then prioritizes segments by attractiveness. Use this when designing products or marketing strategies to avoid building for 'everyone' and instead focus resources on high-fit segments. The methodology produces segment profiles, scoring matrices, and channel/messaging implications for each target group.

methodologies.name: business-model-research
methodologies.description: Business model research analyzes how a business creates, delivers, and captures value through the Business Model Canvas framework and revenue model options (subscription, one-time, transaction, advertising, marketplace). Apply this early when designing a product to ensure viable unit economics and sustainable monetization. The methodology includes canvas templates, unit economics calculators, and stress-testing frameworks to validate business viability before building.

methodologies.name: competitive-intelligence
methodologies.description: Competitive intelligence provides continuous monitoring frameworks and tools for tracking competitor signals (pricing, features, hiring, funding, reviews) in real-time rather than snapshots. Use this to stay aware of competitive moves and market shifts, enabling faster response to threats. Modern platforms with AI-powered analysis surface weak signals and predictive insights, helping anticipate competitor moves rather than reacting after they happen.

methodologies.name: competitor-analysis
methodologies.description: Competitor analysis systematically maps direct, indirect, and future competitors using a positioning matrix, then identifies gaps and opportunities for differentiation. Conduct this early in product development to understand the competitive landscape and find underserved positions. The analysis produces positioning matrices, gap analyses, and differentiation statements showing exactly how to compete effectively.

methodologies.name: continuous-discovery
methodologies.description: Continuous discovery integrates user research into product development cycles through weekly habits (interviews, assumption testing, analytics review) rather than treating discovery as a one-time activity. Use this to keep product decisions grounded in current user needs as markets and preferences evolve. The methodology from Teresa Torres emphasizes weekly customer interviews and assumption testing to maintain product-market fit over time.

methodologies.name: distribution-channel-research
methodologies.description: Distribution channel research identifies and evaluates pathways to reach customers across organic, paid, sales-led, and viral channels by mapping customer discovery sources and modeling channel economics. Apply this when designing go-to-market strategy to focus resources on high-fit channels rather than trying everything at once. The methodology produces channel evaluation matrices, economics models, and testing plans showing which channels to pursue.

methodologies.name: frameworks
methodologies.description: Research frameworks provide reference implementations of core methodologies including 7 Ps ideation, Paul Graham questions, niche evaluation, TAM/SAM/SOM market sizing, STEEP analysis, competitive mapping, JTBD, and value proposition canvas. Use this as a quick lookup guide when applying research methodologies to ensure you're using proven frameworks. Each framework includes definitions, step-by-step processes, and decision thresholds for moving forward.

methodologies.name: market-research-tam-sam-som
methodologies.description: Market research using TAM/SAM/SOM frameworks quantifies market opportunity by calculating Total Addressable Market, Serviceable Available Market, and Serviceable Obtainable Market through top-down (research-based) or bottom-up (customer-based) methods. Use this to set realistic revenue targets and understand market size constraints when evaluating business ideas. The methodology includes validation checks to ensure estimates stay grounded in reality rather than becoming 'precision theater.'

methodologies.name: methodologies-detail
methodologies.description: Methodologies detail provides comprehensive reference documentation for 20 core research methodologies extracted into a single reference document, covering idea generation, market analysis, user research, and business model planning. Use this when you need complete methodology definitions, templates, and examples in one searchable resource. It consolidates frameworks for ideation, pain research, niche evaluation, TAM/SAM/SOM, trend analysis, competitor analysis, user interviews, personas, pricing, and naming.

methodologies.name: methodologies-index
methodologies.description: Methodologies index organizes 22 research methodologies across five focused topic files (idea generation, market analysis, user research, business model planning, naming/domains) with quick lookup tables and file size guidance. Use this as a navigation guide when searching for specific methodologies or understanding which files contain relevant content. It provides file organization, quick lookup by methodology name and agent mode, and migration notes for reference.

methodologies.name: opportunity-solution-trees
methodologies.description: Opportunity-solution trees (OST) provide a visual discovery framework connecting business outcomes to customer opportunities to potential solutions and experiments in a hierarchical structure. Use this to align teams around what to build and how to test it before committing resources. The framework manages discovery cycles, prevents jumping straight to solutions without understanding opportunities, and creates shared understanding across product and engineering teams.

methodologies.name: persona-building
methodologies.description: Persona building creates semi-fictional representations of ideal customers based on real interview data (minimum 10 interviews) by identifying patterns across demographics, goals, frustrations, and behaviors. Build personas when designing products or marketing strategies to replace 'everyone is our customer' thinking with specific, actionable user models. The methodology includes templates, validation approaches, and guidance on maintaining 2-4 personas rather than dozens of fictional profiles.

methodologies.name: product-development-trends
methodologies.description: Product development trends document modern approaches combining agile speed, data-driven precision, and cross-functional collaboration with AI-augmented ideation for rapid gap identification. Use this to understand how product development is evolving in 2026, including AI's role in pattern recognition at scale and the shift toward real-time iteration. The methodology emphasizes speed, continuous feedback, and rapid pivots as market expectations change faster than annual cycles.

methodologies.name: product-development-trends-2026
methodologies.description: Product development trends for 2026 highlight AI-augmented ideation, continuous customer feedback loops, quarterly adjustment cycles, and cross-functional team collaboration as essential capabilities. Apply this when planning product development processes to stay competitive with market pace expectations of days rather than weeks for insights and pivots. The methodology shows how AI surfaces weak signals while humans decide what matters, creating data-grounded product ideas.

methodologies.name: risk-assessment
methodologies.description: Risk assessment methodically identifies, analyzes, and plans for business risks across five categories (market, product, team, financial, operational) using probability-impact matrices and response strategies. Use this when evaluating business ideas, planning launches, or making major strategic decisions to identify what could derail success and prepare mitigation plans. The methodology includes risk registers, pre-mortem templates, and contingency planning frameworks to manage uncertainty systematically.

methodologies.name: survey-design
methodologies.description: Survey design creates effective quantitative research instruments by defining clear research objectives, choosing appropriate question types, structuring surveys for completion, and analyzing results. Use this to quantify patterns discovered in interviews at scale, measure preferences across populations, and track satisfaction over time. The methodology emphasizes pilot testing, neutral wording, appropriate length for completion rates, and sample size requirements for valid conclusions.

methodologies.name: trend-analysis
methodologies.description: Trend analysis identifies emerging patterns in technology, markets, and consumer behavior, then assesses their business viability through strength scoring and timing assessment against adoption curves. Apply this to decide whether to pursue trending opportunities or wait for clarity before entering. The methodology includes signal sources for different trend types, STEEP analysis framework, strength scoring, and guidance on optimal entry points along adoption curves.

methodologies.name: user-research-at-scale
methodologies.description: User research at scale leverages AI-augmented platforms and automation to test with thousands of users simultaneously, conduct real-time sentiment analysis, and identify cross-demographic patterns. Use this when product velocity demands faster research cycles than traditional methods allow or when you need statistical confidence across large user populations. The methodology combines human researchers with AI tools to increase research throughput while maintaining quality insights.

methodologies.name: workflows
methodologies.description: Research workflows provide detailed process flows for idea discovery, product research, and project naming, including context gathering questions, module selection, research depth options, and next steps. Use this to execute structured research programs sequentially rather than ad-hoc, ensuring all important areas get covered systematically. The methodology emphasizes sequential agent execution, data quality standards, and integration points with downstream activities like GTM and SDD.

domains.description: Accessibility Specialist ensures digital products meet WCAG 2.2 and ADA compliance standards. It covers accessibility audits, assistive technology testing (screen readers, magnifiers, voice control), VPAT creation, heuristic evaluation, cognitive accessibility, and inclusive design practices. Use when conducting compliance audits, testing with assistive tech, meeting legal requirements, or designing for inclusion. Includes 22 methodologies across standards, testing, and design approaches.

methodologies.name: a11y-basics
methodologies.description: Accessibility basics teach WCAG POUR principles (perceivable, operable, understandable, robust) and conformance levels (A, AA, AAA) with quick evaluation methods and common fixes for forms and images. Use this to understand accessibility fundamentals and implement quick wins that exclude disabilities from digital products. The methodology includes five-minute tests, pre-commit checklists, and explanation of how automated tools catch only 30% of issues.

methodologies.name: a11y-testing
methodologies.description: Accessibility testing combines automated scanning, manual keyboard testing, screen reader validation, and cognitive accessibility checks in a structured process that prioritizes issues by impact. Apply this when developing products to catch accessibility issues early (70-80% can be prevented at design stage) rather than retrofitting expensively at the end. The methodology includes audit templates, detailed issue reporting formats, and comprehensive testing checklists for all WCAG principles.

methodologies.name: accessibility-first-design
methodologies.description: Accessibility-first design embeds accessibility into the design process from day one through color/contrast planning, semantic HTML structure, inclusive design principles, and proper handoff documentation for developers. Use this approach to prevent accessibility debt rather than paying higher retrofitting costs later, and to build genuinely inclusive products that work for users with diverse abilities. The methodology includes design checklists, common patterns for forms/buttons/modals, and mobile accessibility considerations.

methodologies.name: ada-title-ii-compliance-2026
methodologies.description: Ensures US government digital services meet ADA Title II compliance by April 2026 through WCAG 2.1 AA standards. Covers website accessibility, video captions, document remediation, and federal requirements. Apply this when building public-facing government digital services to avoid lawsuits, federal funding loss, and ensure citizens with disabilities can access services.

methodologies.name: ar-design-patterns
methodologies.description: Designs AR experiences that respect real-world environments while providing seamless digital overlays without overwhelming users or obscuring important information. Covers surface detection, object recognition, lighting integration, and accessibility. Use when building mobile AR applications, furniture visualization, navigation overlays, or maintenance guidance systems.

methodologies.name: cognitive-inclusion-design
methodologies.description: Addresses cognitive disabilities (ADHD, autism, dyslexia, anxiety, learning disabilities) by reducing information overload, supporting predictable navigation, and providing clear feedback. Covers 15-20% of the population often overlooked by standard accessibility. Apply when designing forms, complex workflows, content-heavy sites, or applications serving diverse cognitive needs.

methodologies.name: immersive-design-principles
methodologies.description: Prevents motion sickness, disorientation, and fatigue in VR/AR by balancing immersion with comfort through proper depth layering, smooth transitions, and realistic physics. Addresses vestibular, visual, and cognitive accessibility in immersive environments. Use when developing VR training, AR applications, or spatial computing experiences targeting diverse user comfort levels.

methodologies.name: regulatory-compliance-2026
methodologies.description: Maps global accessibility regulations (US ADA Title II/III, EU EAA, Canada ACA, UK Equality Act, Australia DDA) with their deadlines, standards, and enforcement mechanisms. Tracks compliance requirements across regions. Apply when launching digital products internationally or managing government/enterprise accessibility obligations across jurisdictions.

methodologies.name: spatial-accessibility
methodologies.description: Enables inclusive spatial interface design (AR/VR/MR) through multiple input modalities (gaze, voice, head tracking), audio navigation, haptic feedback, and seated operation. Addresses motor, visual, hearing, and cognitive disabilities in 3D environments. Use when developing spatial computing applications, XR training systems, or immersive experiences for diverse mobility and sensory abilities.

methodologies.name: testing-with-assistive-technology
methodologies.description: Validates accessibility through manual screen reader and keyboard testing beyond automated tools (which catch only 30-50% of issues). Covers NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, TalkBack and keyboard-only navigation testing. Apply after automated scanning to identify real-world barriers that assistive technology users encounter.

methodologies.name: vr-design-patterns
methodologies.description: Prevents motion sickness and disorientation in VR through grounding techniques (stable ground planes, horizon lines, reference objects), teleportation-based locomotion, and comfort settings. Addresses accessibility for wheelchair users and motion-sensitive individuals. Use when building VR games, training simulations, or immersive experiences that need broad user comfort.

methodologies.name: vui-accessibility-inclusivity
methodologies.description: Designs voice interfaces that work for diverse accents, dialects, speech impediments, and non-native speakers through adaptive training, clear prompts, and multi-modal fallbacks. Ensures inclusive voice experiences. Apply when building voice assistants, smart home systems, or voice-first applications serving global audiences.

methodologies.name: wcag-22-compliance
methodologies.description: Addresses 9 new WCAG 2.2 success criteria including focus visibility, drag alternatives, target sizes (24x24px), consistent help location, and accessible authentication without cognitive tests. Prepares for 2025-2026 regulatory adoption. Apply when updating accessibility compliance to future-proof standards or meeting new regulatory deadlines.

domains.description: UI Designer handles visual and interactive design—wireframing, prototyping, design systems, design tokens, component libraries, Figma workflows, voice UI design, and spatial computing.
