Use when creating interfaces, building design systems, designing for voice or AR/VR, or implementing visual designs. Focuses on practical implementation patterns and design system thinking.

methodologies.name: core-vui-design-principles
methodologies.description: Establishes three foundational voice UI principles: simplicity through concise language, natural conversation over robotic speech, and context awareness across turns. Prevents common failures where voice is treated like visual interface. Use as baseline for any voice UI design work.

methodologies.name: cross-platform-token-distribution
methodologies.description: Transforms design tokens across iOS, Android, and Web through Style Dictionary, Tokens Studio, and platform-specific generators producing CSS, Swift, XML, and React Native outputs. Solves multi-platform consistency without manual conversion. Apply when building design systems serving multiple platforms.

methodologies.name: design-system-success-factors
methodologies.description: Identifies four pillars for design system adoption: clear ownership, usable components, strong documentation, and real product usage. Tracks adoption through component coverage, team participation, and contribution rates. Use when launching or evaluating design system maturity and organizational adoption.

methodologies.name: enterprise-xr-applications
methodologies.description: Designs XR for enterprise use cases (training simulations, remote assistance, digital twins, data visualization, team collaboration) requiring long-session comfort, security integration, and deployment management. Differs from consumer XR in ROI focus and accessibility compliance. Apply when building spatial computing for manufacturing, facilities, or training contexts.

methodologies.name: error-handling-in-vui
methodologies.description: Manages voice recognition failures through progressive disclosure: first attempt reprompts simply, second provides examples, third escalates to visual or human support. Prevents user frustration and abandonment. Apply in all voice UI systems to handle the inevitable recognition errors gracefully.

methodologies.name: semantic-tokens-and-modes
methodologies.description: Enables design tokens to adapt across contexts (light/dark/high-contrast themes, desktop/mobile/compact densities, multi-brand scenarios) through Figma Variables and mode collections. Solves theme switching and platform variation without token proliferation. Use when building multi-modal, multi-brand, or multi-platform design systems.

methodologies.name: spatial-computing-overview
methodologies.description: Surveys 2026 spatial computing landscape (Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest, Android XR, Microsoft HoloLens, Magic Leap) and market growth trends. Provides platform context and positioning. Reference when scoping spatial UI projects or evaluating target platform capabilities.

methodologies.name: spatial-design-tools
methodologies.description: Maps tools for spatial design workflow: Figma 3D plugins for concept, ShapesXR/Gravity Sketch for VR prototyping, Unity/Unreal for high-fidelity development. Addresses design-to-development handoff for 3D experiences. Use when establishing spatial design workflows and tool selection.

methodologies.name: spatial-interaction-patterns
methodologies.description: Covers spatial input modalities (hand tracking, controllers, gaze, voice, gesture) and patterns (direct manipulation, ray-casting, gaze+dwell) with accessibility trade-offs. Matches interaction model to user ability and task complexity. Apply when designing 3D interactions for diverse input capabilities.

methodologies.name: spatial-ui-patterns
methodologies.description: Defines VR/AR UI container types (world-locked, head-locked, body-locked, hand-attached) with appropriate use cases and window management. Prevents UI placement mistakes that cause discomfort. Use when laying out information and controls in spatial environments.

methodologies.name: token-organization
methodologies.description: Prevents token proliferation through hierarchical structure (primitives → semantic → component) with lean MVP mindset and clear naming conventions. Reduces bloat that defeats design system purpose. Apply when establishing token naming and structure governance.

methodologies.name: voice-ui-patterns
methodologies.description: Provides comprehensive voice UI design guidance covering prompt writing, error recovery strategies, multimodal (voice+screen) patterns, platform-specific guidelines (Alexa/Google/Siri), conversation design principles, and performance metrics for task completion and user satisfaction. Apply as reference for all voice UI projects.

methodologies.name: vui-iot-integration
methodologies.description: Designs coherent voice control for smart home systems through scene activation, multi-device coordination, and graceful failure handling. Enables commands like 'I'm leaving' to trigger coordinated lock/lights/thermostat/security actions. Use when building IoT device interactions or home automation voice strategies.

methodologies.name: vui-privacy-security
methodologies.description: Establishes voice privacy through transparency (listening indicators), user control (easy opt-out), data minimization, encryption, and deletion rights. Masks sensitive data in banking/health contexts. Apply in all voice systems to build user trust and comply with GDPR/privacy regulations.

methodologies.name: w3c-design-tokens-standard
methodologies.description: Prepares for W3C Design Tokens standard enabling tool interoperability and future-proof token definitions across Figma, Adobe, Sketch. Audit current structure, align naming, document relationships, plan migration. Use when planning long-term design system portability and ecosystem compatibility.

domains.description: User Researcher conducts user-centered research including interviews, usability testing, persona development, journey mapping, heuristic evaluation, surveys, and competitive UX analysis. Use when validating design decisions, understanding user behavior, testing prototypes, or gathering insights for product direction. Includes 30 methodologies covering qualitative and quantitative research.

methodologies.name: audience-segmentation
methodologies.description: Divides your market into distinct groups with different needs and behaviors, identifying which segments to prioritize. Solves the problem of generic positioning that resonates with nobody by segmenting customers by demographics, behavior, psychographics, or needs, then scoring each to decide where to focus. Delivers focused product development, efficient marketing spend, and clear pricing tiers.

methodologies.name: opportunity-solution-trees
methodologies.description: Creates a visual hierarchy connecting business outcomes to customer opportunities and multiple solution approaches. Helps teams align on what problems to solve by organizing discovery thinking into a tree structure that maps outcomes down to specific solutions and experiments. Prevents jumping to solutions prematurely and ensures all ideas are grounded in user needs.

methodologies.name: persona-building
methodologies.description: Converts interview data into semi-fictional user archetypes that represent your ideal customers. Addresses the problem of teams designing for themselves rather than actual users by anchoring product, marketing, and sales decisions in patterns extracted from real research. Reduces opinion-based feature debates and improves user-centeredness.

methodologies.name: survey-design
methodologies.description: Structures quantitative questions to measure user attitudes, preferences, and satisfaction across large samples. Solves the problem of gathering feedback from many users without conducting hundreds of interviews by following best practices for avoiding bias, writing clear questions, and analyzing results. Validates qualitative findings with quantitative data.

methodologies.name: user-research-at-scale
methodologies.description: Applies AI and automation to conduct research with thousands of participants simultaneously instead of dozens. Addresses the constraint that traditional research doesn't scale with product velocity by using automated testing platforms, real-time sentiment analysis, and cross-demographic pattern detection. Delivers faster insights without sacrificing human interpretation.

domains.description: UX Researcher executes user research activities—user interviews, usability testing, A/B testing, persona development, journey mapping, contextual inquiry, and diary studies. Use when validating user needs, testing interfaces, measuring usability, or understanding user workflows. Focuses on practical research execution and synthesis methods.

methodologies.name: cog-walk-basics
methodologies.description: Evaluates whether first-time users can complete tasks without training by stepping through the interface from their perspective. Identifies learnability problems before user testing by asking four key questions about user intent, action visibility, label clarity, and feedback. Catches UI problems early and inexpensively.

methodologies.name: cog-walk-process
methodologies.description: Structures a cognitive walkthrough evaluation from planning through findings by assembling evaluators, defining user personas, walking through each step systematically, and documenting issues with recommendations. Ensures consistent methodology across evaluation sessions and produces actionable results that can be prioritized and tracked.

methodologies.name: content-audit-basics
methodologies.description: Catalogs all content on a website or application, evaluates quality, and decides what to keep, update, or remove. Solves the problem of not knowing what content exists, hidden duplicates, or outdated pages that hurt credibility by creating a systematic inventory with quality scores. Prevents wasted redesign efforts.

methodologies.name: contextual-inquiry
methodologies.description: Observes users performing actual work in their real environment, asking questions as activities unfold. Discovers workarounds, tacit knowledge, and environmental context that lab studies miss by adopting a master-apprentice model where the user teaches the researcher. Produces richer understanding than interviews alone.

methodologies.name: diary-study-basics
methodologies.description: Asks participants to document experiences over days or weeks, capturing behaviors as they happen rather than relying on memory. Addresses the limitation that lab studies show only a moment by revealing how usage evolves over time, habit formation patterns, and real-world context. Best for understanding longitudinal behavior change.

methodologies.name: diary-study-execution
methodologies.description: Operationalizes a diary study by monitoring participant engagement, ensuring data quality, analyzing themes and patterns, and conducting follow-up interviews. Manages the execution complexity of tracking multiple participants over time, handles participant drop-off, and synthesizes diverse entries into actionable insights.

methodologies.name: focus-groups
methodologies.description: Moderates discussions with 6-10 people who share target user characteristics to explore attitudes, generate ideas, and test concepts quickly. Delivers diverse perspectives and reveals group dynamics faster than individual interviews, though at risk of groupthink. Useful for early exploration and getting stakeholder buy-in.

methodologies.name: mobile-ux-basics
methodologies.description: Designs experiences for small screens, touch input, and partial user attention by prioritizing core features and optimizing for mobile context. Solves poor mobile conversion caused by scaling desktop designs down; emphasizes mobile-first thinking, thumb-friendly touch targets, and performance optimization. Mobile is often the primary device.

methodologies.name: personas
methodologies.description: Creates fictional user archetypes based on research data to guide design decisions across teams. Prevents designing by assumption and resolves feature disagreements by anchoring conversations in user goals and pain points. Each persona represents a distinct user type with demographics, behaviors, motivations, and context.

methodologies.name: surveys
methodologies.description: Collects structured responses from many users on satisfaction, preferences, or behaviors to quantify qualitative findings. Uses standardized metrics (NPS, CSAT, SUS) to measure and track user experience at scale; avoids biases through careful question design and proper sampling. Complements interviews with quantitative validation.

methodologies.name: tree-testing
methodologies.description: Validates a website's information architecture by asking users to find content in a text-only version of your navigation structure without visual design. Tests if your navigation matches how users think before spending development effort; produces success rates and path data showing where people get lost. Improves findability iteratively.

methodologies.name: vui-conversation-design
methodologies.description: Designs dialogue flows for voice interfaces by defining intents, entities, prompts, and error recovery paths. Handles the unique constraints of voice (no visual context, one-way communication) by mapping happy paths and edge cases, writing natural language responses, and ensuring users always know what to do next. Treats voice as conversation, not commands.

methodologies.name: vui-market-context
methodologies.description: Provides market data on voice adoption (8.4 billion devices in use, 71% of users prefer voice over typing) and platform landscape (Alexa, Google Assistant, Siri, custom LLMs). Helps determine if voice is right for your product and which platforms to prioritize. Shows voice is mainstream, not niche.

methodologies.name: vui-testing-best-practices
methodologies.description: Tests voice interfaces in real-world conditions with background noise, accents, interruptions, and multi-user scenarios that labs cannot simulate. Measures intent accuracy, task completion, error rates, and user satisfaction to ensure the VUI works when users actually use it. Real-world testing catches problems that lab testing misses.

domains.description: UX/UI Designer orchestrates design activities by coordinating research, visual design, and accessibility. It routes to specialized sub-skills for user research, interface design, or accessibility audits. Use as entry point for design work—it identifies whether you need discovery research, visual design, usability testing, or compliance validation. Includes 82 combined methodologies.

methodologies.name: accessibility-evaluation
methodologies.description: Audits interfaces for WCAG compliance using automated tools, manual keyboard testing, screen reader testing, and cognitive accessibility checks. Produces prioritized issues with WCAG criteria, impact descriptions, and fix recommendations. Critical for ensuring products work for the 16% of US adults with disabilities.

methodologies.name: accessibility-first-design
methodologies.description: Integrates accessibility into design from the start rather than retrofitting later, avoiding 70-80% of issues at the design stage. Requires color contrast ratios, touch target sizes, visible focus states, and semantic HTML from the beginning. Prevents expensive rework later.

methodologies.name: ada-title-ii-compliance-2026
methodologies.description: Guides US government entities to meet ADA Title II digital accessibility requirements by April 2026 (WCAG 2.1 Level AA standard). Covers scope (websites, applications, video, PDFs), compliance steps, and video accessibility requirements. Organizations not compliant by the deadline face legal liability.

methodologies.name: ar-design-patterns
methodologies.description: Applies established patterns for augmented reality (navigation overlays, product visualization, guided training) that blend digital content seamlessly with the physical world. Addresses the unique challenges of AR placement, scale, lighting, and respecting real-world context. Prevents designs that feel disconnected from reality.

methodologies.name: cognitive-inclusion-design
methodologies.description: Designs for cognitive disabilities (ADHD, autism, dyslexia) often overlooked in traditional accessibility by using clear hierarchy, predictable patterns, readable fonts, and reduced cognitive load. Removes arbitrary time limits, provides progress indicators, and offers focus modes. Extends accessibility beyond motor and visual impairments.

methodologies.name: cognitive-walkthrough
methodologies.description: Evaluates interface learnability by stepping through tasks from a first-time user's perspective, asking whether users will know to act, notice the correct action, understand the label, and see progress. Catches usability problems before development by systematically walking through each step and documenting issues with recommendations. Combines the basics and process into a unified evaluation methodology.

methodologies.name: contextual-inquiry
methodologies.description: Contextual inquiry solves the problem of artificial research settings by conducting semi-structured interviews in users' actual work environments. Researchers observe and ask questions as activities unfold naturally, uncovering hidden workarounds, tacit knowledge, and contextual factors that typical interviews miss. Apply this when you need to understand complex workflows, discover why users develop workarounds, or want to study actual behavior rather than self-reported behavior. The methodology delivers deep environmental insights and reveals the gap between what users think they do and what they actually do.

methodologies.name: core-vui-design-principles
methodologies.description: Voice user interfaces fail when designed as visual interfaces because the constraints and user expectations are fundamentally different. Core VUI design prioritizes simplicity and clarity with short responses, natural conversational patterns that adapt to context, and persistent user awareness of what the system understands. Use this when building any voice-controlled feature to ensure users understand what to say and interactions feel like natural conversation rather than command entry.

methodologies.name: cross-platform-token-distribution
methodologies.description: Design tokens must work consistently across iOS, Android, and web platforms, yet each platform has different output formats and constraints. Cross-platform token distribution uses tools like Style Dictionary to transform a single source of tokens into platform-specific outputs—CSS variables for web, Swift structures for iOS, and XML for Android. Apply this methodology when building design systems that span multiple platforms to maintain consistency while respecting each platform's native patterns and conventions.

methodologies.name: design-system-success-factors
methodologies.description: Design systems fail when they lack clear ownership, have unusable components, poor documentation, or no actual adoption. Success requires four pillars: dedicated ownership, components people actually use, strong discoverable documentation, and real adoption across teams. Start with an MVP mindset—ship core components, design tokens, and typography first, then grow iteratively based on feedback rather than trying to build everything upfront.

methodologies.name: design-tokens-fundamentals
methodologies.description: Design tokens bridge the gap between design and development by storing design decisions in a platform-agnostic format, creating a single source of truth for colors, spacing, and typography. Tokens progress from primitive values to semantic aliases to component-specific tokens, enabling consistency across platforms and making theme switching and scaling effortless. Use this as the foundation for any design system to ensure designers and developers speak the same language and changes propagate everywhere automatically.

methodologies.name: diary-studies
methodologies.description: Diary studies capture real usage over days or weeks when snapshot lab studies cannot reveal how behavior evolves or how context changes user experience. Participants record experiences in the moment or shortly after, revealing habit formation, long-term satisfaction patterns, and real-world context that interviews cannot capture accurately. Apply this methodology when you need to understand behavior changes over time, discover how context affects usage, or validate that discovered features actually get used long-term.

methodologies.name: enterprise-xr-applications
methodologies.description: Enterprise XR has fundamentally different requirements than consumer experiences: users need long session comfort, integration with existing business tools, security compliance, clear ROI metrics, and deployment management. Enterprise XR applications span training simulations, remote expert guidance, digital twins for manufacturing, and collaborative visualization. Design these with step-by-step guidance, clear progress tracking, and accessibility that meets enterprise standards rather than consumer expectations.

methodologies.name: error-handling-in-vui
methodologies.description: Voice recognition errors frustrate users and break conversations when handled poorly. Graceful error recovery uses progressive disclosure—first reprompts are short and helpful, second attempts offer examples, and third failures escalate to visual interfaces or human agents. Apply this when designing any voice feature to ensure errors never blame users, always provide immediate feedback, and always offer a way forward, whether that means suggesting example phrases or switching modalities.

methodologies.name: focus-groups
methodologies.description: Focus groups solve the problem of slow, one-on-one exploration by gathering 6-10 participants for moderated group discussion, revealing diverse perspectives and group dynamics that individual interviews cannot capture. The group interaction generates ideas and insights while reducing research time and cost compared to multiple individual interviews. Use focus groups for early concept exploration, testing reactions to ideas, understanding language and terminology, and stakeholder engagement rather than for detailed usability testing or sensitive topics.

methodologies.name: immersive-design-principles
methodologies.description: Immersion can overwhelm or disorient users if not carefully balanced across immersion levels from passthrough AR to full VR. Immersive design requires thoughtful depth layering, realistic motion physics, environmental context response, and smooth mode transitions that enhance rather than distract. Address motion sickness through fixed reference points and reduced motion, disorientation through clear grounding, eye strain through proper depth of field, and arm fatigue through supportive positions and alternative inputs.

methodologies.name: personas
methodologies.description: Teams design for themselves instead of users without shared user understanding that personas provide. Personas are fictional characters based on research representing key user types, helping teams make user-centered decisions by putting a human face to target audiences. Create 3-5 research-backed personas focusing on goals, frustrations, and behaviors rather than just demographics, then reference them throughout design discussions to resolve feature debates and guide prioritization decisions.

methodologies.name: regulatory-compliance-2026
methodologies.description: 2026 brings significant new accessibility regulations including the European Accessibility Act and updated ADA DOJ rules requiring WCAG 2.1 AA compliance for US federal sites and WCAG 2.2 for general US websites. Compliance requires publishing accessibility statements, documenting WCAG conformance, testing with assistive technologies, scheduling regular audits, and planning remediation for discovered issues. Apply this to ensure your product meets legal requirements while creating more usable experiences for everyone.

methodologies.name: semantic-tokens-and-modes
methodologies.description: Tokens need to adapt to different contexts—light and dark modes, different screen sizes, different density levels, and multi-brand scenarios. Semantic tokens map to component properties, and modes allow the same token name to have different values depending on context. This enables design systems to support theme switching, platform-specific adaptations, and responsive design from a single token architecture rather than creating separate sets.

methodologies.name: spatial-accessibility
methodologies.description: Spatial interfaces introduce new accessibility challenges beyond screen readers and keyboard navigation—motor limitations prevent hand tracking, visual impairment makes 3D depth perception difficult, and cognitive load increases in three-dimensional environments. Design spatial experiences with multiple input modalities, adjustable comfort settings, audio cues and haptics for visual content, simplified modes for complex interactions, and always test with diverse users to ensure no one is excluded from immersive features.

methodologies.name: spatial-computing-overview
methodologies.description: Spatial computing blends digital content with physical space through AR, VR, and MR, creating interfaces in three-dimensional space rather than confined to rectangular screens. The market is growing 87% projected in 2026 with platforms like Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest, and Android XR establishing ecosystem dominance. Understanding spatial computing landscape and platform strengths is essential for designers building immersive experiences in this rapidly expanding market.

methodologies.name: spatial-design-tools
methodologies.description: Traditional 2D design tools don't support spatial design adequately, requiring spatial-native tools or plugins. Tools like Figma with 3D plugins, ShapesXR for VR prototyping, Gravity Sketch for VR-native design, and Unity/Unreal for high-fidelity development each serve different stages. Move from low-fi wireframes with spatial annotations to prototypes in VR tools tested in actual headsets, then to high-fidelity development ensuring performance optimization and accessibility review before shipping.

methodologies.name: spatial-interaction-patterns
methodologies.description: Spatial interfaces enable multiple input modalities—hand tracking for natural direct manipulation, controllers for precise input, gaze for passive selection, voice for commands, and gesture for shortcuts. Direct manipulation patterns let users grab and move objects with natural physics, ray-casting points at distant objects with laser-like selection, and gaze-plus-dwell provides accessibility while avoiding arm fatigue. Choose input modalities and interaction patterns based on the user's hands and eyes availability in context.

methodologies.name: spatial-ui-patterns
methodologies.description: Traditional UI components don't work in 3D space—panels need to be positioned relative to the world, head, body, or hands depending on their function. World-locked panels stay fixed in space for reference content, head-locked panels follow gaze for HUIs, body-locked panels follow user position for menus, and hand-attached panels live on wrist or hand for quick access. Design spatial UI with visual anchors, consistent scale, depth-based hierarchy, and respect for personal space rather than placing content too close or requiring constant arm raising.

methodologies.name: spatial-ux-fundamentals
methodologies.description: 2D UI patterns don't translate to 3D spatial interfaces—the fundamental considerations change to include appropriate world-scale sizing, comfortable reach within arm distance, avoiding peripheral blind spots, handling occlusion when content blocks other content, maintaining spatial consistency, and adapting to physical surroundings. Spatial hierarchy divides space into near field for immediate interactions, mid field for content consumption and collaboration, and far field for environmental context, with each zone serving different purposes.

methodologies.name: surveys
methodologies.description: Surveys collect structured data from many users when you need quantitative validation of qualitative insights or want to measure satisfaction across your user base. Different survey types measure satisfaction, post-task feedback, preferences, or behaviors, each requiring different question types and analysis approaches. Use surveys to quantify findings, validate qualitative research, measure satisfaction at scale, and generalize from your sample to the larger population using proper sampling and analysis techniques.

methodologies.name: testing-with-assistive-technology
methodologies.description: Automated accessibility testing catches only 30-50% of issues—manual testing with actual assistive technology like screen readers and keyboard-only navigation is essential. Testing layers include automated checks, manual review, screen reader testing with tools like NVDA or VoiceOver, keyboard navigation without mouse, and real user testing with people with disabilities. Common issues found include missing form labels, no skip navigation, inaccessible custom components, missing alt text, poor focus management, and keyboard traps that automated tools miss.

methodologies.name: token-organization
methodologies.description: Design token bloat defeats the purpose of systematization by reducing discoverability—tokens should follow a hierarchy from primitives to semantic tokens to component-specific tokens used sparingly. Avoid creating a token for every variation by starting lean with MVP mindset, using clear naming conventions like color.surface.primary, documenting purpose for each token, and tracking changes over time. Well-organized tokens make systems maintainable while excessive tokens become impossible to navigate and maintain.

methodologies.name: tree-testing
methodologies.description: Navigation designs seem logical to teams but users cannot find content, requiring data-driven validation before expensive builds. Tree testing evaluates findability by having users navigate text-only site hierarchies to find answers to realistic tasks. Success rates above 80% indicate good structure; below 60% signals problems requiring redesign. Apply tree testing after creating IA drafts to identify label confusion, missing categories, and structural problems before they reach production.

methodologies.name: voice-ui
methodologies.description: Voice interfaces designed like visual interfaces fail because users don't know what to say, commands feel robotic, and error handling is frustrating. Voice UI design creates natural conversations with clear intents and slots, writes sample dialogues before technical implementation, designs contextual prompts for different situations, and handles errors gracefully through progressive disclosure. Apply this comprehensive methodology when building any voice feature to ensure interactions feel like natural conversation rather than command parsing, making voice genuinely useful instead of frustrating.

methodologies.name: vr-design-patterns
methodologies.description: Full VR lacks real-world context and can cause user discomfort without proper design—environments need visible ground planes for orientation, stable horizons, human-appropriate scale, consistent lighting, and visible boundaries. Locomotion methods from teleportation to room-scale walking to controller movement each have different comfort and use-case tradeoffs. UI panels float in space but never lock directly to head, always provide spatial audio feedback, and use gaze plus confirmation for selection to maintain comfort during extended sessions.

methodologies.name: vui-accessibility-inclusivity
methodologies.description: Addresses the challenge of voice interfaces struggling with accents and diverse users by training on diverse voice datasets, supporting multiple language variants, and providing accommodation options like accent calibration. Apply this when designing voice assistants for global audiences to ensure hands-free access for users with visual, motor, or cognitive disabilities, delivering inclusive interaction that respects speech variations and background noise challenges.

methodologies.name: vui-conversation-design
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of confusing voice interactions by structuring conversations around clear components: wake words, intents, entities, prompts, confirmations, and error handling. Use this when building voice experiences to create natural dialogue flows that guide users through multi-turn interactions, prevent misunderstandings, and gracefully recover from speech recognition errors or missing information.

methodologies.name: vui-iot-integration
methodologies.description: Tackles the challenge of coordinating voice control across multiple smart home devices by grouping devices logically, creating intuitive scene names, and handling multi-device actions atomically. Apply when designing voice-controlled smart home ecosystems to deliver coherent user experiences that synchronize related devices, provide clear feedback, and allow users to control complex scenarios with single voice commands.

methodologies.name: vui-market-context
methodologies.description: Establishes baseline understanding of voice technology adoption (8.4+ billion active users, 62% of US adults) and available platforms from Alexa to custom LLM integrations. Use this when planning voice features to contextualize market demand, understand competitive platforms, and make informed decisions about which voice ecosystems to target for your product.

methodologies.name: vui-privacy-security
methodologies.description: Addresses the core concern that voice data is sensitive and persistent by implementing privacy-first design with transparent collection, user control, encrypted storage, and voice history management. Apply when building voice features to establish trust through visible listening indicators, confirmation prompts, and private modes for sensitive operations like banking or health queries.

methodologies.name: vui-testing-best-practices
methodologies.description: Solves the difficulty of testing voice interfaces in controlled environments by establishing testing layers from intent accuracy to real-world stress testing. Use this when validating voice features to test across background noise, diverse accents, interruptions, and long conversations, measuring intent accuracy and task completion to ensure robust real-world performance.

methodologies.name: w3c-design-tokens-standard
methodologies.description: Addresses tool interoperability and platform-agnostic design by standardizing token format across Figma, Adobe, Sketch, and other design tools. Apply this when building design systems to prepare for emerging W3C standards, audit current token structures, and plan migration strategies that enable ecosystem compatibility and future-proof token files.

methodologies.name: wcag-22-compliance
methodologies.description: Implements the 9 new WCAG 2.2 requirements released October 2023 (focus visibility, target size, dragging alternatives, accessible authentication) to meet baseline compliance by 2025-2026. Use this when designing UI to ensure 24x24px minimum touch targets, provide non-drag alternatives for all interactions, and eliminate cognitive function tests for authentication.

domains.description: Specializes in autonomous AI agents and multi-agent systems development. Covers agent patterns (ReAct, plan-and-execute), frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex), LLM tool calling, agentic RAG, multi-agent coordination, MCP integration for Claude tools, and EU AI Act compliance. Includes mandatory XML prompt templating and JSON schema output conventions.

methodologies.name: agent-architectures
methodologies.description: Build production-ready autonomous agents with memory (short-term and long-term recall via embeddings), tool systems (function calling with error handling), and planning capabilities (max iterations, timeout protection). Implement memory management (summarization, relevance filtering), safety guardrails (human approval, sandboxed execution), and monitoring (action logging, token tracking). This modular framework prevents infinite loops, handles errors gracefully, and scales from simple ReAct loops to complex multi-iteration planning.

methodologies.name: agent-patterns
methodologies.description: Implement four core agent patterns—ReAct (Reason→Act→Observe loop for general tasks), Plan-and-Execute (upfront planning with dependency tracking), Reflexion (self-critique and retry), and AutoGPT-style (full autonomy with goals)—to handle different problem complexity levels. ReAct works best for web search and Q&A; Plan-and-Execute excels at multi-step projects; Reflexion learns from mistakes; AutoGPT handles research and automation. Code examples show how to wire tools, manage state, and handle timeouts.

methodologies.name: ai-agent-patterns
methodologies.description: Move beyond simple prompt→response with agent design patterns: Chain of Thought (step-by-step reasoning), ReAct (reasoning + tool use), Plan-and-Execute (plan first then act), and Tool Use (function calling). Compare frameworks—LangGraph (state machines, complex flows), AutoGen (multi-agent conversations), CrewAI (role-based teams), OpenAI Agents SDK (official support). These patterns unlock complex reasoning tasks that single-pass prompts cannot solve.

methodologies.name: ai-governance-compliance
methodologies.description: Implement AI governance frameworks addressing risk classification (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal), transparency requirements (disclose AI nature, emotion recognition use), human oversight, bias detection, and data governance. Use model cards (Hugging Face format), bias detection tools (Fairlearn, AI Fairness 360), explainability methods (SHAP, LIME), and monitoring systems. This ensures compliance with regulations, builds user trust, and prevents costly recalls or legal exposure.

methodologies.name: array-items-wrapper-extraction
methodologies.description: Forbids returning bare arrays as the top-level structured-output schema for variable-cardinality LLM extraction tasks, mandating instead an object wrapper of the shape {items: [...], total_found: int, truncated: bool}. Apply it for entity, citation, line-item or search-hit extraction, document-level fact summarisation, batch classification with N inputs to N outputs, and any OpenAI or Azure strict-mode call where top-level arrays are flatly rejected because additionalProperties is object-only. The wrapper standardises zero/one/many into one shape — eliminating the production bug where the same prompt returns [], null, a dict or a list depending on how many entities the model thinks it found — and exposes total_found so consumers detect 'no entities' without inspecting len(items), plus truncated to flag incomplete extraction explicitly. The methodology aligns with Simon Willison's llm --schema-multi pattern and is skipped only for fixed-N outputs (use a tuple or named fields), single-entity extraction (wrap is overhead), and streaming UIs that need top-level array streaming.

methodologies.name: auto-evict-tool-results
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of long-horizon agents losing the plot when a single tool call (npm log, SQL dump, web fetch) returns tens of thousands of tokens and corrupts the rest of the loop. Apply by wrapping the runtime so any tool result above a fixed token threshold (LangChain's Deep Agents default of 20,000 is a sane starting point) is written to a scratch directory and substituted with a manifest object containing path, preview, total_tokens, and an evicted flag before the message is appended to history. The agent pulls only the slices it actually needs via a paired read_file tool, so context grows by tens of tokens instead of tens of thousands. The value is a deterministic, runtime-enforced bound on per-step effective context — eviction must happen before the model observes the payload, because asking the LLM to self-summarize after the fact cannot un-pollute the turn.

methodologies.name: autonomous-agents
methodologies.description: Autonomous agents combine reasoning, memory, and action capabilities to accomplish complex objectives with minimal human intervention. Choose from ReAct (Reason+Act loop), Plan-and-Execute (upfront planning), Reflexion (self-improvement), or AutoGPT-style (full autonomy). Manage memory (short-term, long-term, recall), define tools with clear descriptions, set iteration limits, and implement error recovery. This reference guides when to use agents vs simpler chains and how to monitor production deployments.

methodologies.name: batch-cache-stack
methodologies.description: Solves the cost problem on async, stable-prefix LLM workloads where synchronous-uncached pricing is the default. Apply when an agent pipeline tolerates 24-hour latency (overnight content runs, eval suites, bulk extraction, dataset labeling) and the system prompt plus tool definitions are byte-identical across items: submit through Anthropic Messages Batches or Gemini Batch Mode AND mark the longest static prefix with cache_control ephemeral. The 50% batch discount composes multiplicatively with the 90% cache-read discount to land cached input tokens at roughly 5% of synchronous-uncached cost. The value depends on prefix stability, so build the cached prefix once outside the per-item loop, freeze its bytes (no timestamps, no runtime-sorted tools, no randomized examples), and verify a sha256 hash before submission; OpenAI's Batch API does not stack with prompt caching, so the provider matters.

methodologies.name: bundle-vs-split-tools
methodologies.description: Solves the tool-routing accuracy collapse that happens past about 25 tools, where definition tokens dominate the prompt and the model picks the wrong tool more often than the right one. Apply the decision procedure: under 20 tools always default to split (each tool a tight, single-purpose prompt); at 20-25 split unless the surface is extremely repetitive (CRUD on many entities); past 30 consider bundling related atomic tools into a composite with a Literal-enum mode argument, but only when modes share an audience, share most of their argument shape, and the model has training-data prior for that bundle pattern (filesystem ops, REST verbs, git commands). The value is empirically measured: bundling DB CRUD across 10 entities cut tool-selection error from 18% to 4%, while bundling unrelated audiences (git plus npm plus docker into one exec tool) inflated errors and was reversed; treat split as the default and bundle only to escape the 25-tool ceiling, never as a first design choice.

methodologies.name: chaos-eval-fault-injection
methodologies.description: Solves the gap between sunny-day eval success rates and real production reliability, where agents that score 90% on benchmarks collapse below 50% under realistic 5-10% fault rates. Apply as a pre-launch readiness gate and regression suite for any agent that depends on external tools, web APIs, MCP servers, or remote LLMs: inject the five canonical failure modes (tool_timeout, tool_error, llm_rate_limit, tool_mutate, service_disconnect) at randomized trajectory points with configurable per-call probabilities AND deterministic step-index injection for reproducible regressions. Grade each run on a four-outcome rubric (recovered, retried_intelligently, escalated_correctly, wrong_confident) and treat any non-zero wrong_confident rate as a launch blocker, since aggregate success rates hide silent corruption inside the fail bucket. The value is the failure-mode catalogue that drives concrete retry-policy parameters, circuit-breaker thresholds, and escalation triggers — the only cheap way to measure resilience before users do.

methodologies.name: cheap-guardrail-tripwire
methodologies.description: Solves the cost and safety problem of public-facing agents on premium models (Opus, GPT-5, o3) where 30-70% of inbound traffic is jailbreak attempts, off-topic chatter, abuse, or spam that should never reach the strong loop. Apply on internet-exposed endpoints by attaching an input_guardrail (OpenAI Agents SDK) that runs a cheap classifier — gpt-4o-mini, Haiku-class, gemini-flash-lite — returning a typed Pydantic verdict; setting tripwire_triggered=True raises an exception and the expensive agent loop is never invoked. Filtered traffic short-circuits at roughly 1% of strong-model cost with no tool side effects possible. Three failure modes erase the win: giving the guardrail tools so it runs a multi-turn loop on every request, using the same model as the main agent (cost ratio collapses to 1.0), or wiring output_guardrails on a streaming endpoint without explicit buffering (forces full-response buffering and breaks token-by-token UX).

methodologies.name: claude-code-headless-default
methodologies.description: Solves the default-overengineering trap of writing custom Agent SDK loops when the Claude Code CLI already ships a battle-tested harness for coding, devops, and research tasks. Apply by making `claude -p` the first implementation for any agent runnable from a shell — cron jobs, GitHub Actions, queue workers, multi-agent orchestrators that can shell out — and only drop to the Agent SDK after one of four concrete switch-triggers fires: non-coding domain that does not benefit from filesystem/git/bash, custom tools that cannot be expressed as MCP servers, programmatic control of the inner loop (intercept every tool call, branch the conversation tree), or SaaS embedding where the host process cannot fork subprocesses. The CLI bundles tool gating, retry policy, context compaction, sub-agents, hooks, plan mode, and skills that Anthropic maintains; reaching for the SDK first re-pays months of harness work. Production invocations must set -p, an explicit --allowedTools allowlist, --max-turns, and --output-format stream-json --verbose so the orchestrator can react to mid-run events.

methodologies.name: compaction-preserve-refs
methodologies.description: Solves the post-compaction failure mode where long-running agents resume with the gist of prior work but lose the file paths, error messages, and IDs they need to actually continue. Apply in any agent loop expected to exceed roughly 30 turns or its context window, in multi-session SDD work where the next session reads the compacted output, and whenever middleware-driven auto-compaction is in play: hard-code a YAML schema with fixed keys (goal, files_touched, decisions, errors_open, refs, next_action) instead of free-form summarization. Preserve verbatim every pointer the agent will dereference — workspace-relative file paths, fully qualified function names, error strings, URLs, PR numbers, ticket IDs, explicit decisions — and drop intermediate reasoning, tool-call argument repetition, verbatim tool output bodies, and conversational filler. The value is that the compacted state becomes a machine-checkable manifest of references rather than prose; a missing key is a hard failure the orchestrator catches before the agent acts on bad state, and resume becomes grep-on-goal plus open-files-touched instead of restarting from zero.

methodologies.name: confidence-thresholded-cascade
methodologies.description: Solves the cost problem of running every request on a premium model when most tasks are easy enough for a cheap model to handle. Apply on high-volume traffic (chatbots, classifiers, batch processing) with mixed difficulty and latency-tolerant paths where confidence is calibrate-able (classification, factual extraction): send the request to a cheap model first with a structured Pydantic schema that includes reasoning, answer, and a self-reported confidence_0_to_1 field; if confidence clears a threshold (typically 0.85) accept it, otherwise escalate to the strong model. FrugalGPT showed up to 98% cost reduction matching the best individual LLM on benchmarks; production deployments commonly see 50-80% savings with no quality regression. The threshold must be calibrated on a representative eval set (run 100+ tasks through the cheap model, bucket actual accuracy by confidence, pick the bucket where accuracy is acceptable) and an explicit IDK exit (requires_escalation flag) catches blind-spot failures that confidence alone misses; cascade fits classification and extraction, not creative generation where confidence is meaningless.

methodologies.name: decimal-as-string-pattern
methodologies.description: Solves silent precision loss when LLMs emit money, big integers, or structured identifiers as floats or unconstrained JSON numbers and downstream parsers introduce binary-rounding drift. Apply for currency (USD, EUR, BTC), exact decimals in finance/science/engineering reports, integers above 2^53, account numbers, phone numbers, postal codes, ISBNs, semver, and ISO-8601 timestamps: declare the schema field as a string with a regex pattern that pins the exact surface form (^\d+\.\d{2}$ for two-decimal currency, the appropriate shapes for IBAN, semver, E.164 phone, ISO-8601 date), then parse to Decimal/int/datetime only after the model has produced a known-good token sequence. On strict-mode and grammar-backed decoders (OpenAI strict, XGrammar, Outlines) the pattern is masked at sampling time so the model literally cannot emit 19.989999 when the schema demands two decimals. Skip the pattern for counts, ranks, and ML scores where a few ULPs of drift do not matter; never reuse a wide ^\S+$ pattern, which adds zero decode-time constraint while earning false confidence.

methodologies.name: discriminated-union-output
methodologies.description: Solves the silent field-bleed and ambiguity that happen when polymorphic structured output is modeled as a flat schema with all-optional fields, where the model can emit url and query simultaneously or skip a required field for the implied shape. Apply when the model must pick one of N distinct shapes — agent action selection (search vs fetch vs finish vs ask-user), polymorphic entity extraction (Person vs Company vs Product), router outputs dispatching to one of N handlers, event-bus payloads where each event type owns its own schema. Define each branch with a Literal[...] discriminator field placed first and combine via Annotated[A | B | C, Field(discriminator="kind")]: Pydantic v2 emits oneOf plus discriminator in the JSON schema and OpenAI strict mode and Outlines compile this into a finite-state grammar that locks the branch the moment the discriminator literal is emitted. Order matters — discriminator at the bottom defeats the grammar-locking benefit. Collapse to a single model with a kind enum when branches share more than ~90% of fields, since two near-identical branches double schema size and prompt-cache footprint without adding constraint.

methodologies.name: embedded-scratchpad-field
methodologies.description: Solves the strict-JSON-mode dilemma where the model must commit to an answer immediately without room for free-form thinking, which depresses accuracy on any non-trivial decision. Apply in strict structured output for decisions that benefit from chain-of-thought, tasks with more than one reasoning step, or anywhere the model is known to commit before thinking: embed a scratchpad, reasoning, plan_steps, or evidence field BEFORE the answer field in the schema. AWS Bedrock measured a +60% accuracy improvement on GSM8k from adding a single reasoning field before answer, and Tab-CoT papers show similar gains on multi-criteria decisions. Pick the field name based on task type (reasoning for analysis, evidence for classification with quotation, plan_steps for multi-step, scratchpad for free-form, tab_cot list-of-step for math derivation), give it a description that names what to think about and references later fields by name to re-ground the model, and never place the scratchpad after the answer — that turns CoT into post-hoc rationalization that costs tokens without quality benefit.

methodologies.name: enum-constraints-closed-vocabularies
methodologies.description: Solves the silent label-drift problem where a free-string classification field lets the model emit synonyms or novel values ("Spam!", "high priority", "support") that crash downstream switch/case code. Apply for any classification or routing field with a finite value set — sentiment, intent, severity, language code, status state, agent action selection, fields that map to a downstream enum column: declare it as Literal[...] in Pydantic or enum in JSON Schema AND serve under strict mode or a constrained decoder (XGrammar, Outlines, vLLM guided generation). Strict-mode decoders mask every non-enum token to probability zero at sampling time, eliminating hallucinated labels; ohmeow and the vLLM docs report category-prediction tasks moving from 80-95% raw to roughly 100% with this single change. For semi-open sets (mostly closed but allowing rare novel values), use a discriminated union with a Literal branch for known values plus a typed "other" branch carrying the free-text candidate, so the common path stays strict and novel labels go through a controlled escape hatch. Listing valid values in a Field description while keeping the type as str gives a soft constraint that drifts under long context or smaller models.

methodologies.name: eu-ai-act-compliance
methodologies.description: August 2026 marks full enforcement of the EU AI Act: high-risk systems require quality management, risk assessment, technical documentation, conformity proof, EU database registration, and human oversight. Risk classification determines obligations—social scoring prohibited, biometrics/employment AI highly regulated, chatbots require transparency. Penalties up to 35M EUR or 7% global revenue. Checklist includes model cards, bias detection, explainability tools, incident reporting, and training data documentation.

methodologies.name: field-descriptions-as-prompts
methodologies.description: Solves the format-compliance and edge-case-handling gap that happens when schema fields rely on the model's priors instead of explicit instructions. Apply on every non-trivial field — descriptions cost roughly 10 tokens each and pay for themselves: write 1-3 sentences covering what the field represents, format/units/range when non-obvious (cents not dollars, ISO-8601, range bounds), edge cases (what to do with missing/ambiguous/contradictory input), and forbidden patterns (specific failure modes observed in production, called out as DO NOT lines). PARSE (2025) measured 60%+ improvement in extraction accuracy from optimizing descriptions, and explicit relationship hints lift complex-reasoning accuracy by up to 40%. Each description appears just before the field's value position in the autoregressive sequence, so it is effectively a per-field micro-instruction; tighten generic prose to bullet-style hints, name dependent fields by name to re-ground ("derived from `body` above"), and never ask for reasoning in a value field — split it into a separate reasoning field. Empty descriptions, descriptions that duplicate the field name, contradictions between name and description, and long flowing prose are all anti-patterns to flag in review.

methodologies.name: file-reference-passing
methodologies.description: Solves the context-economy problem in multi-stage agent pipelines where ferrying summaries or content between stages consumes the prompt budget of every downstream call. Apply at every stage boundary in pipelines that operate on a corpus, in map-reduce patterns over documents, in agents with subagents, and any time more than 2K tokens of "context" would otherwise cross between steps: return only references (file paths, IDs, URLs, doc-keys) — never content — and let stage N+1 load from those references on demand. The neromedia pipeline saves 80K tokens per run by passing 10 selected IDs from a 200-item manifest instead of bodies. Four pattern shapes cover most cases: stage-output-as-refs (scanner returns list[FileRef], processor loads each), subagent-returns-refs-plus-summary (parent context never grows beyond ~500 tokens regardless of subagent depth), manifest-then-fetch (LLM picks K ids from a known manifest, code fetches), and recursive descent for tree-shaped corpora. Validate refs before passing to the next step, constrain to the given manifest where possible, and reject the summary-AND-content anti-pattern that is content-passing in disguise.

methodologies.name: filesystem-as-working-memory
methodologies.description: Solves the premature-summarization failure mode in long-running agents where lossy compression destroys facts that were still recoverable. Apply for research agents reading more than 5 documents, codegen across many files (Claude Code, Aider, OpenHands, Cursor), any task expected to exceed roughly 20 turns, or pipelines where step N+1 consumes step N's output: give the agent four FS primitives (write_file, read_file with offset/limit, ls, grep) backed by real disk, S3, or a virtual FS, and offload any tool result above ~2000 tokens to a stable directory taxonomy (search/, docs/, plan/, scratch/), returning only a path plus a short head snippet. Reserve LLM-based summarization for the moment context utilization crosses 85% AND no offloadable blobs remain in context — file offload is lossless and reversible, summarization is one-way. The FS backing store must persist into the same checkpointer as conversation state so resume-after-failure recovers files alongside messages; splitting persistence boundaries (FS in tmpfs, conversation in Postgres) leaves the agent with paths that 404 on restart.

methodologies.name: gateway-fallback-chain
methodologies.description: Solves the single-vendor availability ceiling where a 30-minute Anthropic outage becomes a 30-minute product outage, and the operational mess of integrating prompt caching, retries, observability, and budget caps separately for every provider. Apply for production agents with availability SLAs above the single-vendor floor, A/B experiments across providers, workloads hitting rate limits during peak hours, and multi-region deployments where the cheapest provider differs per region: route LLM calls through a gateway (OpenRouter, LiteLLM proxy, Portkey, Kong AI Gateway) configured with a primary model AND at least one fallback from a different provider, since same-vendor fallbacks do not protect against vendor-wide incidents. The gateway transparently retries on 5xx, 429, timeout, content-policy refusal, or empty completion and bills only the successful run, keeping tail availability above 99.95% while individual providers run at 99.5-99.9%. Surface 4xx client errors without fallback (those are agent bugs that get masked by silent retry), self-host inside the compliance boundary for regulated workloads, and call the vendor SDK directly for cache-heavy or batch workloads where gateway routing typically loses the cache discount.

methodologies.name: generator-critic-bounded-loop
methodologies.description: Solves the unbounded reflection cost trap where a generator-critic loop without exit conditions keeps iterating in pursuit of perfection while burning budget for diminishing returns. Apply for codegen agents that compile/lint/test their own output, copywriting and summarization with a clear rubric, structured extraction where a critic verifies required fields and citations, and self-correcting RAG: wrap generation in Generator → Critic → Generator with three independent exit conditions checked first-true-wins — critic returns should_continue=False, score delta vs prior iteration is below epsilon for two consecutive iterations, or iteration count hits a hard cap of 3 (never above 5). LangChain Reflection Agents data shows iteration 2 captures 70-80% of achievable lift, iteration 3 adds 10-15%, iterations 4+ contribute below noise. Use cheap critics (Haiku, gpt-4.1-mini) for rubric criticism (style, format, completeness — constrained classification, no reasoning needed) and same-tier critics for correctness (math, code, citations); a mixed two-tier critic (cheap-rubric first, strong-correctness only when rubric passes) cuts critic cost ~60%. Sharing model and temperature between generator and critic causes rubber-stamping; force the critic to emit should_continue explicitly so domain logic stays in the prompt.

methodologies.name: handoff-id-payload
methodologies.description: Solves the O(n²) context blow-up in multi-agent topologies where naively forwarding the conversation between roles makes every downstream agent read every prior agent's reasoning. Apply in role-specialized meshes (researcher → writer → editor; classifier → worker), supervisor/worker topologies, long pipelines where each step's relevant context is a small subset of cumulative state, and cron- or event-driven agents that have no conversation to forward: the handoff payload is a typed object with task_id, target_agent, and decision_metadata — never the conversation history, never the raw input. Agent B reads the actual task state from a shared store (file, queue, DB) keyed by task_id, and supervisors return SupervisorDecision objects as structured output rather than free-form messages naming the next worker. NERO's mesh runs on this: a 53-byte JSON handoff routes to /srv/nero/queue/t_8821.json, where the worker pulls the article body. The store must expose get/append_event/set_status with append-only event logs so a failed handoff can be retried without losing the prior agent's output, and the receiving agent must load from the store on first action — the payload's metadata is for routing decisions only, not for reconstructing inputs.

methodologies.name: headless-cli-four-guards
methodologies.description: Solves the mystery-hang and runaway-loop incidents that hit production every time an agent CLI is run non-interactively without explicit guards. Apply on every cron, CI, GitHub Actions, queue worker, scheduled-agent, background-pool subagent, or self-healing script invocation of claude, codex, aider, or opencode: set all four guards together — a print/headless flag to disable the TUI (Claude Code -p, codex exec, opencode headless), an explicit --allowedTools allowlist with the smallest viable set (NEVER --dangerously-skip-permissions in prod), a --max-turns cap tied to a real workflow length (15-30 for a single coding task, never 999 "to be safe"), and < /dev/null to close stdin. Each missing guard maps to a documented failure mode: no headless flag causes TUI mode to hang on terminal control codes, no allowlist stalls forever on the first permission prompt or runs unbounded tools, no max-turns lets loop bugs drain daily budget within minutes, and open stdin lets the agent read garbage from the parent pipe and behave non-deterministically. The four-guards rule is the minimum viable contract for any headless agent invocation and should be enforced by code review or CI lint.

methodologies.name: idempotent-write-tools
methodologies.description: Solves the silent double-effect problem where transient tool failures, network flakes, and agent retry loops cause writes to run twice — double-charge a card, double-deploy a service, double-send an email — because the orchestrator cannot distinguish "call succeeded but response was lost" from "call never happened". Apply on every tool that mutates external state (payments, emails, file deletes, infra changes, DB writes, external API POSTs) and every tool exposed via MCP gateways with at-least-once delivery: require an idempotency_key parameter that the agent generates ONCE per logical write (a UUID or deterministic hash) and resends on every retry, persist key → result for at least 24h in a key store, and short-circuit on key hit so retries replay the stored result without running the side effect again. For destructive or expensive operations (delete, deploy, send irreversible communication), split into a *_preview tool with no side effects (returns diff, cost estimate, affected IDs, preview_hash) plus a *_apply tool that requires the preview_hash plus the idempotency key — the single-call form must not exist in the catalog. Reads must NOT take the key (bloats schema, confuses the model), and the key must come from the agent, not be generated inside the tool, or every retry gets a fresh key and dedup never fires.

methodologies.name: inverted-header-content-first
methodologies.description: Solves the silent quality drop that happens when one structured-output call must produce both a long body and metadata about it (title, slug, tags, summary) — autoregressive sampling means a title declared before the body is a blind label that the body is then forced to rationalize. Apply it whenever a single SO call generates an article, blog post, product description, code-with-explanation, or any payload where short metadata fields summarize a longer content field; declare the body field first in the schema and every metadata field after it. The practical value is grounding metadata in the actual generated text instead of guesswork, with Castillo (2025) measuring a 13 percentage-point LiveBench accuracy gap from field-order alone. When a streaming UI needs the title before the body finishes, split into two cheap calls instead of reordering the schema, since reordering reintroduces the blind-label problem.

methodologies.name: langchain-agents-architectures
methodologies.description: Build ReAct agents (think step-by-step, use tools, observe results), Plan-and-Execute agents (decompose complex tasks first), and LATS (Language Agent Tree Search with backtracking) using LangChain and LangGraph. Define tools with @tool decorator or StructuredTool with JSON schemas, bind them to models, and handle tool errors gracefully. These architectures handle tool orchestration, multi-step planning, and complex reasoning better than single LLM calls.

methodologies.name: langchain-agents-multi-agent
methodologies.description: Coordinate multiple AI agents—Supervisor (one routes to specialists), Debate (reach consensus through argument), Hierarchical (nested teams with delegation)—using LangGraph state machines. Test agents via mock LLM calls, verify output format, and run end-to-end integration tests. Multi-agent patterns solve problems requiring diverse expertise, verification through debate, and role-based task delegation more effectively than monolithic single-agent systems.

methodologies.name: langchain-basics
methodologies.description: LangChain provides abstractions for prompts, chains, memory, and retrieval to build LLM apps rapidly. Use LCEL (LangChain Expression Language) pipe syntax (prompt|model|parser) for composable, streamable chains. Choose from sequential, router, MapReduce, or fallback patterns depending on control flow. Connect to many LLMs and tools, cache responses, and trace with LangSmith. Start here before agents if you need basic chains and prompt templates.

methodologies.name: langchain-chains
methodologies.description: Compose LLM chains using LCEL pipe syntax: prompt templates → LLM → output parsers. Implement sequential (A→B→C), router (dynamic branching), MapReduce (parallel + reduce), and fallback (resilience) patterns. Use prompt templates with few-shot learning, system messages, and message history placeholders. Parse outputs as JSON, Pydantic, or strings. Streaming yields chunks as they arrive; error handling via retries and fallbacks prevents silent failures.

methodologies.name: langchain-memory
methodologies.description: Store conversation history using four memory types—Buffer (full history for short chats), Summary (compress old messages, scalable), Vector (semantic search over past interactions), Entity (track named entities). Use Conversation Summary Memory for long-form chats; auto-merge retriever for hierarchical chunks. LangGraph workflows use TypedDict state with Annotated fields for accumulation. Human-in-the-loop checkpoints pause execution for approval; subgraphs enable modular composition.

methodologies.name: langchain-observability
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of debugging production LangChain chains blind — without traces captured at invocation time, past failures cannot be reconstructed and intermediate steps, token counts, and latency are lost forever. Apply it on any chain deployed to production, especially conversational agents needing session-level cost and latency monitoring or streaming use cases that yield tokens incrementally to clients. The practical value comes from enabling LangSmith tracing from day one (LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2 plus the @traceable decorator for non-LangChain functions), wiring BaseCallbackHandler subclasses for structured logging instead of verbose=True stdout leakage, and using chain.stream / chain.astream for low-latency UX. It also flags AgentExecutor gotchas like reusing session_ids across users (which merges histories) and uncaught PydanticOutputParser exceptions that turn validation errors into 500s.

methodologies.name: langchain-production-patterns
methodologies.description: Hardens LangChain chains against the real-world failures that silent demos never expose: model outages, transient rate limits, mismatched dependency versions, and the surprise that RunnableParallel runs sequentially in sync invoke despite the name. Apply it to any production chain where outages or 429s are possible, where runtime model swaps (A/B testing gpt-4o vs gpt-4o-mini, OpenAI to Anthropic) are needed, or where batch workloads must run with controlled concurrency. The practical value is concrete: with_fallbacks() converts model errors into degraded-but-correct responses, with_retry(stop_after_attempt=3, wait_exponential_jitter=True) absorbs transient errors, ConfigurableField enables runtime overrides without redeployment, chain.batch with max_concurrency replaces blocking invoke loops, and pinning langchain plus langchain-openai plus langchain-community as a unit avoids Pydantic v1/v2 import-error swamps. It also rules out hub.pull() in air-gapped runtimes and bans deprecated LLMChain in favor of LCEL.

methodologies.name: langchain-rag-pipeline
methodologies.description: Provides the canonical LangChain pattern for grounded generation when the knowledge base is too large to fit in a prompt: load with langchain-community loaders, split with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, embed into Chroma or Pinecone, and wrap the retriever in an LCEL chain that runs a context retriever branch in parallel with a question passthrough. Apply it when prototyping a private-corpus Q&A app, when provider flexibility (OpenAI, Anthropic) is needed without rewriting retrieval, or when teams want composable swappable retrieval components — but skip it for Claude-only single-call tasks where the Anthropic SDK is simpler and cheaper. The practical value is a declarative chain topology that hides the vectorstore choice behind the retriever interface, while the rules catch the boundary-loss bug (use chunk_overlap of 10-20% of chunk_size), the Document-list-as-string bug (always pass through a format_docs helper), and the silent ImportError trap from optional langchain-community backends.

methodologies.name: langchain-workflows
methodologies.description: LangGraph enables stateful workflows with complex control flow. Define state as TypedDict with Annotated fields (operators like add for accumulation), create node functions (pure, return partial state updates), add conditional edges (route based on state), and compile with checkpointers for persistence. Human-in-the-loop patterns pause at approval nodes; subgraphs enable modular nesting; streaming yields state updates; error handlers route on failures. This replaces simple chains when workflows need branching, loops, or human interaction.

methodologies.name: llamaindex-agents-eval
methodologies.description: Build ReAct agents (reasoning + tool use) and OpenAI agents (function calling) over knowledge bases. Evaluate retrieval (MRR, hit rate), response quality (faithfulness, relevancy), and correctness with LLM-based evaluators. Compare agents pairwise. Implement production patterns—caching embeddings, streaming responses, async queries, error handling with retries. LlamaParse (managed parsing) and LlamaCloud (managed indexing) reduce ops overhead. Monitor with callbacks and Langfuse integration.

methodologies.name: llamaindex-basics
methodologies.description: Load documents from files, web, APIs (1500+ connectors via LlamaHub), parse into nodes with semantic chunking or hierarchical grouping, extract metadata (title, summary, keywords), and embed. LlamaIndex specializes in RAG pipelines over knowledge bases; LangChain handles orchestration and agents. Chunk size choice matters—256-512 tokens for precise retrieval, 1024+ for context. Semantic splitting uses embeddings to break on topic boundaries, hierarchical parsing creates parent-child node relationships.

methodologies.name: llamaindex-chat-engine
methodologies.description: Solves stateless query engines' inability to handle conversational follow-ups: chat engines wrap a VectorStoreIndex with conversation memory and rewrite queries like "tell me more" into standalone retrievable forms before fetching context. Apply it for document Q&A with follow-ups, knowledge-base support bots, long research sessions, or any production UX needing streaming. The practical value is choosing condense_plus_context (the safe default for grounded multi-turn chat) over the broken-on-followups context mode and the no-RAG condense_question mode, bounding ChatMemoryBuffer with token_limit=3900 to stop unbounded history overflow on long sessions, and using astream_chat() (not stream_chat()) inside async loops to avoid blocking the event loop. It also flags react mode for tool-calling chats and recommends resetting history between unrelated sessions to prevent cross-session leakage.

methodologies.name: llamaindex-hybrid-retrieval
methodologies.description: Fixes the recall/precision tradeoff in pure vector search by combining vector and keyword retrievers via QueryFusionRetriever's reciprocal rank fusion, then shrinking candidates with a cross-encoder reranker. Apply it on mixed corpora (technical terms plus natural-language questions), when top-k precision matters more than recall, on long documents where fine chunks lose context (use HierarchicalNodeParser plus AutoMergingRetriever to promote parents when many children match), or for multi-perspective queries — but skip it on small corpora (<500 nodes), tight latency budgets (<200 ms), or as a substitute for re-ingestion. The practical value: pre-filter with SimilarityPostprocessor(similarity_cutoff=0.7) to cut reranker input by 50-80%, cap num_queries=2 in production (4 quadruples cost with marginal recall gain), enable use_async=True under async loops, and prefer Cohere Rerank API over local SentenceTransformerRerank in latency-sensitive paths.

methodologies.name: llamaindex-indexes-queries
methodologies.description: Choose index type based on query pattern: VectorStoreIndex (semantic search, primary), KeywordTableIndex (BM25 for keywords), KnowledgeGraphIndex (entities and relationships), TreeIndex (hierarchical summaries). Query engines retrieve context and synthesize responses. Response modes—refine (iterative, accurate), compact (fast, single call), tree_summarize (large context). Retrievers support hybrid (BM25+vector), reranking, auto-merging. SubQuestionQueryEngine decomposes complex questions; RouterQueryEngine routes to specialized indices.

methodologies.name: llamaindex-ingestion-pipeline
methodologies.description: Solves the failure modes of ad-hoc inline RAG ingestion at scale — no caching, no metadata enrichment, full re-embedding on every restart — by chaining loaders, metadata extractors, and node parsers into a reusable IngestionPipeline backed by a persistent vector store. Apply it for any production RAG that must survive restarts without re-embedding, corpora that need TitleExtractor or KeywordExtractor for filtered retrieval, or multi-source pipelines that unify loading and embedding; skip it for prototypes under 100 documents and for streaming real-time data. The practical value comes from concrete defaults — SentenceSplitter(chunk_size=1024, chunk_overlap=200) as baseline before the slower SemanticSplitterNodeParser, attaching source metadata at Document creation (not later) to avoid retrieval gaps, budget-auditing extractors before enabling SummaryExtractor on a 10k-doc corpus, and persisting via Chroma or Pinecone with version-tagged snapshots so VectorStoreIndex.from_vector_store() reloads without re-embedding.

methodologies.name: llamaindex-production-gotchas
methodologies.description: Documents the non-obvious LlamaIndex production failure modes whose symptoms are correctness and stability bugs, not just performance issues: SubQuestionQueryEngine.query() blocks async event loops, QueryFusionRetriever opens unbounded concurrent LLM calls, ReActAgent does not auto-recover from tool errors, and evaluation methods quietly burn LLM tokens. Apply it when deploying any LlamaIndex component in FastAPI/aiohttp, using fusion retrieval under concurrent load, building eval pipelines with FaithfulnessEvaluator/RelevancyEvaluator at scale, or debugging intermittent ReActAgent failures. The practical value is concrete fixes — switch to aquery() in async contexts, wrap the fusion retriever with asyncio.Semaphore(5) to respect provider RPM limits, return structured "ERROR: type: message" strings from tools instead of raising, build eval datasets once with generate_question_context_pairs() and save to disk, and wire LlamaDebugHandler in dev plus Arize Phoenix or Langfuse in production for per-step traces.

methodologies.name: llamaindex-sql-query
methodologies.description: Lets agents and non-SQL business users query relational data in natural language via NLSQLTableQueryEngine, which translates questions to SQL through an LLM, executes against a SQLAlchemy database, and returns both the answer and the generated SQL for inspection. Apply it for NL-driven business reporting, hybrid agents that route SQL queries to the database and document queries to a vector index, or auditing agent SQL via response.metadata["sql_query"]; avoid it for large or fast-changing schemas (50+ tables — use Vanna or SQLAI), write workloads, and static query patterns better served by parameterized SQL. The non-negotiable practical guarantees: connect with a read-only database user (NLSQLTableQueryEngine does not validate generated SQL before execution), restrict include_tables to the relevant subset to keep token cost down and avoid column hallucination, and switch to SQLTableRetrieverQueryEngine with ObjectIndex once the schema exceeds about 15 tables so only top-k schemas reach the LLM.

methodologies.name: llm-judge-rubric-evidence-first
methodologies.description: Replaces noisy "rate this 1-10" judge prompts (Cronbach's alpha below 0.5, worse than coin-flip on borderline cases) with a structured rubric that forces named criteria, evidence quotes from the candidate output before scoring, per-criterion rationales, and JSON output — pushing inter-rater reliability above 0.85.
