Apply it whenever exact-match or unit-test checks are impossible (summaries, plans, agent transcripts, code review comments, pairwise comparisons of agent variants) and when building regression suites for prompt or model changes; skip it when outputs are deterministically verifiable, when only one model family is available for borderline cases (self-preference is unfixable single-stack), and for throwaway prototypes. The practical value is its bias-mitigation playbook: position-swap pairwise judging with tie-on-disagree (cuts order bias 60-90%), length normalization or explicit "longer is not better" instruction (verbosity bias drops from 10-30% to under 5%), cross-family judging to expose self-preference, and a 3-5 run shuffled stability check that rejects judges with alpha below 0.7.

methodologies.name: manifest-then-fetch
methodologies.description: Solves the heavy-tailed-payload problem in tool-calling agents — sql_query, web_fetch, log_search, and read_file occasionally return hundreds of kilobytes that pollute every subsequent turn, burn prompt-cache prefixes, and trigger "lost in the middle" accuracy collapse described by the Maximum Effective Context Window paper (arXiv 2509.21361). Apply it for tools with high payload variance, agents that loop 30+ turns, pipelines where most calls only need a count or first match, or multi-tenant platforms where some tools occasionally return megabytes. The practical value: every tool result becomes a small JSON envelope (execution_id, structured preview ≤200 tokens, size_tokens, storage_uri) with the body stored externally and loaded only when the agent explicitly calls a separate get_full_result(execution_id) tool. The strict rules — preview never contains the answer, preview is structured per tool type, manifest never inlines the body — keep the LLM-facing payload tiny while preserving agent autonomy.

methodologies.name: map-reduce-send-fanout
methodologies.description: Drops wall-clock latency for embarrassingly parallel agent work (per-document scoring, multi-source RAG, multi-candidate generation, batch enrichment) from O(N · turn) to O(turn) — 5-20× on N=20 — by using LangGraph Send, ADK ParallelAgent, or asyncio.gather to fan out per-item LLM calls, then reduce with an Annotated[list, operator.add] (or custom merge) reducer. Apply only when per-item work is independent, every branch is idempotent and retryable, the target state field has a reducer annotation, and concurrency is capped at ≤20 per super-step; never use it for branches that mutate shared external state — use a queue plus workers (SQS, Inngest, Temporal) instead, because LangGraph super-steps fail atomically and non-idempotent side effects double-apply on retry. For N greater than 100 with no real-time deadline, the OpenAI/Anthropic Batch API is 50% cheaper and rate-limit-safe; for 20-100 with a deadline, chunk into batches of 20 and run sequentially.

methodologies.name: max-turns-circuit-breaker
methodologies.description: Stops the silent budget-burn failure mode of tool-calling loops: each tool result still looks like "progress" to the model, so a stuck agent (search → no results → search again with the same query) consumes tokens while the user sees nothing. Apply it on every Runner.run / agent-loop invocation that calls tools, with caps of 5-10 turns for retrieval and Q&A agents and 15-20 for code-editing agents; do not use it for sandboxed long-horizon coding agents (use checkpointing plus human-approval gates instead) or for chat completions without tool use. The practical value is treating max_turns as a circuit breaker rather than an error: explicitly set the cap, wrap the call in a try/except that catches MaxTurnsExceeded, and pass the partial trajectory through a 1-turn cheap recovery model that summarizes what was tried and asks the user to clarify — converting a runaway loop into a graceful answer instead of a 500. Raising the cap to 100 to "stop hitting it" hides the underlying bug; fix the loop, do not extend the leash.

methodologies.name: mcp-gateway-composition
methodologies.description: Solves the three compounding failure modes that hit any agent connecting to more than 5 MCP servers: schema bloat (60+ tool schemas in the system prompt every turn), namespace collisions (every server ships its own search and create_issue), and fan-out auth (each client re-implements OAuth, audit, rate limits). Apply it once an agent connects to more than 5 MCP servers OR sees more than 25 tools in its catalog — the selection-accuracy cliff per Anthropic and Composio guidance — and especially in multi-tenant deployments, cross-team setups with collision risk, or compliance-bound stacks; skip it for solo dev or prototype phases with 1-3 servers. The practical value is picking exactly one composition pattern per gateway: virtual server (curate a subset of upstream tools as one logical server), federation (gateways consume gateways for org-wide rollouts), or per-client visibility (filter by user/role). Mixing patterns creates ambiguous deny rules; lazy toolkit loading keeps the per-turn catalog under 5 tools; and the network layer must enforce gateway-only access or the deny rules are decorative.

methodologies.name: mcp-resource-vs-tool-vs-prompt
methodologies.description: Fixes the most common MCP server design mistake — misclassifying a capability as Tool when it should be Resource, or as Tool when it should be a user-invoked Prompt — using a three-question test (Does it ACT? Is it static-per-URI content? Does the user invoke it as a slash command?). Apply it when designing any MCP server or auditing one for tools that always return the same content, resources that mutate state, or tools meant to be invoked by users; skip it when consuming third-party servers. The practical value is portability across Claude Code, OpenAI Responses API, Vercel AI SDK, and LangChain plus correct primitive contracts: Tools have verb_object names with use-when/NOT-use-when descriptions and explicit MUTATING markers, Resources have stable URIs and deterministic content per URI, and Prompts compose Tools and Resources to launch multi-step user interactions. It also bans the over-broad exec(mode=...) Tool that confuses the model 25% of the time — split into named read_file, write_file, delete_file instead.

methodologies.name: mcp-transport-stdio-vs-http
methodologies.description: Fixes the deployment-shape mismatch that bakes a rewrite into MCP servers right before they go to production: pick stdio iff the server runs as a subprocess of a single client on the same machine (Claude Desktop, Claude Code, Cursor, IDE plugins) and Streamable HTTP iff the server is reached over a network or serves more than one concurrent client. Apply it when designing any new MCP server, migrating an SSE server to the post-2026-03-26 spec, or reviewing an MCP integration PR — flag any new SSE code as tech debt on day one because revision 2025-11-25 removed the dual-endpoint SSE transport. The practical value is the deployment contract each transport implies: stdio inherits the host OS user identity (no OAuth, no port binding, no shared logging), while Streamable HTTP MUST implement OAuth 2.1 with PKCE, idempotent request handling for resumable streams, a single /mcp endpoint, and shared session state in Redis or a DB so replicas behind a load balancer don't lose context mid-loop. Wrapping a stdio server in a TCP-to-stdio bridge defeats every benefit of native HTTP.

methodologies.name: model-context-protocol
methodologies.description: MCP (Model Context Protocol) standardizes how LLMs connect to tools and data sources. Anthropic-developed, tool definitions are reusable across clients. Solves the fragmentation problem where each LLM had proprietary tool formats. Security through protocol design, composable capabilities, standard definitions. MCP servers expose tools; MCP clients (Claude Code, etc.) invoke them. Benefits: reusability, security, composability, cross-client standardization. Foundation for the next-generation LLM tooling ecosystem.

methodologies.name: multi-agent-basics
methodologies.description: Coordinate multiple specialized agents to solve complex problems. Patterns—Sequential (pipeline), Parallel (fan-out/gather), Hierarchical (manager delegates), Debate (agents argue positions), Collaborative (iterate together). Implement agents with consistent system prompts, orchestrators routing messages, and conversation logging. Sequential pipeline transforms output through stages; debate system reaches consensus; hierarchical manager decomposes tasks. Test coordination logic, handle deadlocks, monitor inter-agent communication. Multi-agent systems tackle problems beyond single-agent scope.

methodologies.name: multi-agent-collaborative
methodologies.description: Provides a shared-workspace pattern where each agent produces an initial contribution, iteratively reads and builds on others' contributions, and a separate coordinator (independent LLM call) synthesizes the best elements simultaneously into a final result. Apply it on creative or strategy tasks where diverse perspectives genuinely add value — design, narrative, technical architecture decisions, brainstorming, risk identification — and on quality-critical outputs that benefit from agents critiquing each other across multiple rounds; avoid it under tight latency budgets (N agents × M iterations × LLM call), for tasks with single correct answers (echo-chamber convergence on the same wrong answer), and for branches needing fine-grained shared mutable state. The practical value comes from two non-negotiable rules: synthesis MUST receive all worker outputs simultaneously in one call (iterative merging anchors on whichever contribution arrived first), and a hard max_iterations cap (default 5) plus per-run token budget MUST gate the loop — collaborative patterns are the most expensive multi-agent shape and silently exhaust budget without enforcement.

methodologies.name: multi-agent-conversational
methodologies.description: Implements the AutoGen-style free-form turn-taking pattern for tasks whose solution path emerges from dialogue rather than upfront decomposition — interactive debugging, design reviews, adversarial proposer-critic verification, Socratic questioning, design whiteboarding. Apply it when the conversation itself is the planning step or the output, especially for adversarial setups where one agent proposes and another critiques toward a validated answer; avoid it for known sequential pipelines (cheaper as hierarchical or sequential), tight latency budgets (worst case = max_turns LLM calls), or production systems needing structured audit trails. The practical value is its two control knobs that prevent infinite loops and runaway cost: every instance MUST set both a termination_phrase and a max_turns cap (neither alone is sufficient — the phrase may never appear, max_turns alone has no semantic exit), and the sliding context window MUST be capped (default last 3 turns) to avoid quadratic token growth. The Debate variant adds an independent judge with a structured rubric, and clearly opposing system prompts (proponent vs opponent) are required to force genuine divergence past echo-chamber convergence.

methodologies.name: multi-agent-design-patterns
methodologies.description: Enterprise multi-agent architectures use eight design patterns—Sequential Pipeline, Parallel Fan-Out/Gather, Hierarchical Decomposition, Generator+Critic, Loop (iterative refinement), Human-in-Loop, Router, Blackboard (shared workspace). Framework comparison: CrewAI (low setup, role-based), AutoGen (multi-agent conversations), LangGraph (state machines), AgentFlow (low-code canvas), NVIDIA Nemotron (high throughput). Enterprise adoption accelerating—40% of apps will have AI agents by 2026 (Gartner). Choose pattern based on task structure and expertise division.

methodologies.name: multi-agent-hierarchical
methodologies.description: Decomposes complex tasks into role-scoped worker calls coordinated by a manager agent that creates a JSON assignment plan, dispatches each subtask to the matching specialist, then synthesizes all results into a final answer. Apply it when worker roles genuinely differ (researcher, coder, reviewer with minimal overlap), when subtasks decompose cleanly into a synthesis step, and when production auditability requires a traceable assignment log; avoid it for simple tasks fitting one context window (manager overhead adds 2 extra LLM round-trips), tight latency budgets, or tasks needing fine-grained shared mutable state. The practical value rests on two rules: validate the plan with json.loads() before dispatching and return parse errors back to the manager for re-planning rather than aborting silently, and keep the manager system prompt strictly coordination-only ("Decompose tasks and coordinate workers") — the moment it also "writes code" the pattern collapses to a single bottleneck agent with extra prompt tokens. Model-tier guidance: Opus for the orchestrator, Sonnet for researcher/coder/reviewer workers.

methodologies.name: multi-agent-production-bus
methodologies.description: Decouples agent communication from coordination strategy via a central async MessageBus (direct, broadcast, request modes with full message_history audit trail) wrapped by a ProductionMultiAgentSystem that selects hierarchical, parallel, or sequential execution at run_task() call time. Apply it for production systems that must switch coordination strategy at runtime without rewriting agent code, deployments needing audit trails of every inter-agent message for compliance, async environments where worker blocking is unacceptable, or cost-sensitive runs requiring per-agent token attribution; skip it for two-agent point-to-point pipelines and synchronous-only environments. The practical value comes from two non-negotiable rules: all inter-agent messages MUST use structured JSON-serializable content (free-form text breaks parsing and replay debugging) and every handler MUST be wrapped with asyncio.wait_for(handler(msg), timeout=X) so one slow agent cannot deadlock the bus. It also names the production failure modes explicitly — cost explosion gated by per-agent and per-run budgets, cascading hallucinations needing fact-check nodes, human-in-loop approval gates before irreversible actions, and per-agent role tagging via agentops or LangSmith from day one.

methodologies.name: plan-execute-vs-react
methodologies.description: Solves the choice between two competing agent control loops — Plan-Execute, which front-loads reasoning into one auditable plan and then runs deterministic execution, versus ReAct, which interleaves Thought/Action/Observation step-by-step so each tool result reshapes the next move. Apply when designing or debugging an agent loop: pick Plan-Execute for 3-10 known sub-steps with fixed order, ReAct for exploratory tasks where each step depends on surprising observations, and a Hybrid (top-level plan + per-step ReAct bounded to ~5 turns) for long horizons over 10 steps. The practical value is avoiding the two most common failure modes — pure ReAct on a 30-step refactor that wanders without auditability, and pure Plan-Execute on web search where the blind plan breaks on the first surprise — by matching loop shape to predictability and adding cheap safeguards like max_turns, replan-on-failure, and stuck detection.

methodologies.name: posttool-hook-self-correction
methodologies.description: Solves the unreliability of asking a code-editing agent to remember to lint after each edit by wiring linters, formatters, and typecheckers into Claude Code PostToolUse hooks (matcher Write|Edit) that run automatically on every file mutation. Apply to any code-editing agent, CI bot, or strict-format pipeline where validators must run after every change; non-zero exit codes are surfaced to the model as synthetic tool-result errors on the next turn, and the RLHF-trained retry behavior produces a self-correcting edit without any extra prompting. The practical value is converting a manual lint-then-fix cycle into one auto-corrected turn at zero prompt cost, with the caveats that the validator should be scoped to the changed file (not the whole repo) and slow checks belong on PreCommit or background jobs to avoid stalling the loop.

methodologies.name: preference-trained-router
methodologies.description: Solves the cost and latency problems of weak-to-strong cascades by training a tiny router on Chatbot-Arena-style preference data so it can decide before inference whether each prompt should go to the weak or strong model — one round-trip, no cascade, no reliance on the cheap model's poor self-confidence. Apply in latency-sensitive production with at least ~10k logged (prompt, weak-output, strong-output) triples, mostly stable task distribution, and a workload that mixes easy and hard prompts; pick the cheapest router family (matrix factorization, BERT classifier, kNN ranker, LLM-judge) that hits the quality target on a held-out validation set. RouteLLM reports preserving 95% of GPT-4 quality while routing only 26% of queries to the strong model (~48% cheaper than random at matched quality); deployment requires a nightly canary suite, drift monitoring, scheduled retraining mixing the static Arena seed with the last 4 weeks of traffic, and an optional cascade fallback on weak-routed answers that fail a cheap verifier.

methodologies.name: previous-response-id-reasoning-reuse
methodologies.description: Solves the 20-40% agentic-benchmark regression that happens when reasoning models (o3, o4-mini, gpt-5) are chained turn-to-turn by manually rebuilding a Chat-Completions-style message array, which silently strips the model's intermediate reasoning items. Apply on the OpenAI Responses API for multi-turn agent loops, conversational reasoning sessions, and long planning trajectories: every turn after the first must set previous_response_id=<prior.id> and pass only the new input items so reasoning items are reattached server-side, and under store=false (Zero Data Retention) switch to include=["reasoning.encrypted_content"] and round-trip the encrypted blobs in the input array. The practical value is preserving the model's mid-loop scratchpad across turns, lifting cache-hit rate, cutting latency and tokens, and avoiding the silent quality cliff that hits the moment ZDR is enabled but previous_response_id is left in place (it is ignored without a server-side store).

methodologies.name: progressive-disclosure-skills
methodologies.description: Solves the import-everything anti-pattern in Claude Skills authoring, where pre-loading all instructions into the system prompt blows the context budget and degrades reasoning quality past ~10 skills. Apply when authoring any reusable capability over ~100 lines, migrating a long CLAUDE.md into modular skills, or building a Claude Code agent that ships with several skills under a ~5k-token base context: structure each skill as a strict three-level pyramid — YAML frontmatter (name, trigger-rich description under 30 lines) is always loaded; SKILL.md body is loaded only when the router activates the skill and must stay under 500 lines; scripts/, references/, and assets/ load only when SKILL.md tells Claude to Read them. The practical value is keeping tool-selection accuracy high past 25 skills, paying context cost only at activation, and avoiding the three classic collapses (oversized body, trigger-poor description, inlined references) that flatten the pyramid back into a fat blob.

methodologies.name: prompt-cache-prefix-order
methodologies.description: Solves the silent loss of 80-90% potential cost savings when a prompt's volatile pieces (user names, version strings, latest user turn) sit in front of long stable content, preventing prompt caching from ever warming. Apply on any high-volume Claude/OpenAI/Gemini workload with system prompts over ~1024 tokens, stable tool definitions, or large retrieval contexts reused across calls: order segments stable-first (system → tools → retrieval → conversation history → latest user turn), set a cache_control breakpoint after the longest stable prefix, and keep user-personalized data out of the system prompt. The practical value is paying ~10% of the normal token rate on the cached prefix on every reused call (typical 78%+ savings on 10-question codebase Q&A), with concrete maintenance rules — use 1-hour TTL for batch jobs, expect a one-call miss when tools change, never A/B-test system prompts in production, verify cache_creation_input_tokens > 0 on the first call and cache_read_input_tokens > 0 thereafter — and a checklist for finding volatile bytes when the cache fails to warm.

methodologies.name: reasoning-first-architectures
methodologies.description: Build agents that think before acting using ReAct patterns, Tree-of-Thought exploration, and reflexion loops. These reasoning-first architectures solve the problem of agents making premature or suboptimal decisions by enforcing structured thought processes before action execution. Apply when dealing with complex multi-step problems requiring careful analysis. The value delivered is dramatically improved agent reliability and better decision quality, especially for reasoning-intensive tasks like research and planning.

methodologies.name: record-replay-debugging
methodologies.description: Solves the unreproducibility of non-deterministic agent failures (temperature > 0, retries, parallel tool races, MCP flakiness) where logs and OTel traces show what happened but cannot show why a specific LLM decision was made. Apply by architecting every agent with two interchangeable modes: record captures every LLM request, tool call, RNG draw, clock read, and external state read into one trace; replay re-runs the agent from that trace with zero outbound network IO, raising immediately on any unrecorded boundary, and tagging side-effect tools (send email, charge card) so they are never re-executed. The practical value goes beyond reproduction to fix verification — treat each trace as a fixture and mutate exactly one axis (prompt, tool stub, model, system instruction) per replay, asserting the bug path is gone — and to growing eval suites from real incidents, since a recorded trace plus a must-not-happen assertion is already a perfect deterministic regression test.

methodologies.name: refusal-field-strict-schema
methodologies.description: Solves the parser failures that happen when a model refuses to answer in a strict-mode structured-output call: the schema's finite-state grammar forces the model to either invent fake values for required fields or violate the grammar entirely, surfacing as a generic parser exception instead of a refusal signal. Apply to PII, medical, legal, and financial extraction; content moderation; long batch jobs where one item refusing must not abort the batch; and multi-provider deployments where refusal handling must be uniform — add refusal: str | None = None as the first field, mark every payload field nullable, and branch on response.refusal in a single typed check before reading the payload. The practical value is mirroring OpenAI's native top-level refusal contract inside your own schema so it works identically on Anthropic and local backends, replacing brittle string-matching for "I cannot" / "Sorry" with a typed branch, and keeping payload fields null-safe under refusal so downstream code never reads invented data.

methodologies.name: rerank-before-reasoning
methodologies.description: Solves the precision ceiling of embedding-only retrieval feeding a strong-model reasoner: vector similarity is too coarse for queries with negation, multi-hop intent, or domain jargon, and feeding the raw top-K to Opus or GPT-5 wastes expensive reasoning on plausible-but-wrong context. Apply in any RAG agent or knowledge-base lookup over corpora of at least ~1k chunks where retrieval feeds a strong reasoner, especially in medical, legal, or financial domains: retrieve 50-200 candidates with cheap embeddings (recall pass), rerank with a cross-encoder (Cohere Rerank, BGE-reranker, Voyage rerank-2), then pass top 5-10 to the reasoner. The practical value is a 10-20 percentage-point lift in precision@10 (Pinecone, ZeroEntropy 2025) for ~50-150 ms and a fraction of a cent per 100 candidates, with sizing rules (sweep K and pick the smallest plateau), reranker-family tradeoffs (hosted vs open-weight vs LLM-as-judge), and corpus-rotation triggers for re-evaluation rather than fixed cadence.

methodologies.name: role-specialized-models
methodologies.description: Solves the 3-5x cost overrun of pinning every step of a multi-agent pipeline to the strongest model (or, conversely, the quality regressions of pinning everything to the cheapest one). Apply in multi-step Claude Code agents and LangGraph/CrewAI pipelines whose steps have genuinely different cognitive demands: pin the strongest model to planning and final review (~5% of tokens, ~70% of quality), the mid model to execution and code-writing (~80% of tokens), and the smallest model to classification, slot-filling, and reformatting (~15% of tokens) — split by cognitive ROLE, not by step sequence. The practical value, per Anthropic's advisor strategy, is roughly 3x lower cost per shipped feature with no quality regression on internal eval suites, with two enforcement mechanisms — structured artifact hand-offs (JSON plans, file lists, acceptance checklists) instead of conversation blobs, and stronger-model verification gates after every transition from a stronger to a weaker model — plus a periodic ablation to keep role assignments honest as model versions ship.

methodologies.name: schema-field-order
methodologies.description: Solves the silent hallucinations that happen when a structured-output schema places dependent fields before their dependencies — title/summary/id/confidence committed before the body or answer they should derive from — because LLMs generate fields autoregressively in declaration order and each field can attend only to fields emitted before it. Apply whenever a schema has any semantic dependency between fields (almost always): place inputs/echoes first, then intermediate observations and plans, then the final answer, with metadata (slug, tags, confidence) last. The practical value is free chain-of-thought without extended-thinking APIs — "reasoning before answer" via scratchpad fields, tighter titles derived from real bodies, calibrated confidence after the answer, sharper code-review verdicts after the issues are listed — and field descriptions reinforce ordering by referencing earlier fields explicitly; works identically in Pydantic, OpenAI Responses API, Anthropic tool input schemas, and Gemini's explicit property_ordering.

methodologies.name: schema-version-pinning
methodologies.description: Solves silent schema drift in multi-stage agent pipelines, where a producer change (renaming tags to topics, adding a field) ripples invisibly through stored events, replays, and backfills until old consumers crash deep inside model_validate on an unrelated field. Apply in event-sourced agent systems, cross-team contracts where producer and consumer ship independently, long-running batch jobs that span schema-change releases, and stored conversation logs to be re-processed months later: declare schema_version: Literal["vN"] = "vN" as the first field on every Pydantic SO model, persist it on every emitted event, and route consumers via a typed dict[str, type[BaseModel]] parser registry keyed by version. The practical value is replacing silent drift with three guarantees — every event self-identifies, consumers route by version explicitly, and old/new schemas can run side-by-side during a defined rollback window — while unknown versions raise loudly at the parse boundary instead of corrupting downstream state, mirroring the Kafka schema-registry pattern adapted to Pydantic + LLM strict mode.

methodologies.name: semantic-field-naming
methodologies.description: Solves the under-recognized fact that field NAMES are part of the prompt: generic placeholders like field1, val, data, output, or final_choice force the model to guess what to put there, while specific semantically-loaded names recruit pre-trained priors for free. Apply to every structured-output schema, every Pydantic model, and every tool input schema — make names English, lower_snake_case, at least two tokens, with units in numerics (age_years, total_cents, confidence_0_to_1), direction in booleans (is_paid, has_promo_code, requires_age_verification), and semantic enum values (priority: low/medium/high/critical, never a/b/c). The practical value is documented by Instructor's 2024 finding that renaming a single field from final_choice to answer lifted benchmark accuracy from 4.5% to 95% — a 90-point swing from one token — and includes a common rename map (output/result/data/value/flag → name what it actually is), legacy-migration aliases for back-compatibility, and an A/B harness for measuring the lift on real tasks.

methodologies.name: stream-json-orchestration
methodologies.description: Solves the inability to react mid-run when an orchestrator wraps an agent CLI (Claude Code, Codex, Aider, opencode) as a subprocess and only receives final output, missing every chance to enforce a budget cap, veto a dangerous tool call, or hand off to another agent. Apply whenever spawning an agent CLI subprocess in production for tasks longer than ~30 seconds: invoke with --output-format stream-json plus --allowedTools, --max-turns, closed stdin, and parse stdout line-by-line so each event (system/init, assistant, user/tool_result, result) can drive live UI updates, OpenTelemetry spans, conditional chaining (parse plan → fan out N parallel subagents), cost-cap kills, safety vetoes on tool_use events before they execute, and durable replay logs. The practical value is real-time observability and intervention with no SDK dependency, covering the headless safety bundle (-p + allowlist + max-turns + closed stdin), pause/resume via session_id, fan-out/collect, and the diagnostic checklist for streams that hang or buffer.

methodologies.name: strict-mode-required-fields
methodologies.description: Solves the OpenAI/Azure 400 "additionalProperties must be false when strict is true" error and the silent grammar-rejection failures that break every loose Pydantic model migrated to strict mode. Apply on any call against the OpenAI Responses API or Chat Completions with response_format json_schema strict: true, Anthropic tool-use schemas where invented parameters must be impossible, Azure parse, and Gemini responseSchema with strict enforcement: set additionalProperties: false on every object, list every declared property in required, and encode "may be absent" by typing the field T | None = None (with extra="forbid" on Pydantic) — never by dropping it from required. The practical value is producing schemas the strict-mode FSM compiler accepts on the first try, with build-time assertions on Model.model_json_schema() to catch regressions before traffic hits, plus warnings against empty-string sentinels and against using nullable types as a refusal escape hatch (use a refusal field instead).

methodologies.name: structured-output-mode-picker
methodologies.description: Solves the silent correctness regressions that happen when teams default everything to legacy json_object mode in 2026, even after providers shipped strict Structured Outputs, native tool calls, and grammar-mode libraries (XGrammar, Outlines, GBNF) that are not interchangeable. Apply when choosing constrained decoding for any new agent, pipeline stage, or migration: pick by output consumer — deterministic data extraction → SO strict (response_format json_schema, strict: true); agent action → tool call (provider gives dispatch + parallel-tool selection for free); custom DSL/SQL/regex → real grammar mode on a model that supports it; legacy fallback → json mode only when strict SO is unavailable. The practical value is matching guarantees to the actual consumer's needs, documented by BuildMVPFast 2026, Vellum's mode comparison, and the XGrammar paper (100x throughput vs string-inside-JSON workarounds), with three named anti-patterns: json_object for typed extraction, SO discriminator unions for action dispatch, and forcing SQL through an unconstrained tool-call string argument.

methodologies.name: structured-tool-errors
methodologies.description: Solves the dead-end retry loops and hallucinated explanations that follow when a tool returns a raw stack trace or upstream HTTP body — the agent cannot tell rate-limited from broken from caller-error and either retries blindly or invents a plausible-sounding reason. Apply on every error path of every tool, especially MCP servers and tools wrapping flaky upstreams: return a JSON envelope with code (UPPER_SNAKE machine code), message (one human sentence, no stack trace), recoveryHint as a closed enum (RETRY_LATER | CHECK_INPUT | TRY_ALTERNATIVE | REPORT_TO_USER | NEEDS_AUTH), traceId for human correlation, and optional retry_after_seconds and sanitized details. The practical value, per SHIELDA and Kumaran's MCP error-handling study, is a 30%+ drop in dead-end loops because the agent runner maps each hint to exactly one deterministic action (sleep+retry within budget, send back to model, halt for user, request auth) without burning a model turn on retry-policy hallucination, and the closed enum keeps INVALID_ARGUMENT from being silently retried while keeping RATE_LIMITED from being prematurely escalated.

methodologies.name: subagent-as-context-firewall
methodologies.description: Solves main-context blowup in deep work by using subagents not primarily for parallelism but as a one-way valve: the subagent does the heavy reading, searching, or planning in its own fresh context window and returns ONLY a slim structured report (summary 3-5 sentences, refs as paths/URLs/IDs, follow_up_questions, confidence) so the parent's context grows by ~500 tokens regardless of how much the subagent processed internally. Apply for codebase exploration over many files, untrusted-content sandboxing (where prompt-injection attempts are absorbed by the firewall), speculative branches (try fix A, B, C in parallel), and recursive multi-layer hierarchies where each boundary slims further. The practical value is achieved by enforcing a strict output contract (no source-code dumps, no quotes longer than 10 words, hard-cap output at 500-2000 tokens, fail with confidence: low instead of silent), keeping heavy work inside the firewall and synthesis in the parent, and sharing subagent state via FILES rather than parent context — the same mechanism behind Claude Code's Task tool, OpenAI Swarm, and LangGraph subgraphs.

methodologies.name: terse-default-tool-output
methodologies.description: Solves the most common cause of context blowup in long agent loops: tools that dump full payloads by default (50 log rows, full record JSON, raw query results) and bury the signal the model actually uses for the next step under thousands of redundant tokens. Apply to any tool whose worst-case output exceeds ~500 tokens (list_*, search_*, query_*, log queries, file listings, DB selects): default to a compact shape (one-line summary per hit with primary keys, table of IDs, count + top-N) and add an explicit format: Literal["summary","full"] parameter so the agent can opt into raw payloads only when reasoning needs the detail. The practical value is documented by SkillReducer (~40% compression of descriptions and bodies LIFTED downstream task quality 2.8% — verbose context distracts more than it helps), with two named anti-patterns: summary modes that drop the IDs needed to escalate to full mode, and silent truncation without an explicit truncated: true flag that lets the agent know it didn't see everything.

methodologies.name: tool-description-as-prompt
methodologies.description: Solves the wrong-tool-selection and bad-argument problems that come from treating a tool's description as a docstring afterthought instead of part of the prompt the model sees on every turn. Apply to every tool definition (Anthropic, OpenAI, MCP) — write descriptions as zero-shot teaching for a junior engineer using the tool for the first time: verb-object name (search_docs, apply_patch), one-sentence "use this when ...", one-sentence "do NOT use this when ..." (the highest-leverage line, especially for similar tools that need mutual anti-triggers), input contract beyond the schema, output shape and limits, and explicit side-effect markers (MUTATING in caps, LATENCY hints, do-not-poll instructions, pagination loop caps). The practical value is documented by Anthropic's SWE-bench result that improving tool descriptions without changing the model achieved state-of-the-art, with concrete cures for the most common bugs (model alternates randomly between read_file and grep, calls delete_branch on the working tree, spins polling a long-running batch job) and an audit checklist plus 50-task eval pattern for measuring tool-selection and argument-fill error rates before and after rewrites.

methodologies.name: trajectory-eval-otel
methodologies.description: Solves the problem that single-axis 'percent correct' metrics hide expensive regressions in agent runs by scoring outcome, trajectory (path optimality, loops, wasted steps), and resources (tokens, cost, latency, cache-hit rate) on every run, with traces captured using OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions so spans flow uniformly to Langfuse, Phoenix, Datadog, or any OTLP backend. Apply it to production agents that need replayable debugging, A/B comparisons of prompts and models, cost optimization, regulated audit trails, or multi-agent systems where subagent behaviour must be attributable. The practical value is catching regressions that 'success rate' misses — for example a Sonnet upgrade where outcome stayed at 96% but average steps went from 4.1 to 7.3, or a verbose tool result that quintupled cost without changing success — and turning four-hour debug sessions into twenty-minute replays of the exact failing trajectory. Skip it for one-shot scripts and PII-heavy paths without redaction at the collector boundary.

methodologies.name: two-pass-reason-then-extract
methodologies.description: Addresses the measurable 10 to 15 percent accuracy drop on hard reasoning tasks (math, proofs, deep research synthesis, code with subtle constraints) caused by forcing a strong model into strict structured output during the reasoning step, where the grammar mask burns probability on schema-conforming tokens that are not the optimal reasoning tokens. The remedy is a two-pass cascade: pass 1 runs the strong model in free text or extended-thinking mode without any schema, then pass 2 runs a cheap Haiku-class extractor with strict structured output to fill the schema from the trace. Use it whenever deep reasoning AND a rigid schema are both required and the cheap extractor exceeds 95 percent accuracy on extraction-only inputs; skip it for simple extraction, sub-second latency budgets, or volumes where two model calls cost more than the accuracy recovers. The practical payoff is preserving full reasoning accuracy while still landing strict typing on the consumer, with the extractor cost amortised by caching the stable system prompt.

methodologies.name: verb-object-tool-naming
methodologies.description: Fixes the most common cause of wrong-tool selection in agent catalogs: vague single-word names like search, manage, or do_thing that collide across MCP servers and force the model to read every description on every turn. The rule is to name every tool lowercase verb_object (create_issue, search_files) and prepend a service namespace (github_create_issue, stripe_refund_charge) whenever tools come from more than one source or pass through an MCP gateway. Apply it to any catalog of five or more tools, multi-server or aggregator setups, third-party SDK wrappers, and during tool-eval iteration where renames can be A/B tested. The mechanism is that names are part of the prompt and the model selects by lexical match first, description second; verb_object mirrors the CRUD endpoint conventions Claude saw millions of times in pre-training, while namespaces eliminate collisions — Anthropic's internal evals show prefix and naming schemes meaningfully change selection accuracy. Avoid polymorphic action enums (manage_files(action=copy|move|delete)) and marketing-padded names that dilute the verb.

methodologies.name: weak-model-preselection
methodologies.description: Solves the cost blowout from running a strong reasoning model over long inputs that are mostly irrelevant noise by inverting the pipeline: a small cheap model (Haiku, gpt-4o-mini) filters, classifies, ranks, or extracts references first, and the strong model receives only the filtered subset. The four variants are filter-then-reason, reference-extract-then-load, classify-then-route, and rank-then-select-top-N; the cheap stage MUST return only refs, IDs, booleans, or scores — never raw content — and keep-rate should be tuned to 5 to 30 percent for the savings to materialise. Apply it to corpora over 5K tokens with mostly irrelevant content, RSS pipelines, code review across hundreds of files, support routing, and memory retrieval; skip it when filter mistakes are catastrophic (medical, legal, security) without using the cheap model only as a ranker. Practical examples include the neromedia news pipeline reducing per-hour cost from $3 to $0.07 (40x cheaper) and code review agents going 50x cheaper by narrowing 800 changed files to 20-40 before Sonnet reads them. Always add a fallback when the filter returns zero items and use the same model family to keep tokenization and persona consistent.

domains.description: Configures Claude Code environment: skill creation, agent setup, slash commands, lifecycle hooks, MCP server development and installation. Provides decision tree for creating skills (orchestrators, role-based, process-based), agents with isolated contexts, commands with arguments, hooks for automation, and IDE integrations.

methodologies.name: agents
methodologies.description: Create and deploy specialized subagents with isolated contexts for autonomous task execution. This methodology addresses context pollution and uncontrolled task sprawl by giving each agent a focused scope and explicit tool whitelist. Use when building multi-agent systems requiring parallel execution or task specialization. The outcome is cleaner architecture, easier debugging, and the ability to scale work across isolated execution environments without interference.

methodologies.name: commands
methodologies.description: Build slash commands that accept arguments and deliver fast, single-purpose actions for quick user interactions. Commands solve the problem of context bloat by keeping implementation minimal and lightweight compared to full skills or agents. Apply for frequently-used utilities that need command-line style arguments. The benefit is reduced token consumption and instant user access to commonly repeated actions.

methodologies.name: hooks
methodologies.description: Implement lifecycle automation using pre-tool, post-tool, and session hooks that execute scripts before or after specific events. Hooks solve permission prompts, autoformat code automatically, and enforce security policies without user intervention. Use when you need consistent enforcement of standards across all tool usage or to auto-approve safe operations. The value is reduced friction, consistent quality, and automatic policy application.

methodologies.name: mcp
methodologies.description: Build and configure Model Context Protocol servers to extend Claude's capabilities with custom tools and resources. MCP servers solve the problem of tool sprawl and credential management by providing a standardized interface for integrating external services. Apply when building complex integrations or when you need reusable tool packages. The outcome is modular, shareable tool infrastructure that works across both Claude Code and standalone agents.

methodologies.name: mcp-basics
methodologies.description: Learn the fundamentals of MCP server development in TypeScript or Python with proper tool and resource definitions. This foundation addresses the gap between knowing you want to extend Claude and understanding the protocol mechanics. Use as your starting point before building custom MCP servers. The value is understanding the underlying patterns that enable all MCP integrations, from official servers to custom implementations.

methodologies.name: mcp-servers
methodologies.description: Reference and install 40+ production MCP servers for platforms like GitHub, Slack, PostgreSQL, Figma, Stripe, and more. This catalog solves the discovery problem of "what MCP servers exist for my use case?" Apply when you need external service integration without building from scratch. The benefit is rapidly connecting Claude to your entire tech stack without custom development.

methodologies.name: project-docs-convention
methodologies.description: Implement the CLAUDE.md + AGENTS.md documentation pattern that works across both Claude Code and standalone agents. This convention solves the split-brain problem where Claude Code knows about CLAUDE.md but agents read files directly. Apply in every project directory with source code for consistent context discovery. The value is single-source-of-truth documentation accessible to all agent types.

methodologies.name: skills
methodologies.description: Create reusable procedural knowledge packages that encapsulate workflows, methodologies, and domain expertise. Skills solve the problem of duplicated knowledge by packaging repeatable patterns into discoverable, composable units. Use when you have multi-step workflows needed across projects or when building knowledge libraries. The outcome is a knowledge base that grows more valuable as you add skills and becomes auto-discoverable when needed.

domains.description: Direct integration with LLM APIs covering OpenAI, Claude, Gemini, and local models (Ollama). Includes prompt engineering (few-shot, chain-of-thought), function calling and tool use, structured output with JSON schemas, guardrails for content safety, and cost optimization. Supports multiple API formats and deployment strategies.

methodologies.name: claude-advanced-features
methodologies.description: Claude's extended thinking, computer use, prompt caching, and batch API enable sophisticated reasoning and cost optimization. Use extended thinking for complex multi-step problems, computer use for visual workflows, prompt caching to reduce token costs by 90%, and the batch API for non-time-sensitive bulk processing. These features deliver better reasoning quality, visual task automation, and significant cost savings when integrated thoughtfully.

methodologies.name: claude-api-basics
methodologies.description: Set up authentication, understand model selection, handle rate limiting, count tokens, and track costs for Claude API integration. This foundation addresses the operational requirements of production LLM systems—you cannot build safely without understanding authentication, quota management, and cost. Use as the prerequisite before any API integration work. The benefit is avoiding common gotchas like auth failures, quota surprises, and unexpected bill spikes.

methodologies.name: claude-api-integration
methodologies.description: Integrate Claude into production applications with streaming, async execution, tool use, vision capabilities, and error recovery patterns. This methodology solves the engineering problem of turning API calls into reliable application features that handle edge cases. Apply when building production services that depend on Claude. The outcome is robust integration code that handles errors gracefully, streams responses, and maintains quality under load.

methodologies.name: claude-best-practices
methodologies.description: Optimize prompts, cost, performance, and reliability through model selection, prompt engineering, token management, and production patterns. This knowledge addresses the gap between working prototypes and production-ready systems where efficiency matters. Use when transitioning from proof-of-concept to deployed services. The value is dramatically reduced costs, faster responses, and higher quality outputs through systematic optimization.

methodologies.name: claude-messages-api
methodologies.description: Master the Messages API for text generation, streaming, vision inputs, PDF processing, and multi-turn conversations. This API is the foundation of all Claude interactions and solves the problem of understanding the core message protocol. Apply whenever building any Claude-powered feature. The benefit is knowing exactly how to structure requests, handle responses, and leverage all modality options available.

methodologies.name: claude-tool-use
methodologies.description: Implement tool use and function calling patterns for structured interaction, parallel tool execution, and agentic loops. Tool use solves the problem of moving LLMs from text-only systems to action-oriented agents that can call functions and iterate. Apply when you need the LLM to interact with external systems or when deterministic structure matters. The outcome is agents capable of multi-step task execution with proper error handling.

methodologies.name: cot-basics
methodologies.description: Apply chain-of-thought prompting with zero-shot, few-shot, and self-consistency techniques to improve LLM reasoning. CoT solves the problem of shallow thinking by forcing step-by-step reasoning. Use for any complex reasoning task where intermediate steps matter. The value is substantially better accuracy on logic, math, and analysis problems through structured reasoning.

methodologies.name: cot-techniques
methodologies.description: Advance beyond basic CoT with Tree-of-Thought exploration, Least-to-Most decomposition, and domain-specific reasoning patterns. These advanced techniques solve the problem of hitting CoT limits with very complex problems. Apply when basic chain-of-thought isn't sufficient or when exploring multiple solution paths adds value. The benefit is handling harder problems through structured exploration and hierarchical decomposition.

methodologies.name: function-calling-patterns
methodologies.description: Design production function-calling systems with tool routers, parallel execution, agentic loops, and error recovery. This addresses the architectural challenge of scaling from single-tool calls to multi-tool orchestration. Use when building systems with many tools or complex tool interaction logic. The outcome is scalable, reliable tool execution that handles partial failures and tool errors gracefully.

methodologies.name: gemini-api-integration
methodologies.description: Integrate Google's Gemini API leveraging its 1M token context, native multimodal support, and cost-effectiveness. Gemini solves the problem of processing very long documents, videos, and code repositories that exceed other models' context windows. Apply when you need multimodal processing or have documents exceeding 200K tokens. The value is processing capabilities impossible with smaller context windows and native support for media types.

methodologies.name: gemini-basics
methodologies.description: Understand Gemini models, authentication, text generation, streaming, chat, and safety settings for Google AI integration. This foundation covers the essentials of working with Google's LLM API. Use before diving into advanced Gemini patterns. The benefit is understanding model selection, cost structure, and safety mechanisms specific to Gemini.

methodologies.name: gemini-function-calling
methodologies.description: Implement function calling, Google Search grounding, and embeddings with Gemini for extended capability and real-time information access. Function calling solves the problem of Gemini-specific tool patterns which differ from OpenAI. Apply when using Gemini in production and needing tool interaction. The outcome is proper integration of Gemini's tool capabilities and real-time information grounding.

methodologies.name: gemini-multimodal
methodologies.description: Process images, videos, audio, PDFs, and code using Gemini's native multimodal support with caching for cost savings. Multimodal processing solves the problem of handling diverse input types in a single request. Use when working with video understanding, document extraction, or multi-format analysis. The value is unified processing of any media type and context caching that reduces costs by 75%.

methodologies.name: guardrails-basics
methodologies.description: Implement safety guardrails through input validation, PII detection, prompt injection detection, and content moderation. Guardrails solve the fundamental safety problem of production LLM systems by adding multiple defense layers. Apply in any user-facing application or when handling sensitive data. The benefit is reduced safety risks and compliance with content policies.

methodologies.name: guardrails-implementation
methodologies.description: Build complete guardrails pipelines with output validation, hallucination detection, and monitoring for production safety. This goes beyond basics to create comprehensive safety systems that catch issues both before and after LLM execution. Use in regulated industries or when safety is critical. The outcome is comprehensive safety coverage across input validation, processing, output filtering, and monitoring.

methodologies.name: local-llm-ollama
methodologies.description: Run large language models locally using Ollama for privacy, cost elimination, and offline capability with models like Llama 3 and Mistral. Local LLMs solve the problem of API dependency, data privacy concerns, and per-token costs for high-volume applications. Apply when you have privacy requirements, need offline capability, or want to eliminate API costs. The value is complete data privacy and zero per-inference costs while retaining reasonable performance.

methodologies.name: openai-api-integration
methodologies.description: OpenAI API integration provides access to GPT-4 and other models for text generation, embeddings, and multimodal tasks. Apply this when building production conversational AI, content generation, or code analysis applications. The value comes from providing authentication, model selection guidance, streaming responses, structured output handling, and production-ready error handling with retry logic.

methodologies.name: openai-assistants
methodologies.description: OpenAI Assistants API enables stateful AI agents with persistent conversation threads, file search capabilities, and code interpreter access. Use this when you need long-running conversations, knowledge base integration, or automated data analysis without managing tool infrastructure yourself. It delivers value by handling state management, enabling RAG over documents, and allowing Python code execution.

methodologies.name: openai-chat-completions
methodologies.description: Chat Completions API is the core endpoint for all OpenAI text interactions, supporting streaming, vision analysis, and tool use. Apply it to any application needing text generation, from simple classification to complex multi-turn reasoning. Value derives from low latency, high quality outputs, support for vision understanding, and comprehensive parameter control for output behavior.

methodologies.name: openai-embeddings
methodologies.description: Embeddings and Batch API convert text to vector representations for semantic search and enable cost-reduction through non-urgent batch processing. Use embeddings for RAG, similarity search, and clustering; use Batch API for non-time-sensitive workloads like daily summarization jobs. These deliver value through 50% cost savings (batch) and 80%+ recall improvements (semantic search).

methodologies.name: openai-function-calling
methodologies.description: Function calling enables LLMs to invoke external tools, APIs, and execute code while maintaining structured reasoning. Apply this when building agents that need to interact with external systems, perform precise calculations, or enforce specific output formats. Value comes from parallel tool execution, reliable schema compliance, and the ability to ground LLM outputs in verifiable actions.

methodologies.name: prompt-basics
methodologies.description: Prompt basics covers core concepts like system messages, few-shot examples, and role-based prompting that form the foundation of effective LLM use. Apply whenever you need consistent LLM behavior across tasks, especially for simple ones like classification or extraction. Value lies in improving accuracy 15-30% through better instruction clarity and format specification without token overhead.

methodologies.name: prompt-techniques
methodologies.description: Advanced prompting techniques including chain-of-thought, meta-prompting, prompt chaining, and prompt libraries enable sophisticated multi-step reasoning and template management. Use for complex problems requiring visible reasoning steps, for optimizing prompts via LLM feedback, or for managing production prompt libraries at scale. Value comes from 15-40% accuracy improvements for reasoning tasks and reduced maintenance burden.

methodologies.name: semantic-xml-content
methodologies.description: Defines a convention for authoring methodology bodies as semantic XML where every tag name describes the role of its content (rule, antipattern, example, reference, see-also) rather than its visual presentation, replacing free-form Markdown so agents can apply different policies per content type. The convention rests on four guarantees — role-tagged content, closed vocabulary, machine parseability, no presentation logic — backed by Anthropic's recommendation that XML tags reduce misinterpretation, separate instructions from data, and let the model honour distinctions Claude was fine-tuned to recognise. Use it when authoring content/*.xml inside any new faion-network methodology, writing reference docs an agent will load and apply, migrating Markdown methodologies that need to be agent-actionable, or designing tool-use prompts where input data must be cleanly separated from instructions; skip it for human READMEs, routing AGENTS.md/SKILL.md files, native code files, and one-off prompts. The practical payoff is more reliable rule-following, easier validation against a closed tag glossary, and cheaper future schema migrations because the content is already structured.

methodologies.name: structured-output-basics
methodologies.description: Structured output ensures LLMs return data in consistent, machine-readable formats like JSON with schema validation. Apply when integrating LLM outputs directly into applications, APIs, or automated pipelines where format reliability is critical. Value derives from eliminating parsing errors, reducing downstream validation code, and enabling reliable automation of data extraction workflows.

methodologies.name: structured-output-patterns
methodologies.description: Advanced patterns for production structured output including retry logic, streaming with partial parsing, and service architecture for multi-schema extraction. Use in production systems where output validation failures must be handled gracefully and where batch extraction of diverse data types is needed. Value comes from fault tolerance, 99%+ reliability, and efficient handling of complex extraction scenarios.

methodologies.name: tool-use-basics
methodologies.description: Tool use enables LLMs to call external functions, APIs, databases, and execute code as part of their reasoning process. Apply this when building AI agents that need access to real-time data, precise calculations, or system interactions like file management. Value delivered includes dynamic task adaptation, access to live information beyond training data, and the ability to perform verifiable real-world actions.

domains.description: ML/AI orchestrator coordinating five specialized sub-skills: LLM integration, RAG systems, ML operations, autonomous agents, and multimodal AI. Routes AI/ML tasks based on goal (API integration, RAG, agents, fine-tuning, vision/speech). Handles LLM provider selection, vector database decisions, and deployment strategies.

methodologies.name: agentic-rag
methodologies.description: Agentic RAG combines autonomous agents with retrieval-augmented generation, using agents to iteratively refine queries, grade document relevance, and self-correct outputs. Use when traditional RAG produces hallucinations or fails on complex multi-document queries requiring reasoning across sources. Value comes from 20-30% improvement in answer accuracy and ability to handle ambiguous queries requiring clarification.

methodologies.name: agents-framework-selection
methodologies.description: Solves the framework lock-in problem at the start of an agent project — LangGraph state schemas, CrewAI role configs, and AutoGen conversation patterns are not portable, and switching after 50 percent implementation is expensive — by mapping the consolidated 2025-2026 Python ecosystem (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, LlamaIndex, DSPy, smolagents, controlflow) onto primary production patterns: state machines, role-based teams, conversational debate, data-heavy retrieval, automatic prompt optimization. The rule is to default to LangGraph for production state machines (explicit TypedDict state, checkpointing, conditional routing, LangSmith tracing) unless the use case is explicitly role-based (CrewAI) or conversational multi-agent (AutoGen). Apply it before writing any agent code on a new project, when migrating from an ad-hoc LangChain loop, when adding multi-agent patterns, or when picking observability tooling. The methodology pairs framework choice with mandatory observability (LangSmith or Langfuse from day one), sandboxed execution (E2B), and durable workflow (Temporal) so non-deterministic failures remain debuggable in production.

methodologies.name: agents-memory-system
methodologies.description: Addresses context overflow — one of the three most common autonomous agent failures alongside infinite loops and tool hallucination — by replacing 'dump everything in the system prompt' with a three-tier memory split that mirrors human working memory: short-term FIFO buffer of the last 10 to 20 exchanges, long-term vector store (Qdrant, Chroma, pgvector) of compressed facts and episodes recalled by cosine similarity above a 0.7 threshold with top-k of 3 to 5, and an episodic task-outcome log used by planners to avoid previously failed strategies. Conversation history MUST be summarised every 10 to 20 steps, preserving task-relevant facts and active tool state while dropping redundant exchanges, and recall MUST inject only thresholded top-k results — never all stored memories — to keep the context lean. Apply it to any agent running more than 20 steps per session, recurring-task agents that should learn across runs, multi-session research or content workflows exceeding 50K tokens, or anything that must remember user preferences across sessions; skip for single-turn agents under 10 steps and strict-reproducibility audits where non-deterministic recall conflicts with auditing. The practical payoff is preventing silent context-limit truncation and the loop-because-it-forgot pattern.

methodologies.name: agents-production-deployment
methodologies.description: Defines the minimal production floor for autonomous agents — five components that prevent five distinct classes of failure that are trivial to avoid up front but expensive to debug post-hoc: a typed BaseTool class with shared ToolRegistry and OpenAI/Anthropic format adapters, YAML configuration with environment-variable substitution covering agent type, LLM provider, memory, tools, safety, and logging, JSON structured logging that emits every LLM call (model, tokens) and tool call (name, args, result preview) with agent_id and iteration fields, exponential-backoff retry with jitter plus a circuit breaker (closed → open → half-open) for external services, and a repeatable evaluation harness measuring success rate, latency, iterations, and token cost against a fixed test suite of TaskCase objects. Apply it when moving an agent from prototype to production, scheduled or external-request agents, anything user-visible or business-critical, or when measuring quality regressions over time; skip for one-off research and notebook prototypes with a human watching every step. The practical value is that agent failures are non-deterministic and only debuggable when traces, logs, and a regression gate exist before the failures happen.

methodologies.name: agents-react-pattern
methodologies.description: Implements the canonical Reason+Act loop introduced in Yao et al. 2210.03629: the LLM emits a THOUGHT, takes an ACTION (tool call or final answer), receives the OBSERVATION, and repeats until completion or an iteration cap, interleaving reasoning with action so the agent can adjust after each tool result rather than committing to a fixed plan. Every ReAct agent MUST set a hard iteration limit (20-50 steps), a per-run cost cap, a same-argument deduplication guard (max 3 calls with identical args), and tool descriptions that include parameter names, types, and return format; tools MUST return text errors rather than raise exceptions because agents recover from text far better than from stack traces. Use it for exploratory multi-step tasks, research-plus-synthesis-plus-action workflows that cannot be scripted, code-test-fix loops, multi-source gathering with unknown source count, and variable-length knowledge work; skip for deterministic pipelines (use a DAG), sub-500ms latency budgets, irreversible actions without a human gate (use Plan-and-Execute), or tight cost budgets where ReAct's 5-20x token expansion is unaffordable. ReAct is roughly 50 percent cheaper than Plan-and-Execute on exploratory tasks but requires the iteration and cost guards to avoid runaway loops.

methodologies.name: agents-safety-guardrails
methodologies.description: Implements five categories of guardrail every autonomous agent needs before production deployment because LLM instructions in the system prompt alone are not reliable safety mechanisms: execution safety (max_iterations 20-50, timeout_seconds 60-300, max_tool_calls_per_iteration of 5, same-argument deduplication after 3 identical calls), content safety (prompt-injection validation, PII filtering before logging, output moderation), financial safety (cost caps, rate limiting), action safety (JSON-schema validation of tool inputs, text-only error returns, sandboxed code execution via E2B/Docker/resource-limited subprocess — never exec on the host), and human-in-loop approval gates with APPROVE/MODIFY/DECLINE/ESCALATE options before sending external messages, financial transactions, deletes, production writes, or write-access shell commands. Apply it before any agent reaches production, especially agents with external API/database/filesystem/comms access, code-execution agents, multi-step workflows where wrong intermediate results cascade, and agents handling user data or PII; skip only for read-only research agents and human-supervised sandbox exploration. The methodology specifically addresses the six most common agent production failures (infinite loops, tool hallucination, context overflow, error propagation, cost explosion, unauthorized irreversible actions) plus the catastrophic recursive-self-call pattern (guard with depth counter, raise at depth >= 3) and LangGraph deadlocks from nodes that raise without fallback edges.

methodologies.name: ai-agent-patterns
methodologies.description: AI agent patterns including ReAct, Chain-of-Thought, Plan-Execute, and Tree-of-Thoughts provide structured approaches for autonomous reasoning and task completion. Apply to complex multi-step problems, dynamic environments requiring adaptation, or tasks where visible reasoning is needed for verification. Value comes from 30-50% accuracy improvements for reasoning tasks and clear, auditable decision paths.

methodologies.name: ai-governance-compliance
methodologies.description: AI governance frameworks ensure AI systems are safe, compliant, ethical, and properly audited across model governance, audit trails, responsible AI, and data handling. Apply in regulated industries, enterprises managing multiple AI systems, or organizations needing board-level AI oversight. Value includes regulatory compliance avoiding 7% revenue fines, systematic bias detection preventing PR crises, and centralized model lifecycle management.

methodologies.name: chain-of-thought
methodologies.description: Chain-of-Thought prompting breaks complex reasoning into explicit intermediate steps, improving accuracy on math, logic, and multi-step problems. Apply to any task requiring visible reasoning: mathematical word problems, code debugging, complex decisions. Value comes from 15-40% accuracy improvements, ability to detect and correct errors in reasoning, and transparent decision paths for audit.

methodologies.name: chunking-strategies
methodologies.description: Chunking strategies optimize how documents are split for RAG systems, balancing semantic coherence with retrieval effectiveness. Apply when RAG accuracy is insufficient or context is wasted on irrelevant text. Value comes from 30-40% retrieval precision improvements, reduced token waste per query (enabling larger context windows), and better answer relevance.

methodologies.name: claude-api
methodologies.description: Claude API provides access to Anthropic's Claude models with extended thinking, tool use, vision, and prompt caching for long-context reasoning. Use for tasks requiring deep analysis, long document processing, or complex multi-step problem solving where reasoning transparency matters. Value includes 200K token context (5x GPT-4), 90% caching savings on repeated prompts, and superior reasoning for code/analysis.

methodologies.name: cost-optimization
methodologies.description: Cost optimization strategies including model routing, prompt caching, token reduction, and batching reduce LLM costs 60-90% while maintaining quality. Apply in production systems where cost scales with usage: customer support, content generation, batch processing. Value comes from reducing per-request cost 10-100x depending on strategy combination, enabling profitable AI products.

methodologies.name: decision-framework
methodologies.description: Decision framework helps select the right approach (prompting vs RAG vs fine-tuning) and model for AI tasks based on capability needs and budget constraints. Apply before building any AI feature to avoid over-engineering or poor cost efficiency. Value lies in 40-60% cost savings through correct approach selection and 15-25% performance gains through proper model-to-task matching.

methodologies.name: embedding-generation
methodologies.description: Embedding generation converts text to dense vectors for semantic search, with guidance on model selection, batching, caching, and optimization. Use for RAG systems, similarity search, clustering, and recommendation systems where semantic understanding matters more than keyword matching. Value comes from 30-50% higher retrieval recall than keyword search, enabling semantic deduplication saving 40% storage.

methodologies.name: embeddings-batch-and-cache
methodologies.description: Combines two complementary optimisations that together cut embedding costs by roughly 90 percent on real workloads with no quality loss: batching reduces per-call API overhead up to 10x by sending up to the provider limit (OpenAI 2048 texts, Voyage 128, Cohere varies) per request, and caching with a sha256(model:text) key — Redis with pipeline operations and 30-day TTL in production, SQLite for local dev — eliminates repeated computation when an 80 percent cache-hit rate yields 80 percent cost savings. Apply it to large corpus indexing (thousands to millions of documents), chat or search apps with repeated queries, cost-sensitive pipelines, and high-throughput pipelines where per-call latency would block progress; skip for tiny corpora under 100 documents and unique never-repeating streams. The methodology mandates sorting batch results by the index field on response objects (not by response order), retrying only failed batches with exponential backoff plus jitter on rate limits, and including the model identifier in the cache key to enforce automatic invalidation on model upgrades. Additional levers include Matryoshka dimension reduction to 256d (3-5% loss), INT8 quantization (4x smaller storage, less than 1 percent loss on Voyage and BGE-M3), and TF-IDF-based deduplication above 0.95 cosine similarity.

methodologies.name: embeddings-evaluation
methodologies.description: Solves the gap between MTEB leaderboard scores and real-world performance — domain-specific corpora (legal, medical, code, enterprise) routinely diverge from MTEB by 5-15 percent and can even reverse model rankings — by mandating domain-specific evaluation on 100 to 500 real query-document pairs drawn from query logs and labeled by domain experts, with edge cases (short, long, multilingual, rare entities, acronyms) and balanced relevance distributions. The procedure is: build the evaluation set, shortlist 3-5 candidates from MTEB plus use-case fit, embed corpus and queries with each, measure Recall@1/5/10 (target Recall@10 above 0.7), MRR (target above 0.5), nDCG@10, and p50/p95 latency, calculate cost at expected monthly volume, then run a one-week production A/B test with calculated sample size, business metrics (CTR, satisfaction, completion), and a rollback plan that keeps old vectors until A/B gates pass. Apply it whenever selecting an embedding model, evaluating a switch, diagnosing poor production retrieval, or validating a model update before re-embedding the full corpus. Without it, model selection is guesswork and synthetic LLM-generated query benchmarks inflate recall numbers because they match training distributions.

methodologies.name: embeddings-model-selection
methodologies.description: Routes the 2025-2026 text-embedding model choice across proprietary providers (OpenAI text-embedding-3-large/small, Voyage voyage-4-large/voyage-3.5/voyage-context-3, Cohere embed-v4 and multilingual-v3.0, Mistral) and open-source models (BGE-M3, Qwen3-Embedding, nomic-embed-text-v2, bge-large-en-v1.5, e5-large-v2, all-MiniLM-L6-v2, EmbeddingGemma) by matching use case (RAG, search, clustering, classification), constraints (budget, latency under 10ms, on-prem privacy, language coverage, GPU availability), and provider capabilities (Matryoshka native dimension reduction, INT8/binary quantization, asymmetric input_type for query vs document). Use it when starting a new semantic-search or RAG project, evaluating a switch, choosing between cloud API and self-hosted, or optimizing an existing system; skip for keyword lookups (BM25 wins), tiny corpora under 500 docs, highly specialized domains without an adapted model, and real-time streams where batch latency blocks the pipeline. Two non-negotiable rules: always benchmark candidates on YOUR domain data because MTEB rankings can reverse, and always set input_type 'document' for indexing and 'query' for retrieval on Voyage and Cohere — omitting it drops recall 3-8 percent. The wrong choice costs 3-8 percent retrieval quality or 10x unnecessary spend, and any mid-stream model swap requires full corpus re-embedding because mixed-model vector indexes produce meaningless similarity scores.

methodologies.name: embeddings-production-ops
methodologies.description: Hardens embedding pipelines against the silent failure modes that degrade quality without raising exceptions — embedding drift (mixing old and new model vectors in the same index), silent token truncation at the model limit, rate-limit throttling that corrupts batch order, and missing monitoring until cost spikes — by setting up monitoring on day one (request/response with model identifier, error rate, p50/p95/p99 latency, per-request cost, monthly projection, cache-hit rate, dimension consistency), production-readiness checks (stateless design, auto-scaling, health checks, client-side rate limiting with exponential backoff plus jitter, circuit breakers, fallback model, keyword degradation path), and security controls (env-var secrets, monthly key rotation, encrypted storage, vector-DB access controls, PII review). Apply it when launching a new pipeline, debugging poor production retrieval, planning a model migration, optimizing costs as volume grows, or hardening a prototype. The methodology mandates an eight-step model migration procedure (benchmark on eval data, staging shadow traffic, rollback plan with old index retained, full corpus re-embed because no partial migration is possible, A/B traffic split, post-cutover monitoring, documentation, decommission) and explicit rules: never mix embeddings from different model versions, always count tokens before embedding to avoid silent truncation, always use jittered exponential backoff to dodge thundering-herd retries.

methodologies.name: embeddings-provider-apis
methodologies.description: Provides production-ready integration patterns for the four main embedding provider categories — OpenAI (symmetric, Matryoshka dimensions, batch up to 2048, async with semaphore concurrency), Voyage AI (asymmetric input_type, Matryoshka, INT8/binary quantization, voyage-context-3 for contextualized chunks), Cohere (multimodal embed-v4 with text+images in same vector space, 128K context, multilingual, search_document/search_query/classification/clustering input types), local sentence-transformers (free, GPU-accelerated, 15,000+ HuggingFace models, BGE-M3 with native dense+sparse for hybrid search), and Mistral — each with distinct client setup and capabilities. Apply it when building a new embedding pipeline and needing provider-specific code, implementing asymmetric search, adding GPU acceleration or quantization for storage savings, or integrating multimodal embeddings; use litellm instead if you need a unified abstraction. Three mandatory rules: same model version for indexing and query (a minor version bump can require full re-indexing), always count tokens before embedding because silent truncation at the model limit changes semantic meaning without raising errors, and L2-normalize embeddings before storage so cosine similarity equals dot product (2-3x faster ANN). Common mistakes include caching with text-only keys across model versions, embedding raw HTML/markdown, and forgetting input_type='query' which costs 3-8 percent recall.

methodologies.name: eu-ai-act-compliance
methodologies.description: EU AI Act compliance framework provides risk classification, documentation requirements, and enforcement timeline (full compliance August 2026) for European AI deployment. Apply if you serve EU markets or need to manage regulatory risk. Value comes from avoiding 3-7% revenue fines, systematic compliance processes, and competitive advantage in regulated AI markets.

methodologies.name: fine-tuning-lora
methodologies.description: LoRA and variants (QLoRA, DoRA) enable efficient fine-tuning using adapter layers instead of full model updates, reducing memory 10-20x. Use when you need model customization but lack enterprise hardware; enables 70B models on consumer GPUs. Value comes from 90-95% of full fine-tuning quality at 10-15% of GPU memory cost, enabling rapid experimentation.
