methodologies.name: fine-tuning-openai-data-prep
methodologies.description: Treats training-data quality as the primary determinant of fine-tuning success — a JSONL file with 10 percent noisy examples degrades the entire run, malformed JSONL fails only at job start (not at upload), and silent token truncation per example over 4K wastes API time and budget. The methodology covers JSONL format requirements (top-level messages key, list of role/content objects, at least one user and one assistant message per example), pre-upload validation (JSON syntax, required keys, message structure, role presence), tiktoken-based token counting with cost estimation across gpt-4.1/4.1-mini/4.1-nano price tiers, CSV-to-JSONL conversion for tabular sources, a fluent TrainingDataBuilder for SFT and DPO, and a TrainingDataValidator that produces structured ValidationResult objects with errors, warnings, duplicate detection, and stats. Apply it before every fine-tuning upload, when building data from CSV/database/annotations, when assessing dataset coverage/balance/duplicates/PII, and when generating examples programmatically from documents; skip for already-validated data and OpenAI Playground builds. Minimums: 10 examples to start, 50-100+ recommended, 10-20 percent validation split, under 4K tokens per example, identical system-prompt wording across examples, and PII scrubbed before upload to satisfy DPA and GDPR.

methodologies.name: fine-tuning-openai-deployment
methodologies.description: Defines the production deployment workflow for OpenAI fine-tuned models because fine-tuned inference costs are 2-3x base-model rates, agent failures are non-deterministic, and gradual rollout is the only way to limit blast radius. The methodology supplies a FineTuningPipeline class (logging, error handling, train/val split, async polling), a FineTuneJobManager (list, retrieve, get_events for loss values, cancel, list_models, delete_model to free storage), and a CLI for upload/train/status/list/cancel; deployment proceeds in phases — pre-production smoke tests, staging rollout, gradual 10 → 50 → 100 percent traffic shift with rollback on error-rate increase or quality regression, then post-launch cost and quality monitoring. Apply it when a fine-tuned model has passed quality gates, when managing multiple jobs, when integrating async polling into agentic workflows, and when planning model lifecycle (versioning, retraining triggers, storage cleanup); skip until evaluation gates pass and for experimental jobs that should stay ephemeral. Critical agent gotchas: fine-tuning jobs run 15-120 minutes so polling must be async at 60s intervals, a failed job returns 200 OK on creation so always check job.status not HTTP, list_events is the only training-progress signal, model IDs change on every retrain so they MUST live in config files (never hardcoded), and a human checkpoint before live traffic catches the failures automated metrics miss.

methodologies.name: fine-tuning-openai-dpo
methodologies.description: Direct Preference Optimization trains OpenAI gpt-4.1, gpt-4.1-mini, and gpt-4.1-nano models on JSONL preference pairs — input, preferred_output, non_preferred_output — using a contrastive loss that teaches the model to score the preferred response higher than the non-preferred response, aligning behaviour to subjective criteria like tone, formality, brand voice, or safety guardrails without a separate reward model as in RLHF. Apply DPO after SFT (never on a base model directly because the reference is unstable) when the optimization target is subjective rather than a clear correct answer, for safety refinements that prefer cautious policy-compliant responses over direct unsafe ones, or when human raters reliably prefer one response over another; skip when clear correct answers exist (use SFT), when fewer than 50 quality pairs are available, or for non-supported model families like gpt-4o. Two critical rules: non_preferred responses MUST be realistic and reasonably high-quality (generate them from the base model, not fabricate nonsense, otherwise the model only learns coherent vs incoherent), and the beta hyperparameter MUST be in 0.1-0.5 (default 0.1) — too low lets the model drift far from SFT and catastrophically degrade factual accuracy, too high prevents change. Always regression-test on SFT-quality tasks before deploying.

methodologies.name: fine-tuning-openai-eval
methodologies.description: Mandates evaluating every fine-tuned OpenAI model against the base model on a truly held-out test set before any production traffic is routed, because training-loss curves measure fit not utility, and out-of-distribution behaviour is unpredictable. Quality gates require test accuracy above baseline, format compliance above 95 percent for structured-output tasks, human preference rate above 60 percent in blind A/B comparison, and no regressions on prior held-out cases; all evaluation calls MUST use temperature=0 for deterministic comparison. The methodology supplies a ModelEvaluator using GPT-4o as LLM-as-judge with a 1-5 rubric, an A/B compare_models helper that pits base against fine-tuned with a judge model picking the winner per query, and standard prompts for general quality, format compliance, factual accuracy, and failure analysis. Apply it after every fine-tuning job, when deciding to add epochs or data, when comparing candidate hyperparameters, and for periodic regression testing after retraining. Three antipatterns to avoid: evaluating only on the training distribution (overestimates production quality), using the fine-tuned model as its own judge (it favours its own style), and skipping the human checkpoint that catches subjective failures — inappropriate tone, subtle safety violations, technically-correct-but-user-hostile outputs — that automated metrics miss.

methodologies.name: fine-tuning-openai-sft
methodologies.description: Supervised Fine-Tuning customises OpenAI gpt-4.1, gpt-4.1-mini, gpt-4.1-nano, gpt-4o, and gpt-4o-mini by training on JSONL input-output pairs; the entire lifecycle runs over the REST API with no GPU management, and training cost equals tokens times epochs times the per-token training price ($1-$25 per million tokens depending on model). Apply SFT when clear correct answers exist with 50+ high-quality labelled examples, for domain-specific tasks where the base model underperforms (medical coding, legal extraction, brand voice), to internalise long repeated system prompts and shorten inference, for consistent structured output at high call volume, or when prompt engineering alone cannot reach the quality bar; skip when fewer than 50 examples, real-time data is needed (use RAG), private/on-prem weights are required (OpenAI keeps the weights), requirements iterate within weeks (retrain cost is prohibitive), or call volume is low (the inference premium does not pay off). Two non-negotiable rules: every example MUST be a JSON object with a top-level messages key containing at least one user and one assistant message with consistent system-prompt wording, and you MUST upload a validation file (10 percent split minimum) because without it overfitting is invisible — train-vs-validation loss divergence is the primary signal. Use 3 epochs as baseline, store the returned ft:gpt-... model ID in config alongside the training-data hash (never hardcoded), and remember a failed job still returns 200 OK on creation so always check job.status.

methodologies.name: finetuning
methodologies.description: LLM fine-tuning decision framework and technique comparison helps determine whether to fine-tune, when to use alternatives like RAG, and which framework to choose. Apply before any fine-tuning project to validate necessity and select optimal approach. Value comes from avoiding expensive fine-tuning when cheaper solutions exist, and 40-60% faster training through framework selection.

methodologies.name: gemini-api
methodologies.description: Google Gemini API provides multimodal AI capabilities with native support for images, video, audio, and PDFs up to 2M token context. Use it when you need advanced vision understanding, real-time voice processing, or highly cost-effective inference on complex tasks. Delivers superior performance on medical imaging and multimodal tasks while maintaining low latency and offering context caching for 75% cost savings.

methodologies.name: graph-rag
methodologies.description: GraphRAG combines knowledge graphs with vector retrieval to answer questions requiring entity relationships and multi-hop reasoning, excelling where standard RAG struggles. Deploy it when questions need to understand how entities connect, find global themes across documents, or reason through multiple relationships. Improves multi-hop accuracy by 62% and hallucination reduction by 40% compared to pure vector search.

methodologies.name: guardrails-ai-framework
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of forcing LLMs to return data that conforms to a typed schema with automatic retry-and-fix on validation failure. Apply it for structured data extraction with Pydantic models or RAIL XML specs, for output validation pipelines that compose multiple validators (toxicity, PII, length, format) using Guard().use_many(), and when you want to lean on the Hub ecosystem of 50+ pre-built validators rather than writing custom classifiers. The practical value is the simplest path from raw LLM JSON to typed application objects, with code-first custom validators that follow the same interface as Hub validators and a fix_value mechanism that detects and repairs violations in a single pass. Cap max_reasks (typically 3) and pair it with a separate input-injection layer, since Guardrails AI is primarily output-focused.

methodologies.name: guardrails-concepts
methodologies.description: Provides the conceptual foundation for controlling LLM behavior at runtime without retraining the model — the rail types, strategies, and architecture patterns common to every guardrail framework. Apply it before choosing a specific framework to map requirements onto the five rail positions (input, output, dialog, retrieval, execution) and to pick a strategy per rail (block, filter, transform, warn, log). The practical value is a shared vocabulary for safety design: which rails belong in customer-facing apps, regulated domains, agent pipelines and PII-handling systems, and where guardrails are inappropriate (sub-50ms latency budgets, internal tooling, prototyping). Use it to scope the safety envelope before committing to NeMo, Guardrails AI, or a custom pipeline.

methodologies.name: guardrails-custom-pipeline
methodologies.description: Solves the case where neither NeMo nor Guardrails AI fits — the team needs precise control over latency, no framework dependency, or seamless integration with FastAPI, LangChain, or LlamaIndex middleware. Apply it when validation rules are too specific for off-the-shelf validators, when tiered checking (regex first, LLM-as-judge last) is required to stay inside a tight budget, or when guardrails must compose as runnables in an existing stack. The canonical pattern is a GuardrailsPipeline class that combines four independent components — PIIDetector with regex masking, PromptInjectionDetector with a pattern library, ContentModerator backed by the OpenAI moderation API, and an optional HallucinationDetector — exposed through process_input and process_output methods. The practical value is single-purpose, independently testable components that can be swapped (local classifier instead of OpenAI moderation) and composed into only the checks each pipeline actually needs.

methodologies.name: guardrails-nemo
methodologies.description: Solves dialog-flow control for complex multi-turn conversations where regex and output validators cannot capture the required state machine — order flows, conditional branching, escalation paths, persona enforcement. Apply it for enterprise deployments needing auditable, version-controlled policy, fact-checking against a knowledge base, or multi-agent systems where each agent has its own dialog rail. NeMo Guardrails uses the Colang DSL plus YAML config (loaded via RailsConfig.from_path), with built-in jailbreak detection and fact-checking as first-class rails, and integrates with LangChain, LangGraph, and LlamaIndex on any LLM (best performance on NVIDIA NIM). The practical value is declarative state-machine dialog control that separates policy from code; trade-offs are the Colang learning curve, heavier setup than schema validators, and a runtime LLM dependency, so it is overkill for single-turn APIs or pure schema enforcement.

methodologies.name: guardrails-testing
methodologies.description: Solves the silent-failure problem: guardrails that have not been red-teamed fail against known attack patterns, and a 2% false-positive rate blocks 1 in 50 valid requests. Apply it before any production deployment, monthly afterward to test new jailbreak and injection techniques, after configuration or model updates, and when investigating false-positive reports. The methodology requires three distinct test suites — security (adversarial injection payloads that must be blocked), accuracy (legitimate content that must pass), and performance (latency and throughput under load) — with parametrized pytest cases per component, mocked LLM clients to keep CI fast and cheap, and LLM-assisted prompts to generate adversarial inputs. The practical value is a regression baseline that makes guardrail quality measurable and prevents trust-eroding false positives or undetected bypasses.

methodologies.name: hybrid-search
methodologies.description: Hybrid search combines keyword matching (BM25) with semantic vector search to maximize retrieval quality by capturing both exact terms and conceptual meaning. Use it for technical documentation, legal search, and domain-specific Q&A where queries mix codes, names, and natural language. Achieves 87% relevant document precision in top 10, a 42% improvement over pure vector search.

methodologies.name: image-generation
methodologies.description: AI image generation creates visuals from text descriptions using models like DALL-E 3, Stable Diffusion, or Flux for marketing, design, and prototyping. Use it for on-brand asset generation, rapid UI mockups, or creative exploration where consistency and customization matter. Best models handle text rendering, anatomy, and prompt adherence, costing $0.014-0.12 per image depending on quality.

methodologies.name: langchain
methodologies.description: LangChain orchestrates multi-step AI workflows with composable chains and agentic patterns using LCEL syntax and LangGraph for state management. Choose it for building production agents with tool calling, RAG pipelines, and multi-agent systems when you need explicit control flow and observability. Supports streaming, retry logic, and works with any LLM provider via a unified interface.

methodologies.name: llamaindex
methodologies.description: LlamaIndex specializes in building document-centric RAG and agentic systems with 1500+ data connectors and multiple index types from vector stores to knowledge graphs. Adopt it for enterprise search, contract analysis, or research assistants when you have rich, varied data sources and need sophisticated document parsing and indexing. Superior to LangChain for RAG specifically, offering LlamaParse for complex PDFs.

methodologies.name: llm-decision-framework
methodologies.description: This framework guides you to choose between prompting, RAG, fine-tuning, and hybrid approaches based on requirements, ROI, and constraints. Use it when deciding architecture for a new project: prompt engineering is free and immediate, RAG handles dynamic data, fine-tuning adds domain expertise but requires retraining. Provides cost and complexity analysis to match solution to problem.

methodologies.name: llm-observability
methodologies.description: LLM observability tracks token usage, cost, output quality, hallucination rates, and reasoning steps across your AI application stack. Implement it to monitor production systems, debug prompt performance, track costs by user/team, and evaluate output relevance beyond traditional APM metrics. Platforms like Langfuse, LangSmith, and Helicone provide tracing, cost analytics, and LLM-as-judge evaluation.

methodologies.name: llm-observability-stack
methodologies.description: A complete observability infrastructure combines tracing (Langfuse), cost analytics (Helicone), quality evaluation (Phoenix), and alerting (Prometheus) with OpenTelemetry standards for LLM applications. Build it to handle non-deterministic outputs, monitor multi-turn conversations, track agent reasoning, and prevent cost overruns. 2026 trend: OTEL-native SDKs consolidate vendor fragmentation.

methodologies.name: mcp-architecture
methodologies.description: Solves the N-times-M integration problem where every LLM host (Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot, custom agents) historically needed a custom integration per tool. Apply it when exposing internal tools to multiple AI clients simultaneously, building reusable tool servers, providing app-controlled (not model-controlled) context, or enabling sampling so servers can call back through the host without embedding API keys. The Model Context Protocol is an open standard (JSON-RPC 2.0 over stateful sessions, currently spec 2025-11-25, governed by the Linux Foundation) with a host/client/server architecture and three primitive types — Tools (model-controlled), Resources (app-controlled), Prompts (user-controlled) — over stdio, HTTP+SSE, WebSockets, or Streamable HTTP. The practical value is one server implementation working across all hosts unchanged, with clean security boundaries; skip it for one or two hardcoded tools, sub-millisecond latency budgets, or tool surfaces that change too often for client restarts.

methodologies.name: mcp-client-integration
methodologies.description: Solves the bridge problem between an AI model and an MCP tool server: capability negotiation, clean reconnection, user-consent gates before sensitive operations, and structured handling of tool successes and errors. Apply it when building a custom agent that connects programmatically to MCP servers, configuring Claude Code or Claude Desktop to register local or remote servers, reading resources (files, schemas) from a server, or orchestrating multiple servers from one host. Implementation uses the MCP Python or TypeScript SDK with stdio_client and ClientSession, listing tools with session.list_tools and forwarding tool calls — the model sees MCP tools exactly like native SDK tools, so no special handling is required. Skip the client code entirely when the host already manages connections (just edit the host JSON config) or when an inline tool definition for a single agent is enough.

methodologies.name: mcp-dev-prompts
methodologies.description: Solves the recurring cost of detail-sensitive MCP tasks — tool schema design, JSON-RPC message construction, security validation, multi-server architecture decisions — by encoding spec 2025-11-25 constraints, error code conventions, and security best practices into reusable LLM prompt templates. Apply it when scaffolding a new server, adding a tool to an existing one, debugging connection or execution failures, designing a multi-server architecture across domains, performing a pre-production security review, or generating tests and documentation. The library covers the full lifecycle (server creation, tool addition, resource design, debugging, architecture review, testing, docs) so the LLM produces protocol-correct code rather than plausible-looking code that violates the spec. These are scaffolding prompts, not refactoring prompts — skip them when you already have working MCP code.

methodologies.name: mcp-security
methodologies.description: Solves the arbitrary-code-execution risk inherent in MCP tools — without explicit consent, sanitization, and access controls, a malicious resource (a database row or web page returned as a tool result) can hijack the model with prompt injection, the model can chain tools to escalate permissions, or a lookalike server can substitute for a trusted one at install time. Apply it before deploying any MCP server with write access (file writes, DB mutations, API POSTs), when building a host that enforces policy across servers, when exposing a server over HTTP (which requires auth, unlike stdio), and during pre-production security reviews. The methodology codifies four core principles — user consent, data privacy, tool safety, controlled LLM sampling — plus defenses for the known April 2025 attack vectors (prompt injection via tool responses, permission escalation, lookalike replacement, credential leakage in tool descriptions). The practical value is a checklist that turns safety from an afterthought into enforced gates: separate read from write tools, sanitize tool outputs, verify provenance, pin versions, and never log credentials in tool definitions.

methodologies.name: mcp-server-implementation
methodologies.description: Solves the implementation step of MCP — building tool servers that work unchanged across Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot, and custom Claude SDK agents. Apply it when building a reusable tool server shared by multiple AI clients, exposing database, filesystem, or external API access without hardcoded tool code in each agent, or creating Claude Code agents (~/.claude/agents/) that need domain-specific tools. Implementation uses either the official TypeScript SDK (McpServer with Zod schemas for type-safe tool definitions, StdioServerTransport by default) or the Python SDK (FastMCP for rapid decorator-driven development, mcp.server for low-level control), with stdio as the default transport and HTTP+SSE for multi-client scenarios. The practical value is the same protocol surface as a FastAPI endpoint after FastMCP removes JSON-RPC boilerplate; skip it for one- or two-tool prototypes (inline SDK tools are simpler) and avoid HTTP transport without an auth layer, since stdio trusts the local process by default.

methodologies.name: model-evaluation
methodologies.description: Model evaluation systematically measures LLM performance across quality, latency, cost, and safety using benchmarks like MMLU, HumanEval, and LLM-as-judge scoring. Implement it before production deployment to compare models, tune prompts, and monitor degradation. Key metrics: accuracy, faithfulness, relevance, and hallucination rate with automated assessment on standard benchmarks.

methodologies.name: multi-agent-design-patterns
methodologies.description: Design patterns for multi-agent systems enable specialized agents to collaborate through supervisor routing, hierarchical decomposition, sequential pipelines, or peer-to-peer coordination. Choose pattern based on problem structure: supervisor for request routing, hierarchical for complex goal breakdown, sequential for deterministic workflows, peer-to-peer for resilience. Gartner reports 1,445% surge in enterprise multi-agent interest.

methodologies.name: multi-agent-systems
methodologies.description: Multi-agent systems coordinate multiple LLM agents to solve complex tasks through LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, or OpenAI Swarm frameworks. Select framework by control needs: LangGraph for explicit state management, CrewAI for role-based teams, AutoGen for conversational collaboration. Requires attention to state management, observability, guardrails, and timeout handling in production.

methodologies.name: ollama-agent-integration
methodologies.description: Solves the cost and privacy problem in agent code by exposing Ollama through an OpenAI-compatible endpoint at http://localhost:11434/v1, so existing agents using the OpenAI SDK only need a base_url change. Apply it for privacy-sensitive sub-agents that must not make external calls, zero-cost local CI test agents that mock cloud LLM behavior, agent prototyping before committing to provider pricing, and hybrid setups with health-check-driven cloud fallback when the local server is down or the task needs frontier quality. It works with any OpenAI SDK consumer including LangChain, LlamaIndex, Instructor, and Guidance, and integrates with LiteLLM, Langfuse, and Qdrant. Skip it for tasks needing frontier-quality reasoning, broad multilingual coverage, or real-time latency on small hardware, since CPU inference runs 5-20x slower than GPU and even 70B local models trail frontier cloud models.

methodologies.name: ollama-deployment
methodologies.description: Solves the production failure modes that appear when Ollama is run without proper infrastructure — models reloading from disk on every request (5-30s startup), no rate limiting (one slow request blocks all others), no model persistence across container restarts, GPU not engaged so it falls back to CPU at 5-20x slower speed, and no health monitoring. Apply it when deploying Ollama as a shared service for multiple users, running it in Docker or Kubernetes, operating bare-metal with systemd, fronting it with a reverse proxy that needs auth and rate limiting, or shipping custom model variants with fixed system prompts via Modelfiles. The patterns cover Docker Compose with NVIDIA GPU passthrough, persistent volumes, healthchecks, an nginx reverse proxy with rate limiting and streaming support, and a systemd unit for bare-metal installs. Skip it for single-machine local development (ollama serve is simpler) or one-off batch jobs where Docker overhead is not justified.

methodologies.name: ollama-prompt-engineering
methodologies.description: Closes the quality gap between local Ollama models and frontier cloud models, which have higher hallucination rates and weaker instruction-following because they have less capacity to self-correct. Apply it when designing prompts for local deployments that need cloud-equivalent output quality, building NLP-pipeline templates for extraction, classification, or summarization, picking temperature and context settings per task, or managing multi-turn conversation state with local models. The methodology covers role-specific system prompts (general assistant, code assistant, code reviewer), task templates with explicit output-format constraints and clear delimiters, temperature guidelines per task type, context-window management strategies, and chain-of-thought patterns adapted for local models. The practical value is closing the cloud/local quality gap through structure rather than scale; technique overlap with cloud prompts is high but cloud models tolerate vaguer prompts, and over-constraining them can reduce quality.

methodologies.name: ollama-python-client
methodologies.description: Solves the choice-paralysis between three integration paths (raw REST, official ollama Python library, OpenAI-compatible base_url) and the production failures that appear when Ollama is hit without retries, timeouts, or health checks — cascade failures when a model is loading or unavailable. Apply it when writing Python code against a local Ollama instance, running concurrent batch processing with asyncio, streaming output for real-time UX, generating local embeddings for RAG pipelines, or processing images with local vision models. The recommendation is the official library (pip install ollama) for new code, raw REST only when fine-grained HTTP control is needed, plus async patterns for concurrency and a production service class wrapping retries, timeouts, metrics, and health checks. Skip it for cloud deployments where the OpenAI SDK with a base_url override suffices (see ollama-agent-integration), simple one-off scripting where ollama run is faster, or TypeScript/JavaScript frontends.

methodologies.name: ollama-setup-models
methodologies.description: Solves the entry point to running LLMs locally — installing Ollama, managing models, and matching model size to available RAM/VRAM, which is the single most critical decision because a model that does not fit in VRAM falls back to CPU at 5-20x slower inference. Apply it when data privacy rules forbid external API calls (HIPAA, GDPR, corporate policy), in offline or air-gapped environments, for high-volume classification or extraction where zero marginal cost matters, for local development without API keys or rate limits, and to deploy custom fine-tuned models. The methodology covers install commands, GPU support across NVIDIA CUDA, Apple Silicon Metal, and AMD ROCm, the model catalog with VRAM requirements (Llama 3.2 1B-3B at 4-8GB up to Llama 4 400B at 64GB+, plus Gemma 3, Qwen 2.5, Phi 4, Mistral, DeepSeek-R1, and vision models), and the default endpoint at http://localhost:11434. Skip it when frontier-quality reasoning is required, hardware does not meet minimums (7B needs 8GB, 70B needs 48GB), or multilingual coverage beyond about 20 languages is needed.

methodologies.name: ollama-tool-calling
methodologies.description: Solves replacing cloud LLM tool-calling and structured output with a local model in agent architectures, using Ollama's OpenAI-compatible function-calling API and JSON-schema format parameter. Apply it when building local agents that invoke external tools or APIs without cloud cost, extracting structured data with schema validation, replacing cloud LLMs in CI or privacy-sensitive workflows, or testing agent tool logic locally before deploying against cloud models. The hard requirement is Llama 3.1 8B or larger with num_ctx set to at least 32768 — context windows below 32k cause silent failure where the model returns plain text instead of JSON tool calls — and the methodology covers the tool-schema definition, the execution loop, and Pydantic-derived structured outputs. Skip it for models smaller than 13B (tool-calling reliability degrades), complex multi-tool chains where GPT-4o and Claude are significantly more reliable, and deeply nested output schemas where compliance varies by model and must be tested thoroughly.

methodologies.name: openai-api
methodologies.description: OpenAI APIs provide chat completions, vision, function calling, embeddings, audio, and image generation with GPT-4o as the flagship model. Use the newer Responses API for stateful conversations and reasoning models (o1, o3); migrate from Chat Completions if building new applications. Implement structured outputs for reliable JSON, prompt caching for repeated prefixes, and batch API for 50% cost savings.

methodologies.name: prompt-engineering-evaluation
methodologies.description: Solves the silent-degradation problem: prompt quality cannot be assessed by reading the prompt alone, and teams ship prompts that work on three happy-path examples and fail on real traffic. Apply it before any production deployment, after every prompt modification (regression testing), when outputs are inconsistent across runs, when comparing variants in A/B tests, and when production quality degrades and needs to be quantified. The methodology covers a pre-flight checklist (objective, success criteria, input/output formats, edge cases, constraints, capability match, token budget, sanitization, sensitive data handling), a writing-time structural checklist, test-case design with edge and adversarial inputs, quality metrics, evaluation rubrics, A/B comparison frameworks, debugging workflows for incorrect or inconsistent outputs, deployment checklists, and LLM-assisted prompt analysis prompts. The practical value is a regression baseline that makes prompt quality measurable instead of a matter of opinion.

methodologies.name: prompt-engineering-fundamentals
methodologies.description: Solves the 80/20 of prompt quality — inconsistent outputs, wrong formats, missing context, and brittle pipelines almost always trace back to poorly structured prompts that skip the basics. Apply it when an LLM produces inconsistent or wrong-format output, when missing context is hurting accuracy, for any complex reasoning task that needs step-by-step guidance, and in any production system where tested, versioned prompts reduce failures. The methodology codifies six building blocks of an effective prompt — system prompt, context, task, examples, output format, constraints — with the testable rule that every prompt MUST include at minimum a task description and an output-format spec, adding the others only when they measurably improve reliability. It also covers zero-shot vs few-shot selection, model-specific conventions, terminology, and an iterative design workflow. Skip it (or look elsewhere) when the model lacks the capability, the task needs real-time data (use RAG), domain knowledge is the gap (fine-tune), structured output is the only goal (use the API's JSON schema mode), or cost is the dominant constraint.

methodologies.name: prompt-engineering-production
methodologies.description: Solves the silent-degradation of prompts in production — models update, context fills, edge cases emerge, and without versioning you cannot roll back, without retry logic validated outputs fail at the first malformed response, and without structured-output handling the downstream pipeline breaks on every schema deviation. Apply it for any production LLM call where reliability matters, structured-output use cases where the downstream system depends on schema correctness, tool-use and agent architectures, multi-turn agentic workflows with programmatically generated prompts, and pipelines where prompt changes must be reviewable via git diff and revertable. The methodology requires production prompts to live in source control as Python constants, dataclasses, or files (never in dashboards outside the code review path), with a version comment and last-change date, and provides PromptTemplate and PromptManager classes, retry-with-escalation logic, Pydantic-based structured-output handling, ReAct agent scaffolding, function-calling templates, and agentic workflow patterns. The practical value is treating prompts as code with the same engineering discipline as application logic.

methodologies.name: prompt-engineering-reasoning
methodologies.description: Solves the early-commitment failure of LLMs on complex tasks — without explicit reasoning structure, models skip steps, conflate assumptions, and produce wrong answers to math, multi-step logic, decision analysis, and code-review problems. Apply it for tasks with sequential logic where intermediate steps matter, decision analysis with trade-offs, debugging where cause-and-effect reasoning is required, auditable high-stakes outputs (legal, medical, financial), and cases where reliability outweighs latency or cost. The methodology covers chain-of-thought (anchored to Wei et al. 2022), self-consistency (Wang et al. 2022), critique-and-revise, multi-perspective analysis, tree-of-thought (Yao et al. 2023), ReAct (Yao et al. 2022), and meta-prompting, with the testable rule that CoT prompts MUST separate reasoning from the final answer using explicit XML tags or labeled sections so the model completes all reasoning before committing. Skip CoT on simple well-defined tasks (it just adds tokens and latency), in latency-sensitive real-time pipelines (scratchpads can double or triple response time), and avoid self-consistency in production since multiple samples are expensive — reserve it for offline evaluation.

methodologies.name: prompt-engineering-security
methodologies.description: Addresses prompt injection, jailbreaking, and data exfiltration risks (OWASP LLM01) that arise whenever an LLM consumes user-supplied or external text. Apply it to chatbots, agentic pipelines reading PDFs or web pages, multi-agent systems whose outputs feed each other, and any system whose model outputs trigger real-world actions or touch sensitive data. The methodology layers explicit XML-tag delimiter isolation between system prompt and user input, a SafetyFilter with regex pattern detection (override attempts, role-change, special tokens) plus length truncation, hardened system prompts with scoped fallback responses, optional secondary jailbreak-detection LLM calls, and content-moderation post-filters. Defense-in-depth checklist covers input wrapping, audit logging, rate limiting, anomaly detection, and the special hazards of agent-generated prompts and untrusted external content read by agents.

methodologies.name: rag
methodologies.description: RAG grounds LLM responses in external knowledge by retrieving relevant documents at query time, enabling current information, source citations, and reduced hallucinations. Deploy it when information changes frequently, needs attribution, exceeds context limits, or when cost matters (cheaper than fine-tuning with no retraining needed). Core components: chunking, embedding, vector store, reranking, synthesis.

methodologies.name: rag-evaluation
methodologies.description: RAG evaluation measures retrieval quality (precision, recall, MRR, NDCG) and generation quality (faithfulness, relevance, hallucination rate) using the RAGAS framework or LLM-as-judge. Test both dimensions: retrieval recall predicts available information, generation metrics measure answer accuracy. Implement continuous evaluation to catch pipeline degradation and guide optimization of chunking, embedding models, or rerankers.

methodologies.name: rag-pipeline-design
methodologies.description: RAG pipeline design covers architecture tiers (naive to agentic), component selection (embeddings, vector DB, chunking strategy), and production deployment patterns. Start with naive RAG for simple Q&A, advance to modular RAG for multiple sources, adopt agentic RAG for complex reasoning. Key decisions: Qdrant for self-hosted, Pinecone for managed, semantic chunking for quality, hybrid search for precision.

methodologies.name: reasoning-first-architectures
methodologies.description: Reasoning-first models (OpenAI o3, Claude with Extended Thinking, DeepSeek R1) allocate test-time compute to internal reasoning before visible output, achieving breakthrough performance on complex math, code, and analysis. Use them for problems with multiple solution paths, edge cases, or requiring verification when standard models perform poorly. Costs more per token but delivers o3 at 96.7% MATH-500 vs GPT-4o at 76.6%.

methodologies.name: reranking
methodologies.description: Reranking uses cross-encoders to refine initial retrieval results with more sophisticated relevance scoring, improving precision while accepting higher latency. Add it to production RAG when initial retrieval quality is weak or you need balanced BM25+vector fusion; skip for latency-critical applications under 100ms. Improves RAG accuracy by 20-35% with models like Jina v3, Cohere Rerank, or BGE.

methodologies.name: speech-to-text
methodologies.description: Speech-to-text converts audio to text with models like OpenAI Whisper (99+ languages, $0.006/min) or specialized services like AssemblyAI for diarization. Use it for meeting transcription, podcast processing, voice command recognition, or accessibility when you need accurate multi-language support and flexible latency (batch or real-time). Local deployment via Whisper for privacy or high-volume cost savings.

methodologies.name: structured-output
methodologies.description: Structured output forces LLMs to return JSON-formatted responses that comply with schemas, enabling reliable parsing for APIs and automated workflows. Use native structured output (OpenAI, Gemini) when available for guaranteed schema compliance; fall back to JSON mode or tool calling for other providers. Define schemas with Pydantic for type safety, validation, and IDE support.

methodologies.name: text-to-speech
methodologies.description: Text-to-speech converts written text into natural-sounding audio through AI-powered voice synthesis. Use this when you need to generate spoken content for accessibility, voiceovers, podcasts, or interactive applications. Modern TTS providers like ElevenLabs and OpenAI offer human-quality voices with fine-grained control over tone, speed, and emotion, enabling you to create professional audio content at scale with minimal production overhead.

methodologies.name: tool-use-function-calling
methodologies.description: Tool use and function calling enable LLMs to interact with external systems by generating structured requests that your code then executes, forming the foundation of agentic AI systems. Use this pattern when you need LLMs to make API calls, query databases, or trigger workflows rather than just generate text. This capability transforms models from text generators into action-taking agents that can solve complex multi-step problems by combining reasoning with real-world operations.

methodologies.name: vector-databases
methodologies.description: Vector databases are specialized stores optimized for semantic search by finding similar items based on embedding distance rather than exact matches. Use this to understand which database best fits your use case, from Qdrant's production RAG capabilities to Pinecone's serverless simplicity to pgvector's SQL integration. Choosing the right database at the start prevents costly migrations later and directly impacts your application's search quality, latency, and scaling characteristics.

methodologies.name: vector-db-index-tuning
methodologies.description: Solves the recall, latency, and memory tradeoffs that surface once a vector collection grows past defaults, where a wrong HNSW or quantization choice can mean disk-bound search instead of fitting into RAM. Use it when p95 latency exceeds the SLA, memory approaches the instance limit, recall drops after enabling quantization, or the collection scales beyond 1M vectors; skip it under 100K vectors or on managed services that hide index internals. Provides concrete parameter profiles for HNSW (M, ef_construct, ef_search) per workload (high-recall, balanced, memory-efficient), guidance on scalar/product/binary quantization tradeoffs (4x, 8-32x, 32x memory reduction at 1-2%, 3-5%, 5-10% recall loss), the rule to enable scalar int8 from day one above 500K vectors since retrofitting requires full re-indexing, and engine-specific recipes for Qdrant, pgvector, and Milvus including maintenance_work_mem and IVFFlat avoidance.

methodologies.name: vector-db-monitoring
methodologies.description: Prevents the silent failure modes specific to vector databases — non-linear p99 latency spikes from memory pressure, sudden Qdrant or Weaviate crashes from disk exhaustion, and stale RAG results from sub-second replication lag — by instrumenting the system before users feel the impact. Apply it to any production vector database, when adding one to an existing observability stack, or after collection growth past 1M vectors changes resource patterns; skip for fully managed services with built-in dashboards and dev environments. Defines a Prometheus and Grafana stack with concrete alert thresholds (p95 latency 100/500ms warning/critical, memory and disk 70/85%, replication lag 1/10s), three metric tiers (infrastructure, database, application), four mandatory critical alerts before go-live, and capacity-planning rules including the key insight that vector DBs are memory-bound, not CPU-bound, so auto-scaling must trigger on memory and QPS.

methodologies.name: vector-db-security
methodologies.description: Hardens vector databases against unauthorized access and the often-overlooked semantic leakage where neighbor vectors to a query reveal what a collection contains, even without decoding raw embeddings. Apply it before any deployment touches non-public data, before promoting a dev instance, or when compliance requirements (SOC2, GDPR, HIPAA) mandate audit logging; skip for purely synthetic-data dev work and fully managed providers. Covers per-engine authentication (Qdrant API key, Weaviate AUTHENTICATION_APIKEY, Milvus credentials, pgvector scram-sha-256), TLS enforcement with verified certificates, deployment in private subnets with port-specific security groups (6333/6334, 8080/50051, 19530, 5432) and VPN for admin access, encryption at rest with KMS-managed keys, 90-day key rotation, separate service accounts per pipeline component, and PII handling rules that require stripping sensitive fields from payloads before upsert.

methodologies.name: vector-db-setup-dev
methodologies.description: Eliminates a common RAG bug class — pipeline-logic errors that are actually misconfigured connections, wrong dimensions, or missing indexes — by giving a verified five-minute local baseline against which embedding and retrieval code can be tested deterministically. Use it when starting a new pipeline, evaluating a database before committing, running integration tests that need a real (not mocked) vector store, or migrating off Chroma in-memory; skip for under 10K-document POCs or production deployments. Provides Docker one-liners for Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, pgvector, and Chroma with persistent volumes, Python client snippets for each engine plus Pinecone, and collection-creation patterns that include payload/metadata indexes upfront (since adding them after data load forces multi-hour rebuilds), with idempotent provisioning rules so agent re-runs do not drop data via recreate_collection.

methodologies.name: vector-db-setup-prod
methodologies.description: Closes the gap between dev Docker one-liners and production-ready vector databases — adding the resource limits, health checks, persistent storage classes, replication, and tested failover that hardened deployments demand. Apply it when serving real RAG or semantic-search traffic, migrating from a dev container, provisioning Kubernetes StatefulSets or Helm releases, deploying managed cloud services, or establishing disaster recovery; skip for prototypes under 100K vectors or when no ops team is available (use Pinecone Serverless instead). Documents four canonical patterns — single-node Docker Compose, Kubernetes StatefulSet with PVCs and ingress, managed services (Qdrant Cloud, Weaviate Cloud, Pinecone Serverless), and Terraform for AWS RDS or GCP Cloud SQL pgvector — plus replication options per engine, RPO/RTO tiers from sync replication to daily backup, Helm values for 3-replica clusters with anti-affinity, and a pre-launch checklist enforcing 12-month capacity sizing and 30% headroom.

methodologies.name: video-generation-async-api
methodologies.description: Handles the universal reality that all current AI video generation providers (Runway, Luma, Replicate) are async with 60-300s latency, returning a job ID that must be polled while provider-issued URLs expire in 24-72h. Use it for any video-generation integration, when building polling loops for Gen-3 or Dream Machine, implementing parallel multi-clip generation with ffmpeg assembly, or wiring up provider-select-submit-poll-download workflows; skip when sub-30s real-time generation is required since no provider supports it. Mandates a polling interval of 5-10s with a hard 600s timeout, immediate local download after SUCCEEDED to avoid broken links, full raw-response logging because FAILED states often have empty failure messages, per-provider semaphores to avoid 429 rate-limit storms, idempotency keys, and a human-review checkpoint before any public publication of generated video.

methodologies.name: video-generation-production-service
methodologies.description: Builds the production layer above raw provider APIs that direct calls cannot offer: multi-provider abstraction with retry and fallback, an async HTTP API returning job IDs immediately so callers do not block 60-300s, priority queueing for high-volume workloads, and ffmpeg utilities for clip assembly. Apply it when generating more than a few videos per day, exposing video gen as an internal API, requiring fallback across Runway/Luma/Replicate, processing concurrent requests with priority ordering, or assembling multi-clip sequences with audio overlay; skip for one-off scripts. Provides reference implementations including a VideoGenerationService class with VideoConfig and VideoResult dataclasses, FastAPI BackgroundTasks pattern (with Redis substitution noted for multi-instance production), a VideoQueue with max_concurrent guard, a VideoProcessor wrapping ffmpeg concat/transcode/thumbnail, webhook handlers for Runway and Luma, and provider-specific SDK examples covering Stable Video Diffusion, AnimateDiff, and Zeroscope on Replicate.

methodologies.name: video-generation-prompt-engineering
methodologies.description: Stops agents from wasting expensive video-generation credits on poor output by recognising that each provider was trained on different distributions and responds to different prompt patterns. Use it when writing prompts for Runway, Luma, Sora 2, Veo 3, or Replicate, building reusable prompt templates for content pipelines (news B-roll, social, product), or optimising existing prompts that produce inconsistent quality; skip for tasks needing precise multi-shot character consistency (drift is unsolved) or when controlling Sora/Veo native audio (non-deterministic). Provides the Subject + Action + Setting + Style + Camera + Lighting formula with vocabulary banks per dimension, ready-made templates for product/landscape/character/9:16 social formats, and provider-specific rules — Runway responds to camera-direction vocabulary (tracking shot, dolly, crane), Luma prefers emotional and sensory prose, Sora 2 handles temporal transitions and complex multi-subject scenes, and Veo 3 uniquely processes audio descriptions plus 4K resolution flags — alongside negative-prompt guidance and common-mistake fixes.

methodologies.name: video-generation-provider-selection
methodologies.description: Forces a deliberate provider choice up front because each video API excels at a narrow capability — Runway Gen-3 is the only option with lip-sync and video-to-video, Sora 2 supports up to 60-second clips, Veo 3 is alone in offering native audio and 4K, Luma supports video extension, Replicate exposes 50+ open-source models for cost optimisation. Apply it before any integration code, when evaluating providers for a new content pipeline, switching due to API access or quality changes, or building a multi-provider abstraction; skip when integration is already underway, when precise multi-shot character consistency is required (no provider delivers reliably), or when over 60s of single-call output is needed. Tabulates 2025-2026 providers across max duration, resolution, API status, and pricing, gives a feature support matrix (text/image/video-to-video, video extension, lip-sync, native audio, motion control, character consistency), and codifies five testable selection rules with a $0.05-0.50/second cost-modeling reminder before production rollout.

methodologies.name: vision-accessibility
methodologies.description: Addresses WCAG 2.1 alt-text obligations at scale — manual authoring breaks past a few hundred images, while traditional CV models output category labels rather than the context-anchored descriptions screen reader users actually need. Apply it for accessibility tooling on CMS or e-commerce platforms, publishing pipelines that must be WCAG-compliant before going live, retroactive remediation of large image libraries, and screen-reader support for dynamically generated charts; skip for decorative images (use alt=""), medical or legal accuracy contexts, or real-time live video. Enforces the rules that alt text be ≤125 characters, never start with "image of" or "picture of", include any visible text verbatim, and convey the functional purpose given the surrounding page context (heading, section, slug). Includes Pydantic-typed AltTextGenerator using GPT-4o, structured prompts for short/long descriptions and chart-data extraction with hallucination warnings, and aria-describedby patterns for complex infographics.

methodologies.name: vision-agentic-pipeline
methodologies.description: Replaces monolithic vision calls with a three-subagent chain (image-router → vision-extractor → validation-agent) plus a separate alt-text generator, because no single prompt can simultaneously optimise document extraction, classification, moderation, and accessibility, and different image types need different VLMs. Apply it for agent-driven document processing, visual Q&A over image corpora, multi-provider resilience, high-volume batch document processing, and any pipeline where business-rule validation must gate downstream writes; skip for one-off descriptions, real-time video over 5 FPS, or privacy-sensitive images that cannot leave your infrastructure. Provides a production VisionService with retry, async batch (max_workers=5 for ~150 docs/min), image-hash caching to deduplicate rescans, multi-provider fallback (OpenAI → Claude → Gemini), confidence routing under 0.85 to a human-review queue, image compression to 1024px to cut tokens 50-70%, and the warning that VLMs cannot read EXIF or PDF metadata so use PyMuPDF separately.

methodologies.name: vision-classification-moderation
methodologies.description: Handles label-and-score verdict tasks where VLMs outperform traditional classifiers because they understand both visual semantics and context (distinguishing news/documentary violence from gratuitous content) and generalise to new categories from prompt descriptions alone. Apply it for end-user content moderation, routing uploaded images to category-specific processors, automated labelling of product or media libraries, quality assessment before publishing, and brand safety on programmatic creatives; skip for object detection with bounding boxes (fine-tuned YOLO/DETR is 10-100x cheaper), real-time over 5 FPS video, ultra-high-throughput binary safe/unsafe (purpose-built moderation APIs are faster), or pixel segmentation. Mandates that the full category list be provided and exact-matched in output, structured JSON output via response_format, three-tier severity routing (auto-approve none, queue low for human review, auto-reject medium and high), retry with prompt reformulation for borderline medical/forensic rejections, monthly false-positive/negative tracking per category, and warns against duplicating moderation calls on the high-volume safe path.

methodologies.name: vision-document-extraction
methodologies.description: Pulls structured data from invoices, receipts, contracts, forms, and ID documents using VLMs that combine OCR with layout understanding and field-to-schema mapping in one call, replacing multi-step OCR plus NLP-parse pipelines. Apply it for any document upload extraction, scanned-PDF OCR replacement where layout matters (tables, multi-column), ID-document workflows, automated data entry, and historical archive batch processing; skip for barcode/QR (use zxing), pixel-precise measurements, FDA/CE-cleared medical inference, and GDPR/HIPAA-regulated workloads (use self-hosted Qwen3-VL). Enforces structured JSON output via response_format or tool-use (never prompt-only), Pydantic parsing with try/except and re-prompt on ValidationError, schema-per-document-type prompts (invoice, receipt, contract, ID) for 30-50% higher field accuracy, image-hash caching, second-pass numeric validation since Claude 4 and GPT-4o hallucinate financial totals, and human review when confidence is low or extracted totals exceed business thresholds.

methodologies.name: vision-provider-selection
methodologies.description: Makes the most cost-impactful vision decision — provider choice — explicit and evidence-driven, since each frontier VLM has a distinct strength: Gemini Flash at $0.16/1M tokens for high-volume OCR (~6000 pages/$1), Claude Sonnet's hybrid layout-aware OCR at 2.1% CER on printed text, GPT-4o for consistent structured JSON, Gemini Pro for medical and scientific imagery, Qwen3-VL or GLM-4.5V self-hosted for privacy. Apply it before writing any vision integration code, when evaluating cost/quality tradeoffs for batch pipelines, switching providers due to changed requirements, or selecting a self-hosted model for regulated data; skip for barcode decoding, scaled object detection, real-time video over 5 FPS, or FDA/CE-certified medical inference. Includes a task-to-provider routing table, mandatory rules to compress images to 1024px on the long edge, set GPT-4o detail to high only for complex layouts, and always send sensitive images as base64 since URL fetching creates uncontrolled provider-side logs.

methodologies.name: voice-agents
methodologies.description: Voice agents provide real-time conversational AI over telephony and WebRTC with latency under 1 second for natural turn-taking and speaker presence. Use this to build customer service bots, sales agents, or interactive voice assistants that handle back-and-forth dialogue with speech-to-text, reasoning, and text-to-speech in a continuous loop. Platforms like Retell AI and ElevenLabs handle the infrastructure complexity, letting you focus on conversation logic and business outcomes.

domains.description: Manages ML model operations including fine-tuning (LoRA, QLoRA, OpenAI API), model evaluation and benchmarking, cost optimization through token management and batching, and observability with LangSmith and Langfuse. Covers fine-tuning decision matrices, evaluation frameworks, cost reduction strategies, and LLM monitoring.

methodologies.name: cost-reduction-strategies
methodologies.description: Cost reduction strategies for LLM APIs include response caching, prompt optimization, model routing, and batching to reduce token usage and API costs while maintaining quality. Use this when your LLM costs are becoming a bottleneck or when scaling to production volume makes unit economics critical. Well-executed cost optimization can reduce your monthly bill by 20-50% while often improving latency and reliability through smarter routing and caching.

methodologies.name: evaluation-benchmarks
methodologies.description: Evaluation benchmarks provide standardized test suites for comparing models, measuring performance over time, and monitoring production quality through automated metrics and LLM judges. Use this to systematically track whether your LLM system is improving or degrading as you iterate, and to compare vendors or model versions before switching. Regular benchmarking prevents silent quality regressions and gives you data-driven confidence in upgrades or architecture changes.

methodologies.name: evaluation-framework
methodologies.description: The evaluation framework provides core abstractions for testing LLM outputs against ground truth, using automated metrics, and leveraging LLM-as-judge for complex tasks without clear metrics. Use this to build test harnesses that validate your models work before shipping to production and to detect regressions. A good evaluation framework turns subjective quality assessment into reproducible, quantifiable measurement.

methodologies.name: evaluation-metrics
methodologies.description: Evaluation metrics measure LLM performance across accuracy, quality, latency, cost, safety, and reliability dimensions with task-specific measures for classification, QA, summarization, and code. Use this to select appropriate metrics for your task type and business goals, ensuring you measure what actually matters rather than vanity metrics. Well-chosen metrics provide early warning of problems and guide optimization priorities.

methodologies.name: fine-tuning-lora
methodologies.description: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique that trains only small adapter layers instead of all model weights, reducing memory by 4-32x and enabling training on consumer hardware. Use this when you have 100-1000 domain-specific examples and need faster iteration than full fine-tuning, or when you want to maintain multiple task-specific adapters from one base model. LoRA produces comparable quality to full fine-tuning with a fraction of the cost.

methodologies.name: fine-tuning-openai-basics
methodologies.description: OpenAI fine-tuning allows customizing GPT models on your specific data to improve domain performance, reduce prompt length, and achieve more consistent outputs at lower cost than using larger models. Use this when you have 50+ high-quality examples and need to optimize for your specific use case: customer support, content generation, or classification tasks that benefit from style consistency. Fine-tuned models offer better quality and lower inference cost than few-shot prompting.

methodologies.name: fine-tuning-openai-production
methodologies.description: Production fine-tuning for OpenAI covers uploading training data, configuring hyperparameters, monitoring training progress, evaluating the fine-tuned model, and deploying to production with version control. Use this when you're ready to productionize a fine-tuned model: implementing quality checks, maintaining training history, and iterating as your data evolves. This workflow ensures your fine-tuning investments compound over time rather than getting lost.

methodologies.name: finetuning-basics
methodologies.description: Fine-tuning fundamentals cover techniques from full fine-tuning to LoRA to QLoRA, plus OpenAI's API, with practical guidance on choosing the right technique for your constraints and goals. Use this to understand the landscape of fine-tuning approaches and make informed decisions about infrastructure, cost, and quality trade-offs. The right technique depends on your dataset size, hardware budget, and quality requirements.

methodologies.name: finetuning-datasets
methodologies.description: Fine-tuning dataset preparation covers format selection, cleaning, quality guidelines, hyperparameter tuning, evaluation metrics, and deployment options from GGUF to vLLM. Use this to turn raw data into training-ready datasets and to understand the full lifecycle from raw data through evaluation and production deployment. Data quality is the primary lever for fine-tuning success; this guide helps you get it right.

methodologies.name: llm-cost-basics
methodologies.description: LLM cost basics cover model pricing, cost tracking, and simple routing strategies to select appropriate models based on task complexity, enabling you to reduce costs without sacrificing quality. Use this when building applications where LLM spend scales with usage and you need to prevent runaway bills. Understanding the cost structure of different models and task types lets you make routing decisions that save 30-50% without degrading results.

methodologies.name: llm-observability-stack-2026
methodologies.description: The 2026 LLM observability stack combines tracing (Langfuse), cost analytics (Helicone), and evaluation (Phoenix) to provide comprehensive visibility into production LLM systems with actionable metrics and cost savings. Use this to instrument your application for production readiness: track latency, quality, costs, and errors; version your prompts; and catch quality regressions before users notice. A proper observability setup typically pays for itself through cost optimization and quality improvements.

methodologies.name: lora-qlora
methodologies.description: LoRA and QLoRA provide efficient fine-tuning with LoRA reducing parameters to 0.1-1% and QLoRA adding 4-bit quantization to fit 70B models on 48GB GPUs, enabling consumer-grade training. Use this when you want to fine-tune large open models without expensive GPU infrastructure, or when you need multiple specialized adapters that can be swapped at inference time. The combination makes state-of-the-art fine-tuning accessible and cost-effective.

domains.description: Covers multimodal AI beyond text: vision understanding (GPT-4o Vision, Gemini Vision) for OCR and analysis, image generation (DALL-E 3, Stable Diffusion), video generation (Sora, Runway), speech-to-text (Whisper), text-to-speech, and voice synthesis. Includes model comparisons and real-time voice applications.

methodologies.name: img-gen-basics
methodologies.description: Image generation basics cover DALL-E 3, prompt engineering, batch generation, and caching for creating high-quality images programmatically from text descriptions. Use this to automate image creation for marketing, product photography, or UI mockups without hiring designers. DALL-E 3's photorealistic outputs and revise-prompt feature let you iterate quickly until you get exactly what you need.

methodologies.name: img-gen-tools
methodologies.description: Production image generation covers Stable Diffusion, Flux, and multi-provider fallback services with caching, retry logic, and templates for common workflows like product photos and logos. Use this to build scalable image generation systems with multiple provider options, cost optimization, and quality consistency. A robust service handles failures gracefully, caches results efficiently, and adapts to changing provider capabilities.

methodologies.name: speech-to-text-advanced
methodologies.description: Advanced speech-to-text covers long audio processing, speaker diarization, and production transcription services to handle complex audio scenarios at scale. Use this when you need to transcribe meetings, podcasts, or videos and need speaker labels or word-level timestamps, or when your audio exceeds API limits. Production-grade STT handles edge cases and produces structured output suitable for downstream processing.

methodologies.name: speech-to-text-basics
methodologies.description: Speech-to-text basics cover OpenAI Whisper and local alternatives for converting audio to text with optional timestamps, translation, and cost optimization through model selection. Use this to add audio understanding to your applications, from transcribing voice messages to processing recorded video. Whisper's multi-language support and accuracy make it the go-to choice for most speech-to-text needs.

methodologies.name: tts-basics
methodologies.description: Text-to-speech basics cover OpenAI TTS and SSML for converting text to natural speech with voice selection, speed control, and pronunciation guidance through markup. Use this to add voice output to applications, create voiceovers, or improve accessibility. SSML gives you fine-grained control over pronunciation, pacing, and emphasis without regenerating the entire audio.

methodologies.name: tts-implementation
methodologies.description: Production TTS implementation covers ElevenLabs voice quality and cloning, Google Cloud voices, long-text chunking, streaming for real-time output, and caching for cost efficiency. Use this to build production-grade speech synthesis with multiple providers, fallback options, and cost optimization. ElevenLabs' voice quality and streaming capabilities enable natural-sounding voice interfaces; proper caching prevents regenerating the same speech repeatedly.

methodologies.name: video-gen-basics
methodologies.description: Video generation basics cover prompt engineering, video processing utilities, and best practices for generating videos from text with guidance on motion, camera work, and style. Use this to create professional-quality videos from text descriptions using Runway, Sora, or other generators. Well-crafted prompts with explicit motion and camera movement produce dramatically better results than vague descriptions.

methodologies.name: video-gen-tools
methodologies.description: Runway Gen-3, Replicate, and Luma Dream Machine provide production video generation from text and image prompts. Use Runway for high-quality outputs, Replicate for model variety and experimentation, and Luma for keyframe-based control and editing. A production service abstracts provider complexity and handles retries, making video generation reliable for content pipelines, marketing automation, and creative applications.

methodologies.name: vision-applications
methodologies.description: GPT-4o Vision enables OCR, document analysis, image classification, content moderation, and visual question answering. Use document analyzers for receipt/form extraction, classifiers for product categorization, moderators for safety filtering, and vision services for general image understanding. Production implementations include error handling, structured output, and batch processing to reliably extract insights from visual content.

methodologies.name: vision-basics
methodologies.description: Vision models (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini) analyze images by encoding visual content into semantic representations. Use for understanding layouts, objects, text, and composition; provide clear prompts and leverage structured output for consistency. Models handle multiple images in sequence for narrative analysis, and support detail levels (low/high) to balance quality and token usage.

methodologies.name: voice-basics
methodologies.description: Voice agents combine speech-to-text (Whisper), language understanding (LLM), and text-to-speech (OpenAI TTS) for conversational AI. Use for customer service bots, voice assistants, and IVR systems; architecture requires managing latency (target <1s total), conversation history, and tool integration. Implement with clear prompts and test different voice options to match the application tone.

methodologies.name: voice-implementation
methodologies.description: Real-time voice agents stream audio, use VAD for utterance detection, and handle concurrent operations with proper state management. Production implementations require gRPC for performance, async/await for concurrency, and stateful session management to maintain conversation context. WebSocket endpoints enable browser-based voice apps, while monitoring ensures latency targets are met and failures are handled gracefully.

domains.description: Specializes in retrieval-augmented generation systems. Covers document chunking strategies (semantic, recursive), embedding generation and models, vector databases (Qdrant, Weaviate, Chroma, pgvector), hybrid search combining vector and keyword matching, reranking with cross-encoders, and RAG evaluation metrics.

methodologies.name: agentic-rag-iterative-retrieval
methodologies.description: Solves multi-hop questions where the relevant information is split across documents that share no direct lexical or semantic overlap with the original query, lifting accuracy from 65-75% (single-pass RAG) to 80-90% by letting the LLM judge sufficiency and refine the search. Apply it for multi-hop QA, when single-pass retrieval consistently returns insufficient context, research synthesis tasks (explore-evaluate-refine-conclude), and pipelines whose latency SLA permits 3-10s per query and budget tolerates 3-5x cost; skip for single-turn factual lookup, sub-2s SLAs, well-covered corpora, and adversarial inputs (loops can be exploited via prompt injection in documents). Caps the loop at 3 iterations (hard 5), uses cheaper Haiku/Sonnet for sufficiency and refinement (Opus only for synthesis), deduplicates chunks across iterations, detects query drift via cosine similarity below 0.7 against the original, and sanitises retrieved chunks before passing to the judge to prevent injection-driven false-sufficient verdicts.

methodologies.name: agentic-rag-query-decomposition
methodologies.description: Breaks complex queries — comparisons, multi-part "and also" structures, research questions across known dimensions — into 2-4 atomic sub-queries that each map to a single retrieval call, dramatically improving recall on questions whose relevant chunks live under entirely different topics. Apply it when the query has multiple distinct information needs, in multi-hop QA combining facts from disjoint documents, structured research synthesis, or legal compliance and fact-checking that demands exhaustive sub-topic coverage; skip for simple factual lookups, queries with a single clear need, or when sub-queries emerge only from retrieved context (use iterative retrieval instead). Mandates 2-4 sub-queries (fall back to single-pass under 2, keep top 4 over 4), parallel execution via asyncio.gather, intra-run sub-query result caching, explicit coverage tracking that flags sub-queries returning zero high-confidence chunks rather than silently dropping them, and chunk merging plus deduplication before synthesis to avoid the generator answering each sub-query independently.

methodologies.name: agentic-rag-self-correction
methodologies.description: Cuts RAG hallucination rates from 15-20% (single-pass) to 5-10% by adding a faithfulness verifier that checks each generated claim against retrieved source chunks, with verifier feedback driving targeted re-retrieval rather than blind retries. Apply it for fact-checking and compliance verification, legal and regulatory content where ungrounded claims have real consequences, any pipeline where hallucination must drop below 10%, and structured-plus-unstructured retrieval that needs cross-verification; skip for sub-2s SLAs, creative tasks where source faithfulness is not the goal, or high-volume low-stakes queries where correction cycles cost more than occasional hallucinations. Requires the verifier use a different model or temperature than the generator (same-model self-confirmation defeats the purpose), caps corrections at 2, escalates to human review when verified=False after the cap or when more than 2 ungrounded claims are flagged, and rebuilds the context block from scratch each iteration so newly retrieved chunks actually influence regeneration.

methodologies.name: agentic-rag-tool-using
methodologies.description: Lets the LLM choose between vector_search, keyword_search, sql_query, and web_search on each step rather than always hitting the vector store, enabling answers that fuse structured tabular data with unstructured documents and use web search to cover known corpus gaps instead of hallucinating. Apply it when questions mix structured and unstructured data, when the corpus has known coverage gaps requiring external fallback, when different query types benefit from different retrieval strategies, or when retrieval decisions must be auditable; skip for single-corpus apps, applications with strict data governance forbidding external lookups, or models without reliable structured function calling. Caps tool-calling loops at 3, mandates specific tool descriptions (semantic vs exact vs structured) so the LLM does not default to the first option, uses Haiku or gpt-4o-mini for routing while reserving Opus for final synthesis, caches tool results within a run, gates web_search behind an allow-list, and keeps the routing prompt bounded by passing only ~100-char result summaries on subsequent iterations.

methodologies.name: chunking-basics
methodologies.description: Chunking splits documents for embedding; strategy (fixed-size, sentence, semantic) determines retrieval quality. Size matters: small (200 tokens) loses context, large (1000+ tokens) dilutes relevance; typical sweet spot is 500 tokens with 50-token overlap. Strategy varies by content: markdown for docs, sentences for narrative, fixed for mixed content.

methodologies.name: chunking-code-ast
methodologies.description: Replaces character-based code chunking — which slices function bodies mid-statement and produces incoherent embeddings — with AST-aware splitting that treats each function and class as the atomic unit developers actually search for. Apply it when ingesting source code into a code-search RAG, building a code documentation assistant for "what does function X do" questions, indexing pipelines whose queries map naturally to function or class units, and multi-language codebases with Python primary and JS/TS secondary; skip for TypeScript with decorators or generics (use tree-sitter), unsupported languages where the line-based fallback drops metadata, prose or markdown files, and files with syntax errors that fall through to generic splitting silently. Provides a CodeChunker that walks Python ast FunctionDef/AsyncFunctionDef/ClassDef nodes capturing name, type, docstring, and line range, JavaScript regex patterns for functions/classes/arrows, an explicit warning rule when fallback to line-based splitting kicks in, deduplication by (name, start_line) to remove overlapping class/method chunks, and versioned collection names when re-chunking to avoid stale vector collisions.

methodologies.name: chunking-document-structure
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of context loss when splitting Markdown and HTML documents by character count, which strips away the section hierarchy a retriever needs to judge relevance. Apply when ingesting documentation sites, wikis, README files, or HTML pages with semantic sectioning elements where queries reference section context such as authentication or error handling. MarkdownChunker walks header levels and inherits parent titles into a header_path joined by ' > ', while HTMLChunker uses BeautifulSoup to split on section, article, and div elements with the nearest h1-h6 as the chunk header. The practical value is higher retrieval recall because every sub-chunk carries its navigational anchor in the embedded text, plus traceability via numbered parts when large sections must be word-split. Skip on flat HTML with no structural elements, on raw code, and on prose without headers; always pair with a min_chunk_size guard so single-line list items and tiny code blocks do not pollute the index with near-zero vectors.

methodologies.name: chunking-production-service
methodologies.description: Solves the brittleness of wiring Markdown, code, HTML, and prose chunkers individually at each call site in a mixed-content ingestion pipeline. Apply in production batch jobs that process heterogeneous corpora and need per-document strategy selection, document-level metadata propagation (source path, page, timestamp) to every chunk, and graceful degradation that does not halt overnight runs. ChunkingService accepts a ChunkingConfig (strategy, chunk_size, overlap, min_chunk_size) plus an optional embedding_func, dispatches to SemanticChunker, MarkdownChunker, CodeChunker, or RecursiveChunker, and falls back to word-based splitting on any exception while logging the cause. The practical value is a single chunk(text, strategy, metadata) entry point with centralized configuration, audit-friendly logs, and a strategy-selection matrix that maps Markdown to MARKDOWN, code to CODE, legal/medical prose to SEMANTIC, news to RECURSIVE, and mixed content to RECURSIVE as the default. Validate embedding_func at construction time for SEMANTIC, add a strategy_used field to chunks so fallback output is distinguishable, and version collection names whenever the chunking strategy changes to avoid stale-vector collisions.

methodologies.name: chunking-semantic
methodologies.description: Solves the precision loss that comes from fixed-size or sentence-count splitting, which interrupts coherent concepts and produces ambiguously embedded chunks. Apply in high-stakes prose-heavy domains such as legal, medical, scientific, or news corpora where structural markers are absent and baseline RecursiveChunker retrieval is below target. SemanticChunker embeds every sentence with the same model used at query time, computes cosine similarity between consecutive sentences, and inserts a boundary wherever similarity drops below a threshold (default 0.75), with max_chunk_size sub-splitting and min_chunk_size merging applied afterwards. The practical value is chunks aligned to topic transitions rather than arbitrary byte windows, yielding the most semantically faithful boundaries available without document structure metadata. Avoid for quick prototypes (RecursiveChunker is faster and sufficient under 10k docs), constrained budgets (one embedding call per sentence at index time), languages without NLTK punkt support, and structured documents where header or AST signals are stronger.

methodologies.name: db-chroma
