methodologies.description: Chroma is an in-memory vector database ideal for development and prototyping. Simple API, no external dependencies, supports local persistence. Use for rapid RAG setup without infrastructure overhead; scale up to Qdrant or Pinecone for production workloads exceeding 1M vectors.

methodologies.name: db-comparison
methodologies.description: Vector databases differ in scale, performance, and deployment model. Qdrant (recommended production): self-hosted, 100M+ vectors, hybrid search. Pinecone: fully managed, 1B+ vectors, zero ops. pgvector: leverage existing PostgreSQL, no new infra. Choose based on scale (prototype→Chroma, prod→Qdrant, managed→Pinecone) and infrastructure constraints.

methodologies.name: db-qdrant
methodologies.description: Qdrant is production-grade with 41 QPS @ 50M vectors, hybrid search, and quantization for memory efficiency. Create collections with HNSW indexing, upsert documents with metadata, retrieve with filters and reranking, and monitor with snapshots. Docker deployment and rich query API make it the recommended choice for self-hosted RAG systems.

methodologies.name: db-weaviate
methodologies.description: Weaviate excels at knowledge graphs with GraphQL API and cross-references between entity types. Use when documents need relationship modeling (author→paper, person→company) or when hybrid search (semantic + BM25) is essential. Schema-based design and native GraphQL queries enable complex queries that vector search alone cannot answer.

methodologies.name: embedding-applications
methodologies.description: Embeddings power semantic search, clustering, and classification. Benchmark with MTEB for quality comparison; optimize with caching (80% cache hit = 80% cost reduction) and batching. Integrate embeddings with vector databases; recommend text-embedding-3-large for quality or text-embedding-3-small for cost efficiency.

methodologies.name: embedding-caching
methodologies.description: Solves redundant embedding API spend and latency when the same text is embedded repeatedly across queries and incremental ingestion runs. Apply in high-volume RAG workloads with overlapping document or query sets, in production systems with measurable embedding bills, and any pipeline calling the same model for stable corpora. The methodology covers three patterns: in-memory lru_cache for small workloads, file-based JSON cache for local development and infrequent batch jobs, and Redis with a 30-day TTL for production with concurrent multi-process access. The practical value is direct cost reduction proportional to hit rate, with typical RAG workloads seeing 70-90% hits and 80% hit rate translating to 80% lower API spend. The critical correctness rule is that cache keys must include the model name as a namespace; reusing keys across models causes silent cross-model collisions where vectors from one model are returned for requests from another. Skip for one-time ingestion of fully unique corpora and for ultra-low-latency streaming where Redis round-trips exceed the API call itself.

methodologies.name: embedding-chunking-strategies
methodologies.description: Solves the dual failure of embedding model token limits (OpenAI text-embedding-3-large silently truncates above 8191 tokens) and the precision-recall tradeoff that wrong chunk sizes create. Apply to any corpus being ingested into a vector store, especially documents longer than the model's token limit and code search where function boundaries must be preserved. Covers fixed-size word chunking with overlap, token-based chunking via tiktoken, semantic chunking, and sentence-based strategies, with overlap tuning typically at 10% to maintain cross-chunk continuity. The practical value is direct control over the primary RAG quality lever: focused 256-512 token chunks surface targeted answers while 1000-2000 token chunks preserve context for summarization. The non-negotiable rule is to count tokens (not words or characters) before embedding, because subword tokenization makes word-count splits silently exceed limits; use tiktoken for OpenAI and len(text.split()) * 1.3 as a heuristic elsewhere. Skip for short single-sentence texts already within limits and for structured tabular data where row-level embedding preserves integrity better than semantic splitting.

methodologies.name: embedding-cost-optimization
methodologies.description: Solves the runaway cost of embedding APIs at corpus scale, where text-embedding-3-large at $0.13/1M tokens runs $650/month for 10M 500-token documents. Apply when embedding spend is measurable on the monthly bill, when ingesting more than 100K documents, when re-ingesting stable corpora frequently, or when first-pass retrieval can tolerate a cheaper filter model. The five composable levers are dimension reduction (3072 to 1024 via OpenAI's Matryoshka representation, ~5% quality loss with 67% storage savings), batch API requests (collapse 1000 requests into one), aggressive caching, two-stage retrieval that uses a cheap model for initial recall, and pre-ingestion deduplication. The practical value is typical combined savings exceeding 80% without sacrificing retrieval quality, with concrete cost references showing text-embedding-3-small at $100/month for the same workload that costs $650/month with the large variant, and local GPU inference at ~$5/month in compute alone. Skip for small one-time ingestion under 10K documents where optimization overhead exceeds savings, and for quality-critical domains where cheaper model substitutes have not been benchmarked against retrieval metrics.

methodologies.name: embedding-generation
methodologies.description: Generate embeddings via OpenAI, Cohere, local models (sentence-transformers, BGE), or Ollama. Batch for efficiency (up to 2048 texts per API call). OpenAI text-embedding-3 models support native dimension reduction (3072→256) preserving 95%+ quality at 91% storage savings. Local models offer privacy/offline capability at higher latency.

methodologies.name: embedding-model-selection
methodologies.description: Solves the high-cost mistake of choosing the wrong embedding model, since switching after ingestion invalidates the entire vector index and requires full re-ingestion. Apply at the start of any new vector index, when balancing quality versus cost, when supporting multilingual queries where language differs between query and document, and when deciding between API and local inference. Provides a model comparison covering text-embedding-3-large (3072 dims, best quality, $0.13/1M), text-embedding-3-small ($0.02/1M, 15x cheaper at good quality), mistral-embed, Cohere v3 multilingual, and local options like bge-large-en-v1.5 and bge-m3. The practical value is a one-shot decision framework that weighs dimensions, token limits ranging from 512 (Cohere) to 8191 (OpenAI), cost, language coverage, and latency (CPU inference at production scale is only viable with the smallest models like all-MiniLM-L6-v2 at ~5ms vs bge-large at ~200ms). Skip when exact keyword matching suffices, when the corpus is fewer than 500 static texts (TF-IDF is simpler), when sub-10ms streaming latency is required (use local), and when the domain is so specialized no domain-adapted model exists.

methodologies.name: embedding-models
methodologies.description: Compare models on MTEB leaderboard. text-embedding-3-large: best quality ($0.13/1M). mistral-embed: good quality, good cost ($0.10/1M). BGE-m3: excellent local option, free, multilingual. all-MiniLM: fast inference (384 dims), free. Match model to use case: production RAG→text-embedding-3-large; cost-sensitive→text-embedding-3-small; private→local BGE.

methodologies.name: graph-rag-indexing
methodologies.description: Solves the failure of vector RAG on global, multi-hop, and entity-relationship questions where no single chunk contains the answer and similarity search returns irrelevant fragments. Apply to corpora with dense cross-references such as legal case law, medical literature, citation networks, or enterprise knowledge bases of more than 1,000 documents where queries ask about main themes, entity connections, or paths between concepts. The six-stage pipeline chunks documents, extracts entities and relationships via LLM with temperature=0 and structured JSON output (capped at 15 entities and 20 relationships per chunk to suppress noise), builds a graph in NetworkX or Neo4j, runs Louvain community detection, and generates hierarchical summaries at entity, community, and global grain. The practical value is the Microsoft GraphRAG benchmark improvement: global query accuracy from 45% to 78%, multi-hop QA from 52% to 81%, entity relationship queries from 38% to 85%, and hallucination rate dropping from 18% to 8%. Skip for chunk-answerable local queries (vector RAG is 10-100x cheaper), corpora under 1,000 documents, real-time indexing scenarios (LLM extraction takes seconds per chunk), and teams without graph query (Cypher) skills.

methodologies.name: graph-rag-production
methodologies.description: Solves the production gap that opens when development-stage Graph RAG (NetworkX in-memory) cannot survive concurrent access, persistence across restarts, and corpora that exhaust RAM. Apply when validated Graph RAG must scale, when choosing between LlamaIndex KnowledgeGraphIndex and Microsoft graphrag CLI, when selecting a graph database backend, and when debugging non-deterministic community IDs or stale summaries. Covers backend selection (Neo4j for scale, FalkorDB for speed, Kuzu embedded for zero server cost), LlamaIndex integration with response_mode=tree_summarize and max_triplets_per_chunk at 10 or above, indexing cost management at $4-20 per million tokens, and the known failure modes including LlamaIndex's tight OpenAI coupling (provider migration requires custom adapters) and Microsoft graphrag's config mutation behavior. The practical value is a hardened deployment path with concrete backend tradeoffs and the gotchas that bite agents operating on live graph indexes after a long indexing run. Skip while still in exploration (use NetworkX), when the corpus fits in memory and is single-process, and when no budget exists for managed graph DBs (evaluate Kuzu first).

methodologies.name: graph-rag-retrieval
methodologies.description: Solves the routing problem that no single retrieval strategy serves all query types efficiently — global queries need pre-computed summaries, entity queries need neighbor lookup, relationship queries need shortest-path traversal, and local facts are best served by vector search. Apply once a knowledge graph exists and traffic is mixed across query types, and as the default starting point before committing to graph-only retrieval. Each query is classified by a fast LLM call (gpt-4o-mini, temperature=0) into GLOBAL, ENTITY, RELATIONSHIP, or LOCAL, with LOCAL as the safe fallback on classification failure; hybrid retrieval then combines vector candidate selection with graph neighbor expansion to cover what either approach misses alone. The practical value is that hybrid retrieval outperforms both pure vector and pure graph for most query distributions, while routing avoids paying graph-traversal cost on every query (3-5x latency) or losing accuracy by forcing the wrong strategy. Skip when no graph exists, when all queries are local and chunk-answerable, when graphs exceed 50k nodes with frequent global queries (precompute summaries offline instead), and cap hop depth to 3 on densely connected graphs to avoid exponential path enumeration.

methodologies.name: hybrid-search-basics
methodologies.description: Hybrid search combines vector (semantic) and BM25 (keyword) retrieval, fused with RRF or linear combination. Use when queries mix conceptual terms and exact product names/codes. Dynamically adjust alpha (weight) based on query type: quotes/codes→more keyword, long narrative→more semantic. Achieves better recall than pure vector search.

methodologies.name: hybrid-search-implementation
methodologies.description: Implement via Weaviate (built-in), Qdrant (sparse vectors + fusion), Pinecone (sparse-dense), or Elasticsearch (script_score). Production service normalizes both score types before combining. RRF fusion is more robust than linear averaging. Always use hybrid when exact match queries are expected alongside semantic understanding.

methodologies.name: rag
methodologies.description: RAG pipeline: load documents → chunk → embed → store in vector DB → retrieve → generate with context → cite sources. Best for Q&A over custom docs, domain chatbots, and reducing hallucinations. Optimal chunk size 500 tokens; use hybrid search for technical content; always implement reranking for production quality.

methodologies.name: rag-architecture
methodologies.description: RAG stages: ingestion (load, chunk, embed), indexing (store in vector DB), retrieval (find top-k chunks), generation (produce answer with context). Key decisions: chunking strategy per content type; retrieval method (basic, hybrid, reranking); context management (top-k, ordering). Quality metrics separate retrieval from generation metrics.

methodologies.name: rag-eval-ab-testing
methodologies.description: Solves the blind decision-making that occurs when teams change RAG configurations (chunk size, embedding model, top-K, reranker) and rely on 'feels better' to validate the change. Apply when comparing chunk sizes such as 500 vs 1000 tokens, when adding or swapping a reranker, when evaluating embedding models like text-embedding-3-large vs voyage-3, and as a hard gate before promoting any config change to production. The RAGABTest class takes two configs and two pipelines, runs the same question set through both, and compares latency, source count, and (when extended with the RAGAS loop) faithfulness, precision@5, and answer relevance; questions must run in interleaved order to neutralize temporal bias from index or model drift. The practical value is head-to-head metric comparison that catches the common case where one parameter improves faithfulness while degrading precision@5, plus a documented warning that the baseline compare_results method only measures latency and source count and must be extended with quality metrics before driving promotion decisions. Skip when the test set has fewer than 20 questions (statistical noise dominates), when the two configurations share index state or caches (results are not isolated), and when only latency matters.

methodologies.name: rag-eval-generation-metrics
methodologies.description: Solves the gap that retrieval quality alone leaves: a retriever can return correct documents while the generator still hallucinates or goes off-topic. Apply before any production deployment, when checking for hallucinations, when answers seem verbose or partial, and when sampling 10-20% of production queries for offline monitoring. Implements the RAG Triad — Context Relevance (is retrieved context relevant to the question), Faithfulness (is the answer grounded in that context), and Answer Relevance (does the answer address the question) — via LLM-judge prompts and the RAGAS framework, with the rule that all three must be scored together because high Faithfulness against low Context Relevance means the system is confidently wrong, and high Answer Relevance with low Faithfulness means it sounds correct but is ungrounded. The practical value is a scalable open-text quality signal that BLEU and ROUGE miss, used as a deployment quality gate and to diagnose whether failures originate in retrieval or generation. Skip for real-time per-query scoring (LLM-judge is too slow and expensive — sample offline instead) and for cross-LLM metric comparisons (RAGAS uses OpenAI by default and scores are not comparable across evaluators).

methodologies.name: rag-eval-pipeline
methodologies.description: Solves the impossibility of distinguishing retrieval failures from generation failures during RAG pipeline changes when only ad-hoc visual inspection is used. Apply before production deployment to establish baseline scores, after every pipeline change (chunking, top-K, model swap) to detect regressions, on test sets of at least 20 questions, and on a weekly sampled subset in production for drift detection. The pipeline runs each test question through the production RAG, computes retrieval metrics (precision@5, recall@5, MRR, hit rate) and generation metrics (faithfulness, answer relevance, context relevance) via RAGAS or LLM scoring, aggregates the results, and exports a versioned JSON artifact that can be diffed across changes. The practical value is explicit numeric quality gates such as faithfulness > 0.8 and precision@5 > 0.7 that turn evaluation from a passive report into an active CI blocker. Critical pitfalls: RAGAS contexts must be list[list[str]] (a flat list silently produces wrong metrics, masking retrieval failures with inflated scores), OPENAI_API_KEY must be set before invoking RAGAS evaluate() to avoid cryptic mid-run errors, and small parameter tweaks must be evaluated because a sequence of unvalidated changes can silently degrade quality by 20% in a sprint.

methodologies.name: rag-eval-production-monitoring
methodologies.description: Solves the post-deployment quality drift that hits every RAG system as corpora update, query distributions shift, LLM weights change, and embedding model versions roll forward. Apply to any production RAG with real user traffic, when feedback signals (thumbs up/down, ratings) are available, when latency spikes from embedding timeouts or vector DB overload need detection, and when the corpus is updated regularly. The RAGMonitor class logs latency, source count, and feedback per query into in-memory or time-series storage (Postgres, InfluxDB, Prometheus for production), aggregates rolling 24h summaries, and detects anomalies against thresholds; lightweight metrics (latency, hit rate, num_sources, user_feedback) run on every query while expensive LLM-based faithfulness and relevance run only on sampled batches. The practical value is continuous quality signal at microsecond per-query overhead, with sampled LLM eval triggered on anomaly detection to keep costs manageable (10K daily queries at $0.01/eval would otherwise be $100/day). Critical rule: hardcoded thresholds (latency > 5.0s, negative_feedback > 10) are placeholders only and must be calibrated against actual production baselines or they will flood on-call with noise or miss real degradation. Skip when feedback is too sparse for anomaly detection (under 10 signals/day) — rely on scheduled batch eval instead.

methodologies.name: rag-eval-retrieval-metrics
methodologies.description: Solves the diagnostic blind spot where end-to-end RAG metrics cannot tell whether a faithfulness drop is caused by the retriever returning wrong chunks or the generator ignoring correct ones. Apply on any labeled test set with ground-truth relevant document IDs, when tuning the top-K parameter, when comparing dense vs hybrid retrieval, and as a CI quality gate (e.g., fail deployment if Hit Rate drops below 0.90 or Precision@5 below 0.70). Provides pure-Python implementations with zero API cost for the five core metrics: Precision@K (accuracy in top-K), Recall@K (coverage of relevant docs), MRR (penalizes when first relevant result is ranked low), NDCG@K (graded relevance ranking quality), and Hit Rate (binary at-least-one-relevant). The practical value is free-to-run isolation of retrieval quality from generation quality, runnable on every test batch, with the rule that all five must be scored together — a retriever can hit Hit Rate=1.0 while Precision@K=0.20 means 4 out of 5 returned docs are noise polluting the generation context. Skip when no ground-truth relevance labels exist (collect them via human annotation or synthetic generation first), for real-time per-query online scoring (use sampled faithfulness instead), and as a replacement for generation metrics — always pair them.

methodologies.name: rag-eval-strategy
methodologies.description: Solves the default failure mode where teams pick one familiar metric and use 'looks good' as a quality gate, missing blind-spot regressions like a model that improves faithfulness while quietly degrading recall on a new document category. Apply before adding any RAG evaluation to decide which metrics to implement first, before deploying a new retriever or generator to set numeric gate thresholds, when LLM-judge costs are becoming a concern, and when escalating from cheap signals to the full RAGAS suite on a downward trend. Defines stage-based metric breadth — Development uses Precision@5, Recall@5, Faithfulness for cheap fast feedback; Pre-production uses the full RAGAS suite plus NDCG@K; Production monitoring uses Hit Rate, sampled Faithfulness, Latency; A/B testing measures end-to-end accuracy and user satisfaction — and use-case bundles such as Q&A (Faithfulness + Answer Relevance + Precision@3 with small K), Document Search (NDCG@10 + Recall@20 + MRR with large K), Chatbot (Context Relevance + Coherence + user satisfaction), and Summarization (Faithfulness + Completeness + Conciseness). The practical value is a deliberate cost-tiered evaluation plan with retrieval metrics free-and-always-on, generation metrics on a 10-20% sample, and explicit pre-deployment numeric thresholds. Skip for one-off interactive query debugging — strategy is for systematic batch evaluation only.

methodologies.name: rag-eval-test-set-generation
methodologies.description: Solves the foundational gap that no RAG evaluation is more reliable than its test set, and that without diverse question types easy factual queries mask systematic failures on reasoning or comparative ones. Apply before first deployment, after any pipeline change (chunking, top-K, model swap), when no labeled data exists and the corpus is large enough for synthetic generation to be representative, and when measuring different failure modes via factual, comparative, reasoning, and summarization question types. Combines automatic LLM-based generation (an LLM generates question, expected answer, and source quote from sampled documents) with manual curation of hard edge cases that expose retrieval failures on ambiguous queries. The practical value is statistical coverage from automation plus adversarial stress from manual curation, gated by four required rules: version test sets in source control alongside pipeline code (drift is as dangerous as model drift), include at least 20% hard or edge-case questions, stratify by document source and question type so heterogeneous corpora reveal per-source degradation, and manually spot-check every batch of LLM-generated cases because synthetic generators have a positivity bias and may invent questions the document does not actually answer. Skip for proof-of-concept work without budget, when the test set would be smaller than 20 questions (statistical noise dominates), and when the domain is too specialized for synthetic generation to be reliable.

methodologies.name: rag-implementation
methodologies.description: Complete pipeline: document loading (PDF, MD, TXT), chunking by strategy, embedding with OpenAI/local models, storage in Chroma/Qdrant, retrieval with filtering, and generation with citations. Production systems include query enhancement (expansion, HyDE, rewriting), monitoring, and error handling. Test incrementally from retrieval quality before adding generation.

methodologies.name: reranking-diversity-mmr
methodologies.description: Solves the redundancy problem in two-stage retrieval where the top-k cross-encoder picks are near-duplicate passages from the same source document, wasting LLM context window on overlapping facts. Apply when corpora cluster around a few popular topics, in long-context RAG sending 5-10 passages where redundancy wastes budget, in QA over documents that repeat the same fact (legal contracts, news archives), and when users complain that answers feel repetitive. Implements the MMR formula MMR(d) = lambda * relevance(query, d) - (1 - lambda) * max_sim(d, selected) as a greedy iterative selector — lambda=1.0 is pure relevance, lambda=0.0 is pure diversity, lambda=0.5 is the balanced default — trading a small relevance loss for large diversity gain. The practical value is a context window covering more distinct aspects of the topic, often improving answer quality on broad questions without changing the retriever or generator. Skip when the query asks for a single precise fact (diversity hurts precision), when the corpus is already diverse with no redundancy, when document embeddings were discarded after indexing (recomputation doubles cost since MMR needs candidate embeddings, not just relevance scores), and on latency-critical paths where the extra embedding step is unaffordable.

methodologies.name: reranking-models
methodologies.description: Various reranking services exist including Cohere, Jina, Voyage AI, and LLM-based approaches for production systems. Choose between API services for simplicity or local models for cost savings depending on latency and quality needs. This guide shows integration patterns and service selection criteria for different scales.

methodologies.name: reranking-pipeline-integration
methodologies.description: Solves the fragility of bare cross-encoder calls in production RAG, where a reranker timeout or rate-limit can break the entire pipeline. Apply when building production systems that must switch between local cross-encoder and Cohere or Voyage API rerankers, in high-throughput services that need parallel batch reranking via ThreadPoolExecutor, on any critical path where graceful degradation to initial retrieval order is required on reranker failure, and in agentic systems where a retrieval subagent passes candidates to a generation agent. The RerankingRAG class wraps vector store retrieval and reranking behind a single .retrieve() interface configured by RetrievalConfig (initial_top_k for the candidate pool, rerank_top_k for final results, and a swappable reranker backend across cross-encoder, cohere, or llm). The practical value is a swappable reranker layer with caching, batch processing, and fallback machinery that does not couple downstream generation logic to a specific reranker implementation. Skip for single-query scripts and notebooks (the integration class adds overhead without value), when the reranker is always local and always available (skip the fallback machinery), and when API reranking fees are prohibitive at scale (use a local cross-encoder only).

methodologies.name: reranking-two-stage
methodologies.description: Solves the bi-encoder precision ceiling: encoding query and document independently enables fast ANN search but loses subtle semantic matching that joint attention captures. Apply when initial retrieval returns noisy top-k results, in high-precision domains (legal, medical, compliance) where the top result must be correct, after hybrid search to reconcile BM25 and vector rankings into one ordered list, and when the latency budget exceeds 200ms and quality is the priority. Stage 1 retrieves 50-100 candidates via bi-encoder ANN search in approximately 10ms; Stage 2 jointly scores each (query, document) pair through a cross-encoder in 100-500ms and returns the reranked top-k. The practical value is best-of-both-worlds architecture that buys cross-encoder precision on a small candidate pool while keeping the broad retrieval pass fast. Two non-negotiable rules: retrieve 3-10x more candidates than the final top-k because the reranker cannot recover relevant documents missing from the candidate pool, and never mix or average scores across reranker model families because ms-marco logits are unbounded, Cohere returns 0-1 probabilities, and Voyage uses its own scale. Skip on latency-critical paths under 50ms, when the candidate pool is already 5 or fewer (overhead exceeds benefit), for simple keyword tasks where BM25 alone wins, and when the corpus is multilingual and no suitable cross-encoder covers the language mix.

methodologies.name: vector-database-setup
methodologies.description: Vector databases like Pinecone, Chroma, and Qdrant enable semantic search and similarity queries essential for RAG. Use when building document retrieval systems, recommendations, or duplicate detection at scale. Proper setup with right index types and batching strategies is critical for production performance.
