methodologies.name: multimodal-vui-design
methodologies.description: Voice-only interfaces lack visual feedback and complex data display. Multimodal design combines voice input (primary) with visual output in complementary patterns: voice-initiated searches display results on-screen, form filling via speech gets visual confirmation, and complex information browses using voice navigation with rich content. Apply when building smart displays or VUI-enabled products to provide multiple input fallbacks while keeping voice central.

domains.description: Geek-tier knowledge area covering 52 methodologies that wire AI coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, aider, Windsurf) into the deterministic SDLC floor. Routing happens through this AGENTS.md, which partitions the methodologies into ten prefixed categories: lang- for language toolchains, package management and build (10 entries spanning C#/Roslyn, Go/tygo, JVM/JReleaser, PHP, Ruby/Sorbet, Swift, plus pnpm catalogs, pyproject single-source, ts-strict-isolated and uv-lockfile floors); lint- for linters, formatters and hooks (autofix decision rule, megalinter, pre-commit floor, ruff+biome defaults, shellcheck/hadolint IaC floor, lint-staged); test- for testing tactics (consumer-contract, golden-master, mutation, property-based LLM invariants, self-healing locators, TDD red/green split agents); tracker- for issue-tracker plus agent integration (AI triage, GitHub Copilot Workspace, GitLab Duo, Jira Rovo MCP, Linear agent-as-assignee); kb- for codebase knowledge and agent memory (AGENTS.md context pyramid, RAG with symbol chunking, fresh symbol indexes, versioned memory files); task- for spec-and-branch lifecycle (agent-drafts-spec, fixable triage gate, plan-mode locked execution, spec-kit three-step, worktree runtime isolation); mr- for merge-request automation (codemod refactor agent, error-tracker draft PR, graph-vs-diff reviewer, Renovate handoff, slash-command surface); inc- for incident response (postmortem auto-draft no-publish, read-only investigation default, runbook-as-markdown tagged steps, tool-tier approval gate); sec- for supply-chain, SAST and secrets (CodeQL autofix, secrets defense-in-depth, Trivy pinned scanning); and gov- for governance, audit and identity (signed-JWT approval tokens, conventional-commits enforcement, license-compliance scan, SonarQube AI-code gate). An agent or human dives into this area when an active task crosses the line where an AI agent must produce changes that pass deterministic project gates: choosing the right linter or formatter contract, building a tracker-to-PR pipeline, hardening incident workflows for autonomous tools, gating merges with security or governance checks, or designing how the agent reads code, drafts specs, and isolates runtime. Each methodology folder is self-contained with CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, content/*.xml and optional templates and scripts, so the workflow is to pick the prefix, scan the candidate AGENTS.md files (each under 80 lines), and load only the XML content needed for the decision before applying the methodology's named deterministic checks.

methodologies.name: gov-approval-token-signed-jwt
methodologies.description: Solves the audit-trail problem for human approvals of write or destructive agent actions by replacing chat reactions with cryptographically signed, scope-bound, single-use JWTs that bind sub, aud, scope (tool + target + args_hash), short exp under five minutes, jti for replay protection, and correlation_id. Apply it to any agent that can perform Tier 1+ actions in production, deploy or rollback bots, IAM-mutation agents, and regulated environments where SOC2, GDPR, or the EU AI Act demand non-repudiable approval evidence. The practical value is a verifiable forensic record that survives both regulator review and post-incident analysis: a chat surface can initiate the request, but only the signed callback issued via SSO and KMS satisfies the gate, and self-approval by the agent's own identity is structurally impossible.

methodologies.name: gov-conventional-commits-enforced
methodologies.description: Solves the agent-driven log-rot problem where many small AI-authored commits degrade into useless freeform subjects, breaking changelog and semver automation. Apply it in any release-bearing repo, any project where agents commit autonomously without per-line human curation, and any monorepo using release-please, semantic-release, or changesets. The practical value is a deterministic pipeline from commit message to CHANGELOG entry to npm or PyPI tag: a commitlint commit-msg hook plus a CI PR-title check rejects non-conformant subjects before they reach the remote, the closed type enum (feat, fix, chore, refactor, docs, test, perf, build, ci, style, revert) is exactly the grammar LLMs follow most reliably, and agents are forbidden from --no-verify so the release tooling can mechanically infer the next version and rewrite release notes without human edits.

methodologies.name: gov-license-compliance-scan
methodologies.description: Solves the legal-liability and supply-chain risk that arises when AI agents add dependencies autonomously, by encoding license policy as machine-readable allow/flag/deny SPDX lists and failing the build on any violation. Apply it to any externally distributed product (SaaS, downloadable, OSS), enterprise procurement contexts where customers demand SBOM attestations, and any project subject to the EU Cyber Resilience Act (mandatory September 2026) or US Executive Order 14028. The practical value is twofold: license review shifts from a quarterly human task to a per-PR build gate where copyleft contamination cannot reach proprietary builds, and every tagged release ships a regenerated, cosign-signed CycloneDX 1.6 or SPDX 2.3 SBOM derived from the lockfile and retained for the regulatory window, giving auditors trustworthy machine-readable evidence rather than a stale hand-edited PDF.

methodologies.name: gov-sonarqube-ai-code-gate
methodologies.description: Solves the measurably distinct defect profile of AI-generated code (lookalike bugs, dead code, shallow tests, cognitive-complexity hotspots) that the default Sonar way gate was never tuned to catch. Apply it on teams where AI-authored commits exceed roughly 25% of weekly diff volume, in regulated industries where audit evidence of an AI-aware quality gate is a procurement or SOC2 requirement, and in enterprise monorepos already reporting quality dashboards. The practical value is an iterative, MCP-driven loop: the project binds to a Sonar way for AI Code (or AI Code Assurance Qualified) gate that raises duplication and complexity thresholds and adds the AI trust score, the agent reads findings through Sonar's MCP server and rewrites until clean, and a structured suppression contract (NOSONAR with mandatory reason and sunset date, no agent-level UI exceptions) keeps the gate from decaying into theatre.

methodologies.name: inc-postmortem-auto-draft-no-publish
methodologies.description: Solves the chronic skipped-postmortem problem (timeline writing is the painful 80% of the work) without introducing the worse failure mode of an LLM auto-publishing a wrong root cause to the org-wide canonical doc. Apply it on teams that already practice blameless postmortems with structured signal sources (per-incident Slack, alerts, deploy log, ChatOps action items) and have an autodraft-capable platform such as Rootly, incident.io, FireHydrant, or PagerDuty, especially under EU NIS2 or SOC2 72-hour reporting windows. The practical value is roughly 5x throughput (Rootly's published number) while preserving the postmortem as a learning artifact: the agent drafts into a six-section blameless template with status=draft and no publish credentials, every timeline line ends with a citation to a concrete signal ID so hallucinations are syntactically detectable, and only humans fill the canonical Root Cause field while the agent is restricted to a marked Root Cause Hypothesis section.

methodologies.name: inc-read-only-investigation-default
methodologies.description: Solves the durability gap of prompt-level safety (instructions like "be careful with deletes" are routinely bypassed by jailbreaks and tool-loop confusion) by making the SRE agent's RBAC credentials forbid mutation by default. Apply it during the first 6-12 months of any production agent rollout, in multi-tenant clusters where blast radius can cross customer boundaries, and under SOC2 CC6.3 or ISO 27001 A.9.2.3 least-privilege regimes. The practical value is the Azure SRE Agent / Causely 2026 consensus pattern: the agent boots bound only to a *-readonly ClusterRole granting get/list/watch/log/describe, and any mutation requires a human /agent escalate-trust command verified against the on-caller, a fresh sub-60-second MFA challenge, and a time-boxed remediate role that auto-revokes on incident close or after 60 minutes, structurally eliminating the most common postmortem finding of write credentials lingering after the incident closed.

methodologies.name: inc-runbook-as-markdown-tagged-steps
methodologies.description: Solves the unsafe-action problem caused by free-form prose runbooks that force the SRE agent to interpret hedging language during incidents, the leading 2025-2026 retro finding from incident.io, PagerDuty, and FireHydrant. Apply it on teams with more than ten distinct alert types and a stable service catalog, when migrating away from PDF or Confluence runbooks, and in multi-tool environments where one source of truth must drive every agent. The practical value is up to 50% MTTR reduction with structural safety: each runbook lives as repo-versioned Markdown with a fixed Symptoms / Diagnose / Remediate / Escalate spine, every Remediate bullet carries exactly one HTML-comment tag (<!-- agent:auto --> or <!-- agent:approval -->), the agent executes only auto steps unattended and refuses approval steps until a signed token arrives, and untagged or missing runbooks return a structured runbook_invalid error rather than letting the agent vector-search a wrong-but-plausible playbook from another service.

methodologies.name: inc-tool-tier-approval-gate
methodologies.description: Solves the structural unsafety of relying on prompts ("do not delete data without asking") to constrain destructive tool calls, as exposed by the AWS Kiro production-deletion incident. Apply it for any production SRE agent that can call side-effecting tools (kubectl, cloud APIs, databases, deploy systems), when designing or reviewing an MCP server, and ahead of SOC2 or EU AI Act reviews of agentic workflows. The practical value is a registry-driven executor gate: every callable is classified as T0 (read-only, free), T1 (safe mutation, audit-required), or T2 (destructive, signed approval required), unknown tools default to T2, the agent cannot self-promote tier via prompt or arguments, and T2 calls demand a JWT bound to (tool, target, incident_id) with TTL ≤ 300 seconds and single-use jti — making confused or jailbroken agents structurally unable to delete a PVC or drop a table because their credentials reject the call.

methodologies.name: kb-agents-md-context-pyramid
methodologies.description: Solves the cross-vendor context problem where Claude Code only auto-loads CLAUDE.md while standalone agents (Agent SDK, Codex, aider, Cursor, Copilot Agent) only read AGENTS.md, so putting context in one file silently breaks the other. Apply it on multi-agent repos, multi-package monorepos with per-package gotchas, long-lived projects where decision history must stay searchable but not auto-loaded, and when migrating from a single bloated CLAUDE.md to a routing-friendly layout. The practical value is one source of truth that serves every agent kind: CLAUDE.md is a one-line @AGENTS.md hook, AGENTS.md is a 20-80 line essential brief (hard cap 120, ~5k token break-even), and .agents/INDEX.md catalogues on-demand deep references. Per-directory coverage with sensible skip rules ensures an agent landing in src/auth/ gets auth-specific context without bulk-loading the whole tree.

methodologies.name: kb-codebase-rag-symbol-chunked
methodologies.description: Solves the unusable-top-hit problem of generic RAG starter kits where fixed character or line windows split functions in half, causing the coding agent to reason against partial code as if it were ground truth. Apply it on repos over 10k LOC where the whole tree no longer fits in context, multi-language repos covered by tree-sitter, Q&A or onboarding agents that surface code to humans, and indexing pipelines where reproducing the Anthropic claude-context, Cursor, or Sourcegraph Cody chunker pattern is wanted. The practical value is precision lifts of 30-50% over pure top-k vector search: each chunk is one whole function, class, or method bounded by tree-sitter, padded with file path and enclosing-class signature, and keyed by sha256 of the symbol body so re-embedding runs incrementally on diff (not the whole repo), retrieval is exposed as a stable code_search MCP tool with hybrid kind / path_glob filters, and the index can swap engines without rewriting agent prompts.

methodologies.name: kb-symbol-index-fresh-tags
methodologies.description: Solves the dominant context-bloat source on large repos (cited in Anthropic's Claude Code best practices and Cognition's Devin postmortems): coding agents grep for symbols and load megabytes of surrounding lines when an LSP-grade index could answer the same query in O(1) lookups. Apply it on monorepos over ~50k LOC, polyglot repos covered uniformly by universal-ctags or Serena, sandboxed runtimes where running an LSP daemon is impractical, and refactor-heavy workflows that need call-graph queries. The practical value is 5-20x context-cost reduction (per Cognition and Cursor's published numbers): a committed always-fresh index (tags, .serena/symbols.db, or LSIF/SCIP) is regenerated by a pre-commit hook and gated in CI by a drift check that diffs the committed copy against a fresh regeneration, while AGENTS.md declares a strict lookup protocol — index for symbol queries, grep only for non-symbol literals — embedded verbatim in every coding agent's system prompt.

methodologies.name: kb-versioned-agent-memory-files
methodologies.description: Solves the opacity, non-portability, and silent self-rewriting of vendor memory features (ChatGPT, Cursor memories, Devin knowledge) that fail the basic SDLC requirement of code-review for any artifact driving production behaviour. Apply it on long-lived projects (over three months) where context resets every session, multi-agent setups (Claude Code + Codex + autoheal cron) that must share institutional knowledge, and teams where every change to "what the agent believes about this codebase" should pass human review. The practical value is reviewable, diff-able, portable memory: four committed Markdown files at .aidocs/memory/ split by role (decisions / patterns / mistakes / session), every long-term entry is dated, append-only, and carries a citation (commit, ticket, PR, or transcript), a pre-commit hook rejects non-additive diffs to the three permanent files, and session.md is the lone volatile scratchpad that is overwritten freely but only promotes knowledge upward via deliberate citation-bearing appends.

methodologies.name: lang-csharp-roslyn-analyzer-errors
methodologies.description: Solves the recurring failure mode where LLM agents reintroduce known-bad C# patterns (null dereferences, async-void, missing ConfigureAwait, accidental SemVer-major public API breaks) because warning-severity diagnostics are invisible to their tool-use loop. Apply it on production .NET libraries (NuGet, internal SDKs) where SemVer-major breakage is expensive, services with regular Copilot/Claude/Codex authoring, codebases that have suffered repeated incidents from a single bug class, and multi-target frameworks where TFM consistency matters. The practical value is structural prevention via the compiler: .editorconfig escalates CS8602/CS8618/CS8625, CA2007, VSTHRD100/002, CA2016 and similar IDs to error so the build refuses violating code, TreatWarningsAsErrors stays on at least for Release, and Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.PublicApiAnalyzers with committed PublicAPI.Shipped.txt + PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt files turns every accidental public-surface change (RS0016/RS0017) into a compile failure pinpointing the exact symbol the agent must update or escalate.

methodologies.name: lang-go-tygo-frontend-contract
methodologies.description: Solves the cross-language contract drift that drives full-stack LLM hallucinations: the agent invents a frontend field name that mismatches the backend (or vice versa), tests pass because both ends agreed on the wrong shape, and runtime breaks. Apply it on Go-backend / TypeScript-frontend repos in monorepos or coupled repo pairs, internal RPC endpoints where OpenAPI is overkill but type safety still matters, and any team that has shipped a "wrong field name" production incident. The practical value is mechanical contract enforcement at the toolchain level: Go DTO structs are the single source of truth, tygo generate emits the TypeScript file from AST + JSON tags, the generated .ts is committed, and CI runs `tygo generate && git diff --exit-code` so any drift fails the PR. A required type_mappings block (time.Time → string, uuid.UUID → string, json.RawMessage → unknown) plus a CI grep that fails on emitted any keeps the contract precise rather than punting unknowns to runtime.

methodologies.name: lang-jvm-jreleaser-tag-release
methodologies.description: Solves the sprawl of legacy JVM release pipelines (pom.xml or build.gradle, GPG keys, OSSRH credentials, CHANGELOG hand-edits, Homebrew tap updates) where any one missed step silently breaks downstream consumers, and addresses the June 30 2025 retirement of Sonatype OSSRH that already invalidates configs still pointing at oss.sonatype.org. Apply it on libraries published to Maven Central or other public Maven repos, CLIs distributed via Homebrew/Scoop/SDKMAN/Snap/Chocolatey alongside JARs, polyglot repos with JVM plus Go or Rust artifacts, and any release process containing tribal-knowledge steps an agent would skip. The practical value is one declarative jreleaser.yml triggered by a git tag matching v[0-9]+.[0-9]+.[0-9]+: one CI workflow signs artifacts (GPG/Sigstore, key from env not filesystem), uploads to the new Central Portal endpoint with applyMavenCentralRules, publishes a GitHub Release with conventional-commits notes, and refreshes package-manager descriptors — agents only land code, never edit the deploy YAML.

methodologies.name: lang-php-phpstan9-psalm-taint
methodologies.description: Solves the dynamic-PHP failure modes LLMs reliably emit: untyped mixed parameters and returns that hide errors, plus user-input flowing untracked into mysqli::query, exec, eval, or HTML output. Apply it on Symfony, Laravel, Drupal, or vanilla PHP services with agent contributors, projects with web-facing controllers / ORM builders / file uploads / shell commands, codebases ratcheting strictness over multiple releases, and Packagist-bound libraries where level 10 is feasible. The practical value is two complementary CI gates that must both be green for merge: PHPStan at level 9 (≥ 25-40% faster on 2.1.34's reflection cache) rejects mixed and unshaped arrays so agent output converges on the actual type, while Psalm's --taint-analysis catches SQLi/XSS/command-injection through inter-procedural data-flow and demands a named-sanitizer comment on every escape. The phpstan-baseline.neon is reserved strictly for grandfathered code; new errors there are forbidden.

methodologies.name: lang-ruby-sorbet-strict-floor
methodologies.description: Solves the dynamic-Ruby failure mode where LLMs invent methods, scopes, or association names (e.g., User.find_by_email_and_active) that pass tests but don't exist, by giving Ruby a real static type system tuned for very large codebases (Shopify's Sorbet). Apply it on Rails monoliths over ~50 KLOC with non-trivial agent PRs, multi-team Ruby orgs where a shared type contract reduces cross-team breakage, gems consumed by other internal projects, and Ruby ≥ 3.0 codebases ratcheting from typed: false toward strict. The practical value is a deterministic refusal channel agents can iterate against: every agent-writable file declares # typed: true or strict, Tapioca generates RBIs for every gem (tapioca gem) and Rails DSL (tapioca dsl), and CI runs both `bundle exec srb tc` and `bundle exec tapioca check-shims` plus `tapioca dsl --verify` so adding a gem without its RBI, or editing a model in a way that invalidates the RBI, fails the build instead of crashing on first call.

methodologies.name: lang-swift-harmonize-arch-tests
methodologies.description: Solves the gap that SwiftLint (regex-based) and swift-format (layout-only) leave for architectural intent, where rules like "ViewModels must not import UIKit" or "Domain types must not depend on Network" live only in README prose that LLMs cannot react to. Apply it on multi-target SPM packages and Xcode workspaces, iOS apps following Clean / hexagonal / TCA architectures where layer leaks are the dominant regression class, codebases where AI agents (Xcode 26.3 agentic mode, Cursor-Swift, Claude Code via SwiftAgents) commit production code, and greenfield Swift packages on Swift 5.9+. The practical value is architecture-as-tests in the standard test phase: Harmonize parses the project with SwiftSyntax and exposes a fluent AST API, each layer rule is a Swift Testing @Test (or XCTest) that fails CI with a readable name like ViewModels_must_not_import_UIKit_failed, and the ST-0021 (March 2026) interop lets @Test and XCTest run together via one swift test command — paired with strict swift-format and SwiftLint, the agent's tool-use loop sees the violated invariant by name and self-corrects.

methodologies.name: lint-autofix-vs-flag-decision-rule
methodologies.description: Solves the dominant cause of agents merging confident-looking but wrong patches: treating semantic findings (security, license, design) the same as syntactic ones, so the agent applies a plausible fix that breaks runtime behaviour or hides a real risk. Apply it as the meta-policy wrapping every other lint/SAST/SCA methodology — encoded into the agent's system prompt and into the CI agent's logic so any disqualified fix becomes a draft-PR comment instead of a commit. The practical value is a deterministic four-condition predicate per finding (kind is purely syntactic, the tool exposes a real --fix block, tests pass after, diff is under 50 lines or single rule ID) aligned with BitsAI-Fix, Factory.ai, and DeepMind CodeMender empirical floors. Five canonical classes are always flagged for human review — SAST without fix block, secrets (rotate first via vault then clean), CVEs without a patched version, license conflicts, and SonarQube cognitive-complexity hotspots — because their incorrect autofix has non-local cost that git revert cannot undo cleanly.

methodologies.name: lint-megalinter-polyglot
methodologies.description: Solves the maintenance and reporting fragmentation of running 100+ separate linters across a polyglot repo (Python + TypeScript + Terraform + Markdown + YAML + Dockerfile), giving one CI surface that auto-detects languages, dispatches via Python multiprocessing, and emits unified SARIF for GitHub Code Scanning or GitLab Ultimate. Apply it on repositories with three or more languages or stack zones, monorepos where per-language workflow shards are unwieldy, projects needing uniform SARIF for Advanced Security UIs, and quarterly hygiene scans wanting every linter in one report. The practical value is a clean local-vs-CI split that prevents the dominant anti-pattern of putting MegaLinter into pre-commit (its multi-gigabyte image kills sub-second hooks and trains everyone to --no-verify): native per-language tools (ruff, biome, hadolint, terraform fmt) hold the local hook floor in milliseconds, while a flavor-selected MegaLinter image (cupcake for JS+Py+docs, security/python/javascript/terraform as appropriate) runs only in CI and ratchets up the gate with cross-file rules and a single SARIF feed the agent fixes from.

methodologies.name: lint-precommit-floor
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of broken or unlinted code reaching CI and reviewers by making a pre-commit framework (pre-commit, lefthook, or husky) the deterministic, sub-second gate that runs format, lint, secret-scan, and type-check hooks before the commit object is ever written. Apply it in every code-bearing repository, picking pre-commit for polyglot or Python-heavy stacks, lefthook for monorepos with throughput pressure (roughly 10x faster via parallel Go execution), and husky for pure Node setups; the chosen config must be committed at the repo root and documented in AGENTS.md. The practical value is that the cheapest possible quality floor becomes structural rather than discretionary, and AI agents are bound by contract to fix root causes on hook failure instead of bypassing with --no-verify, which would silently break every downstream check that trusts the local history.

methodologies.name: lint-ruff-and-biome-as-default
methodologies.description: Solves the long-standing chaos of competing Python and JS/TS lint+format toolchains (black, flake8, isort, pyupgrade, ESLint, Prettier disagreeing on the same line) by mandating ruff as the sole Python linter+formatter and biome as the sole JS/TS/JSX/CSS linter+formatter, both single Rust binaries with sub-100ms startup. Apply it to every new project and to migrations off legacy chains, with ruff config consolidated in pyproject.toml under [tool.ruff] and biome config in biome.json, while removing .flake8, .eslintrc, .prettierrc and similar files. The value is a deterministic, sub-second autofix step inside the agent's edit-test-loop, eliminating no-op churn between formatters, covering 900+ ruff rules and Biome v2's type-aware checks, and giving reflexion-friendly inner loops at 10–100x the speed of the previous toolchain.

methodologies.name: lint-shellcheck-hadolint-iac-floor
methodologies.description: Closes the blind spot left by app-code linters like ruff and biome, which never see infrastructure code where production failures (Shellshock-class quoting, rootful Docker images, latest-tag drift, GitHub Actions privilege misuse, Terraform plan drift) most often originate. Apply the full set — shellcheck for shell scripts, hadolint for Dockerfiles, yamllint for YAML, actionlint for GitHub Actions workflows, and tflint plus terraform validate for HCL — at the same hook tier as code linters in every repo containing those formats, with whole-tree CI runs on PR open. The value is that an entire class of operational bugs is caught at the cheapest gate instead of at runtime, and agents are required to fix the source rather than disable rules; any suppression must cite the rule code, include a one-line justification, and reference a tracking issue with a sunset.

methodologies.name: lint-staged-only-not-whole-tree
methodologies.description: Solves the dominant cause of pre-commit hook bypass — slow whole-tree linting that takes 8–12 seconds on a 1500-file monorepo and rewrites unstaged work — by mandating that every linter or formatter wired into a hook receive only the staged file set, while whole-tree variants run exactly once in CI as the final pre-merge gate. Apply it via pre-commit's default pass_filenames, lefthook's {staged_files} template, or lint-staged behind husky, never calling eslint ., ruff check ., or prettier --write . from a hook. The value is hooks that run in roughly 200ms on three staged files, so they remain an invisible quality floor instead of a hated blocker, and graph-aware tools like tsc or mypy use a per-project files: regex with pass_filenames: false to keep type-graph integrity intact.

methodologies.name: mr-codemod-refactor-agent
methodologies.description: Solves the inconsistency of LLM-only cross-cutting refactors — variant-casing slips, broken string templates, drifted import paths across hundreds of call sites — by treating the deterministic 95% as an AST codemod (jscodeshift, ts-morph, libcst, semgrep, codemod.com / Hypermod) and reserving the LLM only for the long-tail 5% where AST has no signal (string templates, prose docs, snapshot fixtures). Apply it to framework upgrades like Angular or AI SDK 5→6, API renames or deprecation removals over 100 sites, lint-rule rollouts, and schema migrations, packaging the work as one PR per logical group with two commits (codemod, then LLM tail-pass) and a CI idempotence gate that re-runs the codemod and asserts git diff --quiet. The value is reviewable, bisectable, audit-logged migrations produced by a three-role pipeline (Architect → Migration → Validator) instead of multi-day manual rewrites.

methodologies.name: mr-error-tracker-draft-pr
methodologies.description: Solves the slow alert→triage→ticket→branch→fix→PR chain for repeating production exceptions by letting the error tracker (Sentry Seer/Autofix, Bugsink, Rollbar, Starsling) ingest the structured signal of stack trace, breadcrumbs, distributed trace, and recent diffs and open a draft PR bidirectionally linked to the alert once both min_events ≥ 10 and min_fixability ≥ 0.7 thresholds clear. Apply it on production runtime errors with clean stack traces and source maps where the first frame maps 1:1 to a code line in a linked repo, while skipping one-off blips, OOM/timeout signals without anchors, and business-logic decisions. The value is that high-signal failures collapse into one reviewable artifact with a four-section PR body (alert URL, root cause, test added, reviewer checklist), guarded by a triad of draft state, dedicated bot identity, and CODEOWNERS-enforced human approval so unreviewed patches never ship.

methodologies.name: mr-graph-vs-diff-reviewer
methodologies.description: Solves the cost-vs-coverage mismatch between AI code reviewers by making the architecture choice explicit: diff-only reviewers (Sourcery, GitHub Copilot Code Review, Codeball, Qodo Merge basic) read the patch plus near neighbours in under 60 seconds and miss cross-module ripple, while graph-indexed reviewers (Greptile, CodeRabbit Pro, Qodo Merge 2.0) build a repo-wide knowledge graph and trace ripple effects at higher latency and cost. Apply diff-only when LOC < 100k with strong module boundaries and a sub-60s feedback target; apply graph-indexed when LOC > 500k, polyglot_languages > 2, weak boundaries, or the dominant bug class is innocent renames breaking downstream callers. The value is reviewer cost and signal both fit the repo's actual shape, with a default of cheap diff-only plus on-demand /review from a graph-indexed bot for risky PRs, avoiding the noise and double-spend of stacking both reviewers on every PR.

methodologies.name: mr-renovate-ai-handoff
methodologies.description: Solves the wasted-token problem of routing every dependency bump through an LLM by splitting work along the semver boundary: Renovate (or Dependabot) auto-merges patch and minor bumps when CI is green and Mend Merge-Confidence is high, while major bumps and security alerts are escalated to a coding agent (Copilot Coding Agent, Claude, Codex, Devin) via an agent-fixable label that opens a draft PR with version bump plus call-site edits plus test fixes. Apply it in any repo with a manifest Renovate covers (npm, PyPI, Go, Rust, Composer, etc.), reserving agent invocation for the breaking 10% that needs code edits, never auto-merging majors. The value is a deterministic fast-path for the 90% Renovate already handles plus a safe escalation lane formalized by GitHub's April 2026 Dependabot→Agent handoff, keeping cost, latency, and risk all within budget.

methodologies.name: mr-slash-command-surface
methodologies.description: Solves the friction of steering PR review bots through dashboards or commit re-pushes by exposing capabilities as in-band PR-comment slash commands (/describe, /review, /improve, /ask), turning every steering action into a comment captured by the audit trail. Apply it to multi-author repos, high-volume PR pipelines (>20/day), open-source projects where contributors lack institutional context, and any repo already running Qodo Merge / PR-Agent, CodeRabbit, or Sourcery. The value is that auto_describe and auto_review stay always-on for the cheap-and-useful 80% while auto_improve remains opt-in to prevent suggestion-patch noise from drowning real review, the issue_comment workflow event is wired so commands actually dispatch, and human-edited reviewer notes survive bot re-runs through HTML-comment-delimited blocks.

methodologies.name: pnpm-catalogs
methodologies.description: Solves dependency version drift in TypeScript/JavaScript monorepos — the recurring "package A wants react@19.0, package B wants react@19.1" merge conflict — by using pnpm 9+ workspaces with the catalog: protocol so each external dependency version lives once in pnpm-workspace.yaml and every package.json references it via "react": "catalog:", with internal packages linked through "workspace:*". Apply it in any monorepo with two or more packages, especially microfrontend or shared-library workspaces and repos where parallel AI agents in worktrees may add deps independently. The value is that one bump updates every workspace atomically, AI agents adding deps cannot accidentally introduce a version split, and pnpm --filter expressions like ...[origin/main] cut CI wall-clock time 5–20x by testing only the affected slice instead of every package recursively.

methodologies.name: pyproject-single-source
methodologies.description: Solves the silent inconsistency of split Python tooling configs (setup.py + setup.cfg + tox.ini + .flake8 + requirements*.txt + pytest.ini) where agents edit one file and forget the others, by concentrating every tool's configuration in a single pyproject.toml with [build-system], [project] (PEP 621), [tool.uv], [tool.ruff], a chosen type checker, [tool.pytest.ini_options], [tool.coverage.run], [tool.bandit], and [tool.mutmut] tables. Apply it to every new Python project, library, service, or CLI, and to any migration where legacy config files still coexist; a CI one-liner fails the build if any forbidden file (setup.cfg, .flake8, tox.ini, pytest.ini, mypy.ini, requirements.txt for first-party deps) reappears. The value is one parseable, diffable, machine-mutable file that AI agents read and mutate reliably, eliminating the drift class where two parallel configs disagree on line length or rule sets.

methodologies.name: sec-codeql-autofix-on-pr
methodologies.description: Solves the lag between SAST findings and remediation by enabling CodeQL Code Scanning on push and pull_request as a required merge-blocking check, with Copilot Autofix posting AI-generated patches as reviewable PR suggestions a human accepts, edits, or rejects rather than auto-merges. Apply it in every GitHub-hosted repo with executable code (especially HTTP, auth, deserialization, SQL/NoSQL or template surfaces), extending coverage to Bash, Dockerfile, Terraform/HCL, and PHP via AI-powered detections in the same pipeline. The value is semantic dataflow analysis catching injection, SSRF, deserialization, and auth-bypass classes pattern matchers miss, while Autofix covers more than 90% of alert types and resolves over two-thirds of findings with little editing, dropping median time-to-fix from roughly 1.5 hours to about 28 minutes; agents read SARIF via gh api and treat new alerts as merge blockers, with dismissals always recorded with a reason for audit.

methodologies.name: sec-secrets-defense-in-depth
methodologies.description: Solves the asymmetric cost of leaked credentials by layering two complementary secret scanners across two lifecycle stages: gitleaks as a sub-second pre-commit hook on staged content blocking secrets before they enter local git history, and TruffleHog with --results=verified in CI scanning the base..head diff and actively calling issuers (AWS STS, GitHub, Stripe, Slack, OpenAI, Anthropic) to confirm tokens are live. Apply it in every repository without exception — even single-developer prototypes — because GitGuardian's 2026 sprawl report measured 29 million new public secrets in 2025 with an 81% surge in AI-service tokens specifically. The value is that obvious leaks are stopped at write time while only verified, exploitable findings reach the merge gate, and the runbook makes issuer-side rotation (not history rewriting) the first action on any verified finding, with confirmation IDs logged in the incident channel for SOC2/ISO 27001 evidence.

methodologies.name: sec-trivy-pinned-supply-chain-scan
methodologies.description: Solves container, IaC, and release-tarball supply-chain risk by running trivy fs and trivy image in CI with --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 to block merges on real vulnerabilities, plus generating a CycloneDX SBOM signed with cosign on every release tag for EU CRA and US EO 14028 compliance. Critically, the trivy-action is referenced by full 40-character commit SHA and the binary version is explicitly pinned, because the March 2026 "TeamPCP" compromise re-pushed malicious commits onto trivy@v0.69.4 and trivy-action@v0.30.0, so floating refs silently shipped a tampered scanner. Apply it in any repo producing a container image, Helm chart, Terraform module, Kubernetes manifest, or release tarball; .trivyignore entries require owner, justification, and # sunset:YYYY-MM-DD with CI failing on expired allowlist items. The value is one scanner replacing four (Anchore + tfsec + dockle + grype), regulatory SBOM coverage, and a hardened pipeline that treats the scanner itself as part of the supply chain.

methodologies.name: task-agent-drafts-spec-before-coding
methodologies.description: Solves the failure mode where AI coding agents jump from a tracker ticket straight to a diff, fabricating intent and consuming a review slot before anyone can correct a misreading, by requiring the agent's first action to be a structured spec comment on the ticket itself with four sections (Current State, Desired State, Proposed Plan as INVEST-decomposed steps, Open Questions) — not a code edit. Apply it whenever an agent is the primary owner of a non-trivial Linear/Jira/GitHub/GitLab ticket with at least one human reviewer who can react; skip it for typo-class agent:auto-approve work, spike research, hot-path incident response, and solo projects with no second reviewer. The value is that misreadings are corrected for the cost of a comment instead of a review cycle, the spec doubles as a permanent audit artifact attached to the ticket, and the approval gate is a positive signal (Linear thumbs-up, Jira /agent approve, github label transition) from a non-bot identity — never silence-as-consent.

methodologies.name: task-agent-fixable-triage-gate
methodologies.description: Solves the wasteful pattern of coding agents (Devin, Copilot Coding Agent, Codex, Cursor Background) spinning on every newly opened ticket — many lacking acceptance criteria, reproducibility, or scope — by inserting a triage gate where agents only listen for an agent-fixable label applied by a human or a read-only triage bot after spam/duplicate/scope checks. Apply it in trackers with more than 50 open issues, mixed agent fleets sharing one backlog, repos with strict CODEOWNERS, and teams adopting their first coding agent; skip it for tiny backlogs or product-discovery tickets that need needs-spec instead. The value is the documented 34% to 67% PR merge-rate jump Cognition reported for Devin in 2025 and a roughly 50x reduction in token waste, with vendor-specific labels (agent:devin, agent:copilot) added by the router only after pickup so agents cannot self-approve their own preconditions and rebalancing across vendor outages stays automatic.

methodologies.name: task-plan-mode-locked-execution
methodologies.description: Solves the prompt-injection and silent-scope-creep failure mode of long autonomous agent runs by requiring any change touching more than three files or running over ~5 minutes to enter read-only Plan Mode first — Edit, Write, MultiEdit and state-mutating Bash stripped from the tool list — produce an explicit plan with four required sections (numbered Steps, Verification commands, Out-of-Scope, Rollback), secure human approval, then execute the locked plan literally without silent re-planning. Apply it to multi-file refactors, schema migrations, dependency major bumps with call-site rewrites, security/PII work, and cross-repo worktree fan-out; skip trivial single-line edits and tasks already gated by spec-kit's tasks.md. The value is the published 2–3x success-rate gain on multi-file tasks and tool-use accuracy moving from 72% to 94%, plus that any plan deviation surfaces as a visible PLAN DEVIATION message requesting re-approval rather than as silent scope expansion the next reviewer must reverse-engineer.

methodologies.name: task-spec-kit-three-step
methodologies.description: Solves the conversational drift of free-form LLM coding sessions — unrecoverable acceptance criteria, silent guesses on ambiguous requirements, no anchor that survives context resets — by running GitHub spec-kit's three-step pipeline /speckit.specify → /speckit.plan → /speckit.tasks before any agent writes code, producing versioned spec.md (WHAT/WHY with [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers), plan.md (HOW with constitution-gate evidence), and tasks.md (parallelizable [P]-tagged work items). Apply it to non-trivial features touching more than ~3 files or one service boundary, regulator-traceable domains (payments, auth, migrations, PII), multi-agent fan-outs needing isolable per-task ACs, and greenfield public APIs; skip true one-line fixes, exploratory spikes, and pure ops work. The value is a constitution of nine immutable principles (library-first, test-first, simplicity gate, anti-abstraction, integration-first testing, etc.) recorded as machine-checked gates with per-principle evidence or CODEOWNER-signed waivers, so the spec acts as a deterministic anchor across model swaps and only tasks.md drives the coding agent.

methodologies.name: task-worktree-runtime-isolation
methodologies.description: Solves the dominant 2026 failure mode of parallel-agent harnesses where teams adopt git worktrees, see filesystem isolation, and assume runtime isolation came with it — but two agents both running npm run dev still collide on port 3000 and two pytest runs against localhost/test_db still corrupt each other's tables. The fix is one task → one branch → one worktree → one agent, with a WT-SCOPE manifest declaring editable globs AND a per-worktree env file allocating PORT from a tracked range, DATABASE_URL with a worktree-unique database name, REDIS_URL with a unique key prefix or DB index, and SECRETS_PROFILE scoped to the slug. Apply it in any concurrent multi-agent run, multi-feature parallel build, fan-out subagent dispatch, model-arena comparisons, and long-running jobs where humans still need the main checkout. The value is git-enforced branch ownership eliminating index-lock contention plus deterministic per-slug runtime resources that make worktrees resumable across crashes and restarts without dangling processes.

methodologies.name: test-consumer-contract-from-spec
methodologies.description: Solves the classic Pact weakness — consumer contracts hand-rolled by humans or LLMs drift from real provider behaviour, mismatch matchers, and produce false greens that hide regressions — by pointing PactFlow's MCP server or the Pact AI skill at the canonical artifact (OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, recorded HAR traffic, typed client code) and generating both the consumer Pact JSON and the matching client test in one reproducible pass committed as the source of truth. Apply it in microservices with more than two consumers per provider, polyglot stacks where each consumer uses a different mock library, agent-authored consumers with access to the provider spec, and third-party API integrations; skip single-consumer monoliths, GraphQL with strong codegen, one-off cron-callers, and providers without any spec artifact. The value is PactFlow's reported up to 60% reduction in test-creation time, deterministic enough output that the provider's release pipeline can run pact-broker can-i-deploy as a required step blocking deployment when any consumer contract for the target environment fails verification, with hand-edits to pacts/*.json explicitly failing CI.

methodologies.name: test-golden-master-legacy-rewrite
methodologies.description: Solves the problem of AI-driven rewrites of untested legacy code that has no spec other than its current observed behaviour, including bugs. Apply it before letting an agent touch a poorly-tested module, during cross-language ports, or for refactors of deterministic transformers where preserving exact output is the success criterion. Capture at least 200 normalized (input, expected_output) pairs from production traffic or fuzzing into a committed JSONL corpus, then gate the rewrite on byte-for-byte parity with per-row review of any intentional divergence. The practical value is a deterministic green signal the agent can iterate against, plus an explicit audit trail for every behavioural change instead of silent semantic drift. Skip it for greenfield code, pervasive nondeterminism that resists normalization, tiny modules, or stateful interactive flows where I/O is not a function of input alone.

methodologies.name: test-mutation-feedback-loop
methodologies.description: Replaces line-coverage gates with mutation testing because agent-written tests routinely hit 90% coverage on assertions that never detect a behaviour change (one teardown reported 93% coverage at 34% mutation score). Apply it to critical business logic like payments, auth and pricing, on PRs authored by coding agents, and when retrofitting tests onto legacy code; scope the runner (Stryker, mutmut, PIT) to changed files with break/low/high thresholds of 70/60/80 to keep CI cost bounded. When CI fails, feed the structured surviving-mutants list back to the agent as concrete targets with the instruction to strengthen assertions rather than touch source under test, terminating only when the score clears or the agent commits an audited equivalent-mutant justification. Meta's ACH work showed 73% of LLM-suggested tests merged at Messenger and WhatsApp under this loop. Skip it for slow suites where mutation cost is unaffordable, equivalent-heavy generated code, churning prototypes, or UI work without checkable invariants.

methodologies.name: test-property-based-llm-invariants
methodologies.description: Solves the gap that example-based tests structurally cannot close: edge cases the developer never thought of, especially in pure transformations like serialization, parsing, math, sorting and schema validation. Apply it whenever a function has a docstring, type signature or oracle implementation that lets an agent classify properties under one of five named patterns (round-trip, idempotence, oracle-equivalence, metamorphic, invariant-on-output) and write them as Hypothesis, fast-check or proptest tests; require every property to carry a quoted source-fragment grounding comment so review is O(seconds) per property and ungrounded hallucinations are deleted rather than weakened. The practical value is shrinking failures to minimal counter-examples instead of one-off cases, reproducing the conditions that let Anthropic's red-team agent surface 984 bug reports across 786 modules at a 56% true-bug rate. Skip it for stateful side-effecting code, UI rendering, tautological specs, or tight CI windows where shrinking exceeds the budget.

methodologies.name: test-self-healing-locators-audited
methodologies.description: Addresses silent test rot in Playwright, Cypress or Selenium suites where AI healers rewrite broken selectors without review and end up clicking the wrong button on a payment screen. Apply it on large E2E suites with frequent UI churn, design-system migrations, or projects already using role-based locators; restrict heal candidates to those matching the original locator's accessibility role and accessible name (about 10 times more stable than DOM-class heals per Microsoft's benchmark) and forbid tag, class, XPath or text-only fallbacks. Force every accepted heal through a one-way valve: write the change to a committed `healed-selectors.diff`, fail CI nonzero, and require an explicit `healer:approved` review marker before a separate apply job consumes the diff, with high-stakes flows excluded entirely via policy. The practical value is roughly 75% recovery on selector-related failures and around 33% maintenance-time reduction without losing the human review gate that prevents wrong-click incidents in production. Skip it for payment, login, role-elevation, or pixel-coordinate suites and for A/B variants where the same role+name appears twice.

methodologies.name: test-tdd-red-green-split-agents
methodologies.description: Stops single-context LLMs from cheating the TDD loop by editing the test until both files pass, which they do reliably when one agent owns both directories. Apply it to agent-driven feature work with testable acceptance criteria, bug-fix tickets with reproducible regressions, and greenfield modules with a known public API. Split the loop into two sub-agents with disjoint tool permissions (RED gets Read+Write restricted to `tests/`; GREEN gets Read+Edit restricted to `src/`) and have an orchestrator run the test command between turns to assert a real failing-to-passing transition, aborting on first-try-pass and forwarding only the failure stanza to GREEN so the AC cannot leak implementation hints. The practical value is mechanical enforcement of TDD discipline at the tool-permission layer instead of trust-based prompting, with reported convergence behaviour single-context loops never achieve. Skip it for UI styling, exploratory spikes, untestable artefacts, throwaway scripts, and refactors of code already covered by tests.

methodologies.name: tracker-ai-triage-classify-route
methodologies.description: Solves the manual-triage bottleneck that emerges around 50 inbound issues per week, where classification, severity scoring, deduplication and routing each cost minutes of engineer time per ticket. Apply it on high-volume queues with at least three months of historical assignment data, multi-team monorepos that already maintain CODEOWNERS, or as a drop-in via Linear's Triage Intelligence and Jira's AI Backlog. Run the agent through six fixed-order steps — classify type, score severity with SLA timer, dedupe by 0.92 cosine over the last 1000 issues, label, route via CODEOWNERS, assign to the least-loaded engineer — gating blockers behind on-call confirmation and short-circuiting on duplicates so the most expensive model calls do not fire on the 15-25% duplicate rate. The agent posts exactly one transparency comment with one row per field so any wrong decision can be vetoed with an `:x:` reaction, and the comment is edited in place to preserve the audit chain. Skip it on small teams, security-sensitive misclassification domains, cold-start projects without history, and solo single-repo work.

methodologies.name: tracker-github-copilot-workspace
methodologies.description: Drives ticket-to-PR work through Copilot Workspace's four explicit gates — spec, plan, diff, PR — to address the roughly 30% agentic PR fail-rate GitHub reported in April 2026 when intermediate gates were skipped. Apply it on GitHub-hosted single-repo issues with concrete acceptance criteria, bug fixes and small-to-medium features where reviewers want to see the agent's chain-of-thought, and greenfield bootstrap tickets where the spec gate is the only chance to align direction before scaffolding lands. The agent emits each artefact in strict order, waits for an explicit human approval event before advancing, and refuses to skip a gate; every PR body must carry both `Closes|Fixes #N` on its own line and a stable `Workspace snapshot:` URL, both CI-enforced via grep so editing them out re-blocks merge. The practical value is reviewable artefacts at the cheapest correction points (spec catches misread requirements, plan catches wrong-file changes) instead of forcing reviewers to reconstruct intent from the diff. Skip it for multi-repo work, SLA-critical paths, autonomous Copilot coding-agent flows, and trivial typos.

methodologies.name: tracker-gitlab-duo-developer-flow
methodologies.description: Routes every GitLab issue-to-MR transition through the Duo Agent Platform's named flows so agent runs inherit protected-branch rules, CODEOWNERS, approval policies and audit attribution instead of bypassing them via side webhooks or admin PATs. Apply it on self-managed or GitLab.com tenants needing native agent runs, pipeline-heavy projects where CI/CD Flows compose with Developer Flow, SAST-heavy workflows where Code Review Flow's `security` standard handles false-positive filtering once, and on-prem orgs that cannot use cloud-only platforms. Declare flows in `.gitlab/duo-flows.yaml` triggered only by issue label transitions or scheduled pipelines, chain Developer Flow into Code Review Flow for the canonical pipeline, and enforce a hard GitLab 18.8 floor at config-load because pre-18.8 deployments deadlock under concurrent label triggers per incident #21171. The practical value is one auditable surface for every agent action, with the project's existing reviewer matrix doing the gating instead of a parallel approval system. Skip it for pre-18.8 GitLab, multi-cloud orgs requiring context outside the GitLab data plane, SLA-tight incident response, and solo projects.

methodologies.name: tracker-jira-rovo-mcp-agents
methodologies.description: Makes Atlassian's Rovo Remote MCP server the single authenticated bridge between AI agents and Jira, Confluence and JSM so that agents inherit the assignee's permissions, project configuration and workflow rules instead of holding admin-scoped bot tokens. Apply it on enterprise Jira Cloud where SOC2 audit-log inheritance is non-negotiable, teams already on Jira plus JSM that want one MCP for engineering and ITSM, and stacks needing workflow-embedded automation rather than side webhooks. Forbid raw REST API access and shared service accounts, log every action's MCP audit ID locally as the linkable record, and wire agents in as on_enter handlers on workflow statuses (In Design, Ready for Implementation) with the next transition gated through Jira's native approver list rather than Slack reactions. The practical value is collapsing the bot-account anti-pattern into the same identity layer Jira already enforces for humans, removing the need for a parallel approval system and giving auditors one surface where agent activity is legible. Skip it for self-hosted Data Center, latency-tight inner loops, workflows that need to bypass project config, and solo projects where OAuth overhead exceeds value.

methodologies.name: tracker-linear-agent-as-assignee
methodologies.description: Treats AI coding agents (Cursor, Devin, Claude Code, Copilot) as first-class Linear assignees with their own user identity instead of as shared bot accounts, so the issue history attributes each action to a specific agent and existing notification, SLA and triage rules apply unchanged. Apply it on self-contained engineering tickets with concrete acceptance criteria in the issue body, high-volume triage backlogs feeding the agent through Triage Intelligence, single-repo bug fixes where Code Intelligence plus an MCP server supplies enough context, and teams that want agent attribution in audit trails without a separate identity service. Subscribe the agent to IssueAssigned webhooks, refuse work learned about through any other channel, and require checkpoint comments on every state transition with a final PR-link comment, while authenticating to Linear's hosted MCP under the bot-user's bounded OAuth scope (member role, no workspace admin, no reuse of the human's token). The practical value is SOC2-grade attribution, cheap rollback via thumbs-down on checkpoints before a PR review burns, and one surface (the issue) for tracking agent progress. Skip it for ambiguous discovery tickets, cross-repo refactors, work needing live user research, and self-hosted environments.

methodologies.name: ts-strict-isolated
methodologies.description: Raises TypeScript's compile-time precision so that AI-generated code surfaces type errors instead of runtime bugs, because LLMs statistically prefer permissive shapes (`any`, unchecked index access, optional fields they never set). Apply it as the default on every new TS project, every monorepo with two or more packages, every published library, and any codebase larger than ~10k lines or with multiple agents editing concurrently. Every `tsconfig.json` enables `strict`, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` and `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`; libraries additionally enable `isolatedDeclarations` plus `composite` with project `references` so non-tsc tools (Biome, oxc, swc) can emit `.d.ts` 10-50x faster and `tsc --build` runs incrementally instead of rechecking the world. The practical value is sub-second feedback for the agentic inner loop, stable public APIs that cannot rely on inference, and 3-10x CI speedups on large monorepos. Skip it on throwaway scripts, app entrypoints that emit no `.d.ts` (keep strict but drop isolatedDeclarations), and codebases mid-migration where flipping flags would block trunk for weeks.

methodologies.name: uv-lockfile-floor
methodologies.description: Standardizes Python projects on Astral's `uv` for environment management, dependency resolution, lockfile and interpreter installation so AI coding agents cannot silently drift the env between calls. Apply it on every new Python 3.10+ project, when migrating from Poetry, pip-tools or `requirements.txt`, in multi-Python-version repos, and in any repo where agents will run pytest, type-checkers or scripts. Commit `uv.lock` as the single source of truth, install in CI with `uv sync --frozen`, mutate dependencies only via `uv add`/`uv remove`/`uv lock --upgrade`, and wrap every tool call in `uv run` (never bare `pytest` or `python`) so the lockfile is re-verified on every invocation. The practical value is reproducible installs across machines, ~10x faster resolution than pip-tools or Poetry, and a contract Codex now bundles natively after OpenAI's March 2026 acquisition of Astral. Skip it for conda/CUDA scientific stacks where pixi is better, legacy-Python or unusual private-registry repos, single-file scripts, and projects that must keep `requirements.txt` for an external consumer until that consumer learns `uv.lock`.
